docs.title: Neuro Scutbook
docs.text:
A bedside neurology reference for MDs, NPs, PAs, and medics — how to work up and manage a patient with neurological symptoms in the clinic, ER, or on the wards, the way a neurology consult would. Every topic pairs a prescriptive consult workflow with a plain-language evidence brief and a standardized military-medicine layer.

Headache triage Acute stroke Military medicine

Fastest way in

Press / anywhere and type a symptom, drug, or trial name.

Disclaimer — read first

This site is educational clinical decision-support. It is not a substitute for professional clinical judgment, formal consultation, or individual medical advice, and it does not establish a provider–patient relationship. Dosing, protocols, and military standards change — verify everything against current guidelines, product labeling, and the governing regulation before acting on it. Use reflects your own professional responsibility.

docs.text:
• Headache

    Red flags and SNNOOP10 triage, then dosed order sets for each phenotype.

    Migraine · Cluster & TACs · Trigeminal neuralgia · Medication overuse · NDPH · Post-traumatic

    Triage hub

  • Stroke & cerebrovascular

    Nine pages, from the acute thrombolysis/thrombectomy window through prevention and the less-common vasculopathies.

    Acute ischemic stroke · TIA · Prevention · ICH · SAH · CVST · Dissection · CAA · Carotid disease

    Start with acute stroke

  • Seizure & epilepsy

    The status epilepticus ladder, ILAE 2022 syndromes, and an antiseizure-medication formulary with interactions and IV↔PO conversion.

    Status epilepticus · Syndromes · ASMs

    Seizure & status epilepticus

  • Traumatic brain injury

    Acute ER management and return-to-activity, plus ten comorbidity sub-pages — the domains that actually drive persistent symptoms after mTBI.

    Headache · Sleep · Mood & PTSD · Cognitive · Vestibular · Vision · Neuroendocrine · Autonomic · Seizures · Pain & substance

    TBI work-up & return to duty

  • Demyelinating disease

    MS on the 2024 McDonald criteria — and the two diseases that are not MS and must not be treated as such.

    Multiple sclerosis · NMOSD · MOGAD

    Multiple sclerosis

  • Neuromuscular junction

    Fatigable and acute weakness at the synapse — the autoimmune prototype, its mimics, and two toxins including a CBRN emergency.

    Myasthenia gravis · LEMS · Botulism · Nerve agent / organophosphate

    Myasthenia gravis

docs.title: The military layer
docs.text:
• Profiles, MEB/MAR2 & deployability

    The question providers actually get stuck on: do I board this Soldier, when, and am I the one who starts it? Answered from AR 40-501, AR 40-502, DA Pam 40-502, DoDI 6130.03, and DoDM 1332.18 — including the MAR2-versus-DES fork, who signs, the real day thresholds, and the full IDES timeline.

    Open the military reference

Every clinical page also carries its own Military medicine box applying that framework to the condition — deployability, profile, EPTS/LOD, retention standards, and MEB/MAR2 referral.

docs.title: How to use this
docs.text:
• On call or at the bedside? Open the topic and read top-to-bottom: red flags → rapid approach → workup → tiered Step 1→2→3 order sets with doses → disposition.
  • Prescribing? Look for the TRICARE box wherever drugs appear — preferred versus PA-gated, and what the authorization needs.
  • Studying or teaching? Jump to the Discover brief at the bottom of each page for the evidence and the key trials.
  • Search — press / anywhere and type a symptom, drug, or trial name.

How current is this?

Every page carries a visible Last reviewed stamp and a Verified footer that states what was checked against source, what is conference or press-release data rather than peer-reviewed, and what still needs confirmation before you act on it. Where a recommendation rests on observational rather than randomized evidence, the page says so.

39 pages across headache, cerebrovascular disease, seizure, traumatic brain injury, demyelinating disease, neuromuscular-junction disorders, and military medicine — with more topics added one at a time, each updated to current evidence before it goes up.

docs.title: How to add a topic
docs.text:
Every clinical page follows the "Headache" format — the tiered, prescriptive style used across the Headache and Stroke pages. The canonical reference implementation is docs/headache/migraine.md; the fill-in skeleton is includes/topic-template.md. To add a page:

  1. Copy includes/topic-template.md to docs/<area>/<topic>.md.
  2. Fill in each section. Delete sections that truly don't apply, but keep the order and the format hallmarks below.
  3. Add the page to the nav: list in mkdocs.yml.
  4. Preview locally (mkdocs serve), build with mkdocs build --strict, then commit and push — the site redeploys automatically.

docs.title: Format hallmarks (the "Headache" style — don't drop these)
docs.text:
• Prescriptive, not just comprehensive. The reader should know exactly what to do at each step — this is "what the neurologist wants you to do first, second, third."
  • A "The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)" numbered block near the top of management.
  • Tiered !!! orderset boxes — Step 1 → 2 → 3 — with a DOSE on every drug and an explicit "what defines failure / when to escalate" between tiers.
  • Decision tables (comorbidity → agent, situation → target) and === "tabs" for parallel option sets.
  • A !!! tip "TRICARE — …" box wherever drugs are prescribed — name the preferred/no-PA go-to vs the PA-gated agents (and what the PA requires); always tell the reader to verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.
  • The standardized !!! military box (fixed 5 fields).
  • A "📚 Discover" evidence brief, linked References, and a *Verified MON YYYY: …* footer that states what was source-checked and flags anything that could not be confirmed.

docs.title: The standard sections (in order)
docs.text:
1. Title + Last reviewed: MON YYYY stamp
  2. One-liner + who it's for
  3. 🚩 Red flags / when to image now (!!! danger)
  4. Diagnosis / classification (quote criteria exactly)
  5. Rapid approach (numbered)
  6. The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
  7. Workup (labs / imaging / LP — an !!! orderset if there's a mandatory set)
  8. Management — tiered !!! orderset Step 1→2→3 with doses, decision tables, Avoid box
  9. TRICARE prescribing box (!!! tip) wherever drugs appear
  10. Disposition
  11. Military box (!!! military)
  12. 📚 Discover — evidence brief
  13. References + *Verified MON YYYY* footer

docs.title: Custom callout boxes
docs.text:
This site defines two custom admonitions in addition to the Material defaults:

!!! danger "🚩 Red flags"
    Emergent / must-not-miss items.

!!! orderset "Step 1 — first-line"
    Copy-pasteable, dosed orders. Tier as Step 1 → 2 → 3.

!!! tip "TRICARE — <topic> prescribing"
    Preferred/no-PA go-to vs PA-gated agents; verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

!!! military "Military medicine — <topic>"
    The five fields: deployability, profile, EPTS/LOD, retention, MEB/IDES.

docs.title: Ground rules
docs.text:
• Copyright: write Discover briefs in your own words and cite. Source PDFs are a reading list — never paste from Continuum, UpToDate, or similar.
  • Currency: update content to current evidence before stamping a review date. No hand-waved dates.
  • De-identify: no PII, no real lot numbers, no patient specifics.
  • Military content: published-doctrine level only; verify against current AR 40-501 / DoDI 1332.18 / aeromedical policy; nothing sensitive.
  • Disclaimer: the site-wide educational-use disclaimer applies to every page.

docs.title: Abbreviations
docs.text: Common acronyms are defined once in includes/abbreviations.md and auto-expand on hover across every page. Add new ones there rather than redefining them per page.

docs.title: Headache — overview & triage
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

The first job in any headache patient is not to diagnose migraine — it is to exclude a secondary headache that will hurt the patient if you miss it. Triage on red flags first, then treat the primary headache.

Scale of the problem (GBD 2023): headache disorders affect ~2.9 billion people and are the 6th leading cause of health loss worldwide, with ~2× the disability burden in women. Tension-type headache is the most prevalent (~25%), but migraine causes ~90% of headache disability — and medication overuse drives a large share of both, so the MOH screen below earns its place.

🚩 Red flags — the full SNNOOP10 screen (any hit changes your pathway)

Flag Worry Do now S — Systemic symptoms (fever, weight loss) Meningitis, abscess, systemic illness Labs, LP (image first if focal/↓LOC/papilledema) S — Secondary risk factors (HIV, immunosuppression) Opportunistic infection, abscess LP + targeted imaging N — Neoplasm history Brain metastasis, primary tumor MRI brain with contrast N — Neurologic deficit / ↓ consciousness / seizure Mass, stroke, encephalitis MRI (CT if emergent) O — Onset sudden / thunderclap (max <1 min) SAH, RCVS, dissection, CVST, apoplexy Non-contrast CT (6-h rule below) → LP/CTA/MRV O — Older age (new/progressive >50) GCA, mass ESR/CRP (start steroids if GCA likely), MRI P — Pattern change / progressive New secondary process Re-work up as a new headache P — Papilledema ↑ICP (IIH, mass, CVST) MRI + MRV, then LP with opening pressure P — Positional — worse standing Low pressure (SIH — CSF leak) MRI (pachymeningeal enhancement/brain sag) → blood patch P — Positional — worse lying / on waking High pressure (IIH, mass) MRI/MRV → LP opening pressure P — Precipitated by Valsalva / exertion / sex SAH, RCVS, posterior-fossa lesion CT + CTA head/neck; MRI P — Pregnancy / puerperium Pre-eclampsia, CVST, RCVS, PRES, apoplexy See the pregnancy box below P — Painful red eye ± autonomic features Acute angle-closure glaucoma (fixed mid-dilated pupil, nausea — vision loss in hours), cavernous sinus, Tolosa-Hunt, pituitary Check IOP / ophthalmology STAT; MRI P — Post-traumatic onset Structural injury / PTH Head-CT rule; see PTH page P — Pathology of the immune system Opportunistic infection, PML, lymphoma LP + MRI with contrast P — Painkiller overuse or a new drug MOH; drug effect Medication review; count acute-med days

Reassuring: an established, stereotyped, self-resolving headache with a normal exam and no red flags — that is where primary headache lives.

Thunderclap workup — the 6-hour CT rule

  • Non-contrast CT within 6 h of onset is ~99% sensitive for SAH — a negative CT in a neurologically intact patient presenting <6 h reasonably excludes SAH without an LP (2023 AHA/ASA).
  • >6 h from onset: CT sensitivity falls (~86–97%) — do an LP for xanthochromia if suspicion remains, and CTA/MRV for RCVS/dissection/CVST.
  • The Ottawa SAH Rule (100% sensitive) can screen out low-likelihood patients before imaging.

When NOT to image (AHS Grade A) — avoid the reflex MRI

Neuroimaging is not needed for headaches consistent with migraine when the neurologic exam is normal and there are no atypical features or red flags (AHS 2020). The prevalence of a significant intracranial abnormality in that group is ~0.2%. Over-imaging drives cost, incidental findings, and anxiety — reserve it for a red-flag hit (relevant where imaging access is limited, e.g., deployed/austere settings).

On the horizon (not clinic-ready): blood biomarkers (GFAP, neurofilament light) are being studied to separate serious secondary from primary headache — promising, not yet validated for use.

docs.title: Rapid approach
docs.text:
1. Screen for red flags (SNNOOP10 table). Any hit → secondary work-up pathway.
  2. Normal exam + no red flags? Classify the primary headache by phenotype.
  3. Route to the diagnosis:
    • Recurrent, moderate–severe, disabling, ± photophobia/phonophobia/nausea/aura → Migraine (if it reached neurology, it is almost never "just tension")
      • Quick screen — ID Migraine (<2 min, AHS-endorsed): in the last 3 months, did headaches (1) limit your activities ≥1 day? (2) cause nausea? (3) make you sensitive to light? ≥2 "yes" → migraine (≈84% sensitive, 93% PPV).
      • "Sinus headache" is migraine until proven otherwise — up to 88% of self-diagnosed sinus headaches meet migraine criteria (recurrent, predictable, photophobia/nausea, normal exam → migraine, not antibiotics).
    • Headache during or after SARS-CoV-2 infection → treat by phenotype (usually migraine- or tension-type-like); COVID is a recognized trigger for NDPH — re-work up if the pattern changes.
    • Bilateral, pressing/tightening, mild–moderate, NOT worsened by activity, no nausea → tension-type headache — analgesics (ibuprofen, high-dose acetaminophen) ± amitriptyline if frequent (VA/DoD 2023). But if it's disabling or frequent enough to reach you, reconsider migraine.
    • Severe unilateral attacks with ipsilateral autonomic features (tearing, ptosis, rhinorrhea), restless/agitated → Trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias (cluster, paroxysmal hemicrania, SUNCT/SUNA)
    • Brief, electric, triggered stabs in a trigeminal division → Trigeminal neuralgia — and remember MS can present this way
    • Neck-driven pain with reduced cervical range of motion, reproducible from the neck → cervicogenic headache — manual therapy/PT ± nerve blocks (a top-3 secondary headache per VA/DoD; common in service members with neck trauma/load-bearing)
    • Headache ≥15 days/month with acute-med overuse → Medication overuse headache
    • Daily and unremitting from a remembered onset, no prior headache history → New daily persistent headache
    • Headache after head/neck trauma → Post-traumatic headache

docs.title: Don't-miss secondary headaches (quick recognition)
docs.text:
RCVS — recurrent thunderclaps, and do NOT give steroids

Reversible cerebral vasoconstriction syndrome — the 2nd commonest cause of thunderclap headache after SAH. Recurrent thunderclaps over days–weeks, often triggered by vasoactive drugs, sexual activity, exertion, or the postpartum state; may cause convexity (not basal) SAH, and CTA/MRA shows "string of beads" vasoconstriction. Use the RCVS² score (≥5 → ~90% sensitive/~99% specific; ≤2 excludes it). Steroids worsen RCVS — the key distinction from primary CNS angiitis. Treat with nimodipine + trigger avoidance.

IIH vs SIH — same "positional," opposite pressure and treatment

  • Idiopathic intracranial hypertension (IIH — high pressure): young, overweight woman, daily headache + transient visual obscurations + pulsatile tinnitus + papilledema. Dx: MRI/ MRV (exclude mass/CVST) → LP opening pressure >250 mm. Rx: weight loss + acetazolamide; surgery (ONSF, CSF shunt, venous stenting) for progressive vision loss (untreated → permanent visual loss in 15–20%). GLP-1 receptor agonists are an emerging option.
  • Spontaneous intracranial hypotension (SIH — low pressure): orthostatic headache (worse standing, better lying) from a CSF leak; MRI shows pachymeningeal enhancement + brain sag. Rx: epidural blood patch. (Opposite positional pattern and opposite treatment from IIH — don't conflate them.)

GCA — don't wait for the biopsy

New headache >50 with jaw claudication, scalp tenderness, visual symptoms, or PMR → ESR/CRP + temporal artery exam. If GCA is likely, start high-dose corticosteroids immediately (vision loss is preventable but irreversible) and arrange temporal-artery biopsy / vascular imaging.

Pregnancy & postpartum headache — a broad, dangerous differential

Secondary causes underlie >⅓ of acute headaches referred for neuro consult in pregnancy. Actively consider: pre-eclampsia/eclampsia, CVST, RCVS, PRES, cervical artery dissection, pituitary apoplexy, and post-dural-puncture headache.

  • Pregnancy-specific red flags: third trimester, elevated BP, thrombocytopenia, elevated liver enzymes or creatinine.
  • Imaging: MRI preferred (limit gadolinium); non-contrast CT acceptable in emergencies.
  • Activate a maternal stroke team (vascular neurology + maternal-fetal medicine + obstetric anesthesiology) for stroke-like symptoms (2026 AHA/ASA).

docs.title: History & exam essentials
docs.text:
History — cover the core elements: onset & tempo, character, location, severity, duration & frequency, associated symptoms (aura, nausea, photophobia, autonomic), triggers, medications (including acute-med days/month — the MOH screen), and comorbidities (mood, sleep, prior head/neck trauma). Have every patient keep a headache diary (frequency, duration, acute-med days, triggers) for ≥1 month — it's the single most useful diagnostic and monitoring tool.

Exam: vitals (including BP and temperature), fundoscopy for papilledema, cranial nerves, visual fields, focal motor/sensory screen, gait, neck stiffness, and temporal artery palpation in older patients. A normal neuro exam is a large part of what makes a primary headache diagnosis safe.

The headache vertical is complete: Migraine, Cluster & TACs, Trigeminal neuralgia, Medication overuse headache, New daily persistent headache, and Post-traumatic headache.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Do TP, et al. Red and orange flags for secondary headaches (SNNOOP10 list). Neurology. 2019.
  2. Department of Veterans Affairs / DoD. Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management of Headache (2023) — the current DoD/VA guideline (TTH, cervicogenic, MOH, gepants/ditans, neuromodulation).
  3. GBD 2023 Headache Collaborators. Global burden of headache disorders, 1990–2023. Lancet Neurol. 2025 (~2.9 billion affected; 6th leading cause of health loss).
  4. Robbins MS. Diagnosis and Management of Headache: A Review. JAMA. 2021 ("sinus headache" = migraine pitfall).
  5. Screening & imaging: Schwedt TJ, et al. Routine migraine screening — AHS position statement (Headache 2025) and Lipton RB, et al. ID Migraine validation (Neurology 2003); Evans RW, et al. AHS neuroimaging-for-migraine guideline (Headache 2020 — Grade A, no imaging if normal exam/no flags).
  6. Rocha EA, et al. RCVS² score. Neurology. 2019; Singhal AB. RCVS review. Int J Stroke. 2023.
  7. SAH work-up: 2023 AHA/ASA aneurysmal SAH guideline; Dubosh NM, et al. 6-h CT sensitivity (Stroke 2016); Perry JJ, et al. Ottawa SAH Rule / 6-h CT rule (Stroke 2020).
  8. CVT: Saposnik G, et al. Diagnosis and Management of Cerebral Venous Thrombosis — AHA scientific statement (Stroke 2024; D-dimer sensitivity falls to ~74% in isolated-headache presentations).
  9. COVID: Mitsikostas DD, et al. Headache attributed to SARS-CoV-2 — systematic review (Eur J Neurol 2024); AAPM&R PASC guidance (2023).
  10. Pregnancy: ACOG Headaches in Pregnancy and Postpartum CPG No. 3 (2022); AHA Maternal Stroke scientific statement (2026).

Verified July 2026: the red-flag screen follows the full SNNOOP10 list (Do 2019); the 6-hour non-contrast CT rule for thunderclap/SAH and the Ottawa SAH Rule reflect the 2023 AHA/ASA SAH guidance; TTH and cervicogenic headache are added per the 2023 VA/DoD Headache CPG; RCVS (RCVS² score, no-steroids rule), the IIH-vs-SIH positional distinction, GCA, and the pregnancy/ postpartum differential follow current review/guideline sources; burden figures are from GBD 2023. ID Migraine (≥2/3 → migraine), the AHS Grade-A "don't image stable migraine" rule, the "sinus headache = migraine" pitfall, post-COVID headache, the headache-diary/structured-history recommendation, acute angle-closure glaucoma, and the emerging (not-clinic-ready) GFAP/NfL biomarker note were added from the cited sources. Specific epidemiologic and score figures were drawn from those sources — confirm before quoting in a formal document.

docs.title: Cluster headache & the trigeminal autonomic cephalalgias
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: the TACs are severe, strictly unilateral headaches with ipsilateral cranial autonomic features (tearing, conjunctival injection, ptosis/miosis, rhinorrhea, eyelid edema). They are separated from one another — and from migraine — mainly by attack duration, frequency, and treatment response. Get these right and the treatment falls out of the diagnosis.

🚩 Red flags — image every new TAC at least once

Despite the classic teaching that self-resolving attacks are benign, a new TAC deserves an MRI brain (with attention to the pituitary and cavernous sinus) to exclude a secondary mimic — pituitary lesions and posterior-fossa pathology can reproduce a TAC phenotype. Image urgently for: fixed Horner's/other cranial-nerve deficit, abnormal exam, side-locked but atypical features, or thunderclap onset.

docs.title: Diagnose by the clock (ICHD-3)
docs.text:
Disorder Attack length Frequency Signature Treatment key Cluster headache 15–180 min 1 every other day → 8/day Agitated/restless; circadian & seasonal bouts O₂ + SC/nasal triptan; verapamil prevention Paroxysmal hemicrania 2–30 min >5/day (>half the time) Female-predominant Absolute response to indomethacin SUNCT / SUNA 1–600 sec ≥1/day, often many Cutaneous triggers (touch, chewing) Lamotrigine; IV lidocaine for crisis Hemicrania continua continuous continuous + exacerbations Background pain with autonomic flares Absolute response to indomethacin

If a strictly unilateral headache with autonomic features responds completely to indomethacin, it is paroxysmal hemicrania or hemicrania continua — a diagnosis you don't want to miss because it is so treatable (the "indomethacin test").

It's under-diagnosed — that's why the clock matters

Mean delay from onset to correct diagnosis is ~5–6 years; cluster is routinely mislabeled as migraine (~25%), tension-type (~19%), or sinusitis (~14%), and women are misdiagnosed more often than men (~61% vs 46%). If a patient describes strictly unilateral, autonomic, clockwork attacks with agitation — call it cluster and treat it, don't recycle a sinusitis diagnosis.

🚩 Screen for depression and suicidality — the "suicide headache"

Cluster carries a markedly elevated suicide risk — attempted suicide ~3.9× the general population (95% CI 2.6–5.8), independent of comorbid depression, and a 2025 cohort confirmed elevated attempted and completed suicide across headache disorders (strongest in ED/inpatient settings). The driver is demoralization during attacks, not classic depression. Ask every cluster patient about mood and suicidal ideation, and treat the headache aggressively — control is the intervention.

docs.title: Rapid approach
docs.text:
1. Confirm strictly unilateral pain with ipsilateral autonomic features and a normal interictal exam.
  2. Image once (MRI, pituitary views) to exclude a secondary cause.
  3. Time the attacks → place them in the table above.
  4. Cluster: at the same visit, start all three — an abortive (O₂ + SC/nasal triptan), a transitional bridge, and a preventive. Clusters are too disabling to titrate slowly.
  5. Short-lasting + autonomic: run the indomethacin trial before anything else.

docs.title: Cluster headache — the prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Confirm + image once (MRI with pituitary views).
  2. Abortive: high-flow oxygen + sumatriptan 6 mg SC (or nasal triptan) — script both, including a home oxygen setup.
  3. Bridge: a prednisone taper or a greater occipital nerve block to cover the gap until the preventive works.
  4. Preventive: verapamil (with the ECG protocol) — start the same day.
  5. Episodic cluster not controlled on verapamil: add galcanezumab 300 mg SC monthly.
  6. Refractory / chronic: neurology referral; lithium, topiramate, or combinations; occipital-nerve stimulation in specialist hands.

docs.title: Acute (abortive) treatment
docs.text:
Step 1 — abort the attack (give oxygen AND a triptan; they're complementary)

Both are Level A. Oral drugs are useless — attacks peak in minutes.

  • High-flow oxygen: 100% at 12–15 L/min via a true non-rebreather mask, 15–20 min, sitting forward. NNT ≈ 2. The commonest "oxygen failure" is a setup failure — a standard nasal cannula or a mask without a reservoir bag won't work. Insist on a non-rebreather. A demand-valve (or Optimask) system may beat a simple mask — patient-preferred (~62%) and it cut rescue-medication use in a crossover trial — so try a demand valve before declaring oxygen a failure.
  • Sumatriptan 6 mg SC — fastest and most effective (relief in ~15 min). Max 2 injections/24 h.
  • Zolmitriptan 5–10 mg nasal or sumatriptan 20 mg nasal — when SC isn't available or is declined (10 mg zolmitriptan NNT ≈ 2–3).
  • Non-invasive vagus nerve stimulation (gammaCore) — evidence-based adjunct/abortive for episodic cluster; useful when triptans are contraindicated or overused.

Do not use for acute cluster

Oral triptans, opioids, and simple analgesics — all too slow for an attack that peaks in minutes. Opioids additionally worsen the course.

docs.title: Transitional bridge (start with the preventive, same visit)
docs.text:
Step 2 — bridge the gap while the preventive ramps up

A preventive takes 1–2+ weeks to work; a bridge suppresses attacks now. Pick one:

  • Greater occipital nerve block (GON block) — suboccipital corticosteroid ± local anesthetic on the symptomatic side; RCT-supported and fast. First choice if you can do it.
  • Prednisone taper — e.g., 60–100 mg/day × 5 days, then taper over ~2–3 weeks. Widely used and effective as a bridge; not for chronic maintenance (steroid toxicity).

docs.title: Prevention
docs.text:
Step 3 — verapamil first-line, with a mandatory ECG protocol

  • Verapamil — start 240 mg/day, titrate by ~80 mg every ~1–2 weeks to effect (commonly 360–480 mg/day; specialists go higher). Use it for the whole bout.
  • ECG protocol (non-negotiable): baseline ECG, then before each dose increase and ~10–14 days after, watching for PR prolongation, heart block, and bradycardia. Verapamil conduction effects are dose-dependent and can appear late.
  • Constipation is the dose-limiting nuisance — start a bowel regimen.
If verapamil is inadequate or contraindicated
  • Galcanezumab (Emgality) 300 mg SC monthly — the only FDA-approved CGRP mAb for cluster, and only for episodic cluster (300 mg, not the 120 mg migraine dose). Give at the start of the bout and continue through it.
  • Lithium — effective (esp. chronic cluster); needs level, renal, and thyroid monitoring.
  • Topiramate — alternative/add-on.
  • Melatonin 10 mg qHS — reasonable adjunct, exploits the circadian biology.

CGRP mAbs have failed in chronic cluster — don't count on them

Every CGRP mAb RCT in chronic cluster has been negative — galcanezumab, erenumab (CHERUB01), eptinezumab (ALLEVIATE), and fremanezumab (episodic + chronic trials stopped for futility); a 2026 network meta-analysis found no significant reduction in weekly attacks for any mAb (numerical signals only, mostly in episodic). Open-label eptinezumab (CHRONICLE) looked tolerable but had no placebo control. For chronic cluster, rely on verapamil/lithium and refractory options — not a CGRP antibody.

Clusters remit — plan for it

In episodic cluster, stop the preventive once the bout ends and the patient is attack-free for ~2 weeks; restart at the first sign of the next bout. In chronic cluster, maintain continuously.

Refractory chronic cluster (specialist territory)

When verapamil ± lithium/topiramate and galcanezumab (episodic) fail — refer to a headache specialist:

  • Occipital nerve stimulation — the best-supported neuromodulation for refractory chronic cluster in specialist hands.
  • Sphenopalatine-ganglion (SPG) stimulation — showed RCT-level efficacy (≈62–67% pain relief at 15 min vs sham), but is currently unavailable — the device manufacturer left the market; new devices are in development, so know it as a future option, not a present one.
  • Emerging — psychedelics (psilocybin, LSD): small trials and strong patient interest; a pulse-dosing psilocybin RCT extension signaled reduced attack frequency, and phase-2 LSD trials are ongoing — but the evidence is underpowered and this is not yet a clinical recommendation. Many patients self-treat — ask about it (interactions, and it's a candid conversation worth having).

docs.title: Paroxysmal hemicrania · hemicrania continua · SUNCT/SUNA
docs.text:
Short-lasting/continuous TACs — run the indomethacin trial first

  • Paroxysmal hemicrania & hemicrania continua → indomethacin trial. Uptitrate 25 mg TID → 50 mg TID (up to 75 mg TID / ~225 mg/day), each step held a few days, with GI protection (PPI). A complete response confirms the diagnosis and is the treatment; taper to the lowest effective dose. Consider "indotest" (IM indomethacin 50–100 mg) where the oral trial is ambiguous.
    • If indomethacin isn't tolerated (GI, renal, or neuropsychiatric effects — common): the best-supported alternatives (case-report level, no consensus guideline) are melatonin 6–21 mg/day, celecoxib (or other COX-2 inhibitor), acemetacin, topiramate, greater occipital nerve blocks (relief lasting months), and non-invasive vagus nerve stimulation as an indomethacin-sparing strategy.
  • SUNCT / SUNA — do not respond to indomethacin. Lamotrigine (titrate slowly for rash), then gabapentin, topiramate, or oxcarbazepine. IV lidocaine (monitored) for a status-like crisis; occipital-nerve procedures in refractory cases.

docs.title: Order set — quick reference
docs.text:
Acute cluster attack (copy-paste)

  • Oxygen 100% at 12–15 L/min via non-rebreather × 15–20 min, sitting forward
  • Sumatriptan 6 mg SC (or zolmitriptan 5–10 mg nasal if SC unavailable)
  • Before discharge: start a GON block or prednisone bridge + verapamil (with baseline ECG), script home oxygen, and arrange neurology follow-up.

TRICARE — cluster prescribing

  • Verapamil, prednisone, lithium, topiramate are generic/formulary, no PA.
  • Galcanezumab (Emgality) is the preferred, no-PA CGRP on TRICARE and carries the episodic-cluster indication — the frictionless CGRP choice here too (dose 300 mg for cluster).
  • Home oxygen requires a prescription documenting the cluster diagnosis and flow rate (12–15 L/min, non-rebreather); arrange the setup explicitly — coverage/logistics are the usual failure point, not efficacy. Verify current DME coverage with the regional contractor.

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
Outpatient. The wins are diagnostic (don't miss an indomethacin-responsive TAC) and logistical (get the patient home oxygen and a verapamil-plus-ECG plan started the same day).

Military medicine — cluster & TACs

Standards below are quoted from DoDI 6130.03 Vol 1/Vol 2 and AR 40-501 (27 June 2019); the DES process is governed by DoDI 1332.18 / DoDM 1332.18. Confirm current editions and aeromedical policy.

  • Deployability: an active cluster bout is severely disabling and typically deployment-limiting — the regimen (home/therapeutic oxygen dependence, verapamil with serial ECGs, injectable triptans, refrigerated CGRP) is hard to sustain in theater. Between bouts, many are functional.
  • Profile (DA Form 3349): temporary profile during a bout; permanent profile for chronic cluster or refractory TACs with duty impact.
  • EPTS / LOD: document onset and periodicity.
  • Retention — DoD & Army: both the joint standard (DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2, para 5.26.g) and AR 40-501 para 3-31g explicitly name "migraine, tension, or cluster headaches" — frequent incapacitating attacks (Army: interfering with duty/social activities ≥3 days/ month) require neurologist referral, permit up to 6 months of prophylaxis, and mandate referral to the DES (MEB/PEB) if not controlled at 6 months. A cluster bout easily meets the frequency threshold.
  • Accession — DoDI 6130.03 Vol 1, para 6.26.g: a history of cluster headaches is disqualifying for entry.
  • Aeromedical / special duty: active cluster and its medications (verapamil, triptans, lithium, topiramate) are generally disqualifying/waiver-dependent, and a history of chronic/recurrent/incapacitating headache is disqualifying for flying duty (AR 40-501, ch. 4).

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• The indomethacin-responsive headaches (PH, HC) are the ones you must not miss — a complete response is both treatment and diagnosis.
  • Cluster acute therapy = oxygen + subcutaneous/nasal triptan (both Level A); oral agents are too slow. Oxygen fails most often for setup reasons — insist on a non-rebreather.
  • Verapamil is first-line prevention and mandates ECG surveillance for dose-dependent conduction block.
  • CGRP mAbs work only for episodic cluster — and only galcanezumab (300 mg). In chronic cluster the class has uniformly failed in RCTs (galcanezumab, erenumab/CHERUB01, eptinezumab/ ALLEVIATE, fremanezumab; 2026 network meta-analysis negative) — don't rely on a mAb there.
  • Transition with a GON block or a short prednisone taper while the preventive ramps up.
  • Under-recognized and dangerous: ~5–6-year diagnostic delay, frequent misdiagnosis (more so in women), and a ~3.9× suicide-attempt risk — screen mood/SI and treat aggressively.
  • Refractory: occipital-nerve stimulation (best-supported); SPG stimulation is RCT-proven but currently unavailable (manufacturer exited); psilocybin/LSD are investigational — ask, because patients self-treat. For indomethacin-intolerant PH/HC: melatonin, celecoxib, topiramate, GON blocks.
  • Always image a new TAC once to exclude pituitary/structural mimics.

Key sources: ICHD-3 (TAC criteria); AHS 2016 & EAN 2023 cluster guidelines; galcanezumab episodic-cluster trial; negative chronic-cluster mAb RCTs (CHERUB01/erenumab, ALLEVIATE/eptinezumab, fremanezumab) + 2026 network meta-analysis; cluster-headache suicide-risk cohorts; demand-valve-oxygen crossover trial; indomethacin-alternative and psilocybin cluster series.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Headache Classification Committee of the International Headache Society. ICHD-3.
  2. Robbins MS, et al. Treatment of Cluster Headache: The American Headache Society Evidence-Based Guidelines. Headache. 2016;56(7):1093–1106.
  3. May A, et al. European Academy of Neurology guidelines on the treatment of cluster headache. Eur J Neurol. 2023;30:2955–2979.
  4. Goadsby PJ, et al. Galcanezumab in episodic cluster headache (pivotal RCT; FDA approval 2019, 300 mg SC monthly).
  5. Negative chronic-cluster CGRP-mAb trials: CHERUB01 (erenumab, JAMA Netw Open 2025); ALLEVIATE (eptinezumab, JAMA Neurol 2025); fremanezumab (episodic + chronic, terminated for futility); CHRONICLE (open-label eptinezumab, Lancet Neurol 2025); 2026 network meta-analysis.
  6. Suicide risk: Norwegian cohort (attempted suicide OR ~3.9) and Danish cohort (JAMA Neurol 2025, attempted + completed suicide across headache disorders).
  7. Emerging / adjuncts: psilocybin pulse-dosing cluster RCT extension and sleep study; ongoing phase-2 LSD trials; demand-valve-oxygen crossover trial; indomethacin-alternative reviews (melatonin, COX-2 inhibitors, acemetacin, topiramate); SPG-stimulation RCTs (device now unavailable).
  8. TRICARE / DoD Uniform Formulary: galcanezumab (Emgality) preferred/no-PA; verapamil, prednisone, lithium, topiramate generic-formulary; home oxygen requires documented diagnosis and flow rate. Verify current status at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Verified July 2026: acute oxygen (100%, ≥12 L/min, NNT ≈ 2), sumatriptan 6 mg SC, and nasal zolmitriptan are Level A per the AHS 2016 and EAN 2023 cluster guidelines; verapamil ≥240 mg/day with ECG monitoring is first-line prevention; galcanezumab is FDA-approved at 300 mg SC monthly for episodic cluster only, and all CGRP mAbs have failed in chronic-cluster RCTs. The suicide-risk, demand-valve-oxygen, indomethacin-alternative, SPG-unavailability, and psychedelic points were supplied by expert review with citations and are characterized as emerging/case-level where noted — confirm the specific figures before relying on them. Service-specific aeromedical and TRICARE DME (oxygen) specifics warrant confirmation before final sign-off.

docs.title: Medication overuse headache
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: in a patient with a pre-existing primary headache, frequent use of acute headache medication drives headaches to ≥15 days/month — both a complication and a self-perpetuating engine. Recognizing it is half the treatment; the other half is starting an effective preventive while unwinding the overused drug.

🚩 Don't diagnose MOH until you've excluded a new secondary headache

A patient whose headaches suddenly escalated may have a new process (mass, CVST, intracranial hypertension). If the pattern changed or there are any red flags, work it up before attributing everything to medication overuse.

docs.title: Recognize the thresholds (ICHD-3: use over >3 months)
docs.text:
Acute medication Overuse threshold Simple analgesics (NSAIDs, acetaminophen) ≥15 days/month Triptans ≥10 days/month Ergots ≥10 days/month Opioids ≥10 days/month Butalbital-containing combinations ≥10 days/month (worst offender) Combination analgesics ≥10 days/month Ditans (lasmiditan) ≥10 days/month — NOT MOH-safe (FDA label warning + preclinical data); count it like a triptan

Gepants (ubrogepant, rimegepant, atogepant, zavegepant) do not cause MOH and carry no monthly cap — they are the acute agent of choice in this population. Ditans are the exception among the new agents — lasmiditan can cause MOH, so don't treat it as interchangeable with a gepant.

docs.title: Rapid approach
docs.text:
1. Count acute-med days at every headache visit — the screening question people forget.
  2. Exclude a new secondary headache if the pattern changed.
  3. Start an effective preventive — don't wait for withdrawal to finish.
  4. Address the overused agent — stop or cap it, with a plan matched to the drug class.
  5. Bridge the withdrawal window and educate — MOH recurs without a durable behavior change and a capped acute plan.
  6. Follow up at a defined interval to confirm improvement.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Confirm MOH (threshold table) and rule out a new secondary headache if the pattern changed.
  2. Start a preventive now — pick by comorbidity, or go straight to a CGRP therapy, which works even with ongoing overuse (Step 1).
  3. Convert the acute plan: stop the abrupt-stoppable culprits, taper the ones that need it, and hand the patient a capped rescue plan — prefer a gepant (Step 2).
  4. Bridge the first few weeks (Step 3).
  5. Educate + set a follow-up (diary, written acute-day cap, return visit).
  6. Severe (opioid/barbiturate) or refractory: structured withdrawal / infusion program.

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Why the combined approach works — MOH is a nociplastic pain state

MOH is increasingly understood as a nociplastic pain condition: central sensitization, impaired descending pain modulation, and reward/cognitive-control-circuit dysfunction maintain the cycle — and these changes are largely reversible with a preventive + reduced overuse. Behavioral features (craving, catastrophizing, cephalalgiophobia) predict poor outcomes, which is exactly why education and follow-up sit alongside the drugs.

Step 1 — start an effective preventive (do this first, don't wait)

  • Pick a migraine preventive by comorbidity — topiramate, amitriptyline/nortriptyline, propranolol/metoprolol, candesartan, valproate (see the migraine preventive ladder for doses/titration).
  • CGRP-targeted therapies (mAbs and gepants) work even while the patient is still overusing and have transformed MOH care — reasonable early, and often the cleanest path. As migraine frequency falls, patients naturally reduce acute-med days, so forced abrupt withdrawal is frequently unnecessary when a CGRP mAb is on board. The dedicated phase-4 MOH trials now prove this:
    • Erenumab 140 mg (JAMA Neurol 2024, the first phase-4 RCT designed for CM-MOH): 69% MOH remission at 6 months vs 53% placebo (OR 2.01), without mandatory withdrawal.
    • Eptinezumab 100 mg IV + brief education (RESOLUTION, Neurology 2026, n=608): ~3.2 more monthly migraine days reduced than placebo (p<0.0001), sustained through 24 weeks.
  • You don't have to withdraw first (MOTS): in the largest strategy RCT (n=720), a preventive without switching/restricting the overused drug was non-inferior to switching for headache frequency — though switching produced more MOH-free patients. So: start the preventive; withdrawal helps but isn't mandatory (VA/DoD 2023 concurs).
  • Combination beats any single lever (2025 network meta-analysis, 16 RCTs): preventive + withdrawal ± nerve block was most effective; all preventive classes (oral, CGRP, Botox) were similarly effective; abrupt withdrawal alone was not.
  • OnabotulinumtoxinA reduces headache days in CM-MOH (~−1.9 vs placebo) but adds no benefit over withdrawal alone (Pijpers 2019), and CGRP mAbs show larger effect sizes in this population — so reach for a CGRP first where you can.
  • On TRICARE, the frictionless CGRP is galcanezumab (Emgality) — preferred, no PA (see box).

Step 2 — convert the acute plan (stop or cap the culprit)

Overused class How to stop Why NSAIDs, triptans, ergots Can stop abruptly No dangerous withdrawal syndrome Opioids Taper Withdrawal symptoms; relapse risk Butalbital / barbiturates Taper (careful) Abrupt stop risks seizures Benzodiazepine co-use Taper Withdrawal seizures
  • Replace with a capped rescue plan: a non-overused acute agent limited to ≤2 days/week.
  • Prefer a gepant (ubrogepant, rimegepant, or zavegepant nasal — useful with nausea/ vomiting during withdrawal) for rescue — no MOH, no monthly cap. Rimegepant also doubles as a preventive. Do not swap in lasmiditan as a "safe" alternative — ditans carry MOH risk.

Step 3 — bridge the withdrawal window (2–4 weeks)

Symptoms flare transiently as the overused drug comes off. Any of:

  • Scheduled NSAID: e.g., naproxen 500 mg BID × 2–4 weeks (if not the overused agent), with an antiemetic (metoclopramide or prochlorperazine) as needed.
  • Greater occipital nerve block — fast, opioid-sparing, pregnancy-safe.
  • Prednisone taper (e.g., 60 mg tapering over ~6 days) — evidence is mixed (some negative RCTs); reasonable in selected patients, not routine.
  • DHE (per the ER/status protocol) for a severe withdrawal flare.

Avoid — and taper existing use

Opioids and butalbital are the highest-risk culprits and the least effective acute drugs. Don't start them; prioritize tapering patients already on them. They are also the classes most likely to require a structured withdrawal program.

TRICARE — MOH prescribing

  • Preventives: oral agents (topiramate, amitriptyline, propranolol, candesartan) are generic/formulary, no PA. Galcanezumab (Emgality) is the preferred, no-PA CGRP — the go-to antibody when you want a CGRP early in MOH.
  • Rescue: triptans are formulary; the gepants (Ubrelvy acute, Qulipta preventive, Nurtec) require a PA + neurologist — but they're the ideal MOH rescue because they carry no cap.
  • Bridge: naproxen, prednisone, antiemetics are all generic/formulary.

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
Outpatient for most, with clear written instructions, a capped acute plan, a diary, and a defined follow-up. A minority with severe overuse (opioid/barbiturate) or refractory disease benefit from a structured withdrawal / infusion program (inpatient or day-hospital).

Relapse is common (~30% at 1 year) — plan follow-up around the predictors

MOH relapses in ~30% at 1 year (up to ~40% in some cohorts). Highest-risk: opioid or combination-analgesic overuse (worst prognosis), resuming the causative drug after withdrawal, high baseline headache frequency, poor sleep, and an underlying tension-type headache (vs migraine). Counter it with structured follow-up, a written acute-day cap, and behavioral support — and be most vigilant in the opioid/butalbital group.

Military medicine — medication overuse headache

Framework only — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03, DoDI 1332.18, and aeromedical policy.

  • Common and under-recognized — service members self-treat frequent headaches with OTC analgesics or leftover opioids/butalbital; screen acute-med days deliberately.
  • Opioid/butalbital overuse carries fitness-for-duty, drug-testing, and readiness implications — an added reason to avoid these classes and taper existing use.
  • Deployability: the underlying primary headache (usually migraine) drives deployability; treating MOH often restores function.
  • Retention — AR 40-501 para 3-31g: frame around the underlying primary headache once overuse is corrected — if incapacitating attacks still interfere with duty ≥3 days/month after an adequate neurology-directed trial (up to 6 months prophylaxis), the standard directs referral to the DES (MEB/PEB).
  • Aeromedical / special duty: sedating acute meds and opioids/barbiturates are disqualifying/waiver-dependent; correcting overuse can support return to status.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• Both levers work, and combination is best. A 2025 network meta-analysis (16 RCTs) found preventive + withdrawal ± nerve block most effective; all preventive classes were similar; and abrupt withdrawal alone was insufficient.
  • You don't have to withdraw first. MOTS (n=720): a preventive without switching the overused drug was non-inferior for headache frequency (switching yielded more MOH-free patients). The dedicated CGRP phase-4 trials seal it: erenumab (JAMA Neurol 2024) → 69% MOH remission at 6 mo without withdrawal; eptinezumab + education (RESOLUTION, 2026) → sustained benefit through 24 weeks. Mandatory "detox" is largely optional in the CGRP era.
  • Gepants carry no MOH risk and no monthly cap — the preferred rescue. Ditans (lasmiditan) are NOT MOH-safe (FDA warning) — don't treat them like gepants.
  • OnabotulinumtoxinA helps modestly but adds nothing over withdrawal alone; CGRP mAbs are the stronger choice in CM-MOH.
  • Opioids and butalbital are the highest-risk culprits (and the worst for relapse) — avoid/taper.
  • MOH is a nociplastic, largely reversible pain state — which is why education and follow-up (relapse ~30%/year) are core, not optional.

Key sources: ICHD-3 (MOH criteria); VA/DoD 2023 headache guideline; MOTS; erenumab phase-4 MOH RCT (JAMA Neurol 2024); RESOLUTION eptinezumab RCT (2026); 2025 network meta-analysis; onabotulinumtoxinA MOH trials; lasmiditan MOH-risk data.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Headache Classification Committee of the International Headache Society. ICHD-3.
  2. Schwedt TJ, et al. Medication Overuse Treatment Strategy (MOTS) trial. Neurology. 2022 — no-withdrawal strategy non-inferior; VA/DoD 2023 headache guideline concurs.
  3. Tepper SJ, et al. Erenumab for nonopioid MOH in chronic migraine — phase-4 RCT. JAMA Neurol. 2024 (69% remission at 6 mo without withdrawal); Jensen RH, et al. Eptinezumab + education (RESOLUTION). Neurology. 2026.
  4. Koonalintip P, et al. Network meta-analysis of MOH strategies (J Headache Pain 2025) — combination best, withdrawal-alone insufficient. onabotulinumtoxinA MOH trials (incl. Pijpers 2019).
  5. Lasmiditan MOH risk: Reyvow FDA label warning; preclinical MOH-model data (Rau/Saengjaroentham).
  6. TRICARE / DoD Uniform Formulary: galcanezumab (Emgality) preferred/no-PA; gepants (Ubrelvy, Qulipta, Nurtec) PA + neurologist; oral preventives and bridge agents generic-formulary. Verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Verified July 2026: ICHD-3 overuse thresholds (≥15 days/month simple analgesics; ≥10 days/month triptans/ergots/opioids/combinations/ditans) are current; gepants (incl. zavegepant) are not associated with MOH, but ditans (lasmiditan) ARE. Contemporary evidence supports starting a preventive early and shows CGRP monoclonal antibodies induce and sustain MOH remission without mandatory withdrawal (erenumab phase-4 RCT; RESOLUTION), that combination strategies beat withdrawal alone (2025 network meta-analysis), and that relapse is ~30%/year (opioid overuse worst). The specific trial figures were supplied by expert review with citations — confirm before relying on them. Service-specific aeromedical/retention specifics warrant confirmation before final sign-off.

docs.title: Migraine
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: recurrent, disabling headache — usually lateralized, throbbing, moderate-to-severe, worse with activity, with photophobia/phonophobia, nausea, and sometimes aura. For a general provider: if a headache patient reaches neurology with a normal exam and no red flags, treat as migraine — it is almost never "just tension."

🚩 Before you call it migraine

Clear the headache red flags first. Thunderclap onset, fever/neck stiffness, focal deficit, papilledema, new headache after 50, new headache in pregnancy, or a change in an established pattern all mean work it up as a secondary headache first.

docs.title: Rapid approach
docs.text:
1. Exclude secondary headache (red-flag screen). Normal exam + no red flags → proceed.
  2. Diagnose by phenotype (ICHD-3, below). Imaging is not required for typical migraine with a normal exam.
  3. Treat the attack — stratified care: match drug strength to attack severity, treat early.
  4. Decide on prevention — if attacks are frequent or disabling, or acute meds are overused.
  5. Screen for and unwind medication overuse.
  6. Disposition: almost always outpatient. Avoid admitting known migraineurs.

docs.title: Diagnosis (ICHD-3, summarized)
docs.text:
Migraine without aura: ≥5 attacks lasting 4–72 h with ≥2 of {unilateral, pulsating, moderate/severe, aggravated by routine activity} and ≥1 of {nausea/vomiting, or photophobia and phonophobia}, not better explained by another diagnosis.

Migraine with aura: ≥2 attacks with ≥1 fully reversible aura symptom (visual, sensory, speech/language, motor, brainstem, or retinal) and ≥3 of these 6 characteristics: (1) at least one symptom spreads gradually over ≥5 min; (2) two or more symptoms occur in succession; (3) each symptom lasts 5–60 min; (4) at least one symptom is unilateral; (5) at least one symptom is positive (e.g., scintillations, pins-and-needles); (6) aura is accompanied by, or followed within 60 min by, headache. Motor aura = hemiplegic migraine; brainstem aura — both are red flags to distinguish from TIA/stroke on first presentation and both contraindicate triptans/ergots.

Chronic migraine: headache ≥15 days/month for >3 months, with migrainous features ≥8 days/month. Screen for medication overuse in every chronic patient.

docs.title: Focused H&P + exam
docs.text:
• Attack profile: frequency (headache days/month), duration, disability (MIDAS/HIT-6 if outpatient), aura, triggers, and — critically — how many days/month they take acute medication (MOH screen).
  • Exam: vitals with BP, fundoscopy, cranial nerves, visual fields, focal screen, gait. Normal between attacks. Any fixed deficit → re-open the secondary work-up.
  • Diary: have the patient keep a headache diary; it drives both prevention decisions and (for service members) profile and disability documentation.

docs.title: Workup
docs.text: Typical migraine with a normal exam needs no imaging or labs. Image (MRI preferred) only for red flags, atypical/persistent aura, or a change in pattern. Do not order EEG for migraine.

docs.title: Acute (abortive) treatment — the ladder
docs.text:
Four rules that matter more than drug choice: (1) treat early — at the first sign of pain, while it is still mild; (2) match strength to severity (stratified care — don't make a severe attack fail acetaminophen first); (3) give an adequate dose and route — under-dosing and a vomited pill are the two commonest "triptan failures"; (4) cap acute days to prevent medication-overuse headache (MOH) — and note the prevention cap is stricter than the diagnostic threshold:

  • Recommended cap (to prevent MOH): triptans, ergots, opioids, and combination analgesics ≤2 days/week (≈8–10 days/month); plain NSAIDs/simple analgesics ≤3 days/week (≈10–15 days/month).
  • ICHD-3 diagnostic threshold for MOH (a higher bar — this is when you label it, see MOH section): ≥10 days/month for triptans/ergots/opioids/combinations, ≥15 days/month for simple NSAIDs/acetaminophen.
  • Gepants are exempt — they are not associated with MOH, so no monthly cap applies. This makes them the acute agent of choice for patients who are already overusing or who need frequent treatment.

Step 1 — mild-to-moderate attack (start here for most)

Any one of these, taken early, with an antiemetic if nauseated:

  • Naproxen 500–550 mg, ibuprofen 400–600 mg, or aspirin 900–1000 mg PO.
  • APAP/aspirin/caffeine (250/250/65 mg, 2 tabs) for those who can't take an NSAID.
  • Antiemetic adjunct: metoclopramide 10 mg or prochlorperazine 10 mg — these treat the headache, not just the nausea, and speed oral-drug absorption.

→ No reliable relief across 2–3 attacks, or attacks are moderate-to-severe from the start → Step 2.

Step 2 — moderate-to-severe attack: a triptan (first-line migraine-specific drug)

Default: sumatriptan 50–100 mg PO at onset; may repeat once after ≥2 h (max 200 mg/day). Even better for moderate/severe attacks: sumatriptan 85 mg + naproxen 500 mg together (co-administration aborts more attacks than either alone) — a reasonable go-to.

Pick the formulation to fit the attack:

  • Fast onset / vomiting / wakes with pain: sumatriptan 6 mg SC (works in ~10 min — the most effective route) or zolmitriptan 5 mg / sumatriptan 10–20 mg nasal.
  • Wants an oral that acts fast: rizatriptan 10 mg ODT or eletriptan 40 mg (most potent oral triptan).
  • Long attacks / high recurrence: naratriptan 2.5 mg or frovatriptan 2.5 mg (long half-life, lower recurrence — also used for menstrual mini-prophylaxis).

"Triptan failure" means ≥2–3 different triptans tried adequately — full dose, taken early, ideally including a non-oral route — not one under-dosed pill. Optimize before escalating.

Do NOT give a triptan (or ergot) if:

Coronary, cerebrovascular, or peripheral vascular disease; uncontrolled hypertension; hemiplegic or brainstem-aura migraine; pregnancy (relative). In any of these, skip Step 2 and go straight to Step 3 — the non-vasoconstrictors.

Step 3 — triptan failed (≥2 tried) OR contraindicated: gepant or ditan

Neither class causes vasoconstriction, so both are safe in vascular disease; gepants do not cause MOH (useful when a patient is already overusing).

  • Gepants (small-molecule CGRP-receptor antagonists — oral/nasal):
    • Ubrogepant (Ubrelvy) 50–100 mg PO; may repeat once after ≥2 h (max 200 mg/day).
    • Rimegepant (Nurtec ODT) 75 mg — max 1/day; uniquely doubles as a preventive (every-other-day), so it's the pick when a patient needs both.
    • Zavegepant (Zavzpret) 10 mg nasal — fast, useful with vomiting.
  • Ditan — lasmiditan (Reyvow) 50 / 100 / 200 mg PO: effective with no vascular contraindication, but sedating with a hard ≥8-hour driving/operating restriction and CNS depression — poor fit for pilots, drivers, and duty. Don't stack with other serotonergic/CNS depressants.
  • DHE (nasal/SC/IV) for prolonged or refractory attacks — see the status-migrainosus order set below.

TRICARE — the acute gepant is Ubrelvy, and it needs a PA

On TRICARE, ubrogepant (Ubrelvy) is the preferred acute gepant, but it is not PA-free. The DoD prior-authorization criteria require: age ≥18, prescribed by or in consultation with a neurologist, and a contraindication/intolerance/2-month failure of ≥2 triptans (sumatriptan, rizatriptan, zolmitriptan, or eletriptan), and it cannot be combined with another gepant/Nurtec. Practically: document two failed triptans and loop in neuro, and the PA goes through. Rimegepant (Nurtec) is non-preferred (higher tier).

Do not use — for acute migraine

Opioids and butalbital-containing combinations (e.g., Fioricet). Poor efficacy, high dependence and MOH risk, and they blunt response to specific drugs. Don't start them; taper patients already on them. This holds in clinic, the ED, and on the ward.

docs.title: Preventive treatment — the ladder
docs.text:
Who needs prevention (start it if ANY apply)

  • ≥4 migraine headache days/month, or
  • ≥2 days/month with significant disability despite good acute treatment, or
  • Acute treatment overused, failing, or contraindicated, or
  • Patient preference, or
  • Regardless of frequency: hemiplegic or brainstem-aura migraine, prolonged/persistent aura, migrainous infarction, or status migrainosus.

Set expectations up front: every preventive gets an adequate trial — 8–12 weeks at target dose (oral) or ~3 months (CGRP) — before you call it a failure. Track with a diary, aim for ≥50% reduction in headache days, and taper after ~6–12 months of good control. Start low, titrate slowly, and change one thing at a time.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Everyone: lifestyle foundation (below) + a written acute plan + a headache diary.
  2. Start ONE oral first-line agent matched to the patient's comorbidities (Tier 1). Titrate to target, hold 8–12 weeks.
  3. Inadequate? Switch to a first-line oral from a different class (or move to CGRP now — AHS 2024 permits CGRP as first-line without failing orals; on TRICARE the frictionless CGRP entry point is Emgality, see below).
  4. ≥2 adequate oral trials failed, or disabling disease: go to a CGRP therapy (Tier 2).
  5. First CGRP fails after ~3 months: switch mechanism — receptor ⇄ ligand antibody (see the box) — or move to an oral gepant preventive.
  6. Chronic migraine (≥15 headache days/month): add onabotulinumtoxinA (PREEMPT); it can be combined with a CGRP therapy in refractory disease.
  7. Still refractory: neurology referral, combine Botox + CGRP, consider IV eptinezumab.
Tier 1 — oral first-line (pick by comorbidity)Tier 2 — CGRP-targeted (co-first-line per AHS 2024)Tier 3 — chronic migraine (≥15 headache days/month)Foundation — non-drug (for every patient)

Well-established, low-cost, on the TRICARE formulary with no PA. Let the comorbidity choose the drug — that's how you get a two-for-one and the best adherence:

Patient / comorbidity Preferred agent Target dose Watch for HTN, palpitations, anxiety, tremor Propranolol (or metoprolol, timolol) Propranolol LA 80–160 mg/day (up to 240) Fatigue, bradycardia, bronchospasm; avoid in asthma; caution in depression Overweight/obese, comorbid seizure Topiramate Titrate 25 mg/week → 50–100 mg/day (divided) Paresthesias, cognitive slowing, kidney stones, teratogenic, flat soda taste Insomnia, comorbid depression/tension-type Amitriptyline / nortriptyline 10–50 mg qHS Anticholinergic effects, weight gain, morning grogginess (nortriptyline milder) Can't use a beta-blocker, wants once-daily Candesartan 8–16 mg/day Dizziness, hyperkalemia, renal function Frequent aura, not childbearing potential Valproate / divalproex 500–1000 mg/day Never in pregnancy potential (neural-tube defects); tremor, weight, LFTs Menstrual-pattern Frovatriptan / naratriptan perimenstrual mini-prophylaxis Frovatriptan 2.5 mg BID × 6 days around menses Short-term, timed to cycle

Per the 2024 AHS position statement, CGRP therapies are a first-line option — there is no requirement to fail two older orals first clinically. (Insurers, including TRICARE, may still require step therapy for the non-preferred agents — see the TRICARE box.) Two formats:

  • Anti-CGRP monoclonal antibodies (injectable/IV; dosed monthly–quarterly). Best adherence, cleanest tolerability — watch constipation and BP with erenumab, injection-site reactions with all.
  • Oral gepants for prevention: atogepant (Qulipta) 10–60 mg daily, or rimegepant (Nurtec) 75 mg every other day — rimegepant uniquely doubles as acute + preventive. Good for needle-averse patients; gepants do not cause MOH.

See the receptor-vs-ligand box below to choose the antibody and to switch intelligently.

  • OnabotulinumtoxinA (PREEMPT protocol): 155 units across 31 fixed sites every 12 weeks (up to 195 units / 39 sites with the optional "follow-the-pain" additions) — chronic migraine ONLY, not episodic.
  • Botox and CGRP therapies are both first-tier for chronic migraine and can be combined in refractory cases.
  • Regular sleep, meals, hydration, aerobic exercise; trigger awareness; limit caffeine and acute-med days; treat comorbid mood, sleep, and anxiety disorders.
  • Neuromodulation devices (e-TNS/Cefaly, remote electrical neuromodulation/Nerivio, sTMS, non-invasive vagus nerve stimulation) — useful when drugs are contraindicated, unwanted, or in pregnancy.

docs.title: CGRP antibodies: receptor vs. ligand — which to start, how to switch
docs.text:
The user's key question, answered directly: only one CGRP antibody blocks the receptor; the other three bind the CGRP peptide (ligand) itself. The oral gepants are all small-molecule receptor antagonists.

Agent (brand) Target Route / interval Notes Erenumab (Aimovig) CGRP receptor (the only receptor mAb) SC monthly (70 or 140 mg) Watch constipation and rising BP; no loading dose Fremanezumab (Ajovy) CGRP ligand SC monthly or quarterly Only mAb with a quarterly option — good adherence Galcanezumab (Emgality) CGRP ligand SC monthly (240 mg load, then 120 mg) Also FDA-approved for episodic cluster Eptinezumab (Vyepti) CGRP ligand IV quarterly Fastest onset; guarantees adherence; good for chronic/refractory Atogepant (Qulipta) · Rimegepant (Nurtec) Receptor (small molecule) Oral Preventive gepants; Nurtec = acute + preventive

How to choose and switch

  • Efficacy is comparable across the antibodies — choose on route/interval, comorbidity, and formulary (on TRICARE that means start Emgality, see below).
  • If the first antibody fails after an adequate ~3-month trial, switch mechanism: a ligand mAb (Emgality/Ajovy/Vyepti) → the receptor mAb (Aimovig), or vice-versa. Switching within the same target is lower-yield than crossing to the other mechanism.
  • Antibody class failing entirely? Try an oral gepant (Qulipta/Nurtec) — different pharmacology, and a reasonable next mechanism before Botox/combination therapy.

TRICARE — start Emgality; it's the one CGRP with no prior authorization

Verified against the DoD Uniform Formulary and Express Scripts PA criteria (2025):

  • Emgality (galcanezumab) is the preferred CGRP antibody and needs NO prior authorization — $0 at an MTF/home delivery. This is your go-to first CGRP on TRICARE: you can start it without fighting a step-therapy PA, which also lines up with AHS's first-line stance.
  • Aimovig (erenumab) and Ajovy (fremanezumab) are non-preferred → PA + step therapy: the patient must have failed Emgality and failed ≥1 drug from two oral prophylactic classes (AEDs: valproate/topiramate · beta-blockers · TCAs/SNRIs), age ≥18, neurologist involvement. So Aimovig is exactly where you land when you need to switch a ligand→receptor mechanism after Emgality fails — the failed-Emgality trial is the PA justification.
  • Oral gepant preventive — Qulipta (atogepant): preferred but PA-required. DoD criteria: ≥18, neurologist consult, failed ≥1 drug from two oral classes, and failed ≥1 CGRP injectable — i.e., TRICARE seats Qulipta behind the injectable mAb. Nurtec ODT (rimegepant) is non-preferred.
  • Bottom line for a TRICARE panel: oral first-line (formulary, no PA) → Emgality (no PA) → Aimovig/Ajovy or Qulipta (PA, after Emgality) → Botox (chronic) / combination. Keep neurology named on the CGRP and gepant scripts — every one of these PAs requires it.

docs.title: Order set — status migrainosus / ER
docs.text:
Graded to the 2025 American Headache Society guideline for parenteral migraine treatment in the ED (Robblee et al.). Two headline points: prochlorperazine and the greater occipital nerve block are now Level A "must offer," and opioids are to be avoided (IV hydromorphone is a Level A "must NOT offer"). Nerve blocks are opioid-sparing, fast, and safe in pregnancy — reach for them early, not just as a last resort.

Escalation cadence: give the whole of Tier 1 together up front, then reassess pain every 30 minutes. At 30 min, if only partial relief, you may repeat the dopamine antagonist once (prochlorperazine 10 mg or metoclopramide 10 mg). If still inadequate at ~60 min, move to Tier 2; reassess again at ~30–60 min before Tier 3. Nerve blocks act within 10–15 min, so they help decide escalation quickly.

Tier 1 — first-line (offer to everyone; exclude red flags first)

Give a dopamine antagonist + an NSAID + a nerve block, not one drug in isolation.

  • Prochlorperazine 10 mg IV (Level A — first choice), or metoclopramide 10–20 mg IV (Level B — if prochlorperazine unavailable). Infuse each over ~15 min. May repeat once at 30 min if partial response.
    • Akathisia/dystonia: many co-give diphenhydramine 25 mg IV; note the 2025 guideline rates diphenhydramine "may not offer" as a migraine treatment and evidence that it prevents akathisia is mixed — use it for the reaction, not for the headache.
  • Ketorolac 15–30 mg IV (Level B) — or dexketoprofen 50 mg IV or diclofenac 75 mg IM where available.
  • Sumatriptan 6 mg SC (Level B) — if not already used and no vascular contraindication.
  • IV fluids — normal saline ~1 L only if the patient is dehydrated (or before chlorpromazine); fluids alone are not a migraine treatment.

Tier 1 — nerve blocks (do these early; opioid-sparing, pregnancy-safe)

  • Greater occipital nerve block (GONB) (Level A — must offer). Bilateral, ~2–3 mL of bupivacaine 0.5% (or 1–2% lidocaine) per side over the greater occipital nerve — landmark: ~1/3 of the way from the occipital protuberance toward the mastoid, medial to the occipital artery pulse. Onset ~10–15 min. Strong adjunct (a 2025 RCT: ~95% vs ~48% ≥50% relief at 2 h as add-on); especially useful with occipital tenderness or when avoiding systemic drugs.
  • Supraorbital / supratrochlear nerve blocks (Level B) — for frontal pain; ~1–2 mL of bupivacaine 0.5% or 1–2% lidocaine per site.
  • Sphenopalatine ganglion (SPG) block (emerging; heterogeneous evidence) — transnasal 2–4% lidocaine via a long cotton-tipped applicator or catheter device (e.g., ~1–2 mL per nostril); simple and low-risk, reasonable as an adjunct.

Tier 2 — add if inadequate at ~60 min; reassess again at 30–60 min

  • Magnesium sulfate 1–2 g IV over ~15–30 min — best evidence in migraine with aura.
  • Dexamethasone 10 mg IV (Level C) — modest acute benefit and reduces 24–72 h recurrence (NNT ~9); give before discharge regardless.
  • Valproate 500–1000 mg IV (Level C) — avoid in pregnancy/childbearing potential.
  • Chlorpromazine 12.5–25 mg IV (Level C) — alternative neuroleptic; give a ~1 L saline preload and watch sedation/orthostatic hypotension. May repeat after ~30 min.

Tier 3 — refractory / status migrainosus (monitored; consider neurology)

  • Droperidol 2.5 mg IV (Level C) or haloperidol 2.5–5 mg IV (Level C) — potent neuroleptics (droperidol relieves ~90% of refractory attacks). Get a baseline ECG for QT (droperidol carries a QT black-box warning); co-treat akathisia. May repeat once after ~30–60 min.
  • Dihydroergotamine (DHE) 0.5–1 mg IV, preceded by an antiemetic (e.g., metoclopramide 10 mg or ondansetron 4 mg IV); may repeat 0.5–1 mg q8h. Contraindicated in coronary/ peripheral vascular disease, uncontrolled HTN, a triptan/ergot within 24 h, or pregnancy. For admitted status migrainosus, the inpatient Raskin protocol (IV DHE q8h over 2–3 days, with an antiemetic) can break the cycle.
  • Specialist/monitored infusions (procedural-sedation monitoring where relevant):
    • IV lidocaine — 1 mg/kg bolus, then ~2 mg/min infusion (do not exceed 4 mg/min; keep level < 5 µg/mL); status-migrainosus courses may run up to ~2 days.
    • Propofol — 0.5 mg/kg bolus, then 0.25 mg/kg every 10 min for up to 1 h.
    • Low-dose ketamine — subacute infusion ~0.1–0.4 mg/kg/h (note: a single low-dose ED bolus was negative in an RCT, so reserve ketamine for monitored refractory use).

Eptinezumab (Vyepti) IV — not a routine ED drug

The 2025 AHS guideline evaluated eptinezumab 100 mg IV for acute treatment: it beat placebo for 2-h headache freedom (23.5% vs 12.0%), but the pivotal RCT was run mostly outpatient. So the guideline rates it Level B ("should offer") only for patients matching that trial population, and Level U (no recommendation) for the general ED population. Practically: don't reach for it in a typical ED visit — it's slow-onset, costly, and unproven for acute ED use. Its real role is outpatient prevention (see the preventive ladder).

Before discharge & what to avoid

  • Dexamethasone 10 mg IV/IM once if not already given — reduces early recurrence.
  • Start/adjust a preventive, give a written acute-medication cap, and arrange follow-up.
  • Avoid opioids and butalbital — Level A must-not-offer (hydromorphone); they worsen outcomes and seed medication-overuse headache.
  • Speak to the nurse directly to sequence meds; if admitted, add scheduled antiemetic and sleep hygiene.

docs.title: Medication overuse headache (MOH)
docs.text: Suspect when headaches are frequent and acute meds are used often — roughly ≥10 days/month for triptans/ergots/opioids/combination analgesics, or ≥15 days/month for simple NSAIDs/ acetaminophen. Management: start a preventive, cap or withdraw the overused agent, bridge symptoms (e.g., NSAID/antiemetic course ± short steroid taper), and counsel. Gepants do not cause MOH and are useful acute agents in this setting.

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
Migraine is an outpatient diagnosis. Treat the attack, start/adjust prevention, arrange follow-up, and give a written acute-med limit. Admission is rarely helpful for a known migraineur.

Military medicine — migraine

Standards below are quoted from DoDI 6130.03 Vol 1 (accession) / Vol 2 (retention) and AR 40-501 (27 June 2019); the DES process is governed by DoDI 1332.18 / DoDM 1332.18. Confirm current editions and service-specific aeromedical policy before you counsel or document.

  • Deployability: most controlled migraine is deployable. Frequent/incapacitating attacks, or a regimen not maintainable in theater, can be deployment-limiting. Consider environment: topiramate (heat intolerance, cognitive effects), sedating TCAs, lasmiditan (≥8-h driving/duty restriction — poor fit for drivers/aircrew), and CGRP mAbs (cold-chain/ refrigeration and resupply) all matter downrange.
  • TRICARE prescribing (see acute/preventive boxes): acute — triptans are formulary/no-PA; Ubrelvy (acute gepant) needs a PA (≥2 failed triptans + neurologist). Preventive — orals are formulary/no-PA; Emgality is the only CGRP with no PA (start here); Aimovig/ Ajovy/Qulipta need a PA and neurologist, after Emgality. Name neurology on every CGRP/ gepant script.
  • Profile (DA Form 3349): temporary profile during titration or a flare; permanent profile for chronic/refractory disease with functional limits documented.
  • EPTS / LOD: migraine often predates service — document onset and pre-service history; it affects existed-prior-to-service and line-of-duty determinations.
  • Retention — DoD & Army: the joint standard (DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2, para 5.26.g) places migraine, tension, or cluster headaches with frequent incapacitating attacks below retention standards. AR 40-501 para 3-31g operationalizes "frequent" for the Army as incapacitating attacks interfering with duty or social activities ≥3 days/month → mandatory neurologist referral, up to 6 months of prophylaxis, and referral to the DES (MEB/PEB) if not adequately controlled at 6 months (or directly, if unlikely to respond).
  • Accession — DoDI 6130.03 Vol 1, para 6.26.e: for applicants, a history in the prior 24 months of headaches that caused missed activity >2×/12 months, required prescription meds

    2×/12 months, or needed prophylaxis is disqualifying for entry (relevant to recruiting/ waivers).

  • Document what the standard turns on: a headache diary showing ≥3 incapacitating days/month, the neurology referral, the prophylaxis trial and its outcome, and concrete duty impact.
  • Aeromedical / special duty: a history of chronic, recurrent, or incapacitating headaches — including ocular or acephalic migraine with visual disturbance — is disqualifying for flying duty (AR 40-501, ch. 4). Many migraine medications are independently disqualifying/waiver- dependent; confirm current aeromedical policy before prescribing.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
Written from primary guidelines and trials; source articles are a reading list, not copied.

What's changed and what matters now:

  • Stratified beats step care. Match acute-drug strength to attack severity from the start; a triptan + NSAID combination outperforms either agent alone for moderate/severe attacks.
  • CGRP therapies are now first-line for prevention. The 2024 American Headache Society position statement (Charles et al., Headache) makes anti-CGRP mAbs (erenumab, fremanezumab, galcanezumab, eptinezumab) and oral gepants (atogepant, rimegepant) a first-line option and removed the prior requirement to fail two 8-week trials of older oral preventives first. They are "a first-line option," not "the" only first-line — choose between an oral agent and a CGRP therapy by patient factors and access.
  • Gepants and ditans expand acute options. Gepants (ubrogepant, rimegepant, zavegepant) and the ditan lasmiditan work without vasoconstriction, making them options in cardiovascular/ cerebrovascular disease where triptans are contraindicated. Gepants do not cause MOH.
  • Receptor vs. ligand antibodies. Only erenumab (Aimovig) blocks the CGRP receptor; fremanezumab (Ajovy), galcanezumab (Emgality), eptinezumab (Vyepti) bind the CGRP ligand. Efficacy is comparable, so choose on route/interval, comorbidity, and formulary — and when one fails, switch mechanism (ligand ⇄ receptor) rather than swap two agents with the same target.
  • Formulary steering is now part of the decision (TRICARE). On the DoD Uniform Formulary, galcanezumab (Emgality) is the preferred, no-PA CGRP antibody — the frictionless first CGRP — while erenumab/fremanezumab, atogepant (Qulipta), and ubrogepant (Ubrelvy) are PA-gated and require neurologist involvement; rimegepant (Nurtec) is non-preferred. This makes "start Emgality" both the clinically first-line and the administratively easiest move.
  • OnabotulinumtoxinA (PREEMPT) remains a first-tier preventive for chronic migraine only.
  • ED care leads with dopamine antagonists + nerve blocks, not opioids. The 2025 AHS parenteral-ED guideline (Robblee et al.) elevated prochlorperazine and the greater occipital nerve block to Level A "must offer," kept metoclopramide/ketorolac/SC sumatriptan as "should offer," and made IV hydromorphone a Level A "must-NOT-offer." Nerve blocks are fast, opioid-sparing, and safe in pregnancy.
  • Avoid opioids and butalbital — they worsen the disease and drive medication-overuse headache.
  • Prevention only works if trialed adequately — 8–12 weeks at target dose before judging failure.

Key sources to cite (verify current versions):

  • ICHD-3 diagnostic criteria (International Headache Society).
  • American Headache Society consensus/position statements on integrating CGRP-targeted therapies and on the initiation of preventive treatment.
  • PREEMPT trials — onabotulinumtoxinA for chronic migraine.
  • Pivotal trials of anti-CGRP mAbs and gepants (e.g., STRIVE/ARISE, and the ubrogepant/rimegepant/ atogepant/lasmiditan registration trials).

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Headache Classification Committee of the International Headache Society. The International Classification of Headache Disorders, 3rd edition (ICHD-3).
  2. Charles A, et al. Calcitonin gene-related peptide-targeting therapies are a first-line option for the prevention of migraine: An American Headache Society position statement update. Headache. 2024.
  3. Ailani J, et al. The American Headache Society Consensus Statement: Update on integrating new migraine treatments into clinical practice. Headache. 2021;61(7):1021–1039 — stratified acute care; failure of ≥2 triptans is the trigger to move to a gepant or ditan; acute/preventive initiation thresholds.
  4. TRICARE / DoD Uniform Formulary and Express Scripts prior-authorization criteria (2025): galcanezumab (Emgality) preferred with no PA; erenumab (Aimovig)/fremanezumab (Ajovy), atogepant (Qulipta), and ubrogepant (Ubrelvy) PA-gated with neurologist and step-therapy requirements; rimegepant (Nurtec) non-preferred. Verify current status at the TRICARE Formulary Search before counseling — DoD P&T updates the tiers periodically.
  5. Robblee J, et al. 2025 guideline update to acute treatment of migraine for adults in the emergency department: the AHS evidence assessment of parenteral pharmacotherapies. Headache. 2026.
  6. Friedman BW, et al. Greater occipital nerve block vs IV metoclopramide for ED migraine (RCT); and 2025 adjunctive-GONB RCT.
  7. PREEMPT clinical program — onabotulinumtoxinA for chronic migraine (155 units/31 sites q12wk; up to 195 units/39 sites with follow-the-pain).

Verified July 2026: the acute agents (zavegepant nasal, ubrogepant, rimegepant, lasmiditan with its ≥8-hour driving restriction) and preventives (anti-CGRP mAbs; oral atogepant/rimegepant) named here match current FDA approvals; CGRP therapies are first-line for prevention per the 2024 AHS position statement (no requirement to fail older orals first); preventive-initiation thresholds (≥4 MHD, or ≥2 with disability) follow the AHS consensus; the ER protocol is graded to the 2025 AHS parenteral- ED guideline (prochlorperazine and greater occipital nerve block are Level A "must offer," hydromorphone Level A "must-not-offer"); and gepants are not associated with medication-overuse headache. TRICARE formulary steering (Emgality preferred/no-PA; Aimovig/Ajovy/Qulipta/Ubrelvy PA-gated with a neurologist requirement; Nurtec non-preferred) is taken from the 2025 DoD Uniform Formulary and Express Scripts PA criteria and should be re-checked at the TRICARE Formulary Search before counseling, as DoD P&T revises tiers periodically. The retention/accession standards are quoted from DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 (5.26.g) / Vol 1 (6.26.e) and AR 40-501 (3-31g); the DES process is governed by DoDI 1332.18 / DoDM 1332.18. Service-specific aeromedical waiver specifics still warrant confirmation before final sign-off.

docs.title: New daily persistent headache
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: a headache that is daily and unremitting from the moment of onset — the patient can often name the exact day it started — persisting >3 months, in someone without a prior significant headache history. It is a diagnosis of exclusion, and the exclusion work is the whole game.

🚩 NDPH is where secondary headaches hide — work it up thoroughly

A distinct-onset daily headache demands an active search for a secondary cause before you accept NDPH:

Consider Screen with CSF pressure disorders (low: leak/SIH; high: IIH) MRI brain ± spine, LP with opening pressure, fundoscopy Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis MRV / CTV Giant cell arteritis (older patients) ESR/CRP Cervicogenic / craniocervical Cervical exam, imaging Infectious / post-infectious (incl. post-viral/COVID) History, targeted labs/LP Sphenoid sinusitis, intracranial mass MRI

docs.title: Diagnosis (ICHD-3)
docs.text:
Persistent headache, daily and continuous from onset (or within 24 h), present >3 months, not better accounted for by another diagnosis. Many patients report a trigger around onset — an infection (classically viral), a stressful life event, or surgery.

Prognostic subtypes (quantify it when you counsel)

Per the Robbins classification: ~76% have a persisting (refractory) course; ~15% remit (median ~21 months); ~8% relapse-remit (median first remission ~5.5 months). Persisting course associates with younger onset and anxiety/depression. So most need long-term management, but a real minority improve — say both.
