Pathophysiology note: the neuroinflammation ("post-viral cytokine") hypothesis is unproven — a prospective 30-cytokine panel found no NDPH-specific signature vs chronic migraine or controls (Cheema 2024). Empiric anti-inflammatory treatment (steroids, doxycycline) stays anecdotal.

docs.title: Rapid approach
docs.text:
1. Confirm the ICHD-3 profile: daily and continuous within 24 h of a distinct remembered onset, present >3 months, no prior chronic headache.
  2. Exclude secondary causes — MRI/MRV + LP with opening pressure are the high-yield tests.
  3. Classify the phenotype (migraine-like vs tension-type-like) once secondary causes are out.
  4. Treat by phenotype and set realistic expectations — NDPH is often refractory.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Confirm the onset pattern (daily-from-a-remembered-day, >3 months, no prior headache history).
  2. Complete the exclusion work-up (order set below) — this is the deliverable that lets the diagnosis stand and that any board will scrutinize.
  3. Classify the phenotype: migraine-like or tension-type-like.
  4. Treat early and in parallel — don't wait for the 3-month threshold. At the same visit, start a transitional bridge AND a phenotype-matched preventive (Step 1). Delay independently predicts worse outcomes.
  5. Fix perpetuators: sleep, mood, and medication overuse (screen every visit).
  6. Set expectations with the numbers: ~¾ persist (refractory), ~15% remit, ~8% relapse-remit.
  7. Refractory? Escalate the transitional therapies, nerve blocks, Botox, CGRP, neuromodulation, headache-specialist referral (Step 3).

docs.title: Workup — the mandatory exclusion set
docs.text:
Order the whole exclusion work-up (normal studies are what let NDPH stand)

This is the most thorough of the primary-headache work-ups. Do it once, completely:

  • MRI brain with and without contrast (mass, sinus disease, SIH signs — pachymeningeal enhancement, brain sag).
  • MRV or CTV — cerebral venous sinus thrombosis.
  • MRI spine if a CSF leak is suspected (postural component).
  • LP with opening pressure and CSF studies — high (IIH) and low (leak) pressure, plus infectious/inflammatory causes. Fundoscopy for papilledema first.
  • ESR/CRP in patients ≥50 (giant cell arteritis).
  • Targeted labs as history dictates (TSH, infectious/post-viral work-up).

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
There's no NDPH-specific approved drug, so borrow the phenotype ladder — but the emerging theme is treat aggressively and early: a transitional bridge produces the highest initial response (~57% benefit at first use), delay predicts worse outcomes, and the biology of refractoriness may begin before the 3-month diagnostic threshold. So don't slow-walk it.

Step 1 — start a transitional bridge AND a preventive at the same visit (early)

Do both together, don't sequence them.

  • Transitional bridge (highest first-use response — start it):
    • Greater occipital ± other nerve blocks — fast, low-risk, repeatable.
    • IV bridge (monitored): methylprednisolone, DHE (Raskin-style), or IV lidocaine. Per the 2026 ASRA inpatient-headache Practice Advisory: IV lidocaine 0.5–1.5 mg/kg/h (or 1–2 mg/min), cardiac monitoring (~51% get ≥50% relief, ~43% sustained at 1 month); IV ketamine ≤1 mg/kg/h outside the ICU (Grade C) with continuous respiratory/hemodynamic monitoring, ± clonidine and/or low-dose benzodiazepine to blunt psychotomimetic effects.
  • Phenotype-matched preventive (start concurrently):
    • Migraine-like → migraine preventive ladder (nortriptyline/amitriptyline, topiramate; CGRP therapy — but temper expectations, below).
    • Tension-type-like → amitriptyline/nortriptyline + non-drug measures (sleep, PT, posture, stress).
  • OnabotulinumtoxinA — reach for it earlier than "last resort": it now has the strongest NDPH-specific data — ~one-third of adults improve (Cheema 2025, comparable to chronic migraine) and up to 70% of pediatric NDPH (Abuhalaweh 2026), with a favorable safety profile. Consider it early, especially in the migraine-like phenotype.
  • Supplements: riboflavin, magnesium; CoQ10 was the only treatment significantly associated with resolution in a pediatric cohort (17% vs 4%, p=0.01) — cheap and reasonable to add.

CGRP mAbs help fewer NDPH patients than you'd expect — NDPH ≠ chronic migraine

In the largest prospective series, CGRP mAbs gave ≥30% improvement in only ~23% of NDPH vs 46–84% in chronic migraine (subjective improvement ~54%). Worth trying, but set realistic expectations — NDPH is not equivalent to CM in treatment response. (Case reports favor eptinezumab/rimegepant/atogepant over erenumab/galcanezumab — low-certainty.)

Step 2 — fix the perpetuators (every visit)

  • Screen and treat medication overuse (MOH page) — it silently sustains NDPH.
  • Sleep, mood, anxiety — treat comorbid depression/insomnia; they gate any response.
  • Post-COVID NDPH — treat by phenotype (migraine-like vs tension-type-like); CGRP mAbs show promise in case series. Headache persisting >3 months post-infection predicts a refractory course — don't over-promise resolution.

Step 3 — refractory NDPH (specialist territory)

Escalate the Step-1 tools and refer to a headache specialist:

  • Repeat/optimize nerve blocks and IV bridges (ASRA dosing above); admit for a monitored lidocaine or DHE course.
  • OnabotulinumtoxinA if not already tried (see Step 1 — it belongs earlier).
  • CGRP therapies — reasonable trial despite the modest NDPH response rate.
  • Neuromodulation (e-TNS, remote electrical neuromodulation, nVNS) — low-risk adjuncts.

Avoid

Opioids and butalbital — ineffective and they seed medication-overuse headache on top of an already-refractory disorder.

TRICARE — NDPH prescribing

There's no NDPH-specific pathway, so prescribe by phenotype: oral preventives (nortriptyline, amitriptyline, topiramate) are generic/formulary, no PA. For a CGRP, galcanezumab (Emgality) is the preferred, no-PA entry point — though note the NDPH-specific signal favors eptinezumab/rimegepant/atogepant, which are PA-gated (and need neurologist involvement). OnabotulinumtoxinA requires the chronic-migraine phenotype for on-label coverage.

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
Outpatient, with a completed and documented secondary work-up and phenotype-directed treatment plus follow-up. Consider a headache-specialist referral for refractory cases.

Military medicine — new daily persistent headache

Framework only — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03, DoDI 1332.18, and aeromedical policy.

  • Document the exclusion work-up — MRI/MRV, LP with opening pressure, ESR/CRP — because NDPH is a diagnosis of exclusion and any board will scrutinize the work-up.
  • Deployability: a daily unremitting headache is frequently deployment-limiting while active and being characterized.
  • Profile (DA Form 3349): temporary during work-up/treatment trials; permanent if it persists with functional limitation.
  • EPTS / LOD: the distinct onset date helps establish timing relative to service.
  • Retention — AR 40-501: an NDPH with a migraine/tension phenotype that is incapacitating ≥3 days/month is handled under para 3-31g (neurology referral → up to 6 months prophylaxis → DES if uncontrolled); refractory persistent severe headache after adequate treatment is captured by para 3-31k (other neurologic conditions) → DES (MEB/PEB). Document the exclusion work-up, frequency, failed trials, and duty impact.
  • Aeromedical / special duty: an active daily headache and its medications are generally disqualifying/waiver-dependent.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• NDPH is defined by its onset pattern, not its character — daily and continuous from a remembered starting point.
  • It is fundamentally a diagnosis of exclusion: the priority is ruling out CSF-pressure disorders (SIH/IIH), CVST, and GCA with MRI/MRV, LP with opening pressure, and inflammatory markers.
  • No NDPH-specific approved drug — but treat early and hit it with a bridge + preventive together. Transitional therapy has the highest first-use response (~57%), and delayed treatment independently predicts worse outcomes — don't wait for the 3-month threshold.
  • OnabotulinumtoxinA now has the strongest NDPH-specific evidence (~⅓ of adults, ~70% of children respond; good safety) — use it earlier, not as a last resort.
  • CGRP mAbs are less effective in NDPH than in chronic migraine (~23% vs 46–84% ≥30% response) — worth trying, but NDPH ≠ CM; set expectations.
  • Prognosis: ~76% persist, ~15% remit (median ~21 mo), ~8% relapse-remit. The neuroinflammation hypothesis is unproven (no NDPH-specific cytokine signature).
  • The IV bridge now has formal guidance — 2026 ASRA advisory (lidocaine, ketamine dosing/monitoring).
  • Diagnosis of exclusion first (SIH/IIH, CVST, GCA); fix perpetuators; CoQ10 is a cheap pediatric signal; avoid opioids/butalbital.

Key sources: ICHD-3; Continuum 2024 NDPH review; Cheema et al. (onabotulinumtoxinA 2025; CGRP mAb 2025; cytokine 2024); Abuhalaweh/Szperka pediatric NDPH cohorts (2024–2026); Robbins prognostic subforms (2010); 2026 ASRA inpatient-headache Practice Advisory; secondary-headache exclusion guidance.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Headache Classification Committee of the International Headache Society. ICHD-3.
  2. New Daily Persistent Headache. Continuum (Minneap Minn). 2024.
  3. Linnet A, et al. Challenges in the management of NDPH at a tertiary headache center — a retrospective real-world study. Headache. 2024.
  4. Treatment evidence: Cheema S, et al. OnabotulinumtoxinA for primary NDPH (Cephalalgia 2025) and CGRP mAb treatment for NDPH (J Headache Pain 2025) and Serum cytokines in NDPH (Front Neurol 2024); Abuhalaweh N, et al. Treatment outcomes in pediatric NDPH (Cephalalgia 2026); Szperka CL, et al. Headache trajectories in new-onset continuous headache (Cephalalgia 2024); Takle M, et al. CoQ10 and NDPH resolution (J Child Neurol 2025).
  5. Robbins MS, et al. Clinical and prognostic subforms of NDPH. Neurology. 2010.
  6. Hoydonckx Y, et al. Practice Advisory for IV management of headache in hospitalized patients (ASRA). Reg Anesth Pain Med. 2026 — IV lidocaine/ketamine dosing and monitoring.
  7. TRICARE / DoD Uniform Formulary: phenotype-directed oral preventives generic/formulary; galcanezumab (Emgality) preferred/no-PA; other CGRP agents + onabotulinumtoxinA (needs chronic-migraine phenotype for on-label) PA + neurologist. Verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Verified July 2026: NDPH remains a clinical diagnosis of exclusion with no NDPH-specific approved drug. The newer treatment emphases — early combined transitional + preventive therapy (delay worsens outcomes), onabotulinumtoxinA as the best NDPH-specific evidence (~⅓ adults / ~70% children), lower CGRP-mAb response than in chronic migraine (~23%), the 2026 ASRA IV lidocaine/ketamine parameters, the negative neuroinflammation biomarker data, prognostic subforms (~76% persisting), the CoQ10 pediatric signal, and post-COVID phenotype-directed care — were supplied by expert review with citations; confirm the specific figures before relying on them. Service-specific aeromedical/retention specifics warrant confirmation before final sign-off.

docs.title: Post-traumatic headache
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: headache that develops in close temporal relation to head and/or neck trauma — the most common symptom after concussion/mild TBI, and a signature problem in the military population (blast, vehicle, fall, and blunt mechanisms). Most persistent post-traumatic headache (PTH) is phenotypically migraine or tension-type, so you treat the phenotype while managing the comorbidities that keep it going.

🚩 Red flags — this is a headache after trauma, so exclude structure first

Pattern Worry Do now Worsening/persistent severe headache, vomiting, ↓ or fluctuating consciousness Expanding subdural / epidural / contusion / SAH Non-contrast CTH now On anticoagulant/antiplatelet or coagulopathy Delayed intracranial hemorrhage (low threshold) CTH, reverse if bleeding Focal deficit, new seizure, worsening cognition Structural lesion, evolving injury CT → MRI Neck pain/trauma, Horner's, pulsatile tinnitus Cervical artery dissection CTA/MRA head & neck Positional headache after LP or trauma CSF leak / intracranial hypotension MRI; consider blood patch Second head impact before recovery Second-impact syndrome (rare, catastrophic) Remove from activity; urgent eval

docs.title: Rapid approach
docs.text:
1. Exclude a structural injury — mechanism, anticoagulation, and exam drive whether you image. Use a validated decision rule for acute head injury imaging.
  2. Define the timeline (ICHD-3): headache developing within 7 days of the injury (or of regaining consciousness / the ability to report). Acute PTH resolves within 3 months; persistent PTH lasts > 3 months.
  3. Classify the phenotype — migraine-like vs tension-type-like — and treat accordingly.
  4. Find and treat the persistence drivers: sleep disruption, mood (depression, PTSD), cervicogenic contribution, vestibular dysfunction, and medication overuse.
  5. Avoid opioids/butalbital and cap acute meds — PTH is a high-risk setting for MOH.

docs.title: Diagnosis (ICHD-3)
docs.text: PTH is a secondary headache attributed to trauma or injury to the head and/or neck. The diagnosis is defined by the temporal relationship (onset ≤ 7 days), not by a specific headache character — which is why the individual phenotype (migrainous vs tension-type) guides treatment. Persistent PTH (> 3 months) is where most of the clinical work lives.

docs.title: Focused H&P + exam
docs.text:
• Injury: mechanism (blast/blunt/vehicle/fall), loss of consciousness, post-traumatic amnesia, number and timing of prior concussions.
  • Headache: phenotype, frequency, acute-med days/month (MOH screen), triggers.
  • Comorbidity screen — the key to persistent PTH: sleep, mood/PTSD, neck pain, dizziness/vestibular symptoms, cognition, photophobia.
  • Exam: full neuro exam, cervical spine and myofascial exam, and a vestibular/oculomotor screen. Fixed deficits reopen the structural work-up.

docs.title: Workup
docs.text: Image acutely (CT) for the red flags above or per a validated head-injury rule; MRI for persistent, atypical, or progressive presentations. Uncomplicated concussion with a normal exam does not need routine imaging. There is no blood or imaging test that "rules in" PTH.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Exclude structural injury (red-flag table / validated head-CT rule) — always first.
  2. Classify the phenotype — migraine-like or tension-type-like.
  3. Treat the phenotype on the matching migraine/tension ladder (Step 1).
  4. Dismantle the perpetuators in parallel — sleep, mood/PTSD, cervicogenic, vestibular, and medication overuse (Step 2). This is where persistent PTH is won or lost.
  5. Prescribe aerobic exercise — it's a treatment, not just "return to activity" (protocol box below); brief relative rest, then progress. Not open-ended rest.
  6. Refractory? Nerve blocks, CGRP therapy, and IV bridges (Step 3); build a multidisciplinary team (headache + sleep + behavioral health + vestibular/PT).
  7. Avoid opioids/butalbital throughout — PTH is a high-risk MOH setting.

Who persists — and why to treat early

Most acute PTH resolves, but a substantial fraction chronifies (TRACK-TBI: ~60% acute PTH at 2 weeks → ~52% still present at 3 months → ~29% at 12 months). Risk factors for persistence: female sex, prior migraine history, fewer years of education, acute PTH lasting >30 consecutive days, HIT-6 >45, and cognitive symptoms (difficulty concentrating — the strongest single association, OR >3), plus comorbid anxiety/depression/sleep disorders.

Treat early — don't wait for it to become "persistent." Scheduled analgesics in the first ~72 h may reduce acute severity; starting a preventive within ~4–6 weeks may prevent central sensitization/chronification (and preclinical data show early onabotulinumtoxinA fully prevented the transition to persistent PTH, while delayed dosing only partly did). Be most aggressive in the high-risk patients above.

Step 1 — treat the phenotype (there is no PTH-specific drug — borrow the ladder)

Lifestyle first (2025 AAPM&R): regular sleep schedule, no skipped meals, hydration, limit alcohol, low-intensity aerobic exercise, and relaxation strategies — start these at the first visit.

Prophylaxis threshold (2025 AAPM&R): offer a daily preventive when headaches are ≥10 days/ month or disabling.

  • Migraine-like PTH → the migraine acute + preventive ladders:
    • Acute: stratified — NSAID → triptan, or a gepant/lasmiditan where triptans are unsuitable. Cap acute days; prefer a gepant (no MOH) given the high overuse risk here — but note the gepant preference is extrapolated from migraine; there are no PTH-specific gepant efficacy data.
    • Only positive acute-PTH RCT: metoclopramide 10 mg + diphenhydramine 25 mg IV beat placebo (a modest ~1.4-point pain improvement) — a reasonable first-line ED combination for an acute PTH attack.
    • Preventive (if frequent/disabling): amitriptyline or nortriptyline 10–50 mg qHS (helps comorbid sleep/mood — often the best first pick in PTH); venlafaxine (SNRI); candesartan (ARB); or propranolol; escalate to CGRP therapy or onabotulinumtoxinA (below) if first-line fails.
  • Tension-type-like PTH → amitriptyline/nortriptyline + analgesic sparing, and treat the neck and sleep drivers directly.

Prefer cognitively-neutral preventives — go easy on topiramate/valproate

Topiramate and valproate are not first-line for PTH — their cognitive side effects compound the TBI patient's existing cognitive complaints (Lancet Neurology algorithm; not among the 2025 AAPM&R named options). Prefer amitriptyline, venlafaxine, candesartan, or propranolol. Prazosin is a useful military-population option — emerging PTH evidence with minimal side effects and a dual benefit for comorbid PTSD nightmares (generic/formulary).

Prescribe sub-threshold aerobic exercise — the best non-drug evidence in concussion/PTH

Exercise is a specific therapy, not generic advice — start it early, even while symptomatic.

  • Start within 24–72 h with light activity (walking); waiting for symptoms to fully resolve is worse. Subthreshold aerobic exercise sped recovery (median 13 vs 17 days) and cut persisting symptoms (21% vs 32%) vs stretching, with a large effect size (g ≈ 1.0).
  • Dose it to a heart-rate target: the HR at the patient's symptom-exacerbation threshold (Buffalo-type test), or simply start at ~50% of age-predicted max HR (220 − age), 20 min/day, and titrate up as tolerated — stay below the level that flares symptoms.
  • Works for persisting PTH too, and regardless of initial symptom burden. For service members, this doubles as a structured, documentable return-to-duty progression.

Step 2 — dismantle the perpetuators (every visit; this is the real work)

  • CBT for headache — the strongest non-drug evidence, and it's from veterans. In the McGeary RCT (JAMA Neurol 2022) of post-9/11 combat veterans with PTH+PTSD, 8 sessions of headache-specific CBT cut headache disability (HIT-6 −3.4) AND improved PTSD symptoms. Crucially, treating the PTSD alone (cognitive processing therapy) improved PTSD but NOT the headache — so refer for headache-specific CBT, not just PTSD therapy.
  • Sleep: treat insomnia/OSA first — poor sleep alone sustains PTH.
  • Mood / PTSD: co-treat; PTH and PTSD strongly track together in service members (prazosin helps PTSD nightmares — see Step 1).
  • Cervicogenic (a distinct PTH subtype): manual therapy of the neck ("Good Practice," 2025 AAPM&R) + targeted physical therapy; greater/lesser occipital nerve blocks for occipital tenderness.
  • Vestibular: cervical + vestibular physical therapy — facilitates return to activity (HR ~3.9 vs rest) when dizziness/neck pain/headache persist >10 days.
  • Medication overuse: cap or withdraw overused acute meds and start a preventive (MOH page).

Step 3 — refractory persistent PTH (specialist / multidisciplinary)

Evidence is largely extrapolated from migraine — set expectations. Reasonable options:

  • Nerve blocks — consider these earlier, not just when refractory. PTH-specific data are strong: ~88% average pain improvement immediately, ~73% sustained at 6 months (File 2025), and GON blocks historically help PTH more than migraine (72% vs 54%). Fast, low-risk, repeatable — a high-yield early move.
  • CGRP therapies — worth trying, but note the PTH-specific signal: the fremanezumab (ligand) phase-2 RCT was negative (a non-significant reduction in moderate-to-severe headache days), while erenumab (receptor) showed an open-label benefit — so if you use a CGRP for PTH, the receptor-targeting agents (erenumab, or an oral gepant) are the more rational bet (low-certainty).
  • OnabotulinumtoxinA — the only treatment with a positive PTH-specific RCT. In military veterans (Zirovich 2021 crossover), headaches/week fell ~43% vs worsened ~35% on placebo (p<0.001), with less pain severity. Combined with the preclinical "early dosing prevents chronification" signal, consider it earlier — don't wait for a strict chronic-migraine phenotype (though that's still what on-label TRICARE coverage needs).
  • Repetitive TMS — small RCTs show short-term benefit (≈58% got ≥50% headache reduction; 60% vs 10% return-to-work in one pilot), but the VA/DoD mTBI CPG suggests against it (small trials, no sustained benefit at 4–6 months, seizure-risk concern) — reserve for specialist/research settings.
  • IV bridge for a severe refractory flare — see the acute-flare box.

Acute refractory PTH flare (migraine phenotype)

Use the status migrainosus ER protocol (dopamine antagonist + NSAID + nerve block; magnesium; dexamethasone before discharge). No opioids or butalbital. Ensure a structural cause has been excluded first — a post-traumatic patient with a new or escalating headache gets imaged, not just medicated.

Avoid

  • Opioids and butalbital — ineffective and drive MOH and dependence.
  • Prolonged strict rest — brief relative rest, then a graded return to activity.

TRICARE — PTH prescribing

Prescribe by phenotype: amitriptyline, nortriptyline, venlafaxine, candesartan, propranolol, and prazosin are all generic/formulary, no PA — the TCAs are an efficient first pick (headache + sleep + mood), and prazosin is a two-for-one with PTSD nightmares. Topiramate is formulary but cognitively costly in TBI — not first-line here. For a CGRP, galcanezumab (Emgality) is the preferred, no-PA option — but the PTH evidence favors receptor-targeting agents, and erenumab (Aimovig) / atogepant (Qulipta) are PA-gated + neurologist. OnabotulinumtoxinA needs the chronic-migraine phenotype for on-label coverage. Nerve blocks, manual/vestibular PT, and headache-specific CBT go through normal specialty/rehab referral.

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
Outpatient with clear return precautions for the red flags above. For concussion, use a graded, supervised return-to-activity progression rather than open-ended rest, and arrange multidisciplinary follow-up (headache + sleep + behavioral health + vestibular/PT) for persistent cases.

Concussion return-to-play/duty essentials (2025 NEJM review)

  • Return to school/cognitive work before return to sport/full duty — early, graded cognitive activity speeds recovery; prolonged absence delays it.
  • Cervical + vestibular PT facilitates return (HR ~3.9 vs rest) when dizziness/neck pain/ headache persist >10 days.
  • SCOAT6 (Sport Concussion Office Assessment Tool-6) is a free office-assessment aid (awaiting prospective validation).
  • Blood biomarkers (GFAP, total tau) are NOT ready for routine clinical use — don't adopt prematurely.

Military medicine — post-traumatic headache / concussion (mTBI)

Standards below are quoted from DoDI 6130.03 Vol 1/Vol 2, AR 40-501, the VA/DoD mTBI CPG, and DoDI 6490.11; the DES process is governed by DoDI 1332.18 / DoDM 1332.18. mTBI is protocolized in DoD — follow the mandated pathway, not just this page.

  • Event-driven protocol: after a potentially concussive event (blast, vehicle, fall, blunt), DoD policy mandates evaluation and a mandatory recovery period — screen with MACE2 and manage per the deployed-concussion protocol before return to duty.
  • Deployability: acute concussion is temporarily non-deployable pending clearance; persistent PTH with cognitive, sleep, or mood comorbidity can be deployment-limiting. Multiple concussions raise the stakes for return-to-duty decisions.
  • Return to activity: use the Progressive Return to Activity stages; symptom recurrence means step back a stage.
  • Profile (DA Form 3349): temporary profile during recovery; permanent profile for persistent, functionally limiting PTH/post-concussive syndrome.
  • EPTS / LOD: combat/duty-incurred TBI is typically in the line of duty — document the event, mechanism, and timeline carefully; it drives both LOD and VA rating.
  • Retention — DoD & Army: the joint standard (DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2, para 5.26.h) places TBI with persistent sequelae — including severe headaches, sensory/cognitive/motor impairment, or seizures — below retention standards. For the Army, a migraine-phenotype PTH incapacitating ≥3 days/month falls under AR 40-501 para 3-31g, and residual persistent severe headaches under para 3-31k → referral to the DES (MEB/PEB). Document objective frequency, failed trials, neuropsych findings, and duty impact.
  • Accession — DoDI 6130.03 Vol 1, para 6.26: for applicants, persistent post-concussive symptoms interfering with normal activities or lasting > 1 month (headache, dizziness, cognitive/memory problems), a history of head trauma with persistent focal deficit, or ≥3 mild brain injuries, are disqualifying for entry.
  • Aeromedical / special duty (AR 40-501 ch. 4, table 4-2): head-injury standards are graded by how long the headache/post-traumatic syndrome persists — e.g., PTH resolving within ~14 days causes a temporary aviation suspension (≈1–3 months by class), while post- traumatic syndrome persisting beyond ~6 weeks causes permanent disqualification / 2-year termination of aviation service. Confirm the exact tier in table 4-2 for the class involved.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
Written from primary guidelines and trials; source articles are a reading list, not copied.

  • Treat the phenotype, not the label. Persistent PTH is most often migraine-like; when it is, it responds best to migraine-directed acute and preventive therapy.
  • Persistence is multifactorial. Sleep disturbance, mood/PTSD, cervicogenic pain, vestibular dysfunction, and medication overuse perpetuate PTH — outcomes improve when these are treated alongside the headache, which is why multidisciplinary care is the standard for persistent PTH.
  • Rest is not the treatment — prescribed aerobic exercise is. Subthreshold aerobic exercise started within 24–72 h speeds recovery (median 13 vs 17 days) and cuts persisting symptoms (21% vs 32%), effect size g ≈ 1.0 — dose it to ~50% age-predicted max HR or a symptom threshold. The strongest non-drug evidence in concussion/PTH.
  • Nerve blocks may be especially effective in PTH (~88% improve, ~73% sustained at 6 months; better than in migraine) — consider them early, not just when refractory.
  • The only positive acute-PTH RCT is metoclopramide + diphenhydramine (modest ~1.4-point benefit) — a reasonable ED go-to for an attack. Gepants are the theoretical acute ideal but unproven in PTH.
  • Treat early and identify who's at risk. ~52% of acute PTH persists at 3 months (TRACK-TBI); female sex, prior migraine, acute PTH >30 days, HIT-6 >45, and cognitive symptoms predict persistence. Scheduled early analgesia + preventive within 4–6 weeks may prevent chronification.
  • The first formal PTH treatment guidance arrived (2025 AAPM&R Action Collaborative): lifestyle first, preventive at ≥10 headache days/month or disabling, named options amitriptyline, venlafaxine, candesartan, and CGRP inhibitors, and manual therapy for cervicogenic PTH.
  • OnabotulinumtoxinA is the only positive PTH-specific RCT (Zirovich 2021, veterans: ~43% fewer headaches vs worsening on placebo) — consider it earlier than "chronic-migraine only."
  • Headache-specific CBT is the strongest non-drug evidence (McGeary 2022: HIT-6 −3.4 + PTSD benefit in veterans) — and treating PTSD alone did not fix the headache.
  • Avoid opioids/butalbital, and avoid topiramate/valproate first-line (cognitive cost in TBI).
  • CGRP evidence is mechanism-relevant: fremanezumab (ligand) phase-2 negative vs erenumab (receptor) open-label positive — prefer receptor-targeting agents if using a CGRP.
  • The evidence base is still thin — a 2026 systematic review found only 2 low-bias RCTs (1 PTH-specific); nearly all treatment is extrapolated.

Key sources: ICHD-3; 2025 AAPM&R Action Collaborative TBI guideline; VA/DoD mTBI CPG (2021); DoDI 6490.11 + TBICoE Progressive Return to Activity; Zirovich 2021 (onabotulinumtoxinA RCT); McGeary 2022 (CBT RCT); TRACK-TBI / McIntosh 2025 (risk factors); Ashina 2021 (Lancet Neurology review); Saleh 2026 systematic review.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Headache Classification Committee of the International Headache Society. ICHD-3.
  2. VA/DoD. Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management and Rehabilitation of Post-Acute Mild Traumatic Brain Injury (2021).
  3. DoD. DoDI 6490.11 — Policy Guidance for Management of mTBI/Concussion in the Deployed Setting.
  4. Traumatic Brain Injury Center of Excellence (TBICoE). Progressive Return to Activity clinical recommendation and provider tools.
  5. Ashina H, et al. Post-traumatic headache attributed to TBI: classification, clinical characteristics, and treatment. Lancet Neurol. 2021 — early-treatment/chronification hypothesis; topiramate/valproate caution; erenumab vs fremanezumab (receptor vs ligand).
  6. Silverberg ND, et al. Action Collaborative on TBI Care — Adapted CPG (Ann Fam Med 2025) — first formal PTH treatment synthesis (prophylaxis ≥10 d/mo; amitriptyline/venlafaxine/candesartan/CGRP; manual therapy for cervicogenic).
  7. Zirovich MD, et al. Botulinum toxin type A for PTH — RCT (Mil Med 2021, positive in veterans); McGeary DD, et al. CBT for veterans with PTH+PTSD — RCT (JAMA Neurol 2022).
  8. Ashina H, et al. PTH prevalence/risk factors (TRACK-TBI) (Mayo Clin Proc 2023); McIntosh SJ, et al. Factors associated with persisting symptoms after concussion (JAMA Netw Open 2025); Saleh S, et al. Pharmacological treatments for post-TBI headache — systematic review (Cephalalgia 2026).
  9. Concussion / exercise / neuromodulation: Leddy JJ. Sport-Related Concussion. NEJM. 2025 (aerobic-exercise Rx, cervical/vestibular PT, SCOAT6, biomarkers); Leddy JJ, et al. Early subthreshold aerobic exercise RCT (JAMA Pediatr 2019); File C, et al. Nerve blocks for refractory PTH (Pain Physician 2025); Stilling J, et al. rTMS for persistent PTH — pilot RCT (J Neurotrauma 2020); Minen MT, et al. PTH treatment review (Curr Pain Headache Rep 2024 — metoclopramide+ diphenhydramine; fremanezumab result).
  10. TRICARE / DoD Uniform Formulary: phenotype-directed oral preventives (amitriptyline, nortriptyline, venlafaxine, candesartan, propranolol, prazosin) generic/formulary; galcanezumab (Emgality) preferred/no-PA; erenumab/atogepant + onabotulinumtoxinA PA + neurologist. Verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Verified July 2026: DoDI 6490.11 (mandates MACE screening across the Services), MACE2, the 2021 VA/DoD mTBI CPG, and the 6-stage Progressive Return to Activity framework are current and correctly characterized. PTH treatment is still largely extrapolated (no robust PTH-specific approved drug — a 2026 systematic review found only 2 low-bias RCTs). The newer emphases — the 2025 AAPM&R treatment synthesis (prophylaxis ≥10 d/mo; amitriptyline/venlafaxine/candesartan/CGRP; manual therapy for cervicogenic; topiramate/valproate cognitive caution), onabotulinumtoxinA as the only positive PTH-specific RCT (Zirovich 2021), headache-specific CBT (McGeary 2022, > PTSD-only therapy), risk factors for persistence (TRACK-TBI/McIntosh), early-treatment/chronification prevention, prazosin, prescribed subthreshold aerobic exercise (2025 NEJM/Leddy — strongest non-drug evidence), quantified nerve-block efficacy (File 2025), metoclopramide+diphenhydramine as the only positive acute-PTH RCT, the no-PTH-specific-gepant-data caveat, and rTMS (VA/DoD suggests against) — were supplied by expert review with citations; confirm the specific figures before relying on them. CGRP mechanism note (fremanezumab-ligand negative vs erenumab-receptor open-label) stands. Retention/accession standards are quoted from DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 (5.26.h) / Vol 1 (6.26) and AR 40-501 (3-31g/k); the graded aviation head-injury standards are AR 40-501 ch. 4, table 4-2 — confirm the exact table-4-2 tier at sign-off.

docs.title: Trigeminal neuralgia
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: paroxysmal, electric-shock facial pain in one or more trigeminal divisions (usually V2/V3), triggered by innocuous stimuli — light touch, chewing, talking, brushing teeth, cold air. Volleys of stabbing pain with pain-free intervals. Multiple sclerosis can present with trigeminal neuralgia — always keep it on the table.

🚩 Red flags for a secondary cause — image everyone

Features that point away from classical TN and toward a structural or demyelinating cause:

  • Sensory loss or other cranial-nerve deficit on exam
  • Bilateral symptoms
  • Age < 40 at onset
  • Poor response to sodium-channel-blocking drugs

Any of these — and honestly, all first presentations — warrant MRI to look for neurovascular compression, a tumor, or an MS plaque at the root entry zone.

docs.title: Diagnose & classify (ICHD-3)
docs.text:
• Phenotype: brief (a fraction of a second to 2 min), shock-like/stabbing, triggered, division-specific pain with normal sensation between attacks. A "concomitant continuous pain" variant (a background aching/burning between the shocks) exists, predicts a harder course, and needs different drugs (see the continuous-pain note in Step 2).
  • Classify: classical (proven neurovascular compression with morphological change — atrophy or displacement of the nerve), secondary (MS, tumor, AV malformation), or idiopathic (no cause found).

🚩 Screen for depression and suicidality — the "suicide disease" is not hyperbole

TN carries a doubled suicide/self-harm risk (standardized incidence ratio ~2.1, higher in women) and roughly a third of patients report suicidal ideation; anxiety, depression, and pain intensity all track with worse suicidality. (A large cohort also found increased stroke and MI risk.) Ask every TN patient about mood and suicidal ideation, treat the pain aggressively, and co-manage psychological burden — pain control is the intervention, and unrelieved TN is a safety issue, not just a comfort issue.

docs.title: Rapid approach
docs.text:
1. Confirm the phenotype (above) with a normal interictal exam.
  2. MRI brain — thin cuts through the pontine cistern / course of CN V for vascular compression, demyelination (MS), or tumor.
  3. Start a sodium-channel blocker (Step 1) — titrate to pain freedom.
  4. Refer for surgery early if pain isn't controlled or the drug isn't tolerated — don't grind through five drugs first.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Confirm phenotype + MRI (exclude MS/tumor, characterize neurovascular compression).
  2. Start carbamazepine or oxcarbazepine, titrate to effect (Step 1). Screen HLA-B*15:02 before carbamazepine in at-risk ancestry.
  3. Inadequate or not tolerated? Add or switch to a second-line agent (Step 2) — lamotrigine, gabapentin/pregabalin, baclofen, or botulinum toxin A.
  4. Acute pain crisis (can't eat/drink/take pills): admit for IV fosphenytoin or lidocaine + rehydration (crisis order set).
  5. Refractory or poorly tolerated on adequate medical therapy: refer for surgery — microvascular decompression is first-line surgery for classical TN with neurovascular compression; ablative procedures for poor surgical candidates.
  6. MS plaque on MRI: pivot to the demyelinating work-up — the TN is a symptom of the larger diagnosis.

docs.title: Workup
docs.text: MRI is mandatory — it both excludes secondary causes (especially MS and posterior-fossa tumors) and characterizes neurovascular compression for surgical planning. Baseline CBC, sodium, LFTs before starting carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine; HLA-B*15:02 genotyping before carbamazepine in patients of Han Chinese, Thai, or other Southeast Asian ancestry (SJS/TEN risk).

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
Step 1 — first-line: a sodium-channel blocker (start here for everyone)

Both are first-line (EAN 2019). Start low, titrate to pain freedom, then hold the lowest effective dose.

  • Carbamazepine — start 100–200 mg BID, titrate by ~100–200 mg every few days; usual effective 600–800 mg/day (range 200–1200 mg/day), divided. Most effective agent; least well tolerated. Monitor CBC, sodium (hyponatremia), LFTs; it has an ECG effect and major CYP-inducing drug interactions. Screen HLA-B*15:02 first in at-risk ancestry.
  • Oxcarbazepine — start 300 mg BID, titrate to 600–1800 mg/day divided. Better tolerated with comparable efficacy and fewer interactions — a reasonable default — but hyponatremia is more common (check sodium).

→ Pain not controlled, or intolerable side effects → Step 2 (and start thinking about surgical referral in parallel).

Natural history — try a drug-free trial after sustained remission

TN remits and relapses: ~50% get a remission of up to 6 months. After sustained pain freedom, attempt a slow taper off the sodium-channel blocker — long-term CBZ/OXC carries cumulative toxicity (hyponatremia, marrow suppression, interactions). Warn the patient that attacks tend to get worse over time despite treatment, so relapse is likely and the drug can be restarted.

Step 2 — add-on / second-line (alone or added to Step 1)

Per EAN 2019, any of these may be used alone or as add-on when first-line fails or isn't tolerated:

  • Lamotrigine — titrate slowly (25 mg, up over weeks to ~200–400 mg/day) for rash/SJS risk; good add-on evidence.
  • Gabapentin / pregabalin — well tolerated; the go-to add-on for the continuous-pain component (see note below).
  • Baclofen — 5–10 mg TID up to ~60–80 mg/day; classic add-on to carbamazepine (taper on discontinuation).
  • Botulinum toxin type A — subcutaneous/submucosal injection; evidence has firmed up — meta-analyses show ~OR 7.5 for ≥50% pain reduction vs placebo (RCTs), well tolerated. A solid adjunctive third-line option, especially in poor surgical candidates or when sodium-channel blockers are contraindicated.
  • Increasingly used off-label: eslicarbazepine (once-daily, fewer interactions than CBZ — a reasonable dibenzazepine switch) and lacosamide (another Na-channel blocker). Phenytoin is a further option.
  • In trials: vixotrigine (Nav1.7-selective) and basimglurant (mGluR5 modulator) — novel mechanisms; not yet approved.

Concomitant continuous pain needs different drugs — and steer away from ablation

Carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine reliably help the paroxysmal shocks but are usually insufficient for the continuous aching/burning component. Add gabapentin/pregabalin or an antidepressant (e.g., a TCA/SNRI) for the continuous pain. Avoid ablative procedures when continuous pain is prominent — lesioning has a high rate of worsening neuropathic pain in this subgroup. (Percutaneous Meckel's-cave steroid/anesthetic injection is an emerging option for refractory continuous-pain cases.)

Acute TN crisis — can't eat, drink, or keep pills down (admit)

A severe exacerbation is a genuine emergency (dehydration, weight loss, unable to take oral meds).

  • Admit for rehydration and IV therapy.
  • IV fosphenytoin — rescue loading (commonly ~10–20 mg PE/kg) reduces pain within ~2 h; cardiac monitoring.
  • IV lidocaine — ~5 mg/kg over 1 h, monitored — relief up to ~24 h; buys time to up-titrate an oral agent or arrange surgery.
  • IV magnesium — a further reported acute-crisis option (adjunct).
  • Up-titrate/optimize the oral sodium-channel blocker in parallel; involve neurology/neurosurgery.

Step 3 — refractory → procedural (refer early; don't over-medicate)

Offer surgery when pain is not sufficiently controlled medically, or medication is poorly tolerated — early referral beats stacking five drugs.

  • Microvascular decompression (MVD) — first-line surgery for classical TN with confirmed neurovascular compression; the most durable result (open posterior-fossa surgery; ~2/3 pain-free at 10 years). Vs Gamma Knife (meta-analysis): MVD gives more pain relief (RR ~1.9), less recurrence (RR ~0.3), and longer pain-free intervals (median ~94 vs ~53 months) — but higher overall surgical complications, though less post-procedure numbness. Counsel the trade-off: durability vs invasiveness.
  • Ablative / less-invasive (for poor surgical candidates or secondary TN): stereotactic radiosurgery (Gamma Knife), percutaneous rhizotomy (radiofrequency, glycerol, or balloon compression). Lower invasiveness, but higher recurrence and sensory-loss/anesthesia-dolorosa trade-offs — and avoid ablation when continuous pain is prominent (worsens neuropathic pain).

TRICARE — TN prescribing

Carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, lamotrigine, gabapentin, pregabalin, and baclofen are all generic, on-formulary, no PA — the entire medical ladder is low-friction. Botulinum toxin type A for TN is off-label and will need a PA with specialist justification. Surgical referral (MVD/radiosurgery) goes through normal specialty-care authorization.

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
Outpatient, unless the patient is in a pain crisis (admit — see order set). Escalate to neurosurgery/ENT when medication fails or isn't tolerated. If the MRI shows an MS plaque, pivot to the demyelinating work-up — the TN is a symptom of the larger diagnosis.

Military medicine — trigeminal neuralgia

Framework only — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03, DoDI 1332.18, and aeromedical policy.

  • The MS question dominates disposition. If TN is secondary to MS, the MS drives retention, profile, and board decisions — work it up accordingly.
  • Deployability: limited during titration; sodium-channel blockers cause sedation, cognitive slowing, hyponatremia, and need lab monitoring — relevant to duty and austere settings. A pain crisis is non-deployable.
  • Profile (DA Form 3349): temporary during titration or crisis; permanent for refractory disease or post-procedural limitations.
  • Retention / MEB: refractory TN with duty impact, or TN secondary to a disqualifying condition (MS, tumor), warrants board referral. Document drug trials, imaging, and any surgery.
  • Aeromedical / special duty: carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine and uncontrolled TN are generally disqualifying/waiver-dependent.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• Sodium-channel blockers (carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine) are first-line (EAN 2019: CBZ 200–1200 mg/day; OXC 300–1800 mg/day); oxcarbazepine is usually better tolerated with comparable efficacy but more hyponatremia.
  • Second-line/add-on: lamotrigine, gabapentin, pregabalin, baclofen, botulinum toxin A (evidence firmed up — ~OR 7.5 for ≥50% relief), phenytoin — alone or as add-on. Eslicarbazepine and lacosamide are increasingly used off-label; vixotrigine (Nav1.7) and basimglurant (mGluR5) are in trials.
  • The "suicide disease" is real — doubled suicide/self-harm risk (SIR ~2.1), ~1/3 with suicidal ideation; screen mood/SI and treat pain aggressively.
  • Concomitant continuous pain responds poorly to CBZ/OXC alone — add gabapentin/pregabalin or an antidepressant, and avoid ablative procedures (worsen neuropathic pain).
  • MRI for everyone — to catch MS and structural causes and to plan surgery.
  • Refer for surgery early. MVD gives the most durable relief (~2/3 pain-free at 10 y) and beats Gamma Knife on pain relief/recurrence/durability (median ~94 vs ~53 months), at the cost of higher surgical complications (but less numbness).
  • Natural history remits and relapses — try a drug-free taper after sustained remission (cumulative CBZ/OXC toxicity), but expect worsening over time.
  • Acute crisis = admit for IV fosphenytoin, lidocaine, or magnesium + rehydration.
  • Screen HLA-B*15:02 before carbamazepine in Southeast Asian ancestry (SJS/TEN).

Key sources: ICHD-3 (TN criteria); EAN 2019 & AAN/EFNS 2008 guidelines; MVD-vs-Gamma-Knife meta-analysis and Barker et al. (durability); botulinum-toxin meta-analyses; TN suicide-risk cohorts; emerging-drug (eslicarbazepine/lacosamide/vixotrigine/basimglurant) reviews.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Headache Classification Committee of the International Headache Society. ICHD-3.
  2. Bendtsen L, et al. European Academy of Neurology guideline on trigeminal neuralgia. Eur J Neurol. 2019;26(6):831–849.
  3. Cruccu G, et al. AAN-EFNS guidelines on trigeminal neuralgia management. 2008; Cruccu G, et al. Trigeminal Neuralgia. NEJM. 2020 (continuous-pain management).
  4. Psychological burden: Ehinger E, et al. TN and risk of cerebrovascular disease, MI, and suicide (J Headache Pain 2026, SIR ~2.1); Fishbein NS, et al. Suicidal ideation in TN (2024).
  5. Surgery / emerging drugs / BoNT-A: Fonseca PEO, et al. MVD vs Gamma Knife meta-analysis (Neurosurg Rev 2026); Barker FG, et al. Long-term MVD outcome (NEJM 1996); Stern JI, et al. Medical management of refractory TN (Curr Opin Neurol 2026 — eslicarbazepine/lacosamide/IV Mg); botulinum-toxin umbrella review (Drugs 2026) and meta-analyses.
  6. TRICARE / DoD Uniform Formulary: carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, lamotrigine, gabapentin, pregabalin, baclofen generic-formulary (no PA); botulinum toxin A for TN off-label (PA + specialist). Verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Verified July 2026: carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine remain first-line per EAN 2019 (CBZ 200–1200 mg/day, OXC 300–1800 mg/day); lamotrigine, gabapentin, pregabalin, baclofen, botulinum toxin A, and phenytoin are second-line/add-on; microvascular decompression is first-line surgery for classical TN; acute crises are managed in-hospital with IV fosphenytoin or lidocaine; HLA-B*15:02 screening precedes carbamazepine in at-risk ancestry. The newer additions — TN suicide/self-harm risk (SIR ~2.1), emerging off-label agents (eslicarbazepine, lacosamide) and pipeline drugs (vixotrigine, basimglurant), strengthened botulinum-toxin evidence, MVD-vs-Gamma-Knife quantification, continuous-pain management, IV magnesium, and the remit/relapse drug-free-trial guidance — were supplied by expert review with citations; confirm the specific figures before relying on them. Service-specific aeromedical/retention specifics warrant confirmation before final sign-off.

docs.title: Military medicine — profiles, deployability & the MEB/MAR2 decision
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: the shared reference behind the Military medicine box on every clinical page. It answers the question providers actually get stuck on — do I board this Soldier, and if so, when, and am I the one who starts it?

Who this is for: any credentialed provider who writes profiles or sees Service members — neurology, primary care, PT/OT, behavioral health. You do not need to be an MEB physician to start this process. In the Army you usually are the person who starts it.

Verify before you counsel or document

Standards change and vary by Service and mission. Treat this as an orientation to the framework, not the authority. Always confirm specifics against the current governing publications for the member's Service and duty status — and remember that the Soldier in front of you may be Reserve or Guard, which changes the pathway.

The 30-second version

1. Has the condition reached MRDP? Clinically stable with no further progress expected, or 12 months on a temporary profile. If no → keep treating, extend the temporary profile (linked!), reassess. Do not refer yet.

2. Does the Soldier meet AR 40-501 Chapter 3 retention standards?

  • No → DES referral. Write a permanent 3 or 4 profile in e-Profile. That is the referral.
  • Yes, but they can't perform their PMOS → MAR2, not a board.

3. Can you act on the first visit? Often yes — by two different routes:

  • Epilepsy — DoDI 6130.03 5.26.k: act upon diagnosis. The "persist despite treatment" gate explicitly does not apply. You are not waiting for anything.
  • Headache — the gate does apply, but AR 40-501 3-31g lets the neurologist refer directly without finishing the 6-month prophylaxis trial if the Soldier is unlikely to respond. Your clinical judgment is the authority.

4. Who signs? You write it. A physician approval authority second-signs a 3/4 — and that signature starts the clock. You do not need the commander's agreement.

docs.title: The two-regulation rule
docs.text:
Use both, in this order. They do different jobs.

DoDI 6130.03, Volume 2 AR 40-501, Chapter 3 Scope Joint — all Services Army only Role The DoD floor: what kinds of conditions fail retention The Army's implementation: the actual number Neuro paragraph 5.26 (sleep = 5.27) 3-31 (sleep = 3-32) Example — headache 5.26.g: migraine/tension/cluster "when manifested by frequent incapacitating attacks" 3-31g: "incapacitating attacks that interfere with duty or social activities three or more days per month"

The pattern to remember: DoD says "frequent." AR 40-501 supplies the number. Cite both in your documentation — the DoD paragraph establishes that the condition is disqualifying at all, and the Army paragraph proves the Soldier crossed the threshold.

Naming

Volume 2 Change 2 (6 Feb 2026) renders as "DoWI 6130.03-V2" following the October 2025 Department of War redesignation. It is the same instruction. Cite it as DoDI 6130.03 — that is what everyone still searches, and what AR 40-501 and DoDI 1332.18 reference.

docs.title: The one question that decides everything
docs.text:
Before you think about boards at all, answer one question: does the Soldier still meet medical retention standards? Everything forks from there — and the two branches go to completely different places.

Situation Where it goes Governing text Permanent profile 3 or 4, and the Soldier meets retention standards (AR 40-501 ch. 3) but may not be able to perform their PMOS MAR2 — an administrative review AR 40-501 glossary; AR 635-40 Permanent profile 3 or 4, and the Soldier does not meet retention standards DES (MEB → PEB) — a disability process AR 40-501 3-3, 3-4 Any permanent limitation in Section 4 (Functional Activities) of DA Form 3349-SG DES referral (or the RC medical disqualification process) AR 40-502 Meets retention standards; permanent limits block all physical tasks for the MOS per DA Pam 611-21, but no functional-activity impairment MAR2 AR 40-502

The decision ladder, in order:

  1. Is it an "upon diagnosis" condition? In an active-duty population that means epilepsy (DoDI 6130.03 5.26.k). → Yes: refer upon diagnosis. Skip to step 5. No: continue.
  2. Has treatment been adequate? (drug, dose, duration documented) → No: keep treating on a temporary profile with linked extensions. Reassess. Yes: continue.
  3. Has MRDP been reached? Either clinically stable with no further progress expected, or 12 months on a temporary profile for this condition. → No: return to step 2. Yes: continue.
  4. Does the Soldier meet AR 40-501 Chapter 3 retention standards?
    • No → step 5.
    • Yes, but cannot perform their PMOS → MAR2. If MAR2 cannot retain or reclassify them, the case comes back here as a DES referral.
    • Yes, and can perform their PMOS → permanent profile with duty limits. No board.
  5. Write the permanent 3 or 4 profile in e-Profile. This is the referral.
  6. Approval authority (physician) second-signs. The clock starts here — MEB 72 days, PEB 82 days, transition 26 days.

MAR2 (Military Occupational Specialty Administrative Retention Review) decides whether the Soldier stays in their PMOS or reclassifies into a new one. It replaced the MOS Medical Retention Board. Soldiers who cannot perform their PMOS and do not qualify for reclassification get referred onward to the DES.

Do not confuse the two

MAR2 is not a mini-MEB and it is not a disability process. Sending a Soldier who fails retention standards to MAR2 delays a board they are entitled to. Sending a Soldier who meets retention standards to the DES gets the packet bounced back to you. The MEB physician's job explicitly includes "help discern MAR2 versus DES referrals" (DA Pam 40-502) — ask them when you are genuinely unsure, but know the fork yourself.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Diagnose and treat adequately. For most conditions the disqualifying standard only applies after appropriate treatment has failed — that is the explicit gate in DoDI 6130.03 5.26.a. Document the drug, dose, and duration of every trial.
  2. Ask whether the gate even applies. For epilepsy it does not — refer upon diagnosis. For headache, check whether you can invoke the neurologist's direct-referral authority under 3-31g instead of running the full 6-month trial. Both are covered below.
  3. Determine whether the Soldier has reached MRDP — the Medical Retention Determination Point. Either the condition has clinically stabilized and no further functional progress is expected, or the administrative 12-month clock has run out.
  4. Compare against AR 40-501 Chapter 3 with the DoDI 6130.03 paragraph beside it. Does the Soldier meet retention standards, yes or no?
  5. Write the permanent profile in e-Profile with the correct PULHES designator and — this is the part providers skip — a specific, functional description of the limitation in Section 4.
  6. Route for second signature. Permanent 3/4 requires an approval authority (a physician). That signature is what actually starts the board.
  7. Counsel the Soldier on what happens next and roughly how long it takes.

Step 1 → the profile is the referral

The single most common misunderstanding: providers think an MEB is a separate packet they have to assemble and submit.

It is not. In the Army, the mechanism for referring a Soldier to the DES is the permanent 3 or 4 profile in e-Profile. AR 40-501 3-4 states that the profiling officer and approval authority refer Soldiers to the DES "by submitting a permanent profile with a 3 or 4 designation for conditions that have met an MRDP, annotated in e-Profile."

Write the profile correctly and the referral has happened.

docs.title: Who can initiate — and whose signature starts the clock
docs.text:
Role Who What they do Profiling officer / profiling provider Any credentialed provider authorized to profile — including a neurologist seeing the Soldier in consultation Writes the profile, assesses retention standards, describes functional limits Second signature (permanent "2") A physician, and must not be the same person as the first signature Reviews and concurs Approval authority (permanent "3" or "4") Must be a physician, designated by the MTF commander, ARNG chief/state surgeon, or USAR command surgeon (USAR RSC surgeons hold it by position) Their signature initiates the administrative review or boarding action MEB physician Designated MEB approval authority Reviews every e-Profile MEB referral before signing: confirms MRDP was reached, bounces inappropriate referrals back, and discerns MAR2 vs DES PEBLO Assigned within 3 days of referral The Soldier's guide through the entire process Unit commander Reviews every profile in the unit; may raise concerns with the profiling officer and request a fitness-for-duty evaluation including a second opinion from another profiling officer — but cannot initiate a DES referral, and cannot block one

The load-bearing sentence, from AR 40-502: "The approval authority ensures that profiles are appropriate for entry into DES; the signature of the approval authority initiates the administrative review or boarding action."

DA Pam 40-502 adds the timeline consequence: "the application of the second signature for a permanent profile that does not meet retention standards initiates the requirement for DES... For AD Soldiers whose condition does not meet retention standards, the second signature on the permanent profile also initiates the DES timeline." For Reserve COMPOs 2 and 3, the clock instead starts when the MEB packet reaches the MEB Tracking Office.

So — can a neurologist initiate?

Yes. A neurologist seeing a Soldier in consultation is a profiling provider and can write the permanent profile that constitutes the DES referral. You will generally need an approval authority's second signature for a 3/4, but you do not need the Soldier's PCM's permission, and you do not need the commander's agreement. For headache and epilepsy specifically, AR 40-501 names the neurologist as the deciding clinician by title.

docs.title: When is a board required, when may you, and when should you not
docs.text:
REQUIRED — you must refer

  • The Soldier has a Chapter 3 disqualifying condition and has reached MRDP. AR 40-501 3-4: Soldiers meeting the para 3-1 definition "will be referred to DES."
  • A temporary profile for the same condition has run 12 months. The Soldier is then administratively defined to have reached MRDP — no further clinical judgment required.
  • Any permanent limitation in Section 4 (Functional Activities) of DA Form 3349-SG. For active-duty Soldiers this means a disability evaluation referral; for RC Soldiers it may instead initiate the medical disqualification process, depending on duty status (AR 40-502).
  • Epilepsy — DoDI 6130.03 5.26.k: not compatible with retention, act upon diagnosis.
  • Headache at ≥3 incapacitating days/month not adequately controlled after a 6-month prophylaxis trial — AR 40-501 3-31g. (You may also refer before the 6 months are up — see the MAY box.)
  • Seizures recurring beyond 6 months after treatment was initiated — AR 40-501 3-31i(4): P-3 profile and DES referral.
  • Chronic insomnia not responding to CBT-I, or requiring sleep medication ≥3 nights/week, over 6 consecutive months — AR 40-501 3-32a.
  • Dementia — DoDI 6130.03 5.26.d: also upon diagnosis. Rare in an active-duty population, but it is the only other condition in 5.26 where the gate is waived.

Do not wait for the other conditions to ripen

DA Pam 40-502: "Once MRDP is met for one condition which does not meet retention standards, referral into the DES must commence regardless of the status of other co-existing conditions." Refer on the first qualifying condition. Others get added to the case file later — they do not need to be resolved first.

MAY — clinical judgment, and you are allowed to act early

These are the situations where AR 40-501 explicitly hands the decision to the specialist:

  • Headache, before the 6-month trial is over. 3-31g: "If the neurologist feels the Soldier is unlikely to respond to therapy, the Soldier can be referred directly to the DES."
  • Suspected epilepsy. 3-31i(1): the neurologist determines whether epilepsy exists and whether the Soldier gets a trial of therapy on active duty or is referred directly to the DES — weighing underlying cause, EEG, seizure type, duration, likelihood of compliance, substance use, and any other factor bearing on control or duty performance.
  • MRDP reached ahead of the administrative clock. DA Pam 40-502: "MRDP may occur before the 12-month administrative timeline if the condition is stable and no further functional progress is expected." You do not have to burn 12 months to prove a point.
  • TBI with residual impairment after adequate treatment — AR 40-501 3-31j.
  • A Soldier previously found fit who now has a deployment-limiting condition may go back through MAR2 for a second review (AR 40-501 3-3g).

DO NOT — these get bounced, and they harm the Soldier

  • Before MRDP. The MEB physician's stated job is to confirm MRDP "has been achieved prior to initiating referral into the DES" and to route premature referrals back to you.
  • Conditions known and waived at accession that have stayed essentially the same in severity and do not meet the para 3-1 definition — AR 40-501 3-3c bars referral.
  • A Soldier who meets retention standards. That is a MAR2, not a DES referral.
  • Deferring because a non-disability separation or retirement is pending. AR 40-501 3-3d is explicit: profiling providers "should not defer initiating a DES referral until the Soldier is being processed for non-disability retirement."
  • Stacking unlinked temporary profiles. Creating a fresh temporary condition instead of linking the extension resets the days-on-profile count, hides the Soldier's progress from the commander, and delays the Soldier's access to the DES. Always link extensions to the original profile.

docs.title: Neurology conditions — the paragraph and the gate
docs.text:
Both citations belong in your note. The gate column is what determines whether you can act today.

Condition DoDI 6130.03 V2 AR 40-501 The gate Migraine / tension / cluster headache 5.26.g — "frequent incapacitating attacks" 3-31g ≥3 incapacitating days/month → mandatory neurologist referral; neurologist may trial prophylaxis up to 6 months; not controlled at 6 months → DES. May refer directly if unlikely to respond. Epilepsy 5.26.k — not compatible with retention; act upon diagnosis. 5.26.a does not apply. 3-31i Neurologist decides: trial of therapy on AD or direct DES referral. Seizure recurrence >6 months after starting treatment → P-3 + DES. Provoked seizures 5.26.j 3-31i Recurrent >6 months after treatment starts and medication effects impair duty, require significant follow-up, or require safety modifications Stroke / cerebrovascular 5.26.b (incl. SAH/ICH, stenosis, aneurysm, AVM, recurrent TIA) 3-31f Stroke: "when residuals affect performance." Recurrent TIA unless etiology identified and definitively treated TBI with persistent sequelae 5.26.h 3-31j After adequate treatment, residual symptoms/impairment meeting the para 3-1 definition → referral required Demyelinating disease / MS 5.26.f 3-31e Chronic or recurrent process (MS, transverse myelitis, recurrent optic neuritis). See also NMOSD and MOGAD — MOGAD is often monophasic and may retain Dementia (rare in AD) 5.26.d — not compatible with retention; act upon diagnosis. 5.26.a does not apply. 3-31k None — upon diagnosis Myasthenia gravis 5.26.l 3-31c Disqualifying unless confined to extraocular muscles ALS / progressive neurogenic atrophy (5.26.i, paralytic disorders) 3-31a Referral to DES Parkinson's / basal ganglia & cerebellar degeneration 5.26.m (tremor/dystonia with functional impairment) 3-31d Progressive degenerative disorders Muscular dystrophies 5.26.e 3-31b Progressive weakness and atrophy Peripheral neuropathy 5.26.i 3-31k Permanent functional impairment Narcolepsy 5.27.a 3-31h (see 3-32c) Neurologist or sleep specialist evaluation required to determine disposition Chronic insomnia 5.27.a 3-32a Fails CBT-I, or needs sleep medication ≥3 nights/week, over 6 consecutive months Binocular diplopia 5.4.e 3-7b Not surgically correctable, severe, constant, within 20° of primary position Recurrent/unexplained syncope 5.26.n 3-31k Interferes with duty

The 5.26.a gate, stated once

Everything in DoDI 6130.03 para 5.26 except epilepsy (k) and dementia (d) — and in an active-duty population that effectively means except epilepsy — is gated by 5.26.a: the condition "must persist despite appropriate treatment and impair function so as to preclude satisfactory performance of required military duties." Both halves are required — persistence and duty impairment. A well-controlled condition does not fail retention standards no matter how serious the diagnosis sounds, and an untreated one has not yet met the standard either.

docs.title: "Is 180 days a mandatory MEB?"
docs.text:
No. This is the most common misconception in this space, and it comes from three real but unrelated numbers that all sit near six months.

What people mean by "180 days" What it actually is A mandatory MEB trigger ❌ Does not exist. The mandatory administrative point is 365 days / 12 months of temporary profile for the same condition. 180 days The threshold at which BDE (O-6) commanders must review the temporary profile monthly. A command-visibility rule, not a medical one. 6 months of temporary profile Triggers referral to a physician or specialist for that condition (DA Pam 40-502) — the point at which someone like you gets consulted. 180 days The IDES completion goal — DoD's target for finishing 80% of cases after referral. A performance metric, not a trigger.

The genuinely mandatory six-month rules in neurology are condition-specific clinical gates, not a global clock: headache prophylaxis failure at 6 months (3-31g), seizure recurrence beyond 6 months after starting treatment (3-31i(4)), and insomnia refractory over 6 consecutive months (3-32a).

The number to hold onto is 12 months. At 12 months on a temporary profile for the same condition, the Soldier has reached MRDP by definition. Exceptions are deliberately rare and require approval by the first general officer in the Soldier's chain of command, in consultation with the senior approval authority and senior medical officer — plus a written treatment plan, an explanation of why no board has been started, and an expected MRDP date.

docs.title: The clock: what actually happens, and when
docs.text: Point What happens Authority 1–7 days DD Form 689 territory — short, self-limited illness. No profile. DA Pam 40-502 >3 days of functional limitation Goes into e-Profile as a temporary profile DA Pam 40-502 Up to 90 days Maximum length a single temporary profile may be written for DA Pam 40-502 4-4c(1) Every 3 months Updated functional assessment required, and required before any extension DA Pam 40-502 120 days Battalion/squadron (O-5) commanders review monthly DA Pam 40-502 4-4c(3) 180 days / 6 months BDE (O-6) monthly review + referral to a physician or specialist for that condition DA Pam 40-502 240 days Senior general officer (above BDE / installation) review DA Pam 40-502 365 days / 12 months Administrative MRDP. Maximum temporary profile duration. Transition to permanent profile; DES or MAR2 as applicable. AR 40-501 3-1c; DA Pam 40-502 4-4c(4) Any time MRDP is reached clinically Transition to permanent profile — do not wait for 12 months DA Pam 40-502 4-4c(1)

docs.title: What happens after you sign: the IDES timeline
docs.text:
Once your referral lands, the case runs on published DoD goals. The overall target is to complete 80% of cases in no more than 180 days, measured from the date of referral to the date of return to duty, separation, or retirement (DoDM 1332.18 Vol 1, Section 6).

Phase Goal Stages MEB Phase 72 days Referral (7d) → Claim Development (7d) → VA Disability Examination (31d) → MEB (20d) → IMR/MEB Rebuttal (7d) PEB Phase 82 days IPEB (11d) → Proposed Ratings (19d, concurrent) → Proposed Disposition (5d) → Elections (6d) → FPEB (24d) → FPEB Appeal (10d) → Rating Reconsideration (2d) → Final Disposition (5d) Transition 26 days Separation or retirement processing

What the boards actually decide:

  • MEB — documents the conditions and determines whether the Soldier meets medical retention standards. If they do, the case can end there. If not, the case file goes to the PEB. The MEB decides medical, not fitness. Any MEB listing a psychiatric diagnosis must include a thorough psychiatric evaluation and the signature of at least one psychiatrist or doctoral-level psychologist.
  • PEB (IPEB, then FPEB on demand) — determines fitness for continued service and, if unfit, the disability disposition and rating.
  • IDES — the integrated pathway that runs the DoD process alongside the VA examination and proposed rating, so the Soldier leaves with a VA rating already in hand.

A referral is not a discharge — say this to the Soldier

Soldiers frequently hear "MEB" as "you're being kicked out," and that fear drives them to under-report symptoms to you. It is worth correcting explicitly.

AR 40-501 3-4 opens by saying that possession of a disqualifying condition "does not mean automatic retirement or separation from the Service." The MEB only determines whether retention standards are met; the PEB decides fitness. A Soldier found unfit may still be continued on active duty (COAD) or continued on active reserve (COAR) under AR 635-40, or granted permanent limited duty or limited assignment status. And because this runs through the IDES, a Soldier who does separate leaves with a VA rating already adjudicated rather than starting a claim from scratch months later.

Referring early and accurately serves the Soldier. Sitting on a referral does not.

Time that doesn't count against the clock

Excludable: administrative absences and accrued leave, cases pending non-disability administrative action, additional recovery or treatment time, continuation on active duty or limited duty for under 12 months, and awaiting SecDef concurrence for flag/general/medical officer cases. Not excludable: delays in gathering documentation you were responsible for (LOD determinations, DBQs, the commander's letter). That last one is squarely on the clinical side — a slow LOD or a missing commander's letter is a delay the DES is not allowed to write off.

docs.title: The five questions (the Military box)
docs.text:
Every clinical page on this site answers these in order:

  1. Deployability — is the condition, or its required treatment/monitoring, compatible with the operational environment? Consider medications needing refrigeration, lab monitoring, or controlled-substance accountability, and anything impairing heat tolerance, alertness, or judgment. The baseline expectation is that a Soldier can deploy within 72 hours of receipt of mission (AR 40-501 3-1).
  2. Profile (DA Form 3349) — temporary or permanent? What PULHES designator, and what are the specific functional limits in Section 4?
  3. EPTS / Line of Duty — existed prior to service, or incurred/aggravated in the line of duty? Note the 6-month rule: for conditions predating enlistment, accession standards apply for the first 6 months of active duty (separation via EPSBD); after 6 months, retention standards govern.
  4. Retention (DoDI 6130.03 + AR 40-501) — does severity, frequency, or treatment response cross a threshold that ends retention?
  5. MEB/MAR2 referral — which one, and is it required or discretionary right now?

docs.title: Documentation that carries a board
docs.text:
Boards are moved by objective, longitudinal, duty-relevant evidence. In practice:

  • Frequency and severity captured over time — headache diaries, seizure logs, encounter and ER records. A board cannot act on "frequent." It can act on "≥3 incapacitating days per month documented across 7 months of diary entries."
  • Adequate treatment trials that failed — name the drug, dose, and duration for each. An inadequate trial reads as an untreated condition, and 5.26.a will not be satisfied.
  • Specific duty impact — missed training, failed or deferred ACFT, non-deployability, work restrictions, safety limitations. Corroborate with the commander where possible; AR 40-501 3-1a expects duty interference "as substantiated by the Soldier's commander or supervisor."
  • Functional limitations written functionally. Section 4 of DA Form 3349-SG drives the fork between MAR2 and DES. "Migraines" is not a limitation. "Cannot wear protective headgear >2 hours; cannot perform sustained overhead work; no duty requiring uninterrupted alertness" is.
  • A clear EPTS/LOD determination and consistent, linked profiles that show the real days-on-profile count.

Worked example — what to actually write

Illustrative composite. Not a real Soldier, not a real record — dates, doses, and figures are invented to demonstrate the level of specificity a board needs.

Same Soldier, same facts. The first version gets bounced; the second carries a board.

❌ Won't carry:

Chronic migraines, refractory. Recommend MEB.

Nothing here is actionable. No frequency, no threshold, no treatment history, no duty impact, no citation. The MEB physician cannot confirm MRDP from this, and it does not establish that any standard was crossed.

✅ Carries:

Dx: Chronic migraine without aura (ICHD-3 1.3), onset 2024, LOD — no EPTS history; pre-accession records reviewed and negative.

Frequency/severity: Headache diary maintained Sep 2025–Mar 2026 documents 8–11 incapacitating days per month, sustained across 7 months. Four ER encounters (dates listed). Exceeds the AR 40-501 3-31g threshold of ≥3 incapacitating days/month.

Adequate trials, failed: propranolol 80 mg daily × 3 months (inadequate response); topiramate 100 mg daily × 4 months (inadequate response, cognitive side effects); erenumab 140 mg monthly × 6 months (inadequate response). Prophylaxis trial per 3-31g completed and exceeded — not adequately controlled at 6 months.

Duty impact: Missed 14 training days in 12 months. Non-deployable. Cannot sustain uninterrupted alertness for guard or driving duties. Commander's statement dated 12 Mar 2026 corroborates.

MRDP: Reached Mar 2026 — condition stable on maximal tolerated therapy, no further functional progress expected.

Standards: Below retention standards per AR 40-501 3-31g and DoDI 6130.03 V2 5.26.g. Both prongs of 5.26.a met: persists despite appropriate treatment and precludes satisfactory duty performance.

Functional limitations (DA 3349-SG Section 4): No duty requiring sustained alertness >4 h; no operation of military vehicles; no duty in high-heat or high-glare environments; cannot wear protective headgear >2 continuous hours.

Disposition: Permanent P-3. Referred to DES.

The difference is not length — it is that every claim is countable, dated, cited, and tied to duty.

Scope & OPSEC

Content here stays at the published-doctrine level. Nothing unit-specific, mission-specific, or otherwise sensitive belongs on this site.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. DoDI 6130.03, Volume 2 — Medical Standards for Military Service: Retention. 4 Sep 2020, Change 2 eff. 6 Feb 2026 (renders as DoWI 6130.03-V2). Neurologic conditions 5.26; sleep disorders 5.27; vision 5.4.
  2. DoDI 6130.03, Volume 1 — Medical Standards for Military Service: Appointment, Enlistment, or Induction (accession). Neurologic 6.26; ears/vestibular 6.5.
  3. AR 40-501 — Standards of Medical Fitness, 27 June 2019. Retention standards Chapter 3 (general/disposition 3-1 through 3-4; neurological 3-31; sleep 3-32; vision 3-7); aviation and special duty Chapter 4.
  4. AR 40-502 — Medical Readiness, 27 June 2019. Profiling, approval authority, and the signature that initiates boarding action.
  5. DA Pam 40-502 — Medical Readiness Procedures, 18 December 2023. MRDP mechanics, temporary profile durations and review thresholds, MEB physician review duties.
  6. DoDI 1332.18 — Disability Evaluation System, 10 November 2022.
  7. DoDM 1332.18, Volume 1 — DES: Processes, 24 February 2023. PEBLO and MEB convening authority procedures; Section 6 IDES timeliness goals and Table 1 phase/stage goals.
  8. DoDM 1332.18, Volume 2 — DES: Quality Assurance, 2024.
  9. AR 635-40 — Disability Evaluation for Retention, Retirement, or Separation. Governs MAR2 and DES processing. Not independently verified for this page — see footer.
  10. DA Pam 611-21 — MOS physical task standards, used in the MAR2 determination.

Verified July 2026 against the source documents: the MRDP definitions, the 90-day/120-day/180-day/ 240-day/12-month profile thresholds, the "profile is the referral" mechanism, the approval-authority signature as the act that initiates boarding, the MAR2-vs-DES fork, and the mandatory/discretionary referral triggers are quoted or paraphrased directly from AR 40-501 (27 Jun 2019), AR 40-502 (27 Jun 2019), and DA Pam 40-502 (18 Dec 2023). Neurologic retention standards are quoted from DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2, para 5.26 (Change 2, 6 Feb 2026) — including the explicit "upon diagnosis, 5.26.a does not apply" carve-outs for epilepsy (5.26.k) and dementia (5.26.d). The IDES 180-day overall goal and the 72/82/26-day phase goals are quoted from DoDM 1332.18 Vol 1 (24 Feb 2023), Section 6 and Table 1. The worked example is an illustrative composite, not a real record. Not independently verified: AR 635-40's internal MAR2 procedures (cited secondhand through AR 40-501, AR 40-502, and DA Pam 40-502); Service-specific pathways outside the Army; and aeromedical waiver specifics, which follow separate Aeromedical Policy Letters. Reserve and Guard pathways differ materially from the active-duty flow described here — confirm COMPO-specific procedures before counseling an RC Soldier.

docs.title: Multiple sclerosis
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: immune-mediated demyelinating disease of the CNS, defined by lesions disseminated in space and time — typically a young adult with relapsing neurologic events that recover incompletely, accumulating disability over decades.

Who this is for: the provider seeing a first demyelinating event, and the one managing an established MS patient's relapse, DMT, or board. In a military population MS matters disproportionately — it presents in the 20–40 window, which is the active-duty demographic, and it is a named referral condition under AR 40-501 3-31e.

🚩 Before you call it MS

MS is over-diagnosed. Misdiagnosis studies consistently find that a meaningful fraction of patients carrying an MS label never had it — most often because non-specific white-matter T2 hyperintensities (migraine, small-vessel disease) were read as demyelination, or because criteria were applied to a syndrome that was never a true demyelinating attack.

Do not diagnose MS on MRI alone. The 2024 McDonald criteria require a compatible clinical syndrome or a rigorously defined radiologically isolated syndrome — not "white spots."

Red flags that argue against MS and for a mimic:

  • Normal brain MRI in someone with suspected MS — reconsider hard.
  • Longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis (≥3 vertebral segments) → NMOSD or MOGAD, not MS.
  • Severe, bilateral, or steroid-dependent optic neuritis, or optic neuritis with a swollen disc → MOGAD/NMOSD.
  • Area postrema syndrome (intractable hiccups, nausea, vomiting) → NMOSD until disproven.
  • Meningeal signs, fever, encephalopathy, seizures → infection, ADEM, autoimmune encephalitis.
  • CSF pleocytosis >50 cells, neutrophils, or very high protein → not typical MS.
  • Absent oligoclonal bands with an otherwise "classic" picture — still possible, but lower your confidence and widen the differential.
  • Progressive myelopathy without relapses → B12/copper deficiency, HTLV-1, dural AV fistula, spondylotic compression, hereditary spastic paraplegia, adrenomyeloneuropathy.
  • Systemic features (rash, arthritis, sicca, oral/genital ulcers, uveitis) → sarcoid, lupus, Sjögren, Behçet.

Send AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG before you start a DMT

This is the single highest-consequence step on the page. NMOSD and MOGAD are not MS, and several MS drugs make NMOSD worse — interferon-beta, natalizumab, and fingolimod have all been associated with severe NMOSD attacks. Starting an MS DMT in an unrecognized NMOSD patient can precipitate blindness or a devastating myelitis.

Send cell-based assay serum AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG in every case of optic neuritis, myelitis, or an atypical demyelinating presentation — before the first DMT dose. Cell-based assay, not ELISA.
