Draw before or well after steroids/PLEX where possible, since treatment lowers titers and produces false negatives.

docs.title: Rapid approach
docs.text:
1. Confirm it is a true demyelinating attack — objective, CNS-localizing, >24 h, no fever/infection.
  2. MRI brain and full spine with and without gadolinium, using an MS protocol.
  3. Send AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG (cell-based) — always, before treatment decisions.
  4. LP for oligoclonal bands and kappa free light chain index when the diagnosis isn't already secure, and always when the presentation is atypical.
  5. Apply the 2024 McDonald criteria — dissemination in space and in time.
  6. Treat the relapse if disabling (steroids → PLEX for refractory).
  7. Start a DMT — early, and in most cases high-efficacy.
  8. Address symptoms and the military disposition — profile, and a DES referral under 3-31e.

docs.title: Diagnosis — the 2024 McDonald criteria
docs.text:
Which version you are using

The current framework is the 2024 revisions of the McDonald criteria, developed by the International Advisory Committee on Clinical Trials (ECTRIMS + National MS Society) and published in The Lancet Neurology in September 2025. The "2024" in the name refers to when the panel reported, not the publication date — expect to see both years cited.

They are new enough that published critique already exists in Lancet Neurology correspondence, largely concerning the risk of over-diagnosis as the criteria become more permissive. Apply them with the mimic list above in hand.

What changed that matters at the bedside:

Change What it means Optic nerve is now a fifth topography for DIS The four classic regions (periventricular, cortical/juxtacortical, infratentorial, spinal cord) are joined by the optic nerve — demonstrated by orbital MRI, OCT inter-eye asymmetry, or visual evoked potentials Kappa free light chain index Accepted as an alternative to oligoclonal bands for demonstrating dissemination in time. Automated, quantitative, and doesn't require electrophoresis Central vein sign (CVS) Supportive imaging biomarker; improves specificity by distinguishing demyelinating from vascular white-matter lesions Paramagnetic rim lesions (PRL) Supportive; marks chronic active lesions and increases diagnostic confidence Radiologically isolated syndrome can now become MS RIS may be diagnosed as MS when DIS is met plus one of: evidence of DIT, CSF positivity, or a threshold number of CVS-positive lesions (e.g. ≥6) Unified across the lifespan and course One framework for relapsing and progressive disease, paediatric through late-life presentations

Dissemination in space (DIS) — lesions in ≥2 of 5 CNS regions:

  1. Periventricular
  2. Cortical or juxtacortical
  3. Infratentorial
  4. Spinal cord
  5. Optic nerve (new in 2024)

Dissemination in time (DIT) — any one of:

  • A new lesion on follow-up MRI compared with a baseline scan
  • Simultaneous enhancing and non-enhancing lesions on a single scan
  • CSF-specific oligoclonal bands (substitutes for DIT)
  • Elevated kappa free light chain index (new in 2024; substitutes for DIT)
  • A second clinical attack

Practical: how the MRI should be ordered

Brain MRI with and without gadolinium, 3 mm slices, including 3D FLAIR, T2, T1 pre/post, and DWI. Full spine (cervical + thoracic) with and without contrast — cord lesions carry diagnostic and prognostic weight and are frequently missed when only the cervical spine is imaged. Add orbital MRI with fat suppression if optic nerve involvement is the question. Ask the radiologist explicitly for central vein sign and paramagnetic rim lesions if your centre has susceptibility-weighted sequences — they now carry diagnostic weight.

docs.title: Disease course — name it, because it drives the DMT
docs.text:
Phenotype Pattern Clinically isolated syndrome (CIS) First demyelinating attack, criteria not yet fully met Radiologically isolated syndrome (RIS) Incidental MS-typical MRI lesions, no clinical attack. Under 2024 criteria a subset now qualify as MS Relapsing–remitting (RRMS) ~85% at onset. Discrete attacks with variable recovery Secondary progressive (SPMS) Gradual accumulation after an initial relapsing course; classified as active/non-active and with/without progression Primary progressive (PPMS) ~10–15%. Progression from onset, no early relapses; older, more male, more often myelopathic

PIRA — progression independent of relapse activity — is the concept that reframed modern MS care: disability accrues even when relapses and new lesions stop. It is why "no relapses" is not the same as "well controlled," and why the field is chasing agents that touch smouldering inflammation.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Is this a true relapse? New or worsening objective neurologic deficit lasting >24 hours, in the absence of fever or infection. Rule out pseudo-relapse — a UTI, any infection, heat, or metabolic stress unmasking prior deficits. Check temperature and urinalysis before you reach for steroids. Treating a pseudo-relapse with steroids does harm and no good.
  2. Treat the relapse if it is disabling. Mild sensory relapses often need nothing.
  3. Confirm the serology is back — AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG — before committing to an MS DMT.
  4. Start a DMT. Do not "watch and wait" on active relapsing disease. Time to first effective therapy is the variable that most predicts long-term disability.
  5. Match the DMT to the patient — disease activity, pregnancy plans, JCV status, comorbidity, monitoring burden, and, for a service member, deployability of the monitoring regimen.
  6. Treat symptoms explicitly — spasticity, fatigue, bladder, neuropathic pain, mood, gait. Symptom burden drives function far more than EDSS does.
  7. Baseline the disability — EDSS or timed 25-foot walk and 9-hole peg, documented. You will need it for the board and for judging DMT failure.
  8. Start the military disposition early. MS is a named DES referral condition.

docs.title: Acute relapse management
docs.text:
Step 1 — high-dose corticosteroids

  • Methylprednisolone 1000 mg IV daily × 3–5 days. No taper required for most relapses.
  • Oral is equivalent at bioequivalent dose — oral methylprednisolone 1000 mg daily × 3–5 days performs comparably to IV in randomised comparisons and is far easier logistically. Use it when IV access or infusion capacity is the constraint.
  • Add gastroprotection (PPI), monitor glucose (especially in diabetics), and warn about insomnia, mood lability, and metallic taste.
  • Do not treat a pseudo-relapse. Fever or infection first — treat that, reassess in days.

Step 2 — plasma exchange, for steroid-refractory relapse

Escalate if there is no meaningful improvement after the steroid course, particularly for a severe attack (dense myelitis, severe optic neuritis with poor acuity, brainstem syndrome).

  • PLEX — 5–7 exchanges over 10–14 days, typically every other day.
  • Start early. Benefit is greatest when initiated within days to a few weeks of onset; waiting months forfeits most of the effect.
  • Response rates in steroid-refractory demyelinating attacks are substantial, and PLEX is especially important in NMOSD, where it should be considered first-line alongside steroids rather than as rescue.

Step 3 — IVIG, when PLEX is unavailable or contraindicated

  • IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days (total 2 g/kg).
  • Evidence in acute MS relapse is weaker than for PLEX — treat it as the fallback, not the equal. It is a reasonable choice when vascular access, haemodynamic instability, or availability rules out exchange, and it is the preferred option in pregnancy.

Do not

  • Do not give steroids for a pseudo-relapse.
  • Do not use chronic or pulsed steroids as a disease-modifying strategy — they do not alter the course.
  • Do not delay DMT initiation waiting for the relapse to fully resolve.

docs.title: Disease-modifying therapy
docs.text:
The central modern question is strategy, not just drug: start high-efficacy immediately, or escalate from a platform agent? The field has moved decisively toward early high-efficacy treatment, driven by observational data showing better long-term disability outcomes and by the recognition that damage accrued early is not recoverable.

The evidence status, stated honestly

The two randomised trials built to settle this — DELIVER-MS (36-month brain volume loss) and TREAT-MS (sustained EDSS-plus worsening) — are the definitive tests, and their primary results were not yet published as of July 2026. DELIVER-MS has completed enrolment (816 participants) and published its treatment-decision data in June 2026, so the primary analysis is imminent — notably, 67% of the observational cohort chose early high-efficacy treatment, which tells you where practice already sits.

Meanwhile the position has firmed. The July 2026 NEJM review by Hauser states that long-term prospective trials and real-world experience indicate the most effective control of relapsing disease is achieved by initiating high-efficacy therapy at the earliest possible time. Counsel accordingly — but say plainly that randomised confirmation is still pending.

docs.title: The tiers
docs.text:
High-efficacyModerate — S1P modulatorsPlatform / lower-efficacyProgressive disease

Anti-CD20 monoclonals — the current workhorses of MS care.

  • Ocrelizumab (Ocrevus) — 300 mg IV × 2 doses two weeks apart, then 600 mg IV every 6 months. Also the only agent approved for PPMS (ORATORIO).
    • Ocrevus Zunovo is ocrelizumab coformulated with hyaluronidase (ocrelizumab/hyaluronidase-zhzo) for subcutaneous injection, approved for the same adult indications as IV. OCARINA II showed comparable pharmacokinetics and efficacy.
    • Paediatric approval, 8 May 2026 — for relapsing–remitting MS specifically, ages ≥10 years and ≥25 kg (55 lb). Note this is narrower than the adult label, which covers relapsing forms including CIS and active SPMS. Based on OPERETTA II vs fingolimod (non-inferior ARR; 48% fewer new/enlarging T2 and 87% fewer enhancing lesions).
  • Ofatumumab (Kesimpta) — 20 mg subcutaneous monthly, self-administered at home. The logistically simplest high-efficacy option and often the right answer for a mobile population.
  • Ublituximab (Briumvi) — IV, 1-hour infusions after the first. Five-year ULTIMATE I/II open-label extension data (JAMA Neurology, February 2026) confirm sustained low annualised relapse rate and a favourable long-term safety profile.
  • Rituximab — off-label, but no longer merely "widely used." OVERLORD-MS (NEJM, July 2026) is the first head-to-head randomised trial: in 216 newly diagnosed relapsing patients across 12 Norwegian and Swedish centres, rituximab was non-inferior to ocrelizumab for freedom from new or enlarging T2 lesions between months 6 and 24 (92.2% vs 94.8%; risk difference −2.6 percentage points, 95% CI −9.4 to 4.3, against a 10-point non-inferiority margin). Clinical activity was minimal in both arms.
    • The caveat is infection: 82% vs 69% of participants had an infection on rituximab versus ocrelizumab, though serious adverse events were similar (8% vs 7%).
    • Earlier observational data (JAMA Neurology, 2023) found higher relapse rates with rituximab in routine practice — likely reflecting dosing and interval heterogeneity rather than a true efficacy gap.
    • Practical read: rituximab is a defensible, far cheaper anti-CD20 choice with randomised support, provided the dosing is disciplined and you accept a higher infection burden. This matters a great deal for formulary-constrained care.

Extended-interval dosing

Extending the interval between anti-CD20 doses — rather than fixed 6-monthly infusions — is increasingly used to reduce infection risk, hypogammaglobulinaemia, and cost, typically guided by B-cell repopulation or immunoglobulin levels. It is a proposed general strategy rather than a labelled regimen, so document the rationale. Relevant to the TRICARE cost conversation and to any patient accumulating infections on standard dosing.

Others:

  • Natalizumab (Tysabri) — 300 mg IV every 4 weeks. Highly effective, but PML risk — stratify with JCV antibody index. High index plus prior immunosuppression plus long duration is the danger zone. A biosimilar (Tyruko) is available.
  • Alemtuzumab (Lemtrada) — induction; durable but carries secondary autoimmunity (thyroid, ITP, anti-GBM) requiring monthly monitoring for 4 years after the last course.
  • Cladribine (Mavenclad) — oral short-course induction over two years.
  • Autologous haematopoietic stem cell transplantation (aHSCT) — off-label, but it appears to provide durable long-term benefit in relapsing MS, and in selected patients with aggressive, highly active disease failing conventional therapy it is a legitimate consideration rather than a fringe option. Requires an experienced transplant centre; treatment-related morbidity is real. A late-stage randomised trial (NCT04047628) is ongoing. Worth raising early in a young service member with aggressive disease, where the alternative is decades of accumulating disability.
  • Fingolimod, siponimod, ozanimod, ponesimod — oral.
  • First-dose bradycardia monitoring (agent-dependent), macular oedema screening, lymphopenia, and VZV serology + vaccination before starting.
  • Severe rebound disease on discontinuation is a real hazard — never stop an S1P agent abruptly without a bridging plan.
  • Siponimod is labelled for relapsing forms (CIS, RRMS, and active SPMS) — not restricted to SPMS — but EXPAND is why it is the usual pick in active SPMS. Requires CYP2C9 genotyping.
  • Interferon-beta (Avonex, Rebif, Plegridy, Betaseron) — flu-like reactions, LFTs. Best pregnancy safety record of the DMTs.
  • Glatiramer acetate (Copaxone, Glatopa; a further generic approved January 2026) — injection site reactions, rare post-injection systemic reaction. Also pregnancy-friendly.
  • Teriflunomide — teratogenic, long half-life, requires cholestyramine accelerated elimination. Avoid in anyone who may become pregnant.
  • Fumarates — all three deliver the same active metabolite, monomethyl fumarate, and share flushing, GI upset, and lymphopenia (PML risk if prolonged and severe). Check absolute lymphocyte count before starting and periodically.
    • Dimethyl fumarate (Tecfidera) — the original; generic available. GI intolerance is the usual reason for discontinuation.
    • Diroximel fumarate (Vumerity) — second-generation, bioequivalent monomethyl fumarate exposure and therefore comparable efficacy, but substantially better GI tolerability — EVOLVE-MS-2 showed a 46% reduction in days with GI symptoms of at least moderate severity versus dimethyl fumarate. The right switch for a patient failing dimethyl fumarate on tolerability rather than efficacy.
    • Monomethyl fumarate (Bafiertam) — the active metabolite given directly; approved for relapsing forms in adults.
  • Ocrelizumab — currently the only DMT approved for PPMS. The effect is modest and concentrated in younger patients with inflammatory activity.
  • Siponimod (Mayzent) — approved for relapsing forms of MS in adults, which by label includes CIS, RRMS, and active SPMS — the same broad indication most relapsing-MS DMTs carry. It is not label-restricted to active SPMS, though EXPAND is the trial that makes it the natural choice there. Requires CYP2C9 genotyping.

BTK inhibitors — the actual state of play. This class targets B cells and CNS-resident microglia, and because these agents are brain-penetrant they are the leading attempt to reach the compartmentalised inflammation behind a closed blood–brain barrier that drives progression. Recent trials have sorted the class sharply.

  • Fenebrutinib — a highly selective, non-covalent BTK inhibitor, and as of mid-2026 the most convincing agent in the class. Three positive phase 3 trials:
    • FENtrepid (PPMS) — met its primary endpoint of non-inferiority to ocrelizumab for 12-week composite confirmed disability progression, with a 12% risk reduction (HR 0.88; 95% CI 0.75–1.03). Presented ACTRIMS, February 2026. Genentech/Roche describe it as the first investigational agent in over a decade to reduce disability progression in PPMS.
    • FENhance 1 and 2 (relapsing MS) — both met their primary endpoint versus teriflunomide, with ARR reductions of 51.1% and 58.5% (n≈1,497 combined, 96 weeks), plus reduced risk of disability worsening (26% and 20%). Presented AAN, 2026. Liver enzyme elevations >3× ULN were comparable to teriflunomide.
    • Not yet FDA-approved — filings were to be submitted together with FENtrepid. If approved it would be the first high-efficacy oral covering both relapsing MS and PPMS.
  • Tolebrutinib — brain-penetrant but relatively non-selective, and its programme is mixed at best. Present it accurately:
    • HERCULES (non-relapsing SPMS) — positive: a 31% reduction in the risk of 6-month confirmed disability progression (HR 0.69; 95% CI 0.55–0.88; P=0.003). Note this is a hazard-ratio risk reduction, not a "delay" — the sponsor's press language differs from the NEJM primary analysis.
    • GEMINI 1 and 2 (relapsing MS) — failed. Not superior to teriflunomide on annualised relapse rate (0.13 vs 0.12 and 0.11 vs 0.11; n=1,873). A pooled 6-month confirmed-disability-worsening signal favoured tolebrutinib (29% risk reduction, nominal P=0.023) but could not be formally tested because the hierarchical testing plan had already failed.
    • PERSEUS (PPMS) — failed its primary endpoint (6-month composite confirmed disability progression); MRI measures improved only. Sanofi will not pursue PPMS registration.
    • Regulatory: FDA Complete Response Letter, December 2025 — not approved in the US. EMA granted conditional marketing authorisation, and the UAE approved it for non-relapsing SPMS in August 2025. Given the GEMINI and PERSEUS results, treat near-term US approval as genuinely uncertain rather than imminent.
  • Remibrutinib — in phase 3 (REMODEL-1 and REMODEL-2); no primary results yet.

The honest bottom line for non-active progressive MS: outside ocrelizumab in PPMS and siponimod in active SPMS, there is still no DMT that clearly helps, and fenebrutinib — though the most promising candidate the field has had — is not yet approved. Say this plainly rather than implying a treatment exists. Rehabilitation, exercise, symptom management, and vascular risk control remain the interventions that change these patients' lives.

docs.title: Choosing — the practical decision table
docs.text:
Situation Reasonable choice Newly diagnosed, active RRMS Anti-CD20 (ofatumumab if self-injection suits; ocrelizumab if infusion is easy) Highly active / aggressive at onset Anti-CD20, natalizumab (if JCV negative), or alemtuzumab/cladribine induction Wants pregnancy soon Glatiramer or interferon; or anti-CD20 timed so infusion precedes conception. Avoid teriflunomide entirely JCV antibody positive, high index Avoid natalizumab; anti-CD20 instead Needs oral, low monitoring S1P modulator or fumarate — accept lower efficacy Established PPMS Ocrelizumab if inflammatory activity; otherwise rehabilitation-led care Active SPMS Siponimod (EXPAND) or an anti-CD20 — "active" means relapses or new/enhancing lesions, and that activity is what predicts benefit. For non-relapsing SPMS there is no approved option in the US (tolebrutinib received a CRL) Cost or formulary constrained Rituximab — now supported by randomised non-inferiority data (OVERLORD-MS), accepting a higher infection rate Aggressive disease failing high-efficacy therapy Consider aHSCT referral to an experienced centre Breakthrough on a platform agent Escalate to high-efficacy — do not switch laterally

Before the first dose of any DMT

Screen: CBC with differential, CMP, hepatitis B (HBsAg + anti-HBc), hepatitis C, HIV, TB (quantiferon), VZV IgG, and pregnancy test. Vaccinate before starting — live vaccines are contraindicated once immunosuppressed, and anti-CD20 blunts vaccine response, so complete the schedule ≥4 weeks before the first infusion where possible.

Hepatitis B reactivation with anti-CD20 is a named, preventable catastrophe. Check it every time. Monitor immunoglobulins on long-term anti-CD20 — hypogammaglobulinaemia accumulates over years and drives infection risk.

TRICARE — MS prescribing

MS DMTs are high-cost specialty drugs and are PA-gated across the board — expect prior authorisation for essentially every agent, usually with a neurologist prescriber requirement and documentation of diagnosis, MRI activity, and prior therapy.

  • Infused agents (ocrelizumab, natalizumab, rituximab, alemtuzumab) are typically handled as a medical benefit rather than pharmacy — the authorisation pathway differs and often runs through the MTF or regional contractor rather than Express Scripts.
  • Self-injected and oral agents (ofatumumab, S1P modulators, fumarates, teriflunomide, glatiramer, interferons) run through the specialty pharmacy benefit.
  • Generics — glatiramer, dimethyl fumarate, fingolimod, and teriflunomide now have generic entrants, which usually eases the PA burden considerably.

Verify every agent at the TRICARE Formulary Search before counseling, and start the PA early — specialty authorisations are the usual cause of a months-long gap between decision and first dose. For an active-duty patient that gap is disease activity you cannot get back.

docs.title: Symptomatic management
docs.text:
Symptom control determines function more than any DMT does. Ask about all of it — patients under-report.

Symptom First-line Spasticity Baclofen 5–10 mg TID titrated (max ~80 mg/day); tizanidine 2–4 mg TID (sedation, hypotension, LFTs); stretching and PT throughout. Intrathecal baclofen or botulinum toxin for focal/severe Fatigue Screen for sleep apnoea, depression, thyroid, anaemia, and deconditioning first — treat what you find. Exercise has the best evidence. Amantadine 100 mg BID, modafinil 100–200 mg daily Neurogenic bladder Post-void residual first — it determines the pathway. Retention → intermittent catheterisation. Storage/urgency → antimuscarinic or mirabegron (fewer cognitive effects); botulinum toxin for refractory detrusor overactivity. Treat UTIs promptly — they trigger pseudo-relapses Neuropathic pain / Lhermitte Gabapentin, pregabalin, duloxetine, amitriptyline Trigeminal neuralgia Common in MS and often bilateral — see trigeminal neuralgia. Carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine first Depression Prevalent and under-treated; suicide risk is elevated in MS. SSRI/SNRI plus psychotherapy. Screen at every visit Cognitive impairment Processing speed most affected. Formal neuropsych testing when duty or employment is in question Gait / walking speed Dalfampridine 10 mg BID (extended release) — improves walking in a responder subset. Contraindicated with seizure history or renal impairment Heat intolerance (Uhthoff) Cooling strategies, activity timing, hydration. Operationally decisive — see the military box

Military medicine — multiple sclerosis

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2, DoDI 1332.18, and service aeromedical policy before counseling. See the full process on Profiles, MEB/MAR2 & deployability.

  • Retention: AR 40-501 para 3-31e names "multiple sclerosis, optic neuritis, transverse myelitis, and similar demyelinating disorders" directly among the causes for referral to the DES. The DoD floor is DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.f — "chronic or recurrent demyelinating processes" — which is gated by 5.26.a (must persist despite appropriate treatment and impair duty performance). Cite both.
  • Referral is expected, and expected early. Under AR 40-501 3-3d, a profiling provider who identifies a Chapter 3 condition should initiate a permanent profile at the time of identification, which is what prompts the DES referral. A confirmed MS diagnosis is not a wait-and-see profile.
  • Deployability: MS is deployment-limiting on several independent grounds, and it is worth naming them separately because they persist even in a patient doing well:
    • Heat intolerance (Uhthoff's phenomenon) — a fundamental incompatibility with hot climates, MOPP gear, and sustained exertion. This alone is often decisive.
    • Infusion-dependent therapy — an every-6-month infusion requirement is not supportable in most deployed settings. Self-injected ofatumumab is the DMT most compatible with field conditions — worth weighing when the patient may remain in service.
    • Monitoring burden — MRI surveillance, labs, JCV index, immunoglobulins.
    • Unpredictable relapse with visual, motor, or cognitive deficit.
  • Profile (DA Form 3349): temporary during the diagnostic work-up and acute relapse; permanent once the diagnosis is established. Write functional limits in Section 4 — heat exposure, sustained exertion, load carriage, vision, alertness — not just "MS."
  • EPTS / LOD: MS onset frequently predates diagnosis by years, so EPTS is genuinely contested in service members. Document the first attributable symptom carefully, review pre-accession records, and address service aggravation explicitly. This determination has large downstream consequences for the Soldier.
  • Aeromedical / special duty: MS is disqualifying for flying duty. Assume permanent suspension and confirm the class-specific standard.
  • What carries the board: relapse dates and objective deficits, MRI lesion burden and new activity over time, EDSS or timed 25-foot walk and 9-hole peg, documented DMT trials, and concrete duty impact. See the worked example of profile language that carries versus language that gets bounced.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
What MS actually is. An adaptive-immune attack on CNS myelin with early and ongoing axonal loss. The relapsing phase is driven by peripheral lymphocyte trafficking across the blood–brain barrier — which is why drugs that deplete B cells or block trafficking work so well early. The progressive phase is dominated by compartmentalised inflammation behind a closed barrier: meningeal lymphoid aggregates, chronic active (rim) lesions, and microglial activation. That compartmentalisation is precisely why peripherally acting DMTs lose traction in progressive disease, and why brain-penetrant strategies like BTK inhibition were pursued.

Epstein–Barr virus is now regarded as a necessary, though not sufficient, cause — the 2022 Bjornevik cohort of over 10 million US military personnel showed a 32-fold increased risk of MS after EBV seroconversion, with no comparable signal for other viruses. The military relevance is direct: that landmark study was built on Department of Defense serum repository samples.

Why "early and effective" won the argument. Relapse-associated worsening and PIRA both contribute to disability, but the inflammatory window in which treatment demonstrably alters the trajectory is early. Axonal loss is not recoverable, brain volume loss proceeds silently, and patients who reach an EDSS milestone rarely come back. The observational literature consistently favours early high-efficacy therapy; DELIVER-MS and TREAT-MS are the randomised tests, and they had not reported primary results as of July 2026.

Where the field is heading. Two frontiers are worth knowing about even though neither is standard care. BTK inhibitors are the first serious attempt to modulate CNS-compartmentalised disease — the Lancet Neurology 2026 class review describes them as potential harbingers of treatments that reach processes behind an intact blood–brain barrier, and fenebrutinib's three positive phase 3 trials are the strongest evidence yet that the strategy works. CD19-directed therapy is the other: anti-CD20 agents spare CD20-negative plasmablasts and plasma cells, and CD19-targeted monoclonals or CAR T cells reach that compartment. Case series in autoimmune disease have reported dramatic benefit, and MS trials are beginning. Neither belongs in a counselling conversation yet, but both explain where the next decade of DMTs comes from.

Key trials and evidence:

  • OPERA I/II — ocrelizumab vs interferon beta-1a in RRMS; superior relapse and MRI outcomes. Established anti-CD20 as front-line therapy.
  • ORATORIO — ocrelizumab in PPMS; the first positive trial in primary progressive disease.
  • ASCLEPIOS I/II — ofatumumab vs teriflunomide; brought subcutaneous self-administered high-efficacy therapy into practice.
  • AFFIRM / SENTINEL — natalizumab efficacy, and the trials that surfaced PML.
  • CARE-MS I/II — alemtuzumab; durable effect, secondary autoimmunity.
  • EXPAND — siponimod in SPMS; benefit concentrated in active disease.
  • ULTIMATE I/II — ublituximab; 5-year extension data, JAMA Neurology 2026.
  • OVERLORD-MS — rituximab non-inferior to ocrelizumab in newly diagnosed relapsing MS (NEJM, July 2026); more infections with rituximab. The first anti-CD20 head-to-head.
  • HERCULES — tolebrutinib in non-relapsing SPMS; 31% reduction in risk of 6-month confirmed disability progression (HR 0.69). US outcome: Complete Response Letter, December 2025.
  • GEMINI 1/2 — tolebrutinib failed vs teriflunomide in relapsing MS.
  • PERSEUS — tolebrutinib failed in PPMS; PPMS registration abandoned.
  • FENtrepid — fenebrutinib non-inferior to ocrelizumab in PPMS (HR 0.88), ACTRIMS 2026.
  • FENhance 1/2 — fenebrutinib vs teriflunomide in relapsing MS; 51.1% and 58.5% ARR reductions, AAN 2026.
  • OPERETTA II — ocrelizumab vs fingolimod in paediatric RRMS; basis of the May 2026 approval.
  • EVOLVE-MS-2 — diroximel vs dimethyl fumarate; 46% fewer days with moderate-or-worse GI symptoms.
  • Bjornevik et al., Science 2022 — EBV and MS causality, from the DoD serum repository.
  • 2024 revisions of the McDonald criteria, Lancet Neurology, September 2025.

  7. Torkildsen Ø, Brustad HK, Høgestøl EA, et al. Rituximab versus ocrelizumab in newly diagnosed relapsing multiple sclerosis (OVERLORD-MS). N Engl J Med. 2026;395(1):44-53. Article

Verified July 2026: the diagnostic framework is the 2024 revisions of the McDonald criteria, published in Lancet Neurology September 2025 — the optic nerve as a fifth DIS topography, kappa free light chain index as an alternative to oligoclonal bands for DIT, central vein sign and paramagnetic rim lesions as supportive biomarkers, and the RIS-to-MS pathway are all taken from that publication and its ECTRIMS slide deck; published critique of over-diagnosis risk exists and is flagged in the text. Tolebrutinib is NOT FDA-approved — the FDA issued a Complete Response Letter in December 2025 after two PDUFA extensions. Its wider programme is mixed and is now stated in full: HERCULES positive (31% risk reduction, HR 0.69 — a hazard-ratio reduction, not a "delay"), but GEMINI 1/2 failed against teriflunomide and PERSEUS failed in PPMS, with PPMS registration abandoned. Fenebrutinib (FENtrepid non-inferior to ocrelizumab in PPMS, HR 0.88; FENhance 1/2 positive vs teriflunomide, 51.1% and 58.5% ARR reductions) is not yet FDA-approved — these are ACTRIMS/AAN 2026 conference and sponsor-release data pending full publication. OVERLORD-MS (rituximab non-inferior to ocrelizumab; more infections) is peer-reviewed, NEJM July 2026. Ocrelizumab's paediatric approval (8 May 2026) is for relapsing–remitting MS specifically, ages ≥10 and ≥25 kg — narrower than the adult "relapsing forms" label — and the January 2026 generic glatiramer approval is current. DELIVER-MS and TREAT-MS primary results were not published as of July 2026; DELIVER-MS has completed enrolment and published decision-making data, and the early-high-efficacy position follows the July 2026 NEJM Hauser review while randomised confirmation remains pending. aHSCT, extended-interval anti-CD20 dosing, and CD19-directed/CAR-T strategies are described as off-label or investigational and are labelled as such. Retention standards are quoted from AR 40-501 3-31e and DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 5.26.f/5.26.a, verified against the source PDFs. Not independently verified: TRICARE formulary tiers and PA criteria for individual DMTs — these are described structurally (specialty/medical benefit split, neurologist requirement) rather than by tier, and must be checked at the TRICARE Formulary Search before counseling. Relapse dosing follows standard practice (methylprednisolone 1 g × 3–5 days; PLEX 5–7 exchanges); symptomatic drug doses are conventional and should be titrated to the individual.

docs.title: MOG antibody-associated disease (MOGAD)
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: a distinct antibody-mediated demyelinating disease targeting myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein — neither MS nor NMOSD. It relapses less often, recovers far better, and is notoriously steroid-dependent, so the management problem is usually the taper rather than the attack.

Who this is for: anyone working up optic neuritis, myelitis, or ADEM — especially in children and young adults, where MOGAD is substantially more common than NMOSD.

🚩 The three-way fork

Optic neuritis and myelitis have three antibody-defined destinations, and they diverge in treatment and prognosis. Send both cell-based assays every time.

MS NMOSD (AQP4+) MOGAD Target Myelin (T-cell driven) Astrocyte AQP4 Oligodendrocyte MOG Optic neuritis Unilateral, retrobulbar, good recovery Severe, posterior/chiasmal, poor recovery Bilateral, anterior, marked disc swelling — but good recovery Myelitis Short segment, peripheral LETM, central LETM, often conus involvement ADEM No Rare Yes — the classic paediatric presentation Course Relapsing, then progressive Relapsing, no progressive phase Frequently monophasic (~30–50% of adults; higher in children); no progressive phase Recovery Variable Poor Good CSF OCB Positive ~85% Usually negative Usually negative Steroid response Good Good, but needs PLEX Excellent — but relapses on withdrawal

docs.title: Diagnosis — 2023 international MOGAD panel criteria
docs.text:
The 2023 international MOGAD panel criteria (Banwell et al., Lancet Neurology) require all three of:

  1. A core clinical demyelinating event — one of six:
    • Optic neuritis
    • Myelitis
    • ADEM
    • Cerebral cortical encephalitis (often with seizures — a MOGAD-specific presentation)
    • Brainstem or cerebellar deficit
    • Cerebral monofocal or polyfocal deficit
  2. Positive MOG-IgG by cell-based assay
  3. Exclusion of alternative diagnoses

Titre matters — this is where the criteria bite

The criteria weight the result by titre, because low-positive MOG-IgG is common in people without MOGAD and is the main source of misdiagnosis.

  • Clear positive (high titre) + a core event + exclusion → MOGAD.
  • Low positive, borderline, or serum-only-positive-without-titre → requires additional supporting clinical or MRI features before the diagnosis can be made.
  • Never diagnose MOGAD on a low-positive antibody alone in an atypical syndrome.

Validation studies report high performance in patients with a high pre-test probability, but modest specificity and positive predictive value when applied broadly — which is exactly the over-testing trap. Send the assay when the syndrome fits, and interpret the titre, not just the sign.

Cell-based assay only. And note that MOG-IgG can be transiently positive after an ADEM-like illness and then disappear — repeat testing at 6–12 months helps distinguish monophasic from relapsing disease.

docs.title: Features that suggest MOGAD
docs.text:
• Bilateral simultaneous optic neuritis with prominent disc oedema and perineural enhancement on orbital MRI — but with good visual recovery, which is what separates it from NMOSD.
  • ADEM — particularly in children; MOGAD accounts for a large share of paediatric ADEM.
  • Cerebral cortical encephalitis with seizures — FLAIR-hyperintense cortical swelling, often unilateral. Frequently misdiagnosed as encephalitis or a first unprovoked seizure.
  • Myelitis with conus medullaris involvement.
  • Fluffy, poorly demarcated brain lesions that resolve substantially on follow-up imaging — quite unlike the persistent, sharply defined lesions of MS.
  • Steroid dependence — improvement on steroids, relapse as they are withdrawn. This pattern is strongly suggestive.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Send cell-based MOG-IgG and AQP4-IgG on every optic neuritis, myelitis, ADEM, or cortical encephalitis.
  2. Interpret the titre, not just positive/negative.
  3. Treat the attack — steroids, with PLEX for severe or refractory presentations.
  4. Taper slowly. This is the single most important management point in MOGAD.
  5. Decide whether it is monophasic or relapsing — repeat serology at 6–12 months; persistent positivity predicts relapse.
  6. Start maintenance only for relapsing disease — not after a single attack in a patient who seroconverts to negative.
  7. Military disposition — profile, and referral under 3-31e.

docs.title: Acute attack treatment
docs.text:
Step 1 — steroids, then taper slowly

  • Methylprednisolone 1000 mg IV daily × 3–5 days.
  • Then an oral taper over weeks to months — not days.

This is the defining management point. MOGAD is exquisitely steroid-responsive and equally prone to rebound relapse on rapid withdrawal. A three-day pulse with no taper, which is perfectly reasonable in MS, frequently produces a relapse within weeks in MOGAD.

Taper over a minimum of 3 months after a significant attack, and longer if relapse occurred during a previous taper.

Step 2 — severe or refractory attack

  • PLEX 5–7 exchanges — for severe optic neuritis or dense myelitis, or steroid failure.
  • IVIG 2 g/kg — an effective alternative, and often preferred in children and in pregnancy.

docs.title: Maintenance therapy
docs.text:
The landscape just changed — and it is not yet FDA-approved

METEOROID, the phase 3 trial of satralizumab (anti-IL-6 receptor) in MOGAD, met its primary endpoint: a 68% reduction in relapse risk versus placebo, with 87% of treated patients relapse-free at 48 weeks versus 67% on placebo, a 66% reduction in annualised relapse rate, and onset of response as early as 8 weeks. Results were presented at the 2026 AAN Annual Meeting.

This is the first successful phase 3 trial in MOGAD. Satralizumab is FDA-approved for AQP4-seropositive NMOSD but is not yet approved for MOGAD — verify current regulatory status before counseling. CosMOG, evaluating rozanolixizumab (FcRn inhibitor), is also underway.

Until an agent is approved, maintenance is off-label and evidence is observational:

Ordered by current comparative evidence — best first. On-treatment annualised relapse rates across observational cohorts run roughly: IL-6 blockade ~0.09, IVIG ~0.13–0.22, MMF ~0.3, rituximab ~0.5.

Option Dosing Notes IL-6 receptor blockade — tocilizumab 8 mg/kg IV every 4 weeks (satralizumab SC is the alternative) The largest comparative evidence base in MOGAD. Vilaseca et al. (JAMA Neurol, 2026; international multicentre, n=116, 90% tocilizumab): ARR fell from 0.64 to 0.09 (IRR 0.08; 95% CI 0.04–0.16 — a 92% reduction). Superior to low-dose IVIG (HR 4.5) but not significantly different from IVIG ≥1 g/kg q4wk (HR 2.0; 95% CI 0.8–4.5). Adverse events in 50% (mostly mild infections), 9% severe infections. Available as a biosimilar — substantially cheaper than satralizumab. Treat this as a first-line option, not a last resort IVIG ≥1 g/kg every 4 weeks — see the dosing warning below The best-established maintenance therapy, especially in children. Performs comparably to IL-6 blockade when properly dosed SCIG (subcutaneous immunoglobulin) Weekly or biweekly, self-administered Steadier immunoglobulin levels without peak-and-trough, self-administered at home. Data are limited but it is increasingly used — and it is the most deployable immunoglobulin option, which matters for a service member Mycophenolate mofetil Oral Reasonable oral maintenance; on-treatment ARR ~0.3 Methotrexate Oral/SC weekly Used in practice; limited MOGAD-specific evidence Azathioprine Oral Slow onset; requires steroid bridging for months. Phase 3 MOGwAI trial ongoing Chronic low-dose steroid — Sometimes the pragmatic bridge, with all the expected long-term costs Rituximab 1000 mg × 2, or 375 mg/m² weekly × 4 The weakest of the commonly used agents in MOGAD — do not carry NMOSD-level expectations across. Only ~55% remain relapse-free in meta-analysis; one long-term study found 61% of MOGAD patients relapsed (vs 28% of NMOSD; HR 2.8), and 79% of those relapses occurred despite CD19+ B cells suppressed below 1% — B-cell depletion simply does not prevent MOGAD attacks in many patients. Highest on-treatment ARR of the group (~0.5)

IVIG dose is the commonest reason IVIG 'fails'

Maintenance IVIG must be dosed at ≥1 g/kg every 4 weeks (commonly 1–2 g/kg q4wk).

In the 2026 comparative study, IL-6 blockade was significantly better than IVIG dosed below 1 g/kg q4wk (HR 4.5) but statistically indistinguishable from IVIG at ≥1 g/kg q4wk. The difference between an effective and an ineffective regimen is the dose, not the drug.

Before calling IVIG a failure and switching, check the dose and interval. Under-dosing is common and eminently fixable.

Who actually needs maintenance — this has shifted

The older teaching was to wait for a second attack, on the reasoning that a large share of MOGAD is monophasic and indefinite immunosuppression over-treats them. That default no longer holds unqualified.

MOGADOR2 (Neurology, 2024; French nationwide incident cohort, Class III evidence) found that relapse risk is highest early, and that initiating maintenance from the first attack substantially reduced it — a reported ~74% relative reduction (OR 0.26), with 2-year relapse risk of roughly 11% with early treatment versus 41% without. Separate work found that both corticosteroids continued ≥12 weeks and steroid-sparing maintenance independently lower relapse risk.

A defensible current approach:

  • Risk-stratify after the first attack rather than reflexively observing or reflexively treating.
  • Favour early maintenance when high-risk features are present: persistent MOG-IgG seropositivity, polyphasic first attack (HR ~2.4), elevated CSF protein (HR ~2.06), severe attack, myelitis at onset, or relapse during a steroid taper.
  • Observation remains reasonable for a single, fully recovered, monophasic-appearing attack in a patient who seroreverts to negative — but recheck serology at 6–12 months rather than assuming.
  • Early relapses predict more relapses: a relapse within 12 months carries HR ~2.11 for long-term relapsing disease, and within 90 days HR ~2.70 in adults. Treat an early relapse as a strong signal to commit to maintenance.

When can maintenance stop? — two 2026 datasets

One of the commonest questions in MOGAD, and until recently unanswerable. Note the two studies give different-looking numbers because they ask different questions — read both.

  • Boudot de la Motte et al. (JAMA Neurol, 2026; n=705 adults): the 1-year cumulative relapse risk after discontinuation was 8.7% (95% CI 1.0–15.9), with no rebound phenomenon. Lower risk was associated with treatment duration >1 year and >2 years since the last relapse.
  • Yeh et al. (Brain, 2026; Oxford NMO service, 190 patients, 236 discontinuation intervals): relapse followed 39% of discontinuations, at a median of 5.4 months. Estimated optimal durations were 10–18 months after a first attack and 20–30 months for relapsing patients, and persistent MOG-IgG positivity at the time of stopping predicted relapse (HR ~1.95 in relapsing disease).

Practical synthesis: de-escalation is genuinely feasible in MOGAD — unlike NMOSD, where stopping a complement inhibitor risks rebound. Aim for at least a year of treatment and two years relapse-free, recheck MOG-IgG before stopping (persistent positivity argues for continuing), and watch closely for the first 6 months, which is when relapses cluster.

For a service member this is the difference between indefinite infusion dependence and a realistic path back to full duty — see the military box.

Do not use MS disease-modifying therapies in MOGAD

Conventional MS DMTs do not prevent MOGAD relapses. Interferon-beta and glatiramer acetate show no relapse-prevention benefit, and there is no reason to expect the S1P modulators to help either.

The evidence for outright harm is strongest in NMOSD rather than MOGAD, so the argument here is futility rather than danger — but the practical consequence is the same: a patient left on an MS drug is an unprotected patient. If someone carrying an "MS" label turns out to be MOG-IgG positive, switch them to a MOGAD-appropriate regimen, do not continue the DMT.

How many patients relapse — the honest range

Reported relapse proportions vary widely, and the driver is follow-up duration and age, not disagreement about the disease. Roughly 30–50% of adults are monophasic, meaning 50–70% relapse with adequate follow-up. Children — particularly with an ADEM phenotype and seroreversion — are considerably more often monophasic (~74–83% in prospective incident paediatric cohorts).

Quote a range with its follow-up window, not a single number, and remember that a patient called monophasic at one year may be reclassified at five.

TRICARE — MOGAD prescribing

Because no agent carries a MOGAD indication, maintenance therapy is off-label by definition, which makes prior authorisation harder rather than routine. Expect to submit clinical justification with serology, attack history, and the failed-taper narrative.

Maintenance IVIG requires authorisation and is administered as a medical benefit; document the relapsing course and prior treatment explicitly. Oral agents (mycophenolate, azathioprine) are inexpensive generics and are the path of least resistance where clinically adequate. Verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search, and expect the off-label status to be the obstacle.

Military medicine — MOGAD

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2, and DoDI 1332.18. See Profiles, MEB/MAR2 & deployability.

  • Retention: AR 40-501 para 3-31e — "multiple sclerosis, optic neuritis, transverse myelitis, and similar demyelinating disorders." MOGAD's two commonest presentations are named in the paragraph directly. DoD floor: DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.f, gated by 5.26.a (persists despite treatment and impairs duty). Cortical encephalitis with seizures additionally engages 5.26.c, and any seizure disorder brings 3-31i and 5.26.k into play — see seizures.
  • The disposition genuinely differs from MS and NMOSD, and this is worth arguing carefully. MOGAD recovers well, and 30–50% of adults are monophasic. A service member with a single attack, full recovery, seroreversion to negative, and no maintenance requirement may legitimately meet retention standards — 5.26.a requires that the condition persist despite appropriate treatment and impair duty, and a resolved monophasic illness does neither.
  • So do not reflexively board a first MOGAD attack. Establish whether the disease is relapsing before committing to a disposition. A temporary profile through the determination period, with repeat serology at 6–12 months, is often the correct action — and MRDP has not been reached until that question is answered.
  • Refer to the DES when the course proves relapsing, residual visual or motor deficit impairs duty, seizures occur, or the patient requires ongoing immunosuppression or maintenance IVIG — an infusion-dependent regimen is not deployable.
  • But maintenance is not necessarily permanent, and that changes the calculus. Unlike NMOSD, MOGAD therapy can often be de-escalated: the 2026 data support stopping after >1 year of treatment with >2 years relapse-free, with a 1-year post-discontinuation relapse risk reported as low as 8.7% and no rebound phenomenon. A Soldier on maintenance today is not automatically a Soldier on maintenance forever.
    • Practical consequence: if the member is otherwise performing and the only barrier is infusion dependence, document the planned de-escalation timeline in the profile and consider a temporary profile with a defined reassessment point rather than moving straight to a permanent profile and DES referral. MRDP has not been reached while a credible plan to come off therapy is in progress.
    • SCIG is the most field-compatible immunoglobulin option if maintenance must continue — self-administered, no infusion centre. Worth specifying if retention is the goal.
  • Profile: temporary during the attack and the monophasic-versus-relapsing determination; permanent once relapsing disease or fixed deficit is established.
  • Aeromedical: a demyelinating event is disqualifying for flying duty; a waiver discussion for a fully recovered monophasic case belongs with the aeromedical authority, not the treating neurologist.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
Why MOGAD recovers when NMOSD does not. MOG sits on the outermost surface of the myelin sheath and the oligodendrocyte membrane, making it accessible to circulating antibody — but the target is myelin itself, and the oligodendrocyte largely survives. Demyelination is followed by genuine remyelination, which is why the fluffy MRI lesions of MOGAD often substantially resolve and why vision recovers even after dramatic bilateral disc swelling. NMOSD, by contrast, destroys astrocytes — a structural, complement-mediated necrosis that cavitates the cord and does not repair. Two antibody-mediated demyelinating diseases, opposite prognoses, largely because of what the antibody binds.

A precision worth keeping. It is tempting to say MOGAD is "not complement-mediated" — that overstates it. MOG-IgG damages myelin through several mechanisms: complement-dependent cytotoxicity, antibody-dependent cellular cytotoxicity, enhanced T-cell infiltration into the CNS, and direct disruption of the oligodendrocyte cytoskeleton. Complement is involved, but it is not the dominant, structurally destructive mechanism it is in AQP4 disease. That is the more accurate reason to expect complement inhibition to be less transformative here than in NMOSD — and it is also why IL-6 blockade, which acts further upstream on the antibody-producing and inflammatory axis, has performed well.

The nosological story. MOGAD spent years as "seronegative NMOSD" or "atypical MS," and patients were treated accordingly — often with agents that neither helped nor were needed. Its recognition as a distinct entity, formalised by the 2023 panel criteria, is one of the clearer recent wins in neuroimmunology. The 2026 METEOROID result marks its transition from a disease managed on observational habit to one with randomised evidence.

Key evidence:

  • Banwell B, et al., Lancet Neurology 2023 — international MOGAD panel proposed criteria.
  • METEOROID (2026) — satralizumab; 68% relapse-risk reduction, the first positive phase 3 in MOGAD.
  • Vilaseca et al., JAMA Neurology 2026 — IL-6 receptor blockade, n=116; ARR 0.64 → 0.09. The largest comparative maintenance dataset in MOGAD.
  • MOGADOR2 (Neurology, 2024) — early maintenance after the first attack cuts relapse risk.
  • Boudot de la Motte et al. (JAMA Neurol, 2026) and Yeh et al. (Brain, 2026) — treatment discontinuation.
  • Chen JJ et al. (JAMA Neurol, 2022) — maintenance IVIG, and the dose–response that makes ≥1 g/kg q4wk the target.
  • Validation cohorts of the 2023 criteria — strong performance at high pre-test probability, modest specificity when applied broadly.

The trial pipeline. MOGAD has gone from no trials to a genuine programme:

Trial Agent Status METEOROID Satralizumab (anti-IL-6R) Positive — first successful phase 3; not yet approved CosMOG Rozanolixizumab (FcRn antagonist) Ongoing TOMATO Tocilizumab (anti-IL-6R) Phase 2/3, ongoing MOGwAI Azathioprine Phase 3, ongoing Rituximab Anti-CD20 Phase 3, ongoing

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Banwell B, et al. Diagnosis of myelin oligodendrocyte glycoprotein antibody-associated disease: international MOGAD Panel proposed criteria. Lancet Neurology, 2023. Criteria PDF · EAN summary
  2. Satralizumab meets primary endpoint in phase 3 METEOROID study in MOGAD — NeurologyLive, AAN 2026.
  3. Genentech — Enspryng reduces MOGAD relapse risk by 68%
  4. Validation of the 2023 international diagnostic criteria for MOGAD
  5. Utility of the 2023 MOGAD criteria in clinical practice — institutional cohort (modest specificity and PPV).
  6. CosMOG — rozanolixizumab in MOGAD
  7. Vilaseca A, Bilodeau PA, Gakis G, et al. Interleukin 6 receptor blockade for relapse prevention in MOGAD. JAMA Neurol. 2026. Article
  8. Deschamps R, Guillaume J, Ciron J, et al. Early maintenance treatment initiation and relapse risk mitigation after a first event of MOGAD in adults: the MOGADOR2 study. Neurology. 2024;103:e209624. Article
  9. Boudot de la Motte M, Gavoille A, Papeix C, et al. Treatment discontinuation in patients with MOGAD. JAMA Neurol. 2026;83(5):490-498. Article
  10. Yeh WZ, Francis A, Cooper S, et al. Optimal strategies for treatment discontinuation in MOG antibody-associated disease. Brain. 2026. Article
  11. Chen JJ, Huda S, Hacohen Y, et al. Association of maintenance IVIG with prevention of relapse in adult MOGAD. JAMA Neurol. 2022. Article
  12. Barreras P, Vasileiou ES, Filippatou AG, et al. Long-term effectiveness and safety of rituximab in NMOSD and MOG antibody disease. Neurology. 2022. PubMed — relapses despite B-cell depletion.
  13. Nepal G, Kharel S, Coghlan MA, et al. Rituximab for relapse prevention in MOGAD: systematic review and meta-analysis. J Neuroimmunol. 2022. PubMed
  14. Molazadeh N, Bilodeau PA, Salky R, et al. Predictors of relapsing disease course following index event in MOGAD. J Neurol Sci. 2024. PubMed
  15. Chen B, Gomez-Figueroa E, Redenbaugh V, et al. Do early relapses predict long-term relapsing disease in MOGAD? Ann Neurol. 2023. PubMed
  16. Sechi E, Gastaldi M, Cortese R, et al. MOGAD: practical recommendations for diagnosis and management. J Neuroimmunol. 2025. PubMed
  17. Carnero Contentti E, et al. Future treatments for MOGAD: the clinical trial landscape. Expert Opin Emerg Drugs. 2025. PubMed
  18. AR 40-501 para 3-31e; DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.f (gated by 5.26.a).

Verified July 2026: diagnostic framework is the 2023 international MOGAD panel criteria (Banwell et al., Lancet Neurology) — the six core clinical events, the cell-based-assay requirement, and the titre-dependent interpretation with supporting-features requirement for low positives are taken from the published criteria; validation studies reporting modest specificity and PPV outside high-pre-test-probability populations are cited and reflected in the cautions. METEOROID figures (68% relapse-risk reduction, 87% vs 67% relapse-free at 48 weeks, 66% ARR reduction, response by 8 weeks) are from the AAN 2026 presentation and the sponsor release — these are conference and press-release data, not yet a peer-reviewed primary publication, and should be re-verified against the full paper when it appears. Satralizumab is NOT FDA-approved for MOGAD as of this review; it is approved for AQP4-seropositive NMOSD. All maintenance therapy described is off-label. IL-6 receptor blockade figures (ARR 0.64→0.09, IRR 0.08, HR 4.5 vs low-dose IVIG, HR 2.0 non-significant vs IVIG ≥1 g/kg q4wk, 9% severe infections) are verified directly from Vilaseca et al., JAMA Neurol 2026 — an international multicentre retrospective cohort (n=116, 90% tocilizumab), not randomised. Rituximab's underperformance (~55% relapse-free in meta-analysis; 61% relapsing with 79% of relapses despite CD19+ suppression <1%) reflects observational and meta-analytic literature, not randomised comparison — a phase 3 rituximab trial is ongoing. The maintenance recommendation after a first attack was revised in this review: MOGADOR2 (Neurology 2024, French incident cohort, Class III) supports early maintenance in risk-stratified patients, superseding a blanket "observe after one attack" default. The two discontinuation studies are reported together because they differ: Boudot de la Motte (JAMA Neurol 2026, n=705) found an 8.7% 1-year relapse risk with no rebound, while Yeh (Brain 2026, 236 discontinuation intervals) found relapse after 39% of discontinuations at a median 5.4 months — different populations and time horizons, and the page presents both rather than the more reassuring one. The monophasic proportion is now given as a range (~30–50% of adults, ~74–83% in paediatric incident cohorts) with the follow-up-duration caveat stated, replacing a flat "~50%". Not independently verified: TRICARE coverage pathways for off-label maintenance. Retention standards quoted from AR 40-501 3-31e and DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 5.26.f/5.26.a, verified against source PDFs; MOGAD is not named in either regulation and is captured under the demyelinating-disorder language — the retention argument for a recovered monophasic case rests on the 5.26.a gate and should be made explicitly rather than assumed.

docs.title: Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder (NMOSD)
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: an antibody-mediated astrocytopathy — not a variant of MS — in which AQP4-IgG attacks aquaporin-4 water channels on astrocytes, producing severe optic neuritis and longitudinally extensive myelitis with poor recovery and high relapse-related disability.

Who this is for: anyone evaluating optic neuritis or myelitis. The entire reason this page exists is that treating NMOSD as MS can blind or paralyse the patient.

🚩 Why this distinction is urgent

Disability in NMOSD is relapse-driven. Unlike MS, there is no meaningful progressive phase — patients accumulate disability in discrete, devastating attacks. A single untreated myelitis can end ambulation; a single severe optic neuritis can end vision. Preventing the next attack is the whole game.

Several MS drugs make NMOSD worse. Interferon-beta, natalizumab, and fingolimod have all been associated with severe NMOSD exacerbations. Starting one in a misdiagnosed patient is an iatrogenic catastrophe.

Therefore: send cell-based serum AQP4-IgG in every optic neuritis and every myelitis, and do not start an MS DMT until it is back.

docs.title: When to suspect it over MS
docs.text:
Feature Points to NMOSD Myelitis Longitudinally extensive — ≥3 contiguous vertebral segments. Central cord, often with swelling and enhancement Optic neuritis Severe, bilateral or rapidly sequential, posterior/chiasmal, poor visual recovery, steroid-dependent Area postrema syndrome Intractable hiccups, nausea, or vomiting lasting days — a highly specific NMOSD presentation and often the first attack. Frequently misread as GI disease Brain MRI Often normal early, or lesions in AQP4-rich regions — periependymal around third/fourth ventricles, hypothalamus, dorsal brainstem, area postrema CSF Pleocytosis can be marked (>50 cells, sometimes neutrophilic); oligoclonal bands usually absent (~15–30%) Demographics Female predominance is strong (~9:1), median onset older than MS, over-represented in Black and Asian populations Coexisting autoimmunity Lupus, Sjögren, thyroid disease, myasthenia gravis Recovery Poor — incomplete recovery from attacks is the norm

Seronegative NMOSD

Roughly 10–25% of clinically definite NMOSD is AQP4-IgG negative. A negative test does not exclude the diagnosis.

In seronegative patients, send MOG-IgG — a substantial share prove to be MOGAD, which is a different disease with a different prognosis and treatment. That is the key point about the 10–25% figure: much of it is not seronegative NMOSD at all, it is unrecognised MOGAD.

True double-negative NMOSD (negative for both AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG) is a diagnosis of careful exclusion requiring the stricter 2015 clinical criteria — and it looks biologically different. A 2026 systematic review found it highly relapsing (73.6% relapsed) with a female-to-male ratio of only about 1.5:1, against roughly 9:1 in AQP4-positive disease. Do not assume it behaves like seropositive NMOSD; treat it aggressively (see below), but recognise the evidence base is thin.

Test with a cell-based assay, not ELISA. ELISA generates false positives at low titre that have caused real harm. And draw before steroids/PLEX where feasible — treatment suppresses titres and produces false negatives.

docs.title: Diagnosis (2015 international consensus criteria)
docs.text:
With AQP4-IgG positive: ≥1 core clinical characteristic + positive cell-based assay + exclusion of alternatives.

Without AQP4-IgG (or unknown status): stricter — ≥2 core characteristics from ≥1 attack, with at least one being optic neuritis, longitudinally extensive transverse myelitis, or area postrema syndrome, plus dissemination in space, plus supporting MRI findings, plus exclusion of alternatives.

The six core clinical characteristics:

  1. Optic neuritis
  2. Acute myelitis
  3. Area postrema syndrome — otherwise unexplained hiccups, nausea, vomiting
  4. Acute brainstem syndrome
  5. Symptomatic narcolepsy or acute diencephalic syndrome with NMOSD-typical MRI lesions
  6. Symptomatic cerebral syndrome with NMOSD-typical brain lesions

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Recognise the pattern — LETM, severe/bilateral optic neuritis, or area postrema syndrome.
  2. Send cell-based AQP4-IgG and MOG-IgG immediately, before steroids where possible.
  3. Treat the attack hard and early — steroids and PLEX. Do not wait to see if steroids work in a severe attack.
  4. Start maintenance therapy without waiting for a second attack. In AQP4-positive NMOSD, the first attack is sufficient indication.
  5. Choose an FDA-approved agent over off-label rituximab where access permits.
  6. Screen for meningococcal disease risk before any complement inhibitor.
  7. Screen for coexisting autoimmunity.
  8. Start the military disposition — this is a DES referral condition.

docs.title: Acute attack treatment
docs.text:
Step 1 — steroids AND plasma exchange, together

  • Methylprednisolone 1000 mg IV daily × 5 days.
  • PLEX 5–7 exchanges over 10–14 days, typically every other day.

The key difference from MS: in NMOSD, PLEX is not rescue therapy — it is first-line alongside steroids for any severe attack. Do not run a full steroid course, wait, reassess, and only then consider exchange. Earlier PLEX predicts better visual and motor outcomes, and the therapeutic window is measured in days.

For a severe attack — poor acuity, dense myelitis — start both in parallel.

Step 2 — if refractory

  • IVIG 0.4 g/kg/day × 5 days where PLEX is unavailable or contraindicated.
  • Escalate maintenance therapy immediately; a breakthrough attack means the current regimen has failed.
  • Consider prolonged oral steroid taper as a bridge while maintenance therapy takes effect — unlike in MS, bridging matters here because the relapse risk is immediate and high.

docs.title: Maintenance therapy — prevent the next attack
docs.text:
Four monoclonal antibodies are FDA-approved for AQP4-IgG-seropositive NMOSD. In a disease where disability is entirely relapse-driven, this is the intervention that changes the patient's life.

Agent Target Dosing Notes Eculizumab (Soliris) C5 complement IV every 2 weeks PREVENT trial. Meningococcal vaccination mandatory Ravulizumab (Ultomiris) C5 complement IV every 8 weeks Approved 2024 (CHAMPION-NMOSD). Same target as eculizumab, engineered for a longer half-life — q8wk instead of q2wk Inebilizumab (Uplizna) CD19 B cells IV every 6 months N-MOmentum. Broader B-cell depletion than anti-CD20 (includes plasmablasts) Satralizumab (Enspryng) IL-6 receptor Subcutaneous, monthly after loading SAkuraSky/SAkuraStar. Self-administered — the most deployable-friendly option

Eculizumab biosimilars do not carry the NMOSD indication

Two interchangeable eculizumab biosimilars are FDA-approved — Bkemv (eculizumab-aeeb) and Epysqli (eculizumab-aagh) — but both were approved under a "skinny label" covering only PNH and atypical HUS. NMOSD remains a Soliris-only indication among eculizumab products, as does generalised myasthenia gravis.

This matters for formulary navigation: a payer or pharmacy substituting a biosimilar for an NMOSD patient would be dispensing off-label. Specify the reference product.

Off-label but genuinely useful options:

Agent Target Notes Tocilizumab IL-6 receptor (same as satralizumab) TANGO (Lancet Neurol 2020, phase 2, open-label, n=118): superior to azathioprine in highly relapsing NMOSD — median time to first relapse 78.9 vs 56.7 weeks, P=0.0026. Real-world data show ARR falling from ~1.9 to ~0.1. Included in the 2026 MENACTRIMS guidelines alongside the approved agents, and a substantially cheaper route to IL-6 blockade when satralizumab PA is denied Rituximab CD20 375 mg/m² weekly × 4, or 1000 mg × 2 doses two weeks apart; re-dose every 6 months or guided by CD19/CD20 B-cell reconstitution. See the caveats below Mycophenolate mofetil Antiproliferative Widely used globally; 5-year relapse-free survival ~51% Azathioprine Antiproliferative Slow onset — requires steroid bridging for months; 5-year relapse-free survival ~35%

How good are the approved agents really — read the comparative data carefully

The often-quoted figure is 5-year relapse-free survival of 100% for the C5 inhibitors, inebilizumab, and satralizumab versus 69.7% for rituximab (with MMF 51.1% and azathioprine 35.3%). That comes from Bilodeau et al., 2026 — a single-centre retrospective cohort at Mass General Brigham (176 patients, 2000–2024, median 9 years' follow-up), not head-to-head randomised data.

The nuance matters, and it cuts against a simple "approved beats rituximab" reading. When the same study combined efficacy with safety and tolerability into a composite relapse/serious-infection/treatment-limiting-event-free endpoint:

  • C5 inhibitors 91% vs rituximab 55% at 5 years — the C5 inhibitors clearly separate.
  • Inebilizumab 38% and satralizumab 79% (3-year data), and neither was statistically superior to rituximab on the composite: inebilizumab HR 1.23 (95% CI 0.24–3.12), satralizumab HR 1.01 (95% CI 0.16–2.68).

So: the C5 inhibitors have the strongest comparative case. For inebilizumab and satralizumab, the advantage over rituximab is a relapse-prevention signal that does not survive into a composite including harms — in a small single-centre cohort with wide confidence intervals. Do not overstate it to a patient or a formulary reviewer.

Rituximab's own cost is concrete: in that cohort 28% of rituximab treatment periods involved a serious infection, 34% a mild infection, and 13% hypogammaglobulinaemia. Long-term anti-CD20 exposure can produce hypogammaglobulinaemia requiring indefinite IVIG replacement — monitor immunoglobulins on every patient, indefinitely.

Indirect ranking — the network meta-analysis

A Bayesian network meta-analysis (J Neurol, 2025; 8 RCTs, 851 patients, 84% seropositive) ranked ravulizumab first for time to relapse — HR 0.00 (95% CrI 0.00–0.03), SUCRA 0.99, a 98% probability of being the superior agent — followed by eculizumab (0.76), tocilizumab (0.49), rituximab (0.47), and inebilizumab (0.45).

These are indirect comparisons across trials with different populations and endpoints, not head-to-head data, and the ravulizumab point estimate of exactly 0.00 reflects zero adjudicated relapses in CHAMPION-NMOSD rather than infinite efficacy. Treat the ranking as directional support for complement inhibition, not as a league table.

Stopping or switching a complement inhibitor — rebound risk

A provider who successfully stabilises a patient on eculizumab needs to know this before any future switch, including one forced by formulary change or deployment.

Disease reactivation clusters in the first ~3 months after eculizumab is stopped, including in patients relapse-free for years. Severe attacks have been reported around five half-lives after the last dose — eculizumab's half-life is only about 11 days, so protection is gone within weeks. One published case describes a severe attack 11 weeks after the final infusion during a switch to satralizumab.

Other series report uneventful switches, so this is a real risk rather than an inevitability — but it must be planned for:

  • Never stop a complement inhibitor without an overlapping bridge to the next agent.
  • Account for onset lag — anti-CD20 and IL-6 agents take weeks to months to protect.
  • Consider steroid cover through the transition.
  • Ravulizumab's half-life is roughly 5× longer than eculizumab's (~64 vs ~11 days), which gives a considerably wider and more forgiving window if a switch away from complement inhibition becomes necessary.

Seronegative NMOSD — no approved options

All four FDA-approved agents are indicated only for AQP4-IgG-positive disease. For seronegative patients the mainstays remain rituximab, mycophenolate mofetil, and azathioprine — this is a genuine unmet need, not a preference.

Treat aggressively anyway. A 2025 multicentre study found high-efficacy immunosuppression reduced relapse risk comparably in both groups — HR 0.27 in AQP4-positive and HR 0.21 in seronegative disease. Seronegativity is not a reason to under-treat.

Before any complement inhibitor — meningococcal risk

Eculizumab and ravulizumab block terminal complement and produce a profound, lasting susceptibility to Neisseria meningitidis. Both carry a boxed warning.

  • Vaccinate against meningococcus (ACWY and B) at least 2 weeks before the first dose.
  • If treatment cannot wait 2 weeks, vaccinate and give antibiotic prophylaxis to cover the interval.
  • Give the patient a wallet card and explicit instructions: any fever is an emergency.
  • Revaccinate on schedule for the duration of therapy.

Do not use in NMOSD

Interferon-beta, natalizumab, and fingolimod are associated with worsening NMOSD and are contraindicated. If a patient labelled "MS" deteriorates unexpectedly after starting one of these, send AQP4-IgG immediately — you may be watching a misdiagnosis declare itself.

Extend the caution to the whole S1P class — siponimod, ozanimod, ponesimod. The published case-series evidence is largely for fingolimod, but the mechanism of concern (lymphocyte sequestration rather than depletion) is shared across the class. There is no reason to take the risk when effective alternatives exist.

TRICARE — NMOSD prescribing

All four approved agents are high-cost biologics requiring prior authorisation, with a neurologist prescriber requirement and documentation of AQP4 seropositivity. The infused agents (eculizumab, ravulizumab, inebilizumab) typically run through the medical benefit; subcutaneous satralizumab through specialty pharmacy.

Start the authorisation the day you make the diagnosis. In a relapse-driven disease, an authorisation delay is not administrative friction — it is unprotected time during which an attack can cost vision or ambulation. Bridge with steroids if there is any lag. Verify current criteria at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

docs.title: Monitoring and long-term care
docs.text:
• Immunoglobulins — check at baseline and periodically on any B-cell-depleting agent. Hypogammaglobulinaemia accumulates over years and can require indefinite IVIG replacement.
  • Meningococcal vaccination status — track and revaccinate on schedule for the whole duration of complement inhibition.
  • Relapse surveillance — clinical, with MRI as indicated. Attacks are stepwise, so document visual acuity, fields, and objective motor/sensory findings at each visit; you cannot reconstruct increments retrospectively.
  • Screen for coexisting autoimmunity — lupus, Sjögren, thyroid disease, myasthenia gravis.

GFAP — an emerging relapse biomarker

Serum/plasma glial fibrillary acidic protein, released from damaged astrocytes, is under study for confirming and predicting relapse — which is mechanistically apt, since NMOSD is an astrocytopathy. In a real-world IL-6-blockade cohort, baseline plasma GFAP >220 pg/mL was associated with substantially higher subsequent relapse risk (reported OR ~20).

Not yet standard of care and not a substitute for clinical judgement, but worth knowing about as it moves toward the clinic.

Pregnancy — plan it deliberately

With a female-to-male ratio near 9:1, most NMOSD patients are women of childbearing age, and this conversation should happen early rather than at the positive test.

  • The 2026 MENACTRIMS guidelines recommend planning conception after ≥12 months of disease stability, and resuming treatment early postpartum — the postpartum period carries elevated relapse risk.
  • Safety data across agents are limited; several appear relatively safe in pregnancy but none is well characterised. Coordinate with maternal-fetal medicine.
  • Do not simply stop therapy on a positive pregnancy test — an unplanned withdrawal, especially of a complement inhibitor, carries the rebound risk described above. Plan the transition.

Inebilizumab now carries indications beyond NMOSD

Inebilizumab (Uplizna) has since been approved for IgG4-related disease and for generalised myasthenia gravis (AChR- or MuSK-positive adults). Not directly relevant to NMOSD management, but it can change formulary positioning and the PA pathway — worth knowing when arguing an authorisation.

Military medicine — NMOSD

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2, and DoDI 1332.18. See Profiles, MEB/MAR2 & deployability.

  • Retention: AR 40-501 para 3-31e covers "multiple sclerosis, optic neuritis, transverse myelitis, and similar demyelinating disorders" — NMOSD falls squarely within it, and its two defining syndromes are named in the paragraph explicitly. The DoD floor is DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.f, gated by 5.26.a. Residual visual or motor deficit also engages 5.26.c (CNS sequelae) and, for diplopia, AR 40-501 3-7b.
  • Deployability: assume non-deployable. NMOSD is materially less compatible with the operational environment than MS:
    • Relapse-driven catastrophic disability — an attack in theatre can cost vision or ambulation before evacuation completes.
    • Infusion-dependent maintenance on a fixed schedule (q2wk to q6mo).
    • Complement inhibitors create a standing meningococcal risk that is difficult to manage forward and dangerous in a crowded field environment.
    • If retention is being contemplated, satralizumab is the only self-administered subcutaneous option and the only regimen with any field plausibility.
  • Profile: permanent, with functional limits reflecting visual acuity, gait, bladder, and heat tolerance.
  • MEB/DES: refer on diagnosis. Document attack dates and residual deficits objectively — visual acuity and fields, EDSS or timed walk — since NMOSD disability is stepwise and the board needs the increments, not a narrative.
  • Aeromedical: disqualifying for flying duty.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
The mechanism explains the phenotype. AQP4 is the dominant water channel on astrocyte end-feet, and it is most densely expressed exactly where NMOSD strikes: the optic nerves, the central grey matter of the spinal cord, and the circumventricular organs — including the area postrema, which lacks a blood–brain barrier and is therefore directly exposed to circulating antibody. That anatomical fact is why intractable hiccups and vomiting are a neurologic presenting syndrome, and why the attack is an astrocytopathy with secondary demyelination rather than a primary attack on myelin. Complement activation following AQP4-IgG binding drives the necrotic, cavitating lesions that account for the poor recovery — and is precisely why C5 inhibition works so well.

The therapeutic era changed the disease. Before 2019, NMOSD was managed with off-label immunosuppression and carried a grim trajectory. Four approved biologics later, relapse-free survival at five years approaches 100% in trial and comparative cohorts. Few neurologic diseases have been transformed so completely in so short a span — which is exactly why the misdiagnosis question matters so much. The cost of calling NMOSD "MS" is no longer a mislabelled chart; it is a forfeited, highly effective treatment.

Key trials:

  • PREVENT — eculizumab; dramatic relapse reduction in AQP4-positive NMOSD.
  • CHAMPION-NMOSD — ravulizumab; extended q8-week dosing, FDA approval 2024.
  • N-MOmentum — inebilizumab (anti-CD19).
  • SAkuraSky / SAkuraStar — satralizumab (anti-IL-6R), with and without background therapy.
  • Wingerchuk et al., 2015 — international consensus diagnostic criteria.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Wingerchuk DM, et al. International consensus diagnostic criteria for neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorders. Neurology, 2015.
  2. Wingerchuk DM, Lucchinetti CF. Neuromyelitis optica spectrum disorder. N Engl J Med. 2022. Article
  3. Bilodeau PA, Wruble Clark M, Ganguly A, et al. Real-world efficacy and safety of NMOSD disease-modifying treatments. Neurol Neuroimmunol Neuroinflamm. 2026;13(2):e200536. Article · PMC — single-centre, n=176; source of the relapse-free-survival and composite-endpoint figures.
  4. Yamout B, Gouider R, Al-Roughani R, et al. Consensus recommendations for the diagnosis and treatment of NMOSD: the MENACTRIMS guidelines. CNS Drugs. 2026. PubMed
  5. Kümpfel T, Giglhuber K, Aktas O, et al. NEMOS revised recommendations, Part II: attack therapy and long-term management. J Neurol. 2024. PubMed
  6. Zhang C, Zhang M, Qiu W, et al. Tocilizumab vs azathioprine in highly relapsing NMOSD (TANGO). Lancet Neurol. 2020;19:391-401. Article (a correction was published in 2025)
  7. John N, Lim A, Sunthar SR, et al. Immunotherapies in neuromyelitis optica: Bayesian network meta-analysis. J Neurol. 2025. Article · PMC
  8. Pittock SJ, Barnett M, Bennett JL, et al. Ravulizumab in AQP4-positive NMOSD (CHAMPION-NMOSD). Ann Neurol. 2023. PubMed
  9. Cree BAC, Kim HJ, Weinshenker BG, et al. Inebilizumab end-of-study results, N-MOmentum open-label period. Lancet Neurol. 2024. PubMed
  10. Maillart E, Dubessy AL, Shor N, et al. Severe relapse after switching from eculizumab to satralizumab in NMOSD. Neurology. 2025. Article
  11. Malvaso A, Bovis F, Greco G, et al. Double-negative NMOSD: a systematic review and meta-analysis. Neurol Neuroimmunol Neuroinflamm. 2026. PubMed
  12. Siriratnam P, Sanfilippo P, van der Walt A, et al. Predictors of relapse risk and treatment response in AQP4-IgG positive and seronegative NMOSD. J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry. 2025. PubMed
  13. Yang S, Zhang C, Zhang TX, et al. Real-world IL-6 receptor blockade in NMOSD. J Neurol. 2023. PubMed — source of the GFAP threshold.
  14. FDA — Bkemv and Epysqli eculizumab biosimilars (skinny label: PNH and aHUS only).
  15. AR 40-501 para 3-31e; DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.f (gated by 5.26.a).

Verified July 2026: four FDA-approved agents for AQP4-IgG-seropositive NMOSD — eculizumab, inebilizumab, satralizumab, and ravulizumab (approved 2024, CHAMPION-NMOSD) — with the ravulizumab q8-week versus eculizumab q2-week dosing distinction confirmed. Eculizumab biosimilars Bkemv and Epysqli are approved under a skinny label (PNH and aHUS only) and do NOT carry the NMOSD indication — verified against the FDA announcement. The comparative figures are now attributed to their primary source — Bilodeau et al., Neurol Neuroimmunol Neuroinflamm 2026;13(2):e200536, a single-centre retrospective cohort of 176 patients, verified directly from the paper — and the composite endpoint is reported alongside the relapse-free-survival headline, because it materially changes the conclusion: only the C5 inhibitors clearly separate from rituximab (91% vs 55%), while inebilizumab (HR 1.23, 95% CI 0.24–3.12) and satralizumab (HR 1.01, 95% CI 0.16–2.68) were not statistically superior to rituximab on the composite.
