One discrepancy is flagged for the reader: a frequently cited figure of >70% hypogammaglobulinaemia on long-term rituximab could not be confirmed in this source, which reports 13% of rituximab treatment periods complicated by hypogammaglobulinaemia (and 28% by serious infection); the page uses the figures verified in the paper. The Bayesian network meta-analysis ranking (J Neurol 2025; 8 RCTs, 851 patients; ravulizumab SUCRA 0.99) is indirect comparison and is labelled as such. TANGO is a phase 2, open-label trial (n=118, six Chinese centres) with a 2025 published correction — tocilizumab is presented as off-label. The 2015 international consensus criteria remain current and are explicitly endorsed by the 2026 MENACTRIMS guidelines; no successor criteria had been published as of this review. Complement-inhibitor discontinuation rebound is described from published case reports and series — the risk is real but not universal (other series report uneventful switches), and the page frames it as requiring a planned bridge rather than as an absolute contraindication to switching. GFAP is explicitly labelled not-yet-standard-of-care. The contraindication of interferon-beta, natalizumab, and fingolimod in NMOSD reflects established case-series evidence. Meningococcal vaccination requirements before complement inhibition follow the agents' boxed warnings. Not independently verified: TRICARE tiers and PA criteria for the individual biologics — verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search. Retention standards quoted from AR 40-501 3-31e and DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 5.26.f/5.26.a, verified against source PDFs; note that NMOSD is not named as a distinct entity in either regulation and is captured under the demyelinating-disorder language — cite it that way rather than implying a dedicated paragraph exists.**

docs.title: Myasthenia gravis
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: an antibody-mediated disorder of the postsynaptic neuromuscular junction — fatigable, fluctuating weakness that worsens with use and through the day and recovers with rest. Ocular, bulbar, limb, and — the one that kills — respiratory muscles.

Who this is for: the provider seeing fluctuating diplopia/ptosis or dysphagia in clinic, and the one facing a patient in myasthenic crisis in the ED. MG is a real active-duty diagnosis and a named referral condition under AR 40-501 3-31c — but the ocular-only carve-out means the disposition is not automatic.

🚩 Myasthenic crisis — respiratory failure is the emergency

The threat is the diaphragm, and pulse oximetry will reassure you right up until the patient crashes. Hypoxia is a late sign; hypercapnic fatigue comes first.

Assess ventilation directly, not with the pulse ox:

  • Single-breath count — ask the patient to count out loud on one breath. Normal reaches ~30; cannot reach 20 = significant respiratory muscle weakness.
  • Forced vital capacity (FVC) — <15–20 mL/kg signals impending failure and probable intubation. Trend it.
  • Negative inspiratory force and rising PaCO₂.
  • Bulbar failure — can't handle secretions, weak cough, nasal voice — is an independent reason to secure the airway.

BiPAP can buy time early but fails fast in true respiratory-muscle fatigue — do not let it delay intubation in a tiring patient. If you are asking whether to intubate, intubate.

Two intubation traps specific to MG

  • Succinylcholine behaves unpredictably (altered receptor pharmacology) and non-depolarizing blockers have prolonged, exaggerated effect — MG patients are exquisitely sensitive. Warn anesthesia; expect a reduced-dose, carefully titrated approach.
  • Do not give IV magnesium to a crashing MG patient (e.g. for a coincident indication) — it blocks neuromuscular transmission and can precipitate or deepen crisis.

docs.title: Rapid approach
docs.text:
1. Recognize the pattern — fatigable, fluctuating, fatigues with use, worse later in the day, spares sensation and reflexes.
  2. Protect the airway first if bulbar or respiratory weakness is present — everything else waits.
  3. Send serology — AChR, then MuSK if negative (see below).
  4. Confirm with the pattern + serology; use EDx (RNS, SFEMG) when serology is negative.
  5. Image the mediastinum (CT/MRI chest) — every MG patient needs a thymoma screen.
  6. Treat — symptomatic (pyridostigmine) + immunotherapy; crisis gets IVIG or PLEX.
  7. Screen for precipitants — infection, and the long list of drugs that worsen MG.
  8. Start the military disposition — profile, and the ocular-vs-generalized determination.

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text: The clinical signature is fatigability. Sustained upgaze reproduces ptosis; repeated abduction fatigues an eye; proximal limbs weaken on repetition. Ptosis and diplopia are the commonest presenting features. Sensation, reflexes, and pupils are normal — a fixed pupil or sensory loss argues against MG and for a mimic.

docs.title: Serology — send in this order
docs.text:
Antibody Frequency Notes AChR (acetylcholine receptor) ~85% of generalized MG First test. Highly specific. Titre does not track severity MuSK (muscle-specific kinase) ~5–8% Send if AChR-negative. Distinct phenotype — prominent bulbar/facial/neck/respiratory weakness, and different treatment (see below) LRP4 ~1–3% Send in double-seronegative patients Seronegative remainder Especially ocular-only MG. A negative panel does not exclude MG — lean on EDx

When serology is negative — electrodiagnostics

  • Repetitive nerve stimulation (RNS) — a decremental response (>10% CMAP decrement at low-frequency stim) supports MG. Widely available; lower sensitivity in purely ocular disease.
  • Single-fiber EMG (SFEMG) — increased jitter, the most sensitive test for MG (>95%), but non-specific and operator-dependent. The test to get when RNS and serology are negative but suspicion is high.
  • The ice-pack test (ptosis improves ≥2 mm after 2 min of ice) is a useful, cheap bedside adjunct for ptosis — not a substitute for confirmation.

Every MG patient needs a chest CT/MRI

10–15% of MG patients have a thymoma, and its presence changes management independently of the MG — a thymoma is a surgical indication regardless of MG severity or serostatus. Conversely, thymoma essentially never accompanies MuSK disease. Image the mediastinum in everyone at diagnosis.

docs.title: Ocular vs generalized — the distinction that drives everything
docs.text:
• Ocular MG — weakness confined to lids and extraocular muscles. Often seronegative.
  • ~50% of ocular MG generalizes, usually within the first 1–2 years. After 2 years of purely ocular disease, the risk of generalizing falls substantially.
  • This split is the hinge of both treatment intensity and the military disposition — the regulations disqualify generalized MG but retain disease confined to the extraocular muscles.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Is the airway safe? Bulbar or respiratory weakness → assess ventilation directly, admit, treat as crisis. Nothing below matters until this is answered.
  2. Confirm the diagnosis — pattern + serology, EDx if seronegative.
  3. Screen the mediastinum — chest CT/MRI for thymoma.
  4. Start symptomatic control — pyridostigmine (less useful in MuSK disease).
  5. Start immunotherapy — most generalized patients need it; steroids + a steroid-sparing agent.
  6. Decide on thymectomy — yes for thymoma (any), and for non-thymomatous AChR-positive generalized MG (MGTX). Not for MuSK disease.
  7. Escalate to targeted biologics when refractory or steroid-dependent — FcRn, complement, or anti-CD19.
  8. Hand the patient a precipitant-drug list and address the military disposition.

docs.title: Myasthenic crisis — acute management
docs.text:
Step 1 — airway and monitoring

  • Admit to an ICU/step-down anyone with a crisis or impending crisis.
  • Serial FVC and single-breath count — every few hours; trend beats snapshot.
  • Intubate for FVC <15 mL/kg, rising CO₂, bulbar failure with secretions, or clinical fatigue. Do not wait for hypoxia.
  • Hold pyridostigmine in intubated crisis patients — it increases secretions and adds little on the ventilator; resume as they recover.
  • Find and treat the precipitant — infection is the commonest; also review every new drug (see the avoid list).

Step 2 — rapid immunotherapy: IVIG or PLEX

Both work; there is no clear efficacy winner, so choose on access and the patient.

  • IVIG 2 g/kg over 2–5 days. Easier logistically, no vascular access issue. Response in 3–5 days.
  • Plasma exchange — 5 exchanges over 7–10 days (1–1.5 plasma volumes each). Often felt to work slightly faster; preferred in severe/fulminant crisis and in MuSK disease, where PLEX is particularly effective.
  • Either controls ~60–70% of severe cases.
  • Start or intensify chronic immunotherapy in parallel — IVIG/PLEX is a bridge, not a maintenance plan. Steroids are usually begun or increased now (watch the early-worsening window, below).

Steroid early-worsening in crisis

High-dose corticosteroids can cause transient worsening in the first 1–2 weeks — a real hazard in a patient already near respiratory failure. In severe disease, cover with IVIG or PLEX first (or concurrently) and let the rapid therapy take hold before, or alongside, pushing steroids up.

docs.title: Symptomatic
docs.text:
Pyridostigmine — symptom control, not disease modification

  • Pyridostigmine 30–60 mg PO every 4–6 h, titrated to symptoms (typical ceiling ~360–480 mg/day; cholinergic side effects limit higher).
  • Muscarinic side effects — cramping, diarrhea, secretions — often need glycopyrrolate or hyoscyamine cover.
  • Less effective, and often poorly tolerated, in MuSK disease — do not lean on it there.
  • It treats symptoms only. Most generalized patients also need immunotherapy.

docs.title: Immunotherapy tiers
docs.text:
First-line immunotherapyRefractory / steroid-dependent — targeted biologics
  • Corticosteroids (prednisone) — the workhorse. Start low and titrate up, or cover a faster start with IVIG/PLEX to avoid early worsening. Effective, but the long-term toxicity is why steroid-sparing agents go on early.
  • Steroid-sparing oral agents — started early to get patients off steroids:
    • Azathioprine — best-established; slow onset (months). Check TPMT before starting.
    • Mycophenolate mofetil — widely used, better tolerated; also slow.
    • Others: tacrolimus, cyclosporine, methotrexate in selected patients.

The class that reorganized MG care. Reserve for inadequate control on, or intolerance of, conventional immunotherapy — and note the serostatus restrictions, which are the practical decision driver.

FcRn inhibitors (lower total IgG, including pathogenic antibody — cyclical dosing):

  • Efgartigimod (Vyvgart IV; Vyvgart Hytrulo SC with hyaluronidase) — AChR+; given in cycles.
  • Rozanolixizumab (Rystiggo, SC) — AChR+ or MuSK+.
  • Nipocalimab (Imaavy) — approved 30 Apr 2025, AChR+ or MuSK+, adults and adolescents ≥12, IV or SC.
  • Batoclimab — phase 3, not yet approved.

Complement C5 inhibitors (block terminal complement — AChR+ only, since complement damage at the endplate is the AChR mechanism; ineffective in MuSK disease):

  • Eculizumab (Soliris) — IV every 2 weeks.
  • Ravulizumab (Ultomiris) — IV every 8 weeks (longer half-life).
  • Zilucoplan (Zilbrysq) — subcutaneous, self-administered daily.
  • All require meningococcal vaccination ≥2 weeks before starting (boxed warning) — same rules as in NMOSD.

B-cell / anti-CD19:

  • Inebilizumab (Uplizna) — anti-CD19, IV every 6 months; FDA-approved for gMG 11 Dec 2025 (AChR+ and MuSK+, MINT trial). Its third indication after NMOSD and IgG4-RD.
  • Rituximab (anti-CD20, off-label) — particularly effective in MuSK disease, where it is often used early rather than as last resort.

docs.title: Serostatus is the practical routing tool
docs.text: Situation Favoured approach AChR+ generalized Pyridostigmine + steroids/steroid-sparing; thymectomy if non-thymomatous; escalate to FcRn, complement, or inebilizumab MuSK+ Pyridostigmine often poorly tolerated; rituximab early; FcRn (rozanolizumab/nipocalimab) or inebilizumab; complement inhibitors do not work; thymectomy does not help Any thymoma Thymectomy regardless of severity or serostatus Ocular-only Pyridostigmine ± low-dose steroids; treat-to-prevent-generalization is debated Crisis / refractory IVIG or PLEX now; escalate chronic therapy

docs.title: Thymectomy
docs.text:
• Thymoma present → thymectomy, always, as oncologic surgery.
  • No thymoma → thymectomy for AChR-positive generalized MG, based on MGTX (the randomized trial): thymectomy plus prednisone was superior to prednisone alone — better QMG scores, lower steroid requirement, fewer hospitalizations for exacerbation — and the benefit was sustained through 5 years. Best evidence in patients age ~18–65 within a few years of onset.
  • MuSK, LRP4, and purely ocular disease — thymectomy is not supported.

Do not use in MG — the precipitant list

Give every patient this list. Many are drugs a covering provider reaches for without knowing the patient has MG.

Never / black-box:

  • Telithromycin — severe, sometimes fatal MG exacerbation. Contraindicated.
  • Fluoroquinolones (cipro/levo/moxifloxacin) — FDA boxed warning in MG.
  • D-penicillamine — causes an MG-like syndrome. Avoid.
  • Botulinum toxin — avoid.

Use only if unavoidable, and watch closely:

  • Aminoglycosides and macrolides (azithro/clarithro/erythromycin).
  • IV magnesium — blocks transmission; dangerous in active disease.
  • Beta-blockers, procainamide, quinine/quinidine.
  • Statins — can worsen or unmask MG.
  • Hydroxychloroquine/chloroquine.
  • Immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, ipilimumab) — can trigger or severely worsen MG, often within 6 weeks; a recognized immune-related adverse event.

TRICARE — MG prescribing

Pyridostigmine, prednisone, azathioprine, and mycophenolate are generic/formulary. The targeted biologics (all FcRn inhibitors, all complement inhibitors, inebilizumab) are high-cost specialty drugs, PA-gated, with a neurologist requirement and documentation of serostatus and failed conventional therapy.

  • Serostatus gates coverage as well as efficacy: complement inhibitors are AChR+ only, so a PA for a MuSK patient will (correctly) be denied — route MuSK disease to rituximab, an FcRn agent, or inebilizumab.
  • Infused agents (eculizumab, ravulizumab, inebilizumab, IV efgartigimod) typically run through the medical benefit; self-administered SC agents (zilucoplan, SC efgartigimod, rozanolixizumab) through specialty pharmacy.
  • IVIG for crisis/bridging is a medical-benefit authorization — start it early.
  • Verify every agent at the TRICARE Formulary Search before counseling.

Military medicine — myasthenia gravis

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2, DoDI 1332.18, and service aeromedical policy. See Profiles, MEB/MAR2 & deployability.

  • Retention — and the carve-out that decides the case. AR 40-501 para 3-31c lists myasthenia gravis "unless clinically restricted to the extraocular muscles." The DoD floor is identical — DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.l, "myasthenia gravis, unless only involving extraocular muscles." Both regulations say the same thing: generalized MG fails retention; purely ocular MG can retain. Cite both, and make the ocular-vs-generalized determination the center of your documentation.
  • Deployability: generalized MG is non-deployable, on multiple independent grounds:
    • Crisis risk — a respiratory emergency that needs IVIG/PLEX and an ICU is incompatible with an austere or afloat environment.
    • Infusion/monitoring-dependent therapy and immunosuppression.
    • Heat, exertion, and sleep deprivation all worsen fatigable weakness — the operational environment is a direct provocation.
    • A long list of common field/formulary drugs are contraindicated (fluoroquinolones and aminoglycosides especially) — a real constraint on deployed medical support.
  • Profile: permanent for generalized disease, with functional limits reflecting bulbar, respiratory, and fatigable-strength limitations and heat intolerance. Ocular-only disease may warrant a lesser profile — but document the generalization surveillance explicitly, since ~50% convert.
  • EPTS / LOD: onset often predates diagnosis; document the first attributable symptom (fluctuating diplopia/ptosis) and review pre-accession records.
  • MEB/DES: refer generalized MG. Document serostatus, crisis history, respiratory function (FVC), medication trials, and duty impact. A first crisis is a sentinel event — it establishes the disease as generalized and, usually, as boardable.
  • Aeromedical: MG is disqualifying for flying duty — fatigable diplopia alone is incompatible with the standard.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
The lesion is the endplate. In AChR-positive MG, antibodies against the acetylcholine receptor drive complement-mediated destruction of the postsynaptic membrane — the folds flatten, receptor density falls, and the safety factor of neuromuscular transmission collapses, so repeated firing fails progressively. That complement mechanism is exactly why C5 inhibitors work in AChR disease. MuSK antibodies are IgG4 and act differently — they disrupt the MuSK–LRP4 clustering pathway that maintains the receptor array without prominent complement activation, which is precisely why complement inhibitors fail in MuSK disease while B-cell depletion (rituximab) works so well. The serostatus is not a label; it is the mechanism, and it predicts which drug will work.

A decade that transformed the refractory patient. For most of MG's history, the patient who failed steroids and azathioprine had few options. Since 2017, three mechanistic classes have arrived: complement inhibition (eculizumab, then ravulizumab and zilucoplan), FcRn blockade (efgartigimod, rozanolixizumab, and nipocalimab, which recycles less pathogenic IgG), and now anti-CD19 depletion (inebilizumab, approved for gMG in December 2025). Refractory MG went from a management dead-end to a menu — the harder question now is sequencing and cost, not whether an option exists.

Thymectomy earned its place by trial. MGTX was the randomized evidence that settled a decades-old practice: in non-thymomatous AChR-positive generalized MG, thymectomy plus prednisone beat prednisone alone, and the benefit — better disease control, less steroid, fewer exacerbations — held through 5 years.

Key evidence:

  • MGTX (NEJM 2016; Lancet Neurology 2019 extension) — thymectomy + prednisone superior to prednisone alone in non-thymomatous AChR+ generalized MG; benefit sustained to 5 years.
  • REGAIN — eculizumab in refractory AChR+ gMG.
  • CHAMPION-MG — ravulizumab; RAISE — zilucoplan.
  • ADAPT — efgartigimod; MycarinG — rozanolixizumab; Vivacity-MG3 — nipocalimab.
  • MINT — inebilizumab in AChR+ and MuSK+ gMG (basis of the Dec 2025 approval).
  • International Consensus Guidance for Management of MG, 2020 update (Narayanaswami et al., Neurology 2021).

docs.title: Related neuromuscular-junction disorders
docs.text:
MG is the postsynaptic, autoimmune prototype — but fatigable or acute weakness at the NMJ has other causes that are treated completely differently:

  • Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS) — presynaptic antibody disease, often paraneoplastic. The MG mimic that is really a lung-cancer search.
  • Botulism — presynaptic toxin; descending paralysis, an antitoxin emergency.
  • Nerve agent / organophosphate toxicity — poisoning of the synaptic enzyme; a CBRN and pesticide emergency treated with atropine and pralidoxime.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Narayanaswami P, Sanders DB, Wolfe G, et al. International consensus guidance for management of myasthenia gravis: 2020 update. Neurology. 2021;96(3):114-122. Article
  2. Wolfe GI, Kaminski HJ, Aban IB, et al. Randomized trial of thymectomy in myasthenia gravis (MGTX). N Engl J Med. 2016;375:511-522. PubMed
  3. Wolfe GI, Kaminski HJ, Aban IB, et al. Long-term effect of thymectomy plus prednisone vs prednisone alone (MGTX 2-year extension). Lancet Neurol. 2019;18:259-268. Article
  4. FDA approves inebilizumab (Uplizna) for gMG, 11 Dec 2025 (MINT trial).
  5. FDA approves nipocalimab (Imaavy) for gMG, 30 Apr 2025 (Vivacity-MG3).
  6. Complement inhibitors in gMG — comparison of schedules, efficacy, safety (eculizumab/ravulizumab/zilucoplan).
  7. MGFA — cautionary drugs in myasthenia gravis
  8. AR 40-501 para 3-31c; DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.l (both: MG disqualifying unless confined to extraocular muscles).

Verified July 2026: the biologic landscape is confirmed against FDA/sponsor sources — FcRn inhibitors efgartigimod (2021), rozanolixizumab (2023), and nipocalimab (30 Apr 2025, AChR+/MuSK+, ages ≥12), with batoclimab still investigational; complement C5 inhibitors eculizumab (2017, q2wk), ravulizumab (2022, q8wk), and zilucoplan (2023, SC daily), all AChR+ only; and inebilizumab (Uplizna) approved for gMG 11 Dec 2025 (AChR+ and MuSK+, MINT). The MuSK-vs-AChR treatment distinctions (complement ineffective in MuSK; rituximab and PLEX particularly effective; thymectomy not indicated) reflect established literature. MGTX figures (thymectomy + prednisone superior, benefit to 5 years) are from the NEJM 2016 trial and Lancet Neurology 2019 extension. Crisis thresholds (single-breath count <20, FVC <15–20 mL/kg) and IVIG/PLEX dosing follow standard practice and the cited crisis literature. The cautionary-drug list is taken from the MGFA — telithromycin and fluoroquinolones carry boxed warnings; D-penicillamine causes MG. Retention standards are quoted from AR 40-501 3-31c and DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 5.26.l, verified against source PDFs. Not independently verified: TRICARE formulary tiers for individual biologics (verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search) and service-specific aeromedical waiver specifics.

docs.title: Botulism
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: a presynaptic toxin that cleaves the machinery of acetylcholine release, producing symmetric, descending, flaccid paralysis with prominent bulbar and autonomic features — and a normal sensorium throughout. The treatment is antitoxin, given on suspicion, plus ventilatory support.

Who this is for: the ED or ICU provider facing acute descending weakness. Botulism is rare, time-critical, and a reportable public-health and potential-bioterrorism event — recognizing it fast is the entire game.

🚩 Recognize it and treat before you confirm

The classic picture: an afebrile, alert patient with acute, symmetric, descending paralysis that starts with the bulbar "4 D's" — diplopia, dysarthria, dysphagia, dysphonia — plus dilated, poorly reactive pupils, dry mouth, and progressive limb weakness, with normal sensation and normal mentation.

  • No fever (unless from a wound or aspiration), no sensory loss, preserved consciousness — these separate it from stroke, GBS, and encephalitis.
  • Descending (cranial nerves → arms → trunk → legs) and symmetric — the opposite of Guillain-Barré's ascending pattern.
  • Antitoxin does not reverse established paralysis — it halts progression. Every hour of delay is more toxin bound. Do not wait for laboratory confirmation, which takes days.
  • Call your state health department / CDC immediately — antitoxin is released only through public health, and reporting is mandatory.

docs.title: Forms — the route changes the antitoxin
docs.text: Form Source Key points Foodborne Preformed toxin — home-canned/fermented foods Outbreak potential; investigate co-exposed contacts Wound C. botulinum in a wound producing toxin in vivo Injection drug use, esp. black-tar heroin. No GI prodrome; needs antibiotics + debridement in addition to antitoxin (avoid aminoglycosides) Infant Spore colonization of the gut (honey, soil/dust) The "floppy baby" — constipation, poor feeding, weak cry, hypotonia. Treat with BabyBIG, not equine antitoxin Iatrogenic Cosmetic/therapeutic botulinum toxin overdose Usually mild/localized, occasionally systemic Inhalational Aerosolized toxin Not natural — implies bioterrorism. Botulinum toxin is a CDC Category A select agent

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
• Clinical and immediate — the syndrome above drives treatment; confirmation is retrospective.
  • Confirmatory: toxin assay (mouse bioassay) and culture from serum, stool, gastric contents, wound, or food — coordinated through public health.
  • Electrodiagnostics can support (facilitation on high-frequency RNS, like a presynaptic disorder) and help exclude mimics.
  • Rule out the mimics actively: Guillain-Barré / Miller Fisher (ascending, areflexia, albuminocytologic dissociation), myasthenic crisis, brainstem stroke, tick paralysis, organophosphate poisoning (which is the opposite autonomic picture — wet, not dry).

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Recognize acute, symmetric, descending, afebrile flaccid paralysis with bulbar onset and clear sensorium.
  2. Protect the airway — serial FVC and bulbar assessment; intubate early for respiratory or bulbar failure. This is the intervention that saves lives.
  3. Call public health NOW for antitoxin release and reporting — in parallel with airway care.
  4. Give antitoxin on clinical suspicion — do not wait for confirmation.
  5. Address the source — wound debridement + antibiotics for wound botulism; supportive care.
  6. Report and investigate — outbreak and bioterrorism implications.

docs.title: Treatment
docs.text:
Step 1 — airway and supportive care

  • Serial FVC, negative inspiratory force, and bulbar assessment. Intubate early — many patients need prolonged mechanical ventilation (weeks to months); recovery requires regeneration of nerve terminals.
  • Meticulous ICU supportive care — the paralysis is survivable with good support and is largely reversible over time.

Step 2 — antitoxin, as fast as possible

  • Adults and children >1 year: heptavalent botulinum antitoxin (HBAT/BAT, equine) — covers toxin types A–G. Released through the state health department / CDC.
  • Infants: human-derived botulism immune globulin (BabyBIG) — obtained from the Infant Botulism Treatment and Prevention Program (California Dept of Public Health). Do not use equine antitoxin in infants.
  • Antitoxin binds circulating toxin and stops further progression; it does not reverse paralysis already established — which is exactly why speed matters.

Wound botulism specifics

Add antibiotics and surgical debridement — but avoid aminoglycosides and clindamycin, which impair neuromuscular transmission and can worsen the weakness. Penicillin/metronidazole are typical choices. Give antitoxin first, then source control.

Military medicine — botulism

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2, and applicable force-health-protection / CBRN guidance. See Profiles, MEB/MAR2 & deployability.

  • This is primarily an operational and public-health event, not a chronic-disease disposition. Botulinum toxin is a Category A biological threat agent; an inhalational or clustered presentation in a deployed or garrison setting must trigger the CBRN and force-health-protection chain immediately, not just individual treatment.
  • Reporting is mandatory — to the servicing MTF, public health emergency officer, and through medical intelligence channels. A single case can be a sentinel event.
  • Recovery and retention: most survivors recover fully over weeks to months, so the usual disposition is a temporary profile through convalescence and rehabilitation, with retention determined by residual deficit under AR 40-501 3-31 if recovery is incomplete — not by the acute episode itself.
  • Deployability: non-deployable during the acute illness and prolonged recovery; reassess once ventilator-independent and strength has returned.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
One of the most potent toxins known, with a precise molecular target. Botulinum neurotoxin is taken up by cholinergic nerve terminals, where its light chain is a protease that cleaves SNARE proteins (SNAP-25, syntaxin, synaptobrevin) — the fusion machinery that docks acetylcholine vesicles to the presynaptic membrane. With SNARE cleaved, no acetylcholine is released, and the muscle is chemically denervated. Recovery is not a matter of clearing the toxin; it requires the terminal to sprout and rebuild new release sites, which is why paralysis lasts weeks to months even after antitoxin. The same mechanism, dosed and localized, is what makes botulinum toxin a therapeutic — from chronic migraine to cervical dystonia — and what makes MG a contraindication to receiving it.

Why "treat on suspicion" is not just caution. Antitoxin can only neutralize toxin that is still circulating and unbound. Once toxin has entered the nerve terminal, no antibody can retrieve it. Every hour of diagnostic delay converts reversible circulating toxin into fixed, bound toxin — so the therapeutic window is measured against the clock, and the decision to treat is made clinically, before any test returns.

Key references:

  • CDC Clinical Guidelines for Diagnosis and Treatment of Botulism, 2021 (MMWR Recomm Rep).
  • Heptavalent botulinum antitoxin (BAT/HBAT) — types A–G, equine.
  • BabyBIG — human botulism immune globulin for infant botulism (California IBTPP).

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Rao AK, Sobel J, Chatham-Stephens K, Luquez C. Clinical guidelines for diagnosis and treatment of botulism, 2021. MMWR Recomm Rep. 2021;70(2):1-30. MMWR
  2. Botulism Antitoxin Heptavalent (A–G, equine) — FDA label
  3. Infant botulism and BabyBIG — CDPH Infant Botulism Treatment and Prevention Program
  4. AR 40-501 para 3-31 (residual deficit); applicable CBRN / force-health-protection guidance for the operational response.

Verified July 2026: the treatment framework — heptavalent (A–G) equine antitoxin (BAT/HBAT) for patients >1 year, BabyBIG for infants, both released only through public health, and antitoxin that halts progression without reversing established paralysis — is taken from the CDC 2021 MMWR botulism clinical guidelines and the FDA antitoxin labeling. The descending-symmetric-afebrile clinical signature and the wound-botulism antibiotic cautions (avoid aminoglycosides/clindamycin) reflect that guidance. Botulinum toxin's status as a CDC Category A select agent is current. Not independently verified: current autoinjector/stockpile logistics at a given installation and exact antitoxin dosing (follow the CDC/label instructions at the time of use). Military framing is operational/public-health; botulism is not a named retention condition — disposition follows residual deficit under AR 40-501 3-31.

docs.title: Lambert-Eaton myasthenic syndrome (LEMS)
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: an antibody attack on presynaptic voltage-gated calcium channels that throttles acetylcholine release — proximal weakness, autonomic dysfunction, and hyporeflexia. Half the time it is the first sign of a small-cell lung cancer.

Who this is for: anyone working up proximal weakness that looks like myasthenia but isn't behaving — and the reason the page exists is that finding LEMS means starting a cancer hunt.

🚩 LEMS is a paraneoplastic red flag until proven otherwise

~50–60% of LEMS is paraneoplastic, and the tumor is almost always small-cell lung cancer (SCLC). The neurologic syndrome frequently precedes the cancer diagnosis by months to years — so a new LEMS diagnosis is a mandate to search, and to keep searching if the first scan is clean.

  • CT chest first; FDG-PET/CT if CT is negative and suspicion is high.
  • Repeat imaging every 3–6 months for ~2 years if the initial search is negative — the tumor may not yet be radiologically visible.
  • The strongest single risk factor is smoking. A smoker with LEMS has SCLC until proven otherwise.

docs.title: The triad that separates it from MG
docs.text:
Feature LEMS Myasthenia gravis Weakness pattern Proximal, legs > arms; gait first Ocular/bulbar first; fatigable On brief exercise Transiently improves (post-exercise facilitation) Worsens Reflexes Reduced or absent — and may return after exercise Normal Autonomic features Prominent — dry mouth, erectile dysfunction, constipation, orthostasis Absent Ocular involvement Mild or late Common, early Antibody P/Q-type VGCC (~85–95%) AChR / MuSK Cancer link SCLC in ~50–60% Thymoma in 10–15%

The counterintuitive sign: strength and reflexes that improve after a few seconds of exercise (facilitation) point to LEMS, not MG. Dry mouth is the most consistent autonomic clue and is easy to ask about.

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text:
• Serology — P/Q-type voltage-gated calcium channel (VGCC) antibody, positive in ~85–95%.
  • Electrodiagnostics are characteristic and confirmatory:
    • Low baseline CMAP amplitude.
    • Decrement on low-frequency (2–3 Hz) RNS — like MG.
    • But marked facilitation — CMAP amplitude increase >60–100% after brief maximal exercise or high-frequency (20–50 Hz) stimulation. This post-exercise facilitation is the signature and distinguishes it from MG.
  • The DELTA-P score (bulbar, erectile dysfunction, weight loss, performance status, and others) stratifies SCLC risk and guides how aggressively to hunt.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Recognize the triad — proximal weakness + autonomic features + hyporeflexia, with facilitation.
  2. Confirm — VGCC antibody + EDx showing post-exercise facilitation.
  3. Hunt for cancer — this is the priority. CT chest → PET/CT → serial imaging for 2 years.
  4. Treat symptoms — amifampridine.
  5. Treat the cause — the tumor (paraneoplastic) or immunotherapy (autoimmune/non-tumor).
  6. Military disposition — and note that a paraneoplastic LEMS is a cancer diagnosis first.

docs.title: Treatment
docs.text:
Step 1 — symptomatic: amifampridine

  • Amifampridine (3,4-diaminopyridine; Firdapse) — the FDA-approved first-line symptomatic therapy (approved 2018; label includes patients ≥6 years). A potassium-channel blocker that prolongs the presynaptic action potential, increasing calcium influx and ACh release.
  • Typical dosing titrated across the day; higher daily maxima (up to 80–100 mg/day for patients

    45 kg) are approved.

  • Lowers the seizure threshold — caution at higher doses and with any seizure history.
  • Pyridostigmine adds little in LEMS and is not a substitute.

Step 2 — treat the underlying driver

  • Paraneoplastic LEMS: treat the SCLC. Cancer treatment frequently improves the neurologic syndrome — the tumor is both the cause and the therapeutic target.
  • Non-tumor (autoimmune) LEMS: immunotherapy — prednisone plus a steroid-sparing agent (azathioprine, mycophenolate); IVIG or PLEX for severe or rapidly progressive weakness.

Same NMJ-drug cautions as MG

LEMS shares MG's sensitivity to neuromuscular-blocking drugs — aminoglycosides, fluoroquinolones, IV magnesium, and neuromuscular blockers can all worsen it. If a paraneoplastic patient is heading for thoracic surgery or chemotherapy, flag the NMJ sensitivity to the anesthesia and oncology teams. See the MG precipitant list.

Military medicine — LEMS

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2, and DoDI 1332.18. See Profiles, MEB/MAR2 & deployability.

  • The disposition is usually driven by the cause, not the LEMS itself. A paraneoplastic LEMS is an SCLC diagnosis — malignancy governs retention (a separate, non-neurologic standard) and the member is non-deployable on that basis alone.
  • For non-tumor autoimmune LEMS, the retention analysis runs like other neuromuscular disease: AR 40-501 3-31 captures neurologic conditions with residual functional impairment, and the DoD floor is DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 5.26.i (peripheral neuropathy / paralytic disorders with permanent functional impairment) applied to the NMJ; document the proximal weakness, autonomic burden, and treatment dependence.
  • Deployability: proximal weakness affecting gait, autonomic instability (orthostasis, heat intolerance), and dependence on amifampridine ± immunotherapy make generalized LEMS non-deployable.
  • Profile: permanent for established disease; functional limits reflecting proximal strength, gait, and heat/orthostatic tolerance.
  • MEB/DES: refer. In a paraneoplastic case, coordinate with oncology — the cancer work-up and the neurologic documentation both belong in the packet.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
Presynaptic, not postsynaptic — and that is the whole story. In LEMS, antibodies against P/Q-type voltage-gated calcium channels on the motor nerve terminal reduce the calcium influx that triggers acetylcholine vesicle release. Fewer quanta are released per impulse, so the first contraction is weak — but a burst of activity transiently loads the terminal with calcium, briefly increasing release and producing the facilitation that defines the syndrome on exam and on EMG. The same channels sit on autonomic terminals, which is why dry mouth and orthostasis travel with the weakness. It is the mirror image of myasthenia gravis, where the defect is postsynaptic receptor loss and repeated activity only worsens transmission.

The tumor connection is mechanistic. Small-cell lung cancer expresses functional voltage-gated calcium channels, and the anti-tumor immune response cross-reacts with the identical channels at the neuromuscular junction. LEMS is, in a real sense, the immune system finding the cancer before the radiologist does — which is exactly why the neurologic diagnosis obligates the search.

Key evidence:

  • Firdapse (amifampridine) FDA approval, 2018 — first approved LEMS therapy; symptomatic first-line.
  • P/Q-type VGCC antibody — the confirmatory serology (~85–95%).
  • DELTA-P score — stratifies SCLC risk at diagnosis.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. FDA approves Firdapse (amifampridine) for LEMS, 2018
  2. Recent advances and therapeutic options in LEMS
  3. LEMS medical management — MDA
  4. AR 40-501 para 3-31; DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26 (neurologic/neuromuscular).

Verified July 2026: amifampridine (3,4-DAP, Firdapse) is the FDA-approved first-line symptomatic therapy (2018; label ≥6 years), confirmed against the sponsor/FDA announcement. The P/Q-type VGCC antibody frequency (~85–95%), the SCLC association (~50–60% paraneoplastic), and the post-exercise-facilitation electrodiagnostic signature reflect established literature. The 2-year serial cancer-surveillance recommendation is standard practice for paraneoplastic LEMS. Not independently verified: exact amifampridine titration schedules (follow current labeling) and TRICARE coverage. Retention framing is applied by analogy to the AR 40-501 3-31 / DoDI 6130.03 5.26 neurologic standards — LEMS is not named separately in either regulation, and a paraneoplastic case is governed primarily by the malignancy standard; cite it that way rather than implying a dedicated NMJ paragraph.

docs.title: Nerve agent & organophosphate poisoning
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: poisoning of the synaptic enzyme — acetylcholinesterase inhibition floods every cholinergic synapse with acetylcholine, producing a cholinergic crisis: muscarinic secretions, nicotinic paralysis, and CNS seizures. Death is by respiratory failure. The antidotes are atropine and pralidoxime, and decontamination protects everyone else.

Who this is for: the provider facing a cholinergic-crisis patient — nerve agent (sarin, VX, Novichok) in a CBRN or terrorism setting, or the far commoner organophosphate/carbamate pesticide poisoning. Same mechanism, same antidotes.

🚩 Recognize the cholinergic toxidrome — and protect yourself first

Two mnemonics, both worth knowing:

  • Muscarinic — DUMBELS: Defecation, Urination, Miosis, Bronchorrhea/Bronchospasm, Emesis, Lacrimation, Salivation. Bronchorrhea and bronchospasm are what kill — the patient drowns in secretions.
  • Nicotinic — "days of the week" MTWtHF: Mydriasis (can occur), Tachycardia, Weakness, Twitching/fasciculations, Hypertension, Fasciculations. Progresses to flaccid paralysis, including the diaphragm.
  • CNS: agitation, seizures, coma.

The classic bedside signal is pinpoint pupils + copious secretions + fasciculations in a collapsing patient.

Decontaminate before you treat — this is a contaminated patient

Nerve agents and OPs are absorbed through skin and off-gas from clothing and vomit. A provider who treats an undecontaminated patient becomes the next casualty.

  • Don appropriate PPE. Remove all clothing (removes the majority of contaminant). Decontaminate skin (copious water; the RSDL kit for military nerve-agent decon).
  • This is a mass-casualty pattern — one index case with a cholinergic toxidrome and no trauma should raise a release/exposure event and trigger the CBRN chain.

docs.title: Mechanism in one line
docs.text: Acetylcholinesterase normally clears acetylcholine from the synapse. Inhibit it, and ACh piles up everywhere — at muscarinic (parasympathetic), nicotinic (neuromuscular junction and autonomic ganglia), and central synapses. This is the mechanistic opposite of myasthenia gravis (too little cholinergic transmission) — and, tellingly, an overdose of pyridostigmine or other cholinesterase inhibitors produces the same crisis.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Protect responders — PPE and decontamination first. Nothing else if the scene isn't safe.
  2. Airway and oxygen — suction the secretions; prepare for intubation.
  3. Atropine, immediately and aggressively — titrate to drying of secretions, not pupils or heart rate.
  4. Pralidoxime (an oxime) — reactivate the enzyme, before it "ages."
  5. Benzodiazepine — for seizures, and empirically in severe exposure.
  6. Escalate and support — mechanical ventilation, ICU, repeat/continuous atropine.
  7. Report — CBRN, public health, and (for occupational/pesticide) the appropriate authority.

docs.title: Treatment
docs.text:
Step 1 — atropine, titrated to secretions

  • Atropine 1–3 mg IV (2–6 mg in severe cases), doubling every 3–5 minutes until bronchial secretions dry and ventilation eases.
  • The endpoint is drying of secretions and improved oxygenation — NOT pupil size or heart rate. Miosis and bradycardia can persist despite adequate atropinization; chasing them leads to under- or over-dosing.
  • Severe poisoning can require tens to hundreds of milligrams over the resuscitation, then a continuous infusion. Do not under-dose out of caution about "normal" doses.
  • Atropine blocks the muscarinic effects; it does nothing for nicotinic weakness/paralysis — which is why the oxime matters.

Step 2 — pralidoxime (oxime), before the enzyme ages

  • Pralidoxime (2-PAM) 1–2 g IV over ~15–30 min (avoid bolus-induced hypertension), then a continuous infusion for severe poisoning.
  • Reactivates acetylcholinesterase — the only intervention that addresses the nicotinic (neuromuscular) weakness.
  • "Aging" is the clock: the agent–enzyme bond becomes irreversible over time and the oxime no longer works. Aging is fast for some agents (soman in ~2 minutes), slower for most OP pesticides (hours). Give the oxime as early as possible — do not withhold it waiting for atropine to work; they are complementary.

Step 3 — benzodiazepine for seizures / severe exposure

  • Diazepam or midazolam for seizures; give empirically in severe nerve-agent exposure even before seizures, as it reduces seizure-related brain injury.
  • Midazolam IM is now the field/status-epilepticus standard (RAMPART) — see status epilepticus.

Military autoinjectors — the field antidote

Warfighter self-aid/buddy-aid for nerve-agent exposure delivers the same three drugs:

  • ATNAA / DuoDote — a single autoinjector combining atropine (2.1 mg) + pralidoxime (600 mg) (ATNAA is the military designation; DuoDote the civilian EMS equivalent; the older Mark I kit was two separate injectors).
  • CANA (Convulsant Antidote, Nerve Agent) — diazepam 10 mg autoinjector for seizures.
  • Doctrine: repeat atropine/2-PAM autoinjectors per symptoms and training, then evacuate to definitive care and continuous therapy.

What not to do

  • Do not give succinylcholine — it is metabolized by the same cholinesterase the agent has inhibited, so its paralytic effect is massively prolonged. Use a non-depolarizing agent if paralysis is needed for intubation.
  • Do not delay the oxime waiting to see the atropine response — aging is irreversible.
  • Do not treat before decontamination unless you are in PPE — you will become a casualty.

Military medicine — nerve agent / organophosphate

Framework — this is a CBRN / operational topic first; retention is a downstream question. Verify against current CBRN medical doctrine, force-health-protection guidance, and — for residual deficit — AR 40-501 and DoDI 6130.03. See Profiles, MEB/MAR2 & deployability.

  • This is the one neurologic emergency that is also a weapons event. A nerve-agent casualty pattern (multiple cholinergic-toxidrome casualties, pinpoint pupils, no trauma) is a chemical attack until proven otherwise and drives the CBRN response, MOPP posture, and medical-intelligence reporting — not just individual resuscitation.
  • Antidote logistics are a readiness item: ATNAA/DuoDote and CANA autoinjector availability, currency, and self-aid/buddy-aid training are force-health-protection responsibilities, and pyridostigmine bromide pretreatment (a reversible cholinesterase protectant) may be fielded under specific threat conditions and medical direction.
  • Retention and deployability turn on residual deficit, not the acute exposure. Most who survive with prompt antidote recover; persistent neurologic sequelae (cognitive, peripheral neuropathy from chronic OP exposure, or an intermediate syndrome of proximal weakness days after exposure) are what would drive a profile and, if impairing duty, a DES referral under AR 40-501 3-31 and DoDI 6130.03 5.26.
  • Report through CBRN, public health emergency, and medical intelligence channels.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
One enzyme, every cholinergic synapse. Acetylcholinesterase terminates cholinergic signaling by hydrolyzing acetylcholine in the synaptic cleft. Organophosphates and nerve agents phosphorylate the enzyme's active site, and while it is inhibited, acetylcholine accumulates at muscarinic, nicotinic, and central synapses simultaneously — which is why the toxidrome is so florid and multi-system. Atropine competitively blocks the muscarinic receptors (drying the lethal secretions), while pralidoxime attacks the problem upstream, prising the phosphoryl group off the enzyme to restore acetylcholinesterase itself — the only way to fix the nicotinic weakness that atropine can't touch.

"Aging" is why minutes matter. The phosphorylated enzyme undergoes a spontaneous chemical change — loss of an alkyl group — that makes the bond permanent. After aging, pralidoxime is useless and recovery depends on synthesizing new enzyme over days to weeks. Aging half-times vary enormously: soman ages in about two minutes, making oxime therapy nearly futile once symptoms are established, whereas many pesticide OPs age over many hours, leaving a real reactivation window. This single kinetic fact drives the doctrine of giving the oxime as early as possible and the military interest in pretreatment to shield a fraction of enzyme before exposure.

The MG connection, both directions. Nerve-agent poisoning is mechanistically an acetylcholinesterase-inhibitor overdose — the same drug class (pyridostigmine) used to treat myasthenia gravis. Push a cholinesterase inhibitor too far and you convert therapy into a cholinergic crisis; that is exactly the "cholinergic crisis vs myasthenic crisis" distinction at the MG bedside, and it is the reason pyridostigmine pretreatment against nerve agents is dosed and monitored so carefully.

Key references:

  • CHEMM (HHS) — Medical Countermeasures: atropine, pralidoxime for nerve-agent/OP exposure.
  • Organophosphorus nerve-agent poisoning — managing the poisoned patient (Br J Anaesth 2020).
  • Nerve agents — StatPearls.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. CHEMM — Atropine sulfate, medical countermeasure (HHS)
  2. Organophosphorus nerve agent poisoning: managing the poisoned patient — Br J Anaesth 2020.
  3. Nerve agents — StatPearls
  4. Applicable CBRN medical doctrine and AR 40-501 para 3-31 / DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26 for residual deficit.

Verified July 2026: the antidote framework — atropine titrated to secretion drying (not pupils or heart rate), pralidoxime before enzyme aging, and benzodiazepine for seizures, with decontamination and PPE first — is taken from the cited HHS CHEMM countermeasure database, the 2020 Br J Anaesth review, and StatPearls. The aging kinetics (soman ~2 min vs slower for many pesticide OPs), the succinylcholine caution (prolonged block via inhibited cholinesterase), and the ATNAA/DuoDote (atropine 2.1 mg + pralidoxime 600 mg) and CANA (diazepam 10 mg) autoinjector compositions reflect that literature and U.S. military fielding. Not independently verified: current unit-level autoinjector stockage and pyridostigmine-pretreatment authorization, which are governed by threat-specific medical direction — confirm through the CBRN/force-health-protection chain. Military framing is operational; nerve-agent exposure is not a named retention condition — disposition follows residual deficit under AR 40-501 3-31.

docs.title: Seizure & status epilepticus
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: a seizure is sudden, hypersynchronous cortical firing; status epilepticus is the neurologic code — treat it on a clock, escalate on schedule, and don't under-dose the benzo (the single most common mistake). This page covers the code, the first-seizure work-up, and picking an antiseizure medication (ASM).

The ER consult usually sounds like: "Known-or-unknown epileptic, two witnessed seizures, got 4 of Ativan, now not responding — should I load them with something, and you're admitting, right?" The answer to "load them?" is yes, now — and check whether that "not responding" is postictal or ongoing nonconvulsive status.

🚩 Status epilepticus — recognize it and start the clock

  • Definition (ILAE two-timepoint): treat at t1 (the seizure won't self-terminate) — 5 min for a generalized tonic-clonic seizure, but 10 min for focal SE with impaired awareness and 10–15 min for absence; or ≥2 seizures without recovery between them. t2 (risk of long-term injury) is ~30 min for GTC. Bottom line: GTC at 5 min = treat now, and don't wait 30 minutes for anything.
  • "Stopped but won't wake up" = nonconvulsive status until proven otherwise — get urgent cEEG. Persistent altered consciousness after visible convulsions stop is NCSE in up to ~20%.
  • Under-dosing benzodiazepines (and then blaming the "refractory" seizure) is the classic error — give the full weight-based dose.
  • Find and fix the cause in parallel — a seizure from hypoglycemia, hyponatremia, a toxin, or eclampsia won't stop on ASMs until the cause is corrected.

docs.title: Status epilepticus — the timed escalation ladder (the code)
docs.text:
Step 0 — Stabilize (0–5 min)

  • ABCs, O₂, position, suction; IV access ×2; continuous ECG, BP, SpO₂.
  • Point-of-care glucose — if low, D50W 50 mL IV + thiamine 100 mg IV (thiamine first/with glucose in the malnourished/alcohol-use patient).
  • STAT labs: CBC, BMP, Ca, Mg, glucose, ASM levels, ± LFTs, tox screen (blood + urine), ABG, β-hCG, PO₄; ECG.

Step 1 — First-line benzodiazepine (5–20 min) — give the FULL dose

Pick by access; these are frequently under-dosed — use the real weight-based dose and repeat once if still seizing.

  • IV lorazepam 0.1 mg/kg (max 4 mg/dose), may repeat ×1 — first choice if IV is in.
  • No IV? IM midazolam 10 mg (>40 kg) — RAMPART showed IM midazolam is at least as good as IV lorazepam and faster to give; also intranasal/buccal midazolam.
  • IV diazepam 0.15–0.2 mg/kg (max 10 mg), or rectal diazepam 0.2 mg/kg if no IV.

Step 2 — Second-line ASM (20–40 min) — the three are EQUIVALENT (ESETT)

ESETT (NEJM 2019) found levetiracetam, fosphenytoin, and valproate stop ~45–47% of benzo-refractory status — no winner. So pick by the patient, give the full load IV:

  • Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV (max 4500 mg) — fewest interactions/contraindications; a good default (esp. cardiac/hepatic disease, pregnancy).
  • Fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg IV (≤150 mg PE/min) — avoid in hypotension/arrhythmia; watch the BP and QT.
  • Valproate 40 mg/kg IV (may add 20 mg/kg) — avoid in hepatic disease and pregnancy/childbearing potential.
  • (Phenobarbital 15 mg/kg IV if none of the above is available.)
  • Broadening landscape: IV lacosamide and brivaracetam are increasingly used as second-line (lacosamide was non-inferior to fosphenytoin for nonconvulsive seizures in TRENdS, with a favorable hemodynamic profile) — reasonable alternatives, though the formal evidence base still rests on the ESETT triad.
  • Continue the second-line agent as you escalate; arrange cEEG.

Step 3 — Refractory status (>40 min, or 2 lines failed) — anesthetize + cEEG

Intubate, admit to ICU, start continuous cEEG, and begin a continuous anesthetic infusion. Target seizure suppression, not routine burst-suppression — induced burst suppression wasn't associated with better outcomes (Fisch 2023), and seizure suppression alone tracked with better function; reserve burst-suppression for seizures recurring despite adequate anesthetic dosing.

  • Midazolam — load 0.2 mg/kg, then 0.05–2.9 mg/kg/h.
  • Propofol — load ~1–2 mg/kg, then 1–15 mg/kg/h (watch propofol-infusion syndrome — avoid in suspected mitochondrial disease or on a ketogenic diet; limit dose/duration).
  • Ketamine — increasingly used (NMDA-mediated late status), usually with a GABAergic agent.
  • Keep the second-line ASM on board for maintenance.

Step 4 — Super-refractory (>24 h, or recurs on weaning)

  • Pentobarbital/thiopental infusion (load 5 mg/kg → titrate; hypotension is expected — pressors ready), titrated to seizure/burst suppression.
  • Hunt hard for the cause — autoimmune/paraneoplastic (NORSE/FIRES), CNS infection, PRES/RCVS, toxic-metabolic; consider immunotherapy (steroids/IVIg/PLEX) empirically in NORSE.
  • Ketogenic diet is used adjunctively in super-refractory SE (especially FIRES/NORSE).
  • Don't reach for unproven add-ons: hypothermia adds nothing beyond standard therapy, and the allopregnanolone (brexanolone) RCT was negative — no proven pharmacologic agent exists for super-refractory SE beyond anesthetics (+ immunotherapy for autoimmune causes).
  • Wean anesthetics slowly once seizure-free ~24–48 h; neurology + epileptology on board.

Copy-paste — first 20 minutes

  • Glucose now; D50 + thiamine if low.
  • Lorazepam 4 mg IV (or midazolam 10 mg IM if no IV) — repeat ×1 if seizing at ~5 min.
  • Levetiracetam 60 mg/kg IV (or fosphenytoin/valproate by patient) as the seizure continues.
  • cEEG, ICU, and treat the cause. No sub-therapeutic benzos, no delay to second-line.

Toxin-driven seizures — the antidote matters

  • INH (isoniazid): benzodiazepine + pyridoxine (vitamin B6) ~70 mg/kg, max 5 g.
  • Tricyclic antidepressants: wide QRS → sodium bicarbonate.
  • Organophosphates: atropine + pralidoxime (+ benzodiazepine).
  • Alcohol/benzo withdrawal: escalating benzodiazepines. Eclampsia: magnesium (+ deliver).

docs.title: Is it actually a seizure? (mimics)
docs.text:
Before you commit to lifelong ASMs, exclude the imitators — syncope (convulsive syncope after a faint; get an ECG/cardiac workup — cardiac syncope can kill), psychogenic non-epileptic seizures (PNES), TIA, transient global amnesia, migraine, and sleep disorders.

  • PNES tells (not diagnostic alone; confirm with video-EEG): side-to-side head shaking, eye closure (epileptic seizures usually have eyes open), pelvic thrusting, asynchronous flailing, a waxing–waning course, prolonged duration, crying/stuttering, preserved awareness despite bilateral movements, and resistance to eye opening. Manage with diagnosis delivery + behavioral health, not more ASMs.

docs.title: First seizure — work-up & whether to treat
docs.text:
First unprovoked seizure — the work-up (outpatient is OK if back to baseline, no deficits, low infection concern)

  • Labs: CBC, BMP, Ca/Mg, glucose, LFTs, tox, ± ESR/CRP; β-hCG.
  • Imaging: MRI brain (with & without contrast) is the study of choice; CT in the ED (and before LP) to exclude a bleed/mass.
  • EEG — best within 48 h (raises yield); urgent if consciousness stays altered.
  • LP only if infection/SAH suspected.

Start an ASM after a first seizure? — individualize (AAN/AES 2015)

Immediate ASM reduces recurrence over the first 2 years but does NOT improve long-term remission or quality of life. Recurrence after a first unprovoked seizure is ~21–45% at 2 years.

  • Treat now if recurrence risk is high: a prior brain insult (stroke/tumor/trauma), an epileptiform EEG, a relevant lesion on MRI, or a nocturnal seizure.
  • Reasonable to wait in a low-risk, normal-work-up first seizure — with a shared decision (weigh ASM adverse effects, ~7–31%).
  • An acute provoked (symptomatic) seizure — fix the provocation; long-term ASM usually isn't needed.

docs.title: Picking an ASM (by seizure type & the patient)
docs.text:
Match the drug to the seizure and the person

SANAD II reset the defaults (Lancet 2021): lamotrigine is first-line for focal epilepsy (beat levetiracetam on treatment failure, tolerability, and cost), and valproate is the most effective for generalized epilepsy (with the childbearing caveat). Levetiracetam is the alternative when you need rapid titration (lamotrigine must be titrated slowly for rash) — e.g., the ED first seizure of unknown type.

Situation Preferred → alternative Focal (most adults) Lamotrigine (best tolerated/cost) → levetiracetam (when rapid titration needed) or lacosamide; oxcarbazepine/carbamazepine Generalized (GTC) Valproate (most effective) → lamotrigine or levetiracetam (valproate off the table in childbearing potential) Absence Ethosuximide, valproate, lamotrigine Myoclonic (e.g., JME) Valproate, levetiracetam (avoid carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine/phenytoin — worsen myoclonus/absence) Childbearing potential Lamotrigine or levetiracetam — avoid valproate (neural-tube defects, low IQ) Older adults Lamotrigine or levetiracetam (fewer interactions, better tolerated) Comorbid depression/mood Lamotrigine (avoid levetiracetam's irritability); renal → lamotrigine; hepatic → levetiracetam/gabapentin
  • Screen HLA-B*15:02 before carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine in at-risk (Southeast/East Asian) ancestry (SJS/TEN); some guidelines also screen HLA-A*31:01 (European/Japanese ancestry — DRESS/SJS).

Drug-resistant focal epilepsy — cenobamate is the standout newer ASM

Among the newer adjuncts, cenobamate gives the best chance of seizure freedom (~32% at 12 months; out-performs brivaracetam, lacosamide, and perampanel on 50%-responder rate even after many failed ASMs). The trade-offs: titrate slowly (DRESS risk with fast titration) and a higher overall adverse-effect rate. Worth it for refractory focal epilepsy.

  • Refractory (failed 2 appropriate ASMs) = drug-resistant epilepsy → refer to an epilepsy center. The 2022 ILAE surgical-referral consensus is emphatic: offer a surgical evaluation to every drug-resistant patient up to age 70, regardless of epilepsy duration, seizure type, localization, or comorbidity (including psychiatric disease or comorbid PNES) — plus consider VNS/RNS/DBS and video-EEG.

docs.title: Classify it (ILAE 2017)
docs.text: Three levels: seizure type (focal / generalized / unknown onset; aware vs impaired awareness; motor vs non-motor) → epilepsy type (focal / generalized / combined / unknown) → epilepsy syndrome. Getting the type right drives ASM choice (e.g., don't give a sodium-channel blocker in a generalized/myoclonic syndrome).

docs.title: Counseling, rescue plan & driving
docs.text:
• Driving is restricted after a seizure — the seizure-free interval is state/country-specific (commonly ~3–12 months; 6 months is typical); some states have mandatory physician reporting. Document the counseling.
  • Safety: no swimming/bathing alone, heights, or operating dangerous machinery until controlled; sleep, alcohol moderation, adherence.

Give a seizure action plan with a rescue medication (for cluster-prone patients)

Prescribe an outpatient rescue benzodiazepine and a written action plan — standard of care for epilepsy at risk of clusters/prolonged seizures. FDA-approved options:

  • Midazolam nasal spray (Nayzilam) — age ≥12.
  • Diazepam nasal spray (Valtoco) — age ≥6.
  • Rectal diazepam gel (Diastat) — age ≥2.

SUDEP — discuss it early, not just when uncontrolled

Sudden unexpected death in epilepsy is ~1/1,000 adults with epilepsy per year, rising to ~6–9/ 1,000 in drug-resistant epilepsy. Per the ILAE SUDEP task force, offer SUDEP communication to every patient at diagnosis and revisit it periodically (not only the uncontrolled). Modifiable risk factors: uncontrolled generalized tonic-clonic seizures (the biggest), nocturnal seizures, medication non-adherence, and lack of nocturnal supervision — target each.

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
Status epilepticus → ICU with cEEG. A first seizure back to baseline with a reassuring work-up can be discharged with urgent neurology follow-up, safety/driving counseling, and a decision on ASM. Admit for a prolonged postictal state, an unremitting/nonconvulsive picture, a concerning cause, or an unsafe home situation.

TRICARE — seizure prescribing

Levetiracetam, lamotrigine, valproate, phenytoin, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, phenobarbital, gabapentin are generic/formulary, no PA — the whole core ladder is low-friction. Newer agents (brivaracetam, lacosamide, cenobamate, perampanel) are typically PA-gated (step therapy / specialist). Emergency IV agents are inpatient-stocked. Verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Military medicine — seizure / epilepsy

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03, DoDI 1332.18, and aeromedical policy before counseling.

  • Retention (Army): AR 40-501 para 3-31i addresses seizure disorders/epilepsy — a diagnosis of epilepsy (recurrent unprovoked seizures), or seizures not controlled off medication, generally falls below retention → DES (MEB/PEB). The joint floor is DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.c (CNS conditions with persistent sequelae, incl. seizures). Document seizure type, frequency, provoking factors, work-up, ASM trials, and control.
  • Accession: a history of epilepsy/recurrent seizures is disqualifying for entry (DoDI 6130.03 Vol 1) — waivers hinge on a documented seizure-free, medication-free interval.
  • Deployability: active epilepsy, ongoing ASMs, and the need for lab/level monitoring and reliable sleep are deployment-limiting. A provoked seizure with a corrected cause may return to duty per neurology review.
  • Aeromedical / special duty: a seizure history (and most ASMs) is disqualifying for flying and many special-duty positions — waiver-dependent; confirm the standard for the class.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• Treat status on a clock, not on a hunch. The AES 2016 algorithm: stabilize (0–5 min) → full-dose benzo (5–20 min) → second-line ASM (20–40 min) → anesthetize + cEEG (>40 min).
  • The second-line three are equivalent (ESETT 2019) — levetiracetam ≈ fosphenytoin ≈ valproate (~45–47% each); choose by comorbidity, and give the full weight-based load. Levetiracetam is the cleanest default.
  • Benzos are under-dosed — that's the commonest "refractory status" that wasn't. RAMPART: IM midazolam ≥ IV lorazepam when there's no IV.
  • "Won't wake up" → cEEG for nonconvulsive status.
  • First seizure: immediate ASM cuts early recurrence but not long-term remission (AAN/AES 2015) — treat when recurrence risk is high (lesion, epileptiform EEG, nocturnal), otherwise a shared decision.
  • Second-line is broadening — lacosamide (non-inferior to fosphenytoin, TRENdS; kinder hemodynamics) and brivaracetam are increasingly used alongside the ESETT triad.
  • Refractory SE: target seizure suppression, not routine burst-suppression (Fisch 2023); no proven drug beyond anesthetics (allopregnanolone RCT negative; hypothermia adds nothing); ketogenic diet in FIRES/NORSE.
  • SANAD II reset the maintenance defaults — lamotrigine first-line for focal epilepsy, valproate most effective for generalized (levetiracetam is the rapid-titration alternative). For drug-resistant focal epilepsy, cenobamate gives the best seizure-freedom odds (slow titration, DRESS risk).
  • Two failed ASMs = drug-resistant → refer for surgical evaluation — the 2022 ILAE consensus: offer it to every such patient up to 70, regardless of type/duration/comorbidity.
  • Give a rescue plan (Nayzilam/Valtoco/Diastat) and discuss SUDEP at diagnosis (biggest modifiable factor: uncontrolled GTC seizures).

Key sources: AES 2016 status-epilepticus guideline (Glauser et al.); Gettings et al., Lancet Neurol 2025 SE review; ESETT (Kapur, NEJM 2019); TRENdS (lacosamide); RAMPART (Silbergleit, NEJM 2012); Fisch et al. (burst-suppression, Neurology 2023); AAN/AES 2015 first-seizure guideline; SANAD II (Marson, Lancet 2021); cenobamate comparative-effectiveness (JAMA Neurol 2026); ILAE 2022 surgical-referral consensus; ILAE SUDEP task force (Epilepsia 2026); ILAE 2017 classification.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Glauser T, et al. Evidence-Based Guideline: Treatment of Convulsive Status Epilepticus in Children and Adults — AES. Epilepsy Curr. 2016.
  2. Kapur J, et al. Randomized Trial of Three Anticonvulsant Medications for Status Epilepticus (ESETT). NEJM. 2019.
  3. Silbergleit R, et al. Intramuscular vs intravenous therapy for prehospital status epilepticus (RAMPART). NEJM. 2012.
  4. Gettings JV, et al. Diagnosis and Management of Status Epilepticus. Lancet Neurol. 2025; Husain AM, et al. Lacosamide vs fosphenytoin for nonconvulsive seizures (TRENdS) (Ann Neurol); Fisch U, et al. Burst suppression & outcomes in refractory SE (Neurology 2023).
  5. Krumholz A, et al. Management of an unprovoked first seizure in adults (AAN/AES). Neurology. 2015; Fisher RS, et al. ILAE 2017 classification.
  6. Maintenance ASMs: Marson A, et al. SANAD II (focal & generalized, Lancet 2021); Smith PEM Initial management of seizure (NEJM 2021); Kanner AM, Bicchi MM (JAMA 2022); Cerulli Irelli E, et al. Cenobamate vs brivaracetam/lacosamide/perampanel (JAMA Neurol 2026).
  7. Epilepsy care: Jehi L, et al. ILAE surgical-referral consensus (Epilepsia 2022); Aschner A, et al. ILAE SUDEP-communication scoping review (Epilepsia 2026); seizure-cluster rescue reviews (Nayzilam/Valtoco/Diastat).
  8. Source scutbook: Seizure (M. Holtkamp) — ER checklist, escalation, toxin antidotes, PNES semiology, first-seizure work-up, ASM-by-population approach (modernized to AES 2016 / ESETT).
  9. TRICARE / DoD: core ASMs generic-formulary (no PA); newer agents PA-gated. Retention: AR 40-501 para 3-31i, DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.c (accession Vol 1). Verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Verified July 2026: the timed status-epilepticus algorithm follows AES 2016 + the Lancet Neurology 2025 review; ESETT establishes levetiracetam/fosphenytoin/valproate as equivalent second-line (lacosamide/brivaracetam emerging — TRENdS); refractory SE targets seizure (not routine burst-) suppression (Fisch 2023), with allopregnanolone negative and hypothermia unhelpful. The ILAE two-timepoint definition (GTC 5 min / focal-impaired-awareness 10 min), SANAD II maintenance defaults (lamotrigine focal / valproate generalized; levetiracetam for rapid titration), cenobamate for drug-resistant focal epilepsy, the 2022 ILAE surgical-referral and SUDEP-communication consensus, rescue-medication options, and ILAE 2017 classification are current. The specific figures were supplied by expert review with citations — confirm before quoting. Retention citations (AR 40-501 3-31i; DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 5.26.c) and state-specific driving/reporting rules should be confirmed against the current source before counseling.*

docs.title: Antiseizure medications (ASMs)
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: a bedside formulary and prescribing guide. Two questions decide almost everything: broad- or narrow-spectrum (match the syndrome), and does the patient have interaction/comorbidity/pregnancy constraints that pick the agent for you. Start low, go slow, monotherapy first.

🚩 Three prescribing traps

  • These aggravate generalized/absence/myoclonic seizures — use broad-spectrum if the type is generalized or unknown: carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, eslicarbazepine, phenytoin, gabapentin, and pregabalin (and tiagabine/vigabatrin can worsen myoclonus).
  • Enzyme inducers (carbamazepine, phenytoin, phenobarbital, primidone) fail oral contraceptives and lower warfarin/DOAC/many drug levels; valproate is an inhibitor (it doubles lamotrigine — halve the lamotrigine dose and titrate even slower).
  • Valproate and topiramate are the big teratogens — avoid valproate in anyone who could become pregnant; lamotrigine and levetiracetam are the safest in pregnancy.

docs.title: Broad vs narrow spectrum (the first fork)
docs.text:
• Broad-spectrum (focal and generalized): levetiracetam, lamotrigine, valproate, topiramate, zonisamide, perampanel, clobazam, cannabidiol, brivaracetam, rufinamide, phenobarbital.
  • Narrow-spectrum (focal only; may worsen generalized): carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, eslicarbazepine, phenytoin, lacosamide, gabapentin, pregabalin, tiagabine, vigabatrin.
  • Absence-specific: ethosuximide.

First-line defaults (SANAD II)

  • Focal epilepsy → lamotrigine (beat levetiracetam on time-to-remission, tolerability, and cost; per-protocol superior to levetiracetam and zonisamide). Levetiracetam is the alternative when you need rapid titration (ED, unknown type) or lamotrigine is contraindicated; lacosamide is another option.
  • Generalized epilepsy → valproate (most effective) — but not in anyone who could become pregnant → lamotrigine or levetiracetam (levetiracetam preferred over lamotrigine in JME).

docs.title: The working formulary
docs.text:
ASM Spectrum Mechanism Typical adult maintenance Watch for / monitoring Enzyme effect Levetiracetam Broad SV2A 500–1500 mg BID Irritability/mood/psychosis; renal dose None (clean) Brivaracetam Broad SV2A (higher affinity) 25–100 mg BID Fewer behavioral AEs than LEV; don't co-prescribe with LEV (shared target); useful switch from LEV ~10:1–15:1, no titration Minimal Lamotrigine Broad Na-channel + others 100–200 mg BID (titrate slowly) Rash/SJS (worse w/ fast titration or valproate); may worsen myoclonus in JME (~4–8%); low-risk in pregnancy None (UGT; valproate ↑ it, inducers ↓ it) Valproate Broad Multiple (GABA, etc.) 500–1500 mg/day (÷) Teratogen, hepatotoxicity, hyperammonemia, pancreatitis, tremor, weight, thrombocytopenia Inhibitor (↑ lamotrigine) Topiramate Broad Multiple 50–200 mg BID Word-finding/cognition, paresthesias, kidney stones, glaucoma, weight loss, teratogen (cleft) Weak inducer (high dose) Zonisamide Broad Na/Ca 100–400 mg/day Stones, oligohidrosis, cognition, weight loss; sulfa Minimal Perampanel Broad AMPA antagonist 4–12 mg qHS Black-box: behavioral/psychiatric; dizziness Minor Carbamazepine Narrow Na-channel 600–1200 mg/day (÷) HLA-B*15:02 (SJS), hyponatremia, leukopenia, aplastic anemia, diplopia; auto-induction Strong inducer Oxcarbazepine Narrow Na-channel 900–2400 mg/day (÷) Hyponatremia, rash (HLA-B*15:02), diplopia Moderate inducer Eslicarbazepine Narrow Na-channel 800–1600 mg daily Hyponatremia; once-daily, fewer interactions Mild inducer Phenytoin Narrow* Na-channel 300–400 mg/day (level 10–20) Zero-order kinetics, gingival hyperplasia, ataxia/nystagmus, hirsutism, SJS, cerebellar atrophy Strong inducer Lacosamide Narrow Na-channel (slow) 100–200 mg BID PR prolongation (ECG); well tolerated Minimal Gabapentin Narrow α2δ Ca-channel 300–1200 mg TID Sedation, weight, edema; renal dose None Pregabalin Narrow α2δ Ca-channel 75–300 mg BID Sedation, weight, edema; misuse potential (Schedule V); renal dose None Ethosuximide Absence only T-type Ca 500–1500 mg/day GI upset, idiosyncratic; absence only Minimal Phenobarbital / primidone Broad GABA-A PB 60–180 mg/day (level 20–40) Sedation, dependence, cognition Strong inducer Cenobamate Narrow (focal; generalized trials ongoing) Na + GABA-A Titrate to 200 (–400) mg daily DRESS if titrated fast — start 12.5 mg, go slow; best seizure-freedom among newer focal ASMs Inhibits/induces (adjust others) Fenfluramine (syndrome-specific) 5-HT / sigma-1 per weight FDA-approved Dravet + Lennox-Gastaut; requires echocardiography (valvulopathy/pulmonary-HTN monitoring) Minimal Ganaxolone (syndrome-specific) Neurosteroid GABA-A per weight FDA-approved for CDKL5 deficiency disorder; somnolence Minimal

Phenytoin is sometimes used for generalized convulsive seizures acutely, but is not broad-spectrum for absence/myoclonic.

docs.title: Routes of administration & PO ↔ IV conversion
docs.text:
The bedside question is usually: "my patient just went NPO / is being transferred — can I keep the same drug IV?" Only some ASMs have an IV form; those that do convert 1:1 (same total daily dose).

ASM Routes available PO ↔ IV conversion Levetiracetam PO (tab/soln/XR), IV 1:1 (same total daily dose), infuse over 15 min Brivaracetam PO, IV 1:1; IV push or short infusion Valproate / divalproex PO (tab/XR/soln), IV 1:1; can give as intermittent infusions Phenytoin PO (cap/susp), IV 1:1; use fosphenytoin IV (dosed in mg PE = phenytoin-equivalents; safer than IV phenytoin) Lacosamide PO, IV 1:1; infuse ~15–30 min (watch PR interval) Phenobarbital PO, IV (also IM) 1:1 Lamotrigine PO only (no IV — plan a bridge if NPO) Carbamazepine / oxcarbazepine / eslicarbazepine PO only (susp available for CBZ/OXC) (no IV) Topiramate / zonisamide / perampanel PO only (no IV) — perampanel/zonisamide once-daily helps Gabapentin / pregabalin PO only (no IV) Ethosuximide PO only (no IV) Cenobamate / primidone PO only (no IV)

Practical NPO rules

  • Seamless IV switch (1:1): levetiracetam, valproate, lacosamide, brivaracetam, phenobarbital, and phenytoin (as fosphenytoin). Keep the same total daily dose.
  • No IV option: lamotrigine, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, eslicarbazepine, topiramate, zonisamide, gabapentin/pregabalin, ethosuximide, perampanel, cenobamate, primidone — for a truly NPO patient, use the suspension via NG/OG tube where one exists, or temporarily bridge with an IV-available broad-spectrum agent (usually levetiracetam or valproate) until the home drug can be resumed. Never just skip doses of a chronic ASM — withdrawal seizures/status.
  • Enteral tube: most tablets/capsules can't be crushed if XR — switch to immediate-release or a suspension; separate phenytoin suspension from tube feeds (binding lowers absorption).

docs.title: Practical prescribing
docs.text:
Start · monitor · switch · stop

  • Start: monotherapy, lowest effective dose, titrate to seizure control or side effects before adding a second agent.
  • Drug-resistant epilepsy = failure of 2 tolerated, appropriately-chosen, adequately-dosed ASMs (mono- or combination; ILAE/Kwan 2010) → refer to an epilepsy center — early. Median time to surgery is still ~20 years despite better outcomes with earlier referral; don't sit on it (see the seizure page).
  • Check levels only when useful — narrow-therapeutic agents (phenytoin, carbamazepine, valproate, phenobarbital), suspected toxicity/non-adherence, pregnancy, or renal/hepatic change. Levetiracetam/lacosamide/lamotrigine don't need routine levels.
  • Switch by cross-taper — titrate the new drug up to an effective dose before tapering the old one off; never stop abruptly (withdrawal seizures/status).
  • Stop/withdraw only after a sustained seizure-free period (commonly ~2–4 years) with a shared decision — taper slowly, and counsel on driving/recurrence.

Idiosyncratic reactions to know on sight

  • SJS/TEN/DRESS: lamotrigine (rapid titration), phenytoin, cenobamate (fast titration), and the carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine/eslicarbazepine class → stop the drug, don't rechallenge.
    • HLA screening (broadening): classically HLA-B*15:02 in Southeast/East Asian ancestry before carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine; newer guidance (2026 UK CERSI-PGx) recommends testing HLA-B*15:02, HLA-B*15:11, and HLA-A*31:01 in all treatment-naïve patients before carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine/eslicarbazepine — HLA-A*31:01 covers the full spectrum (SJS/TEN, DRESS, maculopapular) and is common across ethnicities (~5–9% Japanese, ~2–3% European).
  • Hepatotoxicity / hyperammonemic encephalopathy / pancreatitis: valproate.
  • Aplastic anemia / agranulocytosis: carbamazepine (and felbamate).
  • Hyponatremia: carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, eslicarbazepine.
  • Irreversible visual-field loss: vigabatrin (mandatory eye monitoring).
  • Behavioral/psychiatric: levetiracetam (irritability), perampanel (black box).

Pregnancy & people who can become pregnant (2024 AAN/AES/SMFM guideline)

  • Level A — avoid valproate (major congenital malformations, autism, lower IQ) where clinically feasible; Level A — use lamotrigine, levetiracetam, or oxcarbazepine as lower-risk options (oxcarbazepine is now explicitly included). Level B — avoid topiramate (cleft, small-for- gestational-age) and valproate (SGA).
  • Don't destabilize a controlled pregnancy — Level B: once pregnant, be cautious about removing/replacing an effective ASM (even valproate) if it's controlling GTC seizures; a convulsive seizure can be worse than the drug. Optimize before pregnancy.
  • Many newer ASMs (lacosamide, eslicarbazepine, perampanel, cenobamate) have limited pregnancy data — counsel accordingly.
  • Folic acid ≥0.4 mg/day for anyone of childbearing potential on an ASM.
  • Lamotrigine clearance rises up to ~2–2.7× by the third trimester — increase the dose with levels, then taper back within ~2–3 weeks postpartum to avoid toxicity. (Levetiracetam and oxcarbazepine also fall in pregnancy.)
  • Enzyme-inducers reduce contraceptive efficacy — counsel on reliable contraception.
  • Valproate in anyone who could become pregnant requires a formal pregnancy-prevention programme / documented counseling (EMA/FDA).

On the horizon

Azetukalner (XEN1101) — a Kv7.2/7.3 potassium-channel opener (novel mechanism) — cut focal seizures dose-dependently in a phase-2b RCT (~53% vs ~18% placebo) with no titration needed (~10-day half-life); phase-3 trials (focal and generalized) are ongoing. The most advanced pipeline agent.

docs.title: Emergency IV loading
docs.text:
For status epilepticus loading doses (levetiracetam 60 mg/kg, fosphenytoin 20 mg PE/kg, valproate 40 mg/kg, etc.) and the timed escalation, see the seizure & status epilepticus page.

TRICARE — ASM formulary

Levetiracetam, lamotrigine, valproate/divalproex, phenytoin, carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, phenobarbital, gabapentin, topiramate, zonisamide, ethosuximide are generic/formulary, no PA — the whole core ladder is low-friction. Newer agents — brivaracetam, lacosamide, cenobamate, perampanel, eslicarbazepine, cannabidiol — are typically PA-gated (step therapy / specialist justification). Verify current status at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Military medicine — ASMs

Being on a chronic ASM for epilepsy generally tracks with the retention/deployability picture on the seizure page (AR 40-501 3-31i / DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 5.26.c; flying-duty disqualifying).
