Beyond the diagnosis, sedating agents, cognitive side effects (topiramate, phenobarbital), lab-monitoring needs, and enzyme-interaction with other duty-relevant meds are all deployment considerations. Prefer non-sedating, interaction-light agents (levetiracetam, lamotrigine, lacosamide) where a service member is being maintained pending disposition.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• Spectrum first: broad-spectrum (levetiracetam/lamotrigine/valproate) is safe for any type; narrow sodium-channel agents can worsen generalized/absence/myoclonic epilepsy.
  • Interactions are the hidden hazard: inducers (carbamazepine/phenytoin/phenobarbital) fail contraceptives and lower warfarin/DOAC/drug levels; valproate inhibits (doubles lamotrigine). Levetiracetam, lacosamide, gabapentin, pregabalin, brivaracetam are interaction-light.
  • Monotherapy, lowest effective dose, cross-taper to switch, don't stop abruptly. Check levels only for narrow-therapeutic drugs or a clinical question.
  • Pregnancy (2024 AAN/AES/SMFM): lamotrigine, levetiracetam, or oxcarbazepine preferred; avoid valproate and topiramate; folic acid ≥0.4 mg; lamotrigine clearance rises up to ~2.7× and must be tapered back postpartum; don't destabilize a controlled pregnancy to switch off valproate.
  • Refractory focal epilepsy: cenobamate gives the best seizure-freedom among newer adjuncts (~32% at 12 mo vs 11–17% for brivaracetam/lacosamide/perampanel; slow titration for DRESS).
  • Brivaracetam: don't co-prescribe with levetiracetam; use it as a switch from levetiracetam (~10:1) when behavioral side effects are the problem.
  • Screen HLA broadly (B*15:02 / B*15:11 / A*31:01) before the carbamazepine class. Azetukalner (Kv7 opener) is the notable pipeline agent.
  • Know which ASMs go IV (1:1) for the NPO/transfer patient: levetiracetam, valproate, lacosamide, brivaracetam, phenobarbital, phenytoin (as fosphenytoin). The rest are PO-only — bridge with an IV agent rather than skip doses.

Key sources: SANAD II (Marson, Lancet 2021 — lamotrigine focal / valproate generalized); Smith (NEJM 2021) & Kanner/Bicchi (JAMA 2022) ASM reviews; AAN/AES/SMFM pregnancy guideline (Pack et al., Neurology 2024); cenobamate comparative-effectiveness (JAMA Neurol 2026); UK CERSI-PGx HLA guideline (2026); Kwan et al. drug-resistant definition (2010); XEN1101/azetukalner phase-2b (JAMA Neurol 2023).

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Marson A, et al. SANAD II (lamotrigine focal; valproate generalized). Lancet 2021; Smith PEM. Initial Management of Seizure in Adults. NEJM. 2021; Kanner AM, Bicchi MM. Antiseizure Medications for Adults With Epilepsy. JAMA. 2022.
  2. Pack AM, et al. Teratogenesis/neurodevelopmental outcomes after in-utero ASM exposure — AAN/AES/ SMFM practice guideline. Neurology 2024 (avoid valproate/topiramate; use lamotrigine/levetiracetam/ oxcarbazepine; folic acid ≥0.4 mg; don't switch mid-pregnancy if GTC-controlled).
  3. Cerulli Irelli E, et al. Cenobamate vs brivaracetam/lacosamide/perampanel (JAMA Neurol 2026); Bresnahan R, et al. Brivaracetam — do not combine with levetiracetam (Cochrane 2022).
  4. HLA / pipeline: UK CERSI-PGx HLA testing for carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine/eslicarbazepine (2026, all ancestries; B*15:02/B*15:11/A*31:01); Kwan P, et al. ILAE drug-resistant definition (2010); French JA, et al. Azetukalner (XEN1101) phase-2b (JAMA Neurol 2023).
  5. Source scutbook: Seizure (M. Holtkamp) — per-drug MOA/metabolism/adverse/spectrum formulary.
  6. TRICARE / DoD: core ASMs generic-formulary (no PA); newer agents PA-gated. Retention: AR 40-501 3-31i, DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 5.26.c. Verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Verified July 2026: spectrum classification and the aggravation list (CBZ/OXC/ESL/PHT/gabapentinoids), enzyme-interaction profiles, narrow-therapeutic-index monitoring, the SANAD II first-line defaults (lamotrigine focal / valproate generalized; levetiracetam for rapid titration), the 2024 AAN/AES/SMFM pregnancy recommendations (avoid valproate/topiramate; lamotrigine/levetiracetam/oxcarbazepine preferred; folic acid ≥0.4 mg; don't destabilize a controlled pregnancy; lamotrigine clearance rises ~2–2.7×), the broadened HLA screening (B*15:02/B*15:11/A*31:01), cenobamate as the top newer focal agent, the brivaracetam-vs-levetiracetam interaction/switch point, fenfluramine/ganaxolone indications, and the drug-resistance definition (Kwan 2010) reflect current references; azetukalner is investigational. Route/IV availability and 1:1 PO↔IV conversion (levetiracetam, valproate, lacosamide, brivaracetam, phenobarbital, fosphenytoin) are as labeled. The specific comparative figures were supplied by expert review with citations — confirm before quoting. Doses are typical adult maintenance ranges — confirm against a current drug reference and adjust for renal/hepatic function; retention citations should be confirmed against AR 40-501 / DoDI 6130.03 before counseling.

docs.title: Epilepsy syndromes
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: the syndrome — not just the seizure type — tells you which drug to use, which drug will make it worse, the prognosis, and the counseling. The single highest-yield distinction for an adult on call: is this a genetic generalized epilepsy (use broad-spectrum, avoid sodium-channel blockers) or a focal epilepsy? Get that right and most decisions follow.

🚩 The one mistake to avoid — sodium-channel blockers in generalized epilepsy

Carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, eslicarbazepine, phenytoin, and gabapentin/pregabalin can aggravate absence and myoclonic seizures (and precipitate absence/myoclonic status). So if you can't tell focal from generalized, start a broad-spectrum agent (levetiracetam, lamotrigine, or valproate) — never a narrow sodium-channel blocker into an undifferentiated or generalized picture. (Lacosamide is sometimes used for generalized tonic-clonic seizures, but its role in IGE is debated — don't reach for it as a default there.)

docs.title: How syndromes are organized (ILAE 2022)
docs.text: Three diagnostic levels — seizure type → epilepsy type (focal / generalized / combined / unknown) → epilepsy syndrome. The 2022 ILAE grouping: neonatal/infantile, childhood-onset, variable age of onset, and the idiopathic (genetic) generalized epilepsies. For adult practice, the two buckets that matter most are the genetic generalized epilepsies and the focal epilepsies.

docs.title: Genetic (idiopathic) generalized epilepsies — the big adult-relevant group
docs.text:
The four IGEs — recognize them, and treat them broad-spectrum

Normal development/exam, generalized spike-wave on EEG, often photosensitive, triggered by sleep deprivation and alcohol. First-line = valproate (most effective) or levetiracetam/lamotrigine — with valproate avoided in childbearing potential (→ lamotrigine/levetiracetam).

  • Juvenile myoclonic epilepsy (JME) — the classic adult IGE: early-morning myoclonic jerks (dropping the coffee cup), generalized tonic-clonic seizures, and often absence; onset in the teens; usually lifelong (needs long-term therapy). Counsel hard on sleep and alcohol. In childbearing potential, levetiracetam is the preferred non-valproate first-line — head-to- head it beat lamotrigine specifically in JME (treatment-failure HR ~0.47), and lamotrigine worsened myoclonus in a subset. Valproate is the most effective overall but off the table in anyone who could become pregnant.
  • Juvenile absence epilepsy (JAE) — absence + GTC, onset around puberty; lifelong.
  • Childhood absence epilepsy (CAE) — frequent brief absences, onset 4–10 y; often remits. Ethosuximide is first-line for absence-only (valproate/lamotrigine if GTC coexists).
  • Epilepsy with generalized tonic-clonic seizures alone (GTCA).
  • Eyelid myoclonia with absences (Jeavons syndrome) — a distinct childhood-onset generalized epilepsy: eyelid myoclonia, eye-closure and photic sensitivity; often drug-resistant and lifelong (valproate/levetiracetam; avoid Na-channel blockers).

When first-line monotherapy fails in a woman with IGE

Both substituting another monotherapy and adding on are reasonable — but the best- supported non-valproate dual therapy is levetiracetam + lamotrigine (most effective combination in a large series of women aged 10–50). Reach for that pairing before considering valproate under a pregnancy-prevention framework.

docs.title: Focal epilepsies
docs.text:
Mesial temporal lobe epilepsy with hippocampal sclerosis (MTLE-HS) — the surgically-remediable adult syndrome

A formal ILAE 2022 syndrome and the most common focal epilepsy in adults (often a history of childhood febrile seizures).

  • Semiology: an aura (rising epigastric sensation, déjà vu, fear, or an olfactory/gustatory hallucination) → impaired awareness with automatisms (lip-smacking, hand-fumbling) → postictal confusion.
  • Frequently drug-resistant — and when it is, surgery is highly effective (often curative); refer early (2022 ILAE surgical consensus — see the seizure page); MRI (hippocampal atrophy/T2 signal) + video-EEG.
    • Anterior temporal lobectomy (ATL) — the reference operation (~⅔ seizure-free at 2 y).
    • Laser interstitial thermal therapy (LITT) — minimally invasive alternative (2-yr seizure-freedom ~53–62%, somewhat below ATL but far lower complication rates and shorter stay) — favor it for dominant-hemisphere MTLE (better naming/memory preservation) or when morbidity is the priority. First-line for hypothalamic hamartoma.
  • Medically: treat as focal — lamotrigine, levetiracetam, lacosamide, or oxcarbazepine/carbamazepine.

Variable-age-of-onset focal syndromes (ILAE 2022) — recognize these in adults

  • Sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy (SHE) — formerly nocturnal frontal lobe epilepsy: clusters of brief, stereotyped hyperkinetic/dystonic seizures out of sleep, often misdiagnosed as a parasomnia/night terrors. Genetic (CHRNA4, DEPDC5) or structural (focal cortical dysplasia). Carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine are often effective; video-EEG (and DEPDC5 raises a surgical-lesion question).
  • Epilepsy with auditory features (ADLTE / LGI1) — focal seizures with auditory auras (buzzing, ringing); autosomal-dominant LGI1; generally drug-responsive. (Don't confuse the genetic LGI1 epilepsy with acute anti-LGI1 autoimmune encephalitis.)
  • Reading epilepsy — reflex seizures (jaw/perioral myoclonus) triggered by reading.

Self-limited focal epilepsies of childhood (e.g., centrotemporal spikes / "rolandic")

Normal children, nocturnal focal seizures (hemifacial twitching, drooling, speech arrest) with centrotemporal spikes; usually outgrown by adolescence — often needs no or minimal treatment. ILAE dropped "benign" → "self-limited" for a reason: ~1–8% evolve into epileptic encephalopathy with spike-wave activation in sleep (EE-SWAS) with real cognitive cost — so watch for cognitive/behavioral regression and get a sleep EEG if it appears.

docs.title: Developmental & epileptic encephalopathies (DEE)
docs.text:
Mostly pediatric, but these patients transition into adult care, so recognize them:

  • Lennox-Gastaut syndrome — multiple seizure types (esp. tonic/atonic → drop attacks), cognitive impairment, slow spike-wave; drug-resistant. Broad-spectrum + valproate, lamotrigine, rufinamide, clobazam, cannabidiol, fenfluramine (now FDA-approved for LGS as well as Dravet); VNS/corpus callosotomy for drops.
  • Dravet syndrome (SCN1A) — infantile onset, fever/vaccination-triggered prolonged seizures. AVOID sodium-channel blockers — carbamazepine, oxcarbazepine, phenytoin, AND lamotrigine (it is a sodium-channel blocker) — they worsen it. Base: valproate + clobazam; the three evidence-based add-ons are fenfluramine, stiripentol, and cannabidiol (network meta-analyses favor fenfluramine and stiripentol over cannabidiol for seizure response). Precision therapies (antisense oligonucleotides, gene therapy) are in trials.
  • West syndrome / infantile spasms — spasms + hypsarrhythmia + developmental regression; treat urgently (ACTH/steroids; vigabatrin, esp. tuberous sclerosis).
  • NORSE / FIRES — new-onset refractory status epilepticus (FIRES = its febrile-triggered form, a formal ILAE 2022 DEE): a previously healthy person → febrile illness → explosive refractory status → drug-resistant epilepsy. Follow the 2022 international consensus: aggressive anesthetic control + an early autoimmune/cytokine work-up and empiric immunotherapy — anakinra (IL-1) and tocilizumab (IL-6) achieve seizure control in a majority in series (guided by cytokine profiling); ketogenic diet. Outcomes remain poor — get epileptology/neuro-ICU involved early.

docs.title: Two syndrome-specific rules that catch people
docs.text:
Get the syndrome right or the drug backfires

  • Generalized (IGE) or myoclonic/absence → broad-spectrum only (levetiracetam/valproate/ lamotrigine); never carbamazepine/oxcarbazepine/eslicarbazepine/phenytoin/gabapentin.
  • Dravet (SCN1A) → avoid sodium-channel blockers, including lamotrigine.
  • Childbearing potential with IGE → levetiracetam or lamotrigine, not valproate — and valproate in anyone who could become pregnant requires a formal pregnancy-prevention programme / documented counseling (EMA/FDA).
  • Drug-resistant = failure of 2 appropriately chosen, tolerated ASMs (ILAE/Kwan 2010) → refer for surgical evaluation early (MTLE is often curable; ATL or LITT).

Two things that are easy to skip

  • Genetic testing is now part of syndromic diagnosis — send it for DEEs, early-onset epilepsy, drug-resistant epilepsy, or a suggestive family history (a specific gene, e.g., SCN1A/KCNQ2/DEPDC5, increasingly steers treatment — and precision therapies are emerging).
  • Screen for depression and anxiety — they affect ~20–30% of adults with epilepsy and drive quality of life and adherence more than seizure count alone.

For drug details, dosing, and the full comparison, see the antiseizure-medications page; for status epilepticus and the first-seizure work-up, the seizure page.

Military medicine — epilepsy syndromes

A syndromic diagnosis of epilepsy (recurrent unprovoked seizures) is handled as on the seizure page: AR 40-501 para 3-31i / DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.c → generally below retention → DES; a history of epilepsy is disqualifying for accession and for flying duty. A self-limited childhood syndrome that fully remitted (off medication, seizure free) is the scenario most likely to support accession waiver / retention — document the remission.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• Syndrome drives the drug. The critical adult split is genetic generalized epilepsy (JME, JAE, CAE, GTCA, Jeavons — broad-spectrum; avoid Na-channel blockers incl. eslicarbazepine) vs focal epilepsy.
  • JME is lifelong — in childbearing potential, levetiracetam is the preferred non-valproate first-line (beat lamotrigine head-to-head; lamotrigine can worsen myoclonus); levetiracetam + lamotrigine is the best non-valproate combination when monotherapy fails.
  • Know the variable-age focal syndromes — sleep-related hypermotor epilepsy (misdiagnosed as parasomnia) and LGI1 auditory epilepsy; MTLE-HS is now a formal syndrome and the surgically-remediable one (ATL or LITT — refer early).
  • Dravet (SCN1A): sodium-channel blockers — including lamotrigine — make it worse; the evidence-based add-ons are fenfluramine, stiripentol, cannabidiol (fenfluramine now covers LGS too). NORSE/FIRES needs early immunotherapy (anakinra/tocilizumab).
  • Self-limited childhood focal epilepsy is usually outgrown — but watch for evolution to EE-SWAS. Send genetic testing in DEE/early-onset/drug-resistant epilepsy, and screen for depression/anxiety.

Key sources: ILAE 2022 syndrome papers (Hirsch, IGE; Specchio, childhood; Riney, variable-age); ILAE 2017 classification; ILAE 2022 surgical-referral consensus; Cerulli Irelli et al. (LEV vs LTG in JME, JAMA Neurol 2023; second-line combos, JAMA Netw Open 2025); Lattanzi et al. (Dravet network meta-analysis, Drugs 2023); Wickstrom et al. (NORSE/FIRES consensus, Epilepsia 2022); Yardi et al. (epilepsy surgery/LITT, Lancet Neurol 2026); Asadi-Pooya et al. (Adult Epilepsy, Lancet 2023).

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Hirsch E, et al. ILAE Idiopathic Generalized Epilepsy Syndromes. Epilepsia. 2022; Specchio N, et al. Childhood-onset syndromes. Epilepsia. 2022; Riney K, et al. Syndromes with onset at a variable age (Epilepsia 2022) — SHE, MTLE-HS, LGI1.
  2. Fisher RS, et al. ILAE 2017 operational classification; Kwan P, et al. ILAE drug-resistant epilepsy definition (2010).
  3. IGE treatment: Cerulli Irelli E, et al. Levetiracetam vs lamotrigine in female IGE (JME) (JAMA Neurol 2023) and Second-line ASMs for women 10–50 with IGE (JAMA Netw Open 2025).
  4. Dravet / NORSE / surgery: Lattanzi S, et al. Dravet network meta-analysis (Drugs 2023); Wickstrom R, et al. NORSE/FIRES international consensus (Epilepsia 2022); Yardi R, et al. Epilepsy surgery incl. LITT (Lancet Neurol 2026); Asadi-Pooya AA, et al. Adult Epilepsy (Lancet 2023).
  5. Retention: AR 40-501 para 3-31i, DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.c (accession Vol 1) — verify against the source.

Verified July 2026: the syndrome framework follows the ILAE 2022 position papers (IGEs; variable- age syndromes incl. SHE, MTLE-HS, LGI1; self-limited focal epilepsies — note EE-SWAS evolution; DEEs incl. FIRES) and ILAE 2017 classification. Newer points — levetiracetam > lamotrigine in JME and the LEV+LTG second-line combo, fenfluramine/stiripentol/cannabidiol for Dravet (lamotrigine contraindicated), NORSE/FIRES immunotherapy (anakinra/tocilizumab), LITT vs ATL for MTLE, and genetic-testing/psychiatric-screening guidance — were supplied by expert review with citations; confirm before quoting. Retention citations should be confirmed against AR 40-501 / DoDI 6130.03 before counseling.

docs.title: Acute ischemic stroke
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: sudden focal neurologic deficit from arterial occlusion — "time is brain." Every minute of an untreated large-vessel occlusion loses ~1.9 million neurons (Saver's oft-cited MCA-territory estimate). Your job on the code is to move fast, in order: last-known-well → glucose → CT → blood pressure → reperfusion decision.

The ER consult usually sounds like: "Older patient, weak on one side, started a couple hours ago — they're in the scanner now, no NIHSS yet, no labs." Your value is imposing the checklist on the chaos. Be fast and a little abrupt; it saves brain.

🚩 The clock is the whole game — establish these first

  • Last known well (LKW), not "time found." The entire reperfusion algorithm keys off this number. Nail it from the patient, family, EMS, or phone records.
  • Check a glucose immediately — hypo/hyperglycemia is the great stroke mimic. It is the only mandatory lab before thrombolysis.
  • Get the patient to non-contrast CT now (+ CTA head/neck if no contraindication) — you are excluding hemorrhage and finding a large-vessel occlusion.
  • Don't over-treat blood pressure. In a possible reperfusion candidate the target is <185/110; otherwise leave it alone (permissive hypertension) unless >220/120.
  • Mimics: hypoglycemia, seizure/Todd's, complex migraine, Bell's palsy, functional, toxic-metabolic. But a focal deficit is a stroke until proven otherwise — don't stall the code chasing a mimic.

docs.title: Acute stroke checklist — what I want done, in order
docs.text:
Code stroke — the sequence

  1. LKW + glucose — the two numbers that gate everything.
  2. Focused exam + NIHSS (compact scale below). Note anticoagulant/antiplatelet use.
  3. STAT non-contrast CT head; CTA head & neck (skip CTA only if true contrast contraindication — most kidneys tolerate it, don't wait on a creatinine in a code).
  4. Labs (don't wait on them except glucose): CBC/platelets, PT/INR/aPTT, BMP, troponin, type & screen, hCG if applicable; ECG + telemetry.
  5. Two large-bore IVs. Manage BP to <185/110 only if pursuing lytic (labetalol 10–20 mg IV or nicardipine gtt 5–15 mg/h).
  6. Reperfusion decision — thrombolysis? thrombectomy? both? (below). Consent in parallel, don't let it delay the bolus.
  7. Weigh the patient (dosing is weight-based; don't guess high).
  8. If LVO: activate neuro-interventional / arrange transfer immediately — in parallel with giving lytic ("drip and ship").

Time goalposts (AHA/ASA Target: Stroke)

Beat these clocks — every step is a race:

Milestone Goal Door → stroke team / physician ≤10–15 min Door → CT initiated ≤25 min Door → CT read ≤45 min Door → needle (thrombolysis) ≤60 min (standard); ≤45 min aspirational Door → arterial puncture (EVT) ≤90 min (direct arrivals) Door-in–door-out (transfer for EVT) ≤60 min

The published guideline standard is door-to-needle ≤60 min, with ≤45 min aspirational; some centers push a ≤30-min institutional goal, but that is not the guideline benchmark.

docs.title: Compact NIHSS (rapid field version)
docs.text:
A fast, practical aid — not a substitute for the formal 15-item NIHSS you'll document.

Domain Quick check 1a–1c LOC Alert / drowsy / stupor; month + age; blink eyes + squeeze hand 2 Gaze Eyes midline, partial gaze palsy, or forced deviation 3 Visual fields Full / partial / complete / bilateral hemianopia 4 Facial palsy Symmetric / minor / partial / complete 5–6 Motor arm/leg Arm at 90° (10 s), leg at 30° (5 s): drift → falls → no movement, each limb 7 Ataxia Finger-nose, heel-shin — out of proportion to weakness 8 Sensory Pinprick: normal / mild / severe loss 9 Language Aphasia: none / mild / severe / mute-global 10 Dysarthria Normal / slurred / unintelligible 11 Extinction/neglect None / one modality / profound

NIHSS ≤3 = "minor"; ≥6 with cortical signs (gaze, aphasia, neglect) predicts a large-vessel occlusion — get the CTA and call for thrombectomy.

docs.title: Imaging & the reperfusion fork
docs.text:
• Non-contrast CT: rules out hemorrhage; look for early ischemic change / ASPECTS.
  • CTA head & neck: finds the LVO (ICA, M1/M2, basilar) and any tandem/dissection.
  • CT perfusion (or MRI DWI/FLAIR): needed for extended-window decisions (late thrombolysis and 6–24 h thrombectomy) — quantifies core vs. salvageable penumbra.

Two independent decisions, made in parallel — a patient can get both:

docs.title: Reperfusion — Step 1: IV thrombolysis
docs.text:
IV thrombolysis — offer within 4.5 h of LKW (Class 1)

Two agents, co-equal Class 1 within 4.5 h (2026 AHA/ASA); tenecteplase is the practical default:

  • Tenecteplase (TNK) 0.25 mg/kg IV, single bolus (max 25 mg) — one push, no infusion, no line to babysit; easier for transfers. Preferred where available.
  • Alteplase (tPA) 0.9 mg/kg IV (max 90 mg) — 10% as a bolus over 1 min, remainder over 60 min.

Label vs. guideline — a 3-hour nuance for tenecteplase

The FDA approved TNKase (tenecteplase) for AIS on 3 Mar 2025. The indication itself is broad — "acute ischemic stroke in adults," no time window stated — but the label's Recommended Dosage section says to "initiate treatment as soon as possible and within 3 hours after onset." So using TNK in the 3–4.5 h window is guideline-supported (2026 AHA/ASA, Class 1) but beyond the labeled dosing timing — fine to do, just know the distinction and document it. (Alteplase is similar: ≤3 h labeled, 3–4.5 h by guideline/ evidence.) The agents are clinically co-equal across 4.5 h; the labeling nuance is a timing footnote, not a reason to prefer one drug.

Before you push, confirm the four: "Do I have the CT, the glucose, the blood pressure (<185/110), and the ECG?" Weight-based dose — weigh, don't eyeball.

Absolute contraindications (do NOT give lytic)

  • Hemorrhage on CT, or extensive early infarct >⅓ MCA territory.
  • BP that won't come below 185/110 despite treatment.
  • Glucose <50 and deficit resolves with correction.
  • Platelets <100k, INR >1.7, elevated aPTT / therapeutic heparin ≤48 h, or a DOAC ≤48 h (unless specific reversal/normal assay).
  • Ischemic stroke, intracranial/spinal surgery, or serious head trauma within 3 months; known intracranial neoplasm (intra-axial), AVM, or aneurysm at risk; active internal bleeding; infective endocarditis; suspected aortic dissection.

Historically 'absolute,' now RELATIVE (weigh case-by-case — the 2026 guideline liberalized these)

Rapidly-improving or mild-but-disabling deficits, seizure at onset (if deficit is from the stroke, not the postictal state), major surgery <14 d, GI/GU bleed <21 d, recent MI, pregnancy/early postpartum, recent arterial puncture. Don't reflexively exclude — discuss with stroke/neuro.

The "million-dollar presentation" (say this to consent/activate): "__-year-old with [risk factors] presents with acute [deficit/syndrome], LKW [time]. BP __, NIHSS __, glucose __, INR __, platelets __. CT shows [no bleed], CTA shows [occlusion/patent]. No lytic contraindication."

Consent, in plain language (benefit is at 90 days, not at the bedside)

Thrombolysis is the best chance of returning to independence after stroke — roughly a ~10% absolute increase in good outcome at 3 months. The risk is bleeding: symptomatic intracranial hemorrhage — historically ~6% with alteplase (NINDS definition) vs ~1% without, though contemporary series (and the stricter ECASS-III definition) run ~2–3%, including with tenecteplase. Part of that "6% → 2–3%" gap is definitional, not purely the drug. Overall mortality is not increased. The benefit is not seen immediately — it shows up over the following weeks.

Extended-window thrombolysis (perfusion-guided) — the window now stretches to 24 h

When >4.5 h or wake-up/unknown onset with a favorable perfusion or DWI–FLAIR mismatch. The evidence is now tenecteplase-based and reaches 24 h, including non-LVO patients:

  • Wake-up / unknown onset, ≤~9 h: DWI–FLAIR mismatch (WAKE-UP) or perfusion mismatch (EXTEND, 4.5–9 h from LKW) → lytic is reasonable.
  • 4.5–24 h, LVO without access to thrombectomy — TNK: TRACE-III (NEJM 2024) showed benefit for tenecteplase with perfusion mismatch (mRS 0–1: 33% vs 24%). This is the "thrombectomy isn't available/transfer is too far" scenario.
  • 4.5–24 h, non-LVO with perfusion mismatch — TNK: OPTION (JAMA 2026) showed benefit (mRS 0–1: 44% vs 34%, NNT 11) but with more sICH (2.8% vs 0%).
  • TNK-PLUS (JAMA 2026): tenecteplase bridging before EVT in the 4.5–24 h window.
  • Caveat: extended-window lysis raises sICH risk — reserve it for genuine mismatch and counsel accordingly. And if thrombectomy is immediately available for an LVO, do that — TIMELESS was negative for TNK when EVT was on hand (lysis didn't add benefit).

docs.title: Reperfusion — Step 2: endovascular thrombectomy (LVO)
docs.text:
Mechanical thrombectomy — for large-vessel occlusion; give lytic too if eligible

Thrombolysis and thrombectomy are not either/or — a lytic-eligible LVO patient gets both (don't wait to see if the lytic works). Activate neuro-IR / transfer the moment CTA shows an LVO.

  • Anterior LVO (ICA, M1 ± proximal M2), 0–6 h: thrombectomy, Class I (standard-window trials enrolled ASPECTS ≥6).
  • 6–16 h: Class I if DAWN or DEFUSE-3 clinical–imaging mismatch met (perfusion/DWI).
  • 16–24 h: Class IIa by DAWN criteria.
  • Large-core infarct — thrombectomy still helps, down to ASPECTS 3: the large-core trials (SELECT2, ANGEL-ASPECT, TENSION [to 12 h], TESLA) specifically enrolled ASPECTS 3–5 (some 0–5), and benefit held down to ASPECTS 3. The ATLAS individual-patient meta-analysis (Lancet 2026, 6 RCTs) is the definitive pooled evidence: NNT 4.2 for ≥1-point mRS shift, NNT 8.6 for functional independence, and reduced mortality (NNT 14.6). Don't write off a low-ASPECTS patient — get neuro-IR involved.
  • Basilar-artery occlusion: thrombectomy up to ~12 h (ATTENTION) and ~24 h (BAOCHE) (both 2023) — posterior strokes are devastating and under-treated; don't anchor on a low NIHSS.

docs.title: After reperfusion — the post-lytic order set
docs.text:
Post-thrombolysis orders (first 24 h)

  • ICU/stroke-unit monitoring ≥24 h. Neuro checks + BP q15 min ×2 h → q30 min ×6 h → q1 h to complete 24 h.
  • Keep BP <180/105 ×24 h (labetalol pushes ± nicardipine gtt).
  • Hold all antithrombotics ×24 h — no aspirin/clopidogrel/heparin/prophylactic anticoagulation.
  • Avoid for 24 h: central lines, arterial sticks, NG tube, urinary catheter, LP (or any non-compressible puncture).
  • A statin is fine to start (high-intensity — see secondary prevention).
  • Repeat non-contrast CT at 24 h before starting antithrombotics. Any acute decline → STAT CT (think symptomatic hemorrhage).

Symptomatic ICH after thrombolysis — stop and reverse

Sudden decline, new headache, vomiting, or BP spike within 24 h:

  1. STOP the lytic infusion. STAT non-contrast CT.
  2. Labs: CBC/platelets, PT/INR/aPTT, fibrinogen, type & cross; repeat q2–4 h until stable.
  3. Cryoprecipitate 10 units IV (replaces fibrinogen — first-line); recheck fibrinogen, **goal

    150 mg/dL**, repeat as needed.

  4. Tranexamic acid 1 g IV (or aminocaproic acid) as antifibrinolytic.
  5. Platelets if thrombocytopenic; correct any coagulopathy.
  6. Neurosurgery for possible decompression/EVD; treat elevated ICP; frequent neuro checks.

docs.title: Blood pressure — the rules change by pathway
docs.text:
Situation Target How Before thrombolysis <185/110 Labetalol 10–20 mg IV, or nicardipine 5–15 mg/h gtt After thrombolysis (24 h) <180/105 Same; nicardipine/clevidipine gtt if labile Post-thrombectomy, successful reperfusion Individualized, usually ≤180; do NOT drive SBP <140 Over-lowering a reperfused brain harms — see warning No reperfusion (permissive HTN) Treat only if >220/120, then lower ~15% Restart home meds ~24–48 h once stable

Don't over-lower after a successful thrombectomy

The 2025 ACC/AHA hypertension guideline carries a Class 3: Harm recommendation against lowering SBP <140 mmHg in the first 24–72 h after successful EVT reperfusion — intensive lowering worsened outcomes. Keep it controlled (≤180) but don't chase a low number. For an incompletely reperfused vessel, avoid over-lowering to preserve collateral flow.

docs.title: Not a reperfusion candidate? Start antiplatelet therapy
docs.text:
Antiplatelet — after 24 h if lysed, or now if not lysed (and CT shows no bleed)

  • Aspirin 160–325 mg within 24–48 h (rectal/NG if dysphagic) — the one proven early drug for everyone not getting a lytic.
  • Minor stroke (NIHSS ≤3) or high-risk TIA (ABCD² ≥4): short-course DAPT. Start aspirin + clopidogrel (clopidogrel 300–600 mg load → 75 mg/day; ASA 81 mg) for 21 days, then single-agent — the benefit is concentrated in the first 21 days (CHANCE/POINT); longer DAPT just adds bleeding.
    • Ticagrelor + aspirin (THALES) is an alternative, useful with CYP2C19 loss-of-function (clopidogrel non-responders).

docs.title: Secondary prevention — start it before discharge
docs.text:
Secondary prevention is owned on this admission and continued in clinic. The full mechanism-by-mechanism playbook lives on the Stroke prevention page — the essentials to start in-hospital:

Start before discharge (every patient)

  • High-intensity statin — atorvastatin 80 mg (or rosuvastatin 20–40); target LDL <70 (add ezetimibe ± PCSK9). Start in hospital.
  • Antithrombotic matched to mechanism — single antiplatelet for most; 21-day DAPT for minor stroke/high-risk TIA (see above); DOAC for AF, started early (≤4 days, CATALYST); never reflexively combine antiplatelet + anticoagulant.
  • BP toward <130/80 once stable; diabetes (GLP-1/SGLT2i), smoking cessation, diet/ exercise, weight.
  • Determine the mechanism (cardioembolic / large-artery / small-vessel / ESUS) — it drives carotid revascularization, PFO closure <60, and prolonged rhythm monitoring. → full detail on the prevention page.
  • Inpatient basics: dysphagia screen before anything PO, DVT prophylaxis, early mobilization.

docs.title: TIA — a treated emergency, not "a pass"
docs.text: A TIA is a transient deficit without infarction but with a high, front-loaded early stroke risk — same-day work-up and immediately-started prevention. Full detail (ABCD², the work-up order set, 21-day DAPT, mechanism-directed therapy) is on the dedicated TIA page. In brief: ABCD²-stratify → MRI + vessel imaging + rhythm monitoring today → start antiplatelet/DAPT + statin now → treat the mechanism. Don't send a real TIA home without a plan.

docs.title: Supportive care & admission
docs.text:
Stroke-unit admission bundle

  • Dysphagia screen before any PO (meds, food, water) — aspiration is a top early killer.
  • Glucose: avoid hypoglycemia and treat hyperglycemia — the 2026 guideline doesn't set a stroke-specific number; the general inpatient 140–180 target is reasonable (and SHINE showed no benefit from tight <110 control). Treat fever (worsens outcome); normoxia (O₂ only if <94%).
  • DVT prophylaxis — intermittent pneumatic compression immediately; pharmacologic prophylaxis once bleeding risk allows (and ≥24 h post-lytic).
  • Early mobilization + therapy (PT/OT/SLP); swallow eval; secondary-prevention teaching.
  • Telemetry ≥24–48 h hunting for AF.

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
Stroke-unit or ICU (all post-lytic/post-thrombectomy). Transfer any LVO to a thrombectomy-capable center emergently. Admit TIAs or observe with an expedited same-day work-up — the early recurrence risk is real.

Military medicine — stroke / cerebrovascular disease

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03, DoDI 1332.18, the JTS Clinical Practice Guideline "Management of Stroke and Cerebrovascular Emergencies in the Deployed Setting" (03 Jul 2024), and service aeromedical policy before counseling.

  • Deployed setting: stroke in theater is a telemedicine + evacuation problem — thrombolysis and thrombectomy are rarely available forward. Follow the JTS deployed-stroke CPG: rapid recognition, glucose, CT if available, BP management, and urgent MEDEVAC to a capability.
  • Young-stroke work-up matters more here: service members skew young — pursue dissection (blast/blunt/neck trauma), PFO, hypercoagulable states, endocarditis, and drug use; the etiology drives both treatment and the board.
  • Deployability: an acute stroke/TIA is non-deployable during work-up and recovery; residual deficit or a high-risk unfixed source is deployment-limiting.
  • Profile (DA Form 3349): temporary during recovery; permanent for residual neuro/cognitive deficit or seizure.
  • Retention / MEB: DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2, para 5.26.b lists cerebrovascular conditions — (3) stroke and (6) recurrent TIA "unless the underlying etiology is identified and definitively treated" — as below retention standards when they persist despite treatment and impair duty (gating clause 5.26.a). The Army implements this at AR 40-501 para 3-31f (stroke, when residuals affect performance). So: define the etiology, treat it, and document NIHSS/mRS, deficits, and the secondary-prevention plan → DES (MEB/PEB) if residuals impair duty. A TIA with a fully identified and definitively treated cause can retain — the etiology work-up is what the standard turns on.
  • Aeromedical / special duty: a history of stroke/TIA is disqualifying for flying duty, and most secondary-prevention regimens (anticoagulation especially) are independently waiver-dependent. Confirm the exact standard for the class involved.

TRICARE — secondary-prevention prescribing

The acute drugs (tenecteplase, alteplase, thrombectomy) are inpatient — not formulary-gated. For outpatient secondary prevention, high-intensity statins, aspirin, and clopidogrel are generic/formulary; DOACs are covered (a preferred DOAC exists on the Uniform Formulary — verify the current preferred agent and any PA at the TRICARE Formulary Search, as DoD P&T revises tiers).

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• The whole field is time + vessel. Two parallel decisions: thrombolysis (drug, ≤4.5 h or perfusion-selected later) and thrombectomy (large-vessel occlusion, out to 24 h). Eligible patients get both.
  • Tenecteplase has arrived (with a label caveat). The 2026 AHA/ASA guideline makes TNK 0.25 mg/kg and alteplase 0.9 mg/kg co-equal Class 1 within 4.5 h; TNK's single bolus is operationally simpler. Note the FDA label approves TNK only to 3 h — 3–4.5 h is guideline-based but off-label. Reteplase may be next: RAISE (2025) found reteplase (two 18 mg boluses) superior to alteplase for excellent 90-day outcome (79.5% vs 70.4%) without more sICH — not yet in guidelines, but a signal that the thrombolytic field is still moving.
  • Extended-window lysis now reaches 24 h — and it's tenecteplase. Beyond WAKE-UP/EXTEND (~9 h), TRACE-III (LVO without EVT access) and OPTION (non-LVO with mismatch) show TNK benefit out to 24 h with perfusion selection — at the cost of more sICH (OPTION 2.8% vs 0%). TNK-PLUS studies pre-EVT bridging. But if EVT is immediately available, do EVT — TIMELESS was negative for adding TNK.
  • Late thrombectomy windows opened up. DAWN and DEFUSE-3 established 6–24 h thrombectomy with mismatch; SELECT2, ANGEL-ASPECT, TENSION (≤12 h), TESLA extended it to large-core infarcts (ASPECTS 3–5, benefit down to 3); the ATLAS meta-analysis (Lancet 2026) pooled it (NNT 4.2 mRS shift, 8.6 independence, 14.6 mortality). ATTENTION/BAOCHE (2023) cover basilar occlusion.
  • Short DAPT, not long. For minor stroke / high-risk TIA, 21 days of aspirin+clopidogrel (CHANCE/POINT) beats aspirin alone; beyond 21 days you only add bleeding. THALES offers ticagrelor+ASA.
  • Anticoagulate AF early — it reduces recurrence. CATALYST (Lancet 2025; pooling ELAN/OPTIMAS/ TIMING) shows early DOAC ≤4 days lowers recurrent ischemic stroke/sICH at 30 days with no excess sICH — early is now preferred, not just tolerated. The 1-3-6-12 wait is out.
  • Post-EVT, don't over-lower BP. The 2025 ACC/AHA hypertension guideline flags SBP <140 in the first 24–72 h after successful reperfusion as Class 3: Harm.
  • Chase LDL <70 with a high-intensity statin (± ezetimibe/PCSK9); treat the mechanism, not just "a stroke."

Key sources: 2026 AHA/ASA acute ischemic stroke guideline & Target: Stroke time goals; DAWN; DEFUSE-3; SELECT2; ANGEL-ASPECT; TENSION; TESLA; ATLAS (meta-analysis); ATTENTION; BAOCHE; WAKE-UP; EXTEND; TRACE-III; OPTION; TNK-PLUS; TIMELESS; RAISE (reteplase); CHANCE; POINT; THALES; ELAN; OPTIMAS; TIMING; CATALYST; 2025 ACC/AHA hypertension guideline; SPARCL/TST (lipids).

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. 2026 Guideline for the Early Management of Patients With Acute Ischemic Stroke: AHA/ASA. Stroke. 2026.
  2. Nogueira RG, et al. Thrombectomy 6–24 h after stroke with mismatch (DAWN). NEJM. 2018; Albers GW, et al. DEFUSE-3. NEJM. 2018.
  3. Large-core thrombectomy: SELECT2, ANGEL-ASPECT (2023), TENSION (≤12 h), TESLA — ASPECTS 3–5; pooled in the ATLAS individual-patient meta-analysis (Lancet 2026; NNT 4.2 mRS shift, 8.6 independence, 14.6 mortality). Basilar: ATTENTION and BAOCHE (both 2023).
  4. Extended-window tenecteplase: TRACE-III (NEJM 2024, LVO 4.5–24 h without EVT); OPTION (JAMA 2026, non-LVO mismatch 4.5–24 h); TNK-PLUS (JAMA 2026, pre-EVT bridging); TIMELESS (negative when EVT immediately available). Reteplase: RAISE (2025) — superior to alteplase for 90-day excellent outcome.
  5. Wang Y, et al. CHANCE; Johnston SC, et al. POINT — 21-day DAPT in minor stroke/high-risk TIA; THALES — ticagrelor+aspirin.
  6. Fischer U, et al. Early vs later anticoagulation for stroke with AF (ELAN). NEJM. 2023; OPTIMAS; TIMING; and the CATALYST individual-patient meta-analysis (Lancet. 2025) — early DOAC ≤4 days reduces recurrence without excess sICH.
  7. 2025 ACC/AHA hypertension guideline — Class 3: Harm against SBP <140 mmHg 24–72 h after successful EVT reperfusion.
  8. Source scutbook: STROKE (M. Holtkamp) — acute checklist, "million-dollar presentation," consent framing, and post-lytic order set (modernized to the 2026 guideline).
  9. JTS Clinical Practice Guideline: Management of Stroke and Cerebrovascular Emergencies in the Deployed Setting (03 Jul 2024).

Verified July 2026: dual thrombolytic option (tenecteplase 0.25 mg/kg or alteplase 0.9 mg/kg, both Class 1 ≤4.5 h) follows the 2026 AHA/ASA guideline. TNKase gained its FDA AIS indication on 3 Mar 2025 — the indication states no time window, but the label's Recommended Dosage section says to initiate within 3 h, so 3–4.5 h use is guideline-based (Class 1) yet beyond labeled timing (confirmed against the TNKase prescribing information). Extended-window lysis now reaches 24 h and is tenecteplase-based (TRACE-III, OPTION, TNK-PLUS) with increased sICH; TIMELESS was negative when EVT was available; RAISE (reteplase) is emerging, not yet guideline-incorporated. The precise RAISE outcome figures and the ATLAS meta-analysis NNTs were supplied by expert review and are not yet independently re-verified here — confirm before final sign-off. Thrombectomy windows (0–6 h Class I; 6–16 h DAWN/DEFUSE-3 Class I; 16–24 h DAWN Class IIa; large-core SELECT2/ANGEL-ASPECT/TENSION/TESLA down to ASPECTS 3, pooled in ATLAS; basilar ATTENTION/BAOCHE, both 2023) are current. BP targets (<185/110 pre-lytic, <180/105 post-lytic, permissive <220/120; no SBP <140 within 24–72 h of successful EVT per 2025 ACC/AHA) and time goals (door-to-CT ≤25 min, door-to-needle ≤60/≤45 min per Target: Stroke) are current; 21-day DAPT (CHANCE/POINT) and early DOAC ≤4 days (CATALYST reduces recurrence) are correctly characterized. Retention citation confirmed against the source document: DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b (cerebrovascular — stroke .b(3), recurrent TIA .b(6)), gated by 5.26.a, implemented at AR 40-501 para 3-31f. The TRICARE preferred DOAC was not pinned to a citable source — verify before counseling. The deployed-stroke framing follows the JTS 2024 CPG.

docs.title: Carotid artery disease
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: atherosclerotic stenosis of the extracranial internal carotid — a fixable source of stroke. The whole decision turns on two questions: is it symptomatic, and how tight? — because symptomatic high-grade stenosis is a revascularize-within-2-weeks emergency, while asymptomatic disease is increasingly a best-medical-therapy call.

🚩 Symptomatic carotid stenosis is time-critical

"Symptomatic" = an ipsilateral TIA, ischemic stroke, or amaurosis fugax (transient monocular blindness) in the last 6 months. These patients have a high early recurrence risk — revascularize within 2 weeks, the window where benefit is greatest. Don't let a "stable" carotid lesion sit while you arrange elective surgery.

docs.title: Grade the stenosis
docs.text:
• Screen/measure with carotid duplex ultrasound, confirm with CTA or MRA (NASCET method: narrowest lumen vs. the normal distal ICA).
  • Symptomatic categories: 70–99% (clear benefit), 50–69% (benefit in selected patients), <50% (medical therapy only). Asymptomatic: typically discussed at ≥70%.

Look beyond the stenosis number — plaque morphology and two easy-to-miss lesions

Stroke risk depends on stenosis severity and plaque morphology (ESVS/SVS 2022–23).

  • Vulnerable-plaque features — intraplaque hemorrhage (IPH), ulceration, a lipid-rich necrotic core on MRI vessel-wall imaging — independently raise recurrent-stroke risk (PARISK). A symptomatic low-grade (<50%) plaque with IPH/ulceration can be the culprit even though it doesn't meet a revascularization threshold — an evolving area; treat medically and consider vessel-wall imaging in "cryptogenic" ipsilateral events.
  • Carotid web — a shelf-like intimal projection at the carotid bulb; a cause of recurrent stroke in the young (notably younger women) that's easily missed on routine studies — look for it on CTA in an otherwise-cryptogenic anterior-circulation stroke; antithrombotic ± revascularization.
  • Near-occlusion — revascularization benefit is uncertain/reduced; often managed medically.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Is it symptomatic? (ipsilateral TIA/stroke/amaurosis in 6 months) — if yes, this is urgent.
  2. Grade the stenosis (duplex → CTA/MRA).
  3. Start best medical therapy for everyone (statin, antiplatelet, BP, risk factors) — now.
  4. Symptomatic 70–99% (or selected 50–69%): revascularize within 2 weeks — CEA first-line.
  5. Asymptomatic ≥70%: shared decision — intensive medical therapy vs. revascularization by risk / life expectancy / procedural risk.
  6. Choose the procedure (CEA vs CAS vs TCAR) by anatomy, age, and surgical risk.

docs.title: Best medical therapy — for everyone (symptomatic or not)
docs.text:
Start this in every carotid patient

  • High-intensity statin — atorvastatin 80 mg, target LDL <70 (add ezetimibe/PCSK9 as needed).
  • Antiplatelet — aspirin (or clopidogrel); short-course DAPT only in the acute minor-stroke/high-risk-TIA window (see prevention).
  • BP control toward <130/80, diabetes management, smoking cessation, diet/exercise.
  • This is the backbone — revascularization is added to it, never a substitute.

docs.title: Revascularize — who, when, and how
docs.text:
Symptomatic stenosis

  • 70–99% → revascularize, ideally within 2 weeks of the event (CEA first-line for standard-risk patients). Benefit falls off with delay.
  • 50–69% → revascularize in selected patients — greater benefit in men, older patients, hemispheric (vs retinal) events; individualize.
  • <50% → medical therapy only.
  • Timing caveat: avoid revascularizing a large acute infarct in the first ~48 h (hemorrhage risk) — coordinate with stroke neurology.

Asymptomatic stenosis (≥70%) — a shared decision

  • Intensive medical therapy is the default comparator and is often sufficient. Offer revascularization when peri-procedural stroke/death risk is <3% and life expectancy is >5 years, weighing plaque features and progression.
  • Evolving evidence: the CREST-2 program (reported 2025) tested revascularization added to intensive medical management in asymptomatic ≥70% stenosis — carotid stenting + IMM reduced stroke vs IMM alone, while endarterectomy added no significant benefit over IMM. Interpret as newly-reported and practice-shifting-but-not-yet-fully-digested — confirm against the published guideline update before quoting.

Procedure choice — CEA vs CAS vs TCAR

  • Carotid endarterectomy (CEA) — first-line for most; lower peri-procedural stroke than transfemoral stenting (especially age >70), at the cost of slightly more MI.
  • Transfemoral carotid artery stenting (CAS) — for high surgical risk / hostile neck / re-stenosis, or younger patients; higher peri-procedural stroke in the elderly.
  • Transcarotid artery revascularization (TCAR) — stenting via a small neck incision with flow reversal; lower stroke than transfemoral CAS in registries (RCT-level data still maturing); FDA-approved for standard-risk since 2022.

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
Symptomatic high-grade stenosis → urgent vascular surgery / neuro-interventional referral (target <2 weeks) with medical therapy started immediately. Asymptomatic → optimize medical therapy and a shared-decision revascularization discussion. All → the full secondary-prevention plan.

Military medicine — carotid disease

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03, and aeromedical policy.

  • Population skew: symptomatic atherosclerotic carotid disease is largely an older / retiree / veteran issue; in young active-duty stroke, think dissection first (dissection page).
  • Retention: carotid disease that causes stroke/TIA is a cerebrovascular condition under DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b (stroke b(3); vascular stenosis b(2); recurrent TIA b(6) unless etiology identified and definitively treated), Army AR 40-501 3-31f — a revascularized, asymptomatic carotid with no residual deficit may retain per specialty review.
  • Deployability / aeromedical: symptomatic disease and the peri-procedural period are deployment- and flight-status-limiting/waiver-dependent.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• Symptomatic 70–99% stenosis = revascularize within 2 weeks (CEA first-line; NASCET/ECST) — early benefit is large and time-sensitive.
  • 50–69% symptomatic benefits selected patients (men, older, hemispheric events); <50% is medical only.
  • Best medical therapy for everyone — high-intensity statin (LDL <70), antiplatelet, BP <130/80, risk-factor control — and it's the yardstick asymptomatic disease is measured against.
  • CEA vs CAS vs TCAR: CEA lowest peri-procedural stroke (esp. >70); CAS for high surgical risk; TCAR (flow reversal) outperforms transfemoral stenting in registries.
  • Asymptomatic disease is shifting — intensive medical therapy is highly competitive; CREST-2 (2025) suggests stenting + IMM lowers stroke vs IMM alone with no added CEA benefit (interpret cautiously, very recent).
  • Beyond the % — morphology matters: intraplaque hemorrhage/ulceration (MRI vessel-wall imaging) raises risk and can make a symptomatic <50% plaque the culprit; don't miss a carotid web in young cryptogenic stroke; near-occlusion gains little from revascularization.

Key sources: NASCET/ECST (symptomatic CEA); CREST (CEA vs CAS); CREST-2 (asymptomatic, 2025); ESVS 2023 and SVS carotid guidelines; TCAR registry data.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. NASCET and ECST — CEA for symptomatic carotid stenosis (70–99%, and selected 50–69%).
  2. Brott TG, et al. CREST (CEA vs CAS); CREST-2 program (asymptomatic ≥70%, reported 2025 — stenting + intensive medical management vs IMM).
  3. ESVS 2023 and SVS clinical practice guidelines on extracranial carotid disease (stroke risk = stenosis severity + plaque morphology); PARISK study (intraplaque hemorrhage → recurrent ipsilateral events); carotid-web and near-occlusion literature; TCAR registry (Vascular Quality Initiative).
  4. Kleindorfer DO, et al. 2021 AHA/ASA secondary-prevention guideline (carotid + medical therapy).
  5. TRICARE / DoD: statins/antiplatelets generic-formulary. Retention: DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b (stenosis b(2), stroke b(3), TIA b(6)), Army AR 40-501 3-31f.

Verified July 2026: symptomatic 70–99% carotid stenosis warrants revascularization within ~2 weeks (CEA first-line; NASCET), 50–69% in selected patients; best medical therapy (high-intensity statin to LDL <70, antiplatelet, BP <130/80) applies to all; CEA generally has lower peri-procedural stroke than transfemoral CAS (TCAR superior to transfemoral CAS in registries). The CREST-2 2025 readout (stenting + IMM > IMM alone; no added CEA benefit in asymptomatic ≥70%) is very recent — confirm against the formal guideline update before acting on it. Retention framing per DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b, Army AR 40-501 3-31f — verify before counseling. Plaque-morphology risk (intraplaque hemorrhage/ulceration on vessel-wall MRI), carotid web (young cryptogenic stroke), and near-occlusion (limited revascularization benefit) are added from current literature — an evolving area beyond the stenosis-percentage thresholds.

docs.title: Cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA)
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: amyloid-β deposition in cortical and leptomeningeal small vessels — the leading cause of lobar spontaneous ICH in older adults, and a driver of vascular cognitive impairment. The two decisions that matter: recognize it (lobar bleed, microbleeds, superficial siderosis on MRI) and keep it away from anticoagulation, because it re-bleeds.

🚩 Recognize the CAA phenotypes — and don't reflexively anticoagulate/antiplatelet

  • Lobar intracerebral hemorrhage in an older adult (cortical/subcortical, often recurrent) — the classic presentation (contrast with deep/hypertensive bleeds).
  • Transient focal neurological episodes ("amyloid spells") — recurrent, stereotyped, spreading sensory/motor/visual symptoms (often from cortical superficial siderosis). They mimic TIA — but anticoagulating or thrombolysing them can be catastrophic (high ICH risk). Image before you treat as ischemia.
  • Cognitive impairment — CAA contributes to dementia (and co-exists with Alzheimer's).
  • Convexity (non-aneurysmal) SAH in the elderly.

docs.title: Diagnose — MRI markers & Boston criteria 2.0
docs.text:
Get an MRI with a blood-sensitive sequence (GRE or SWI)

Hemorrhagic markers (strictly lobar distribution): lobar cerebral microbleeds, cortical superficial siderosis (cSS), lobar ICH, convexity SAH. Non-hemorrhagic markers (Boston 2.0): severe enlarged perivascular spaces in the centrum semiovale and multispot white-matter hyperintensities.

  • Boston criteria v2.0 (2022) added cSS and the non-hemorrhagic white-matter features — now allowing a diagnosis of probable CAA even without a hemorrhage. Deep microbleeds point away from CAA (toward hypertensive small-vessel disease). Know its blind spot: validation shows ~75% sensitivity / 95% specificity overall — but only ~55% sensitivity for non-ICH (cognitive) presentations vs ~90% for ICH. CAA can be advanced enough to impair cognition before it meets the hemorrhagic criteria.
  • Edinburgh CT criteria (when there's no MRI): subarachnoid blood + finger-like projections on CT (spec ~87%, sens ~81%) support CAA in a lobar bleed — and they're now prognostic: an IPD meta-analysis tied the Edinburgh risk tiers to 5-year recurrent-ICH rates of ~12% / 16% / 26% (low/intermediate/high). Best used as a rule-in tool.
  • CSF biomarkers (adjunct): CAA lowers both Aβ42 and Aβ40 (Alzheimer's lowers Aβ42 only) — useful to distinguish the two and to flag concomitant CAA before anti-amyloid therapy.
  • CAA-related inflammation (CAA-ri): subacute encephalopathy, seizures, or headache with asymmetric confluent white-matter vasogenic edema on MRI — steroid-responsive; recognize it because it's treatable (immunosuppression), unlike CAA itself.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Suspect CAA from a lobar bleed, amyloid spells, or lobar microbleeds/cSS.
  2. MRI with GRE/SWI; apply Boston 2.0; consider CAA-ri if there's vasogenic edema.
  3. Aggressive blood-pressure control — the one intervention that clearly lowers recurrence.
  4. Antithrombotic decision — risk-stratify (bleeding phenotype vs. thrombotic need); it's not a blanket "never."
  5. For AF needing stroke prevention: weigh DOAC vs. LAAO vs. antiplatelet by re-bleed risk.
  6. CAA-ri → immunosuppression (steroids, below); screen before anti-amyloid dementia drugs (ARIA risk).

docs.title: Management
docs.text:
No CAA-specific drug — manage the bleeding risk (and don't over-restrict)

  • Blood pressure control is the dominant lever — long-term <130/80 reduces recurrent ICH.
  • Anticoagulation is high-risk, not absolutely forbidden — stratify (2026 NEJM review). CAA re-bleeds at ~7–8%/year vs ~1% for non-CAA ICH, amplified by cortical superficial siderosis.
    • AF + microbleeds only (no ICH, no cSS): a DOAC may be reasonable. (COCROACH IPD meta-analysis: OAC after ICH in AF cut ischemic MACE HR 0.27 with no statistically significant rise in hemorrhage.)
    • Prior lobar ICH, disseminated cSS, or multiple bleeds: avoid anticoagulation → LAAO, antiplatelet monotherapy, or a trial. Pooled LAAO data in AF + prior ICH: ischemic stroke and recurrent-ICH each ~2%/year (~4% in the CAA/parenchymal-hemorrhage subgroup); RCTs vs. DOAC are still needed.
    • If you anticoagulate, use a DOAC (not warfarin), and if resuming after an ICH, the risk–benefit balance is best around ~7–8 weeks post-bleed.
  • Antiplatelets — safer than long assumed: RESTART showed antiplatelet resumption after ICH did not significantly increase recurrent ICH (aHR ~0.51); the 2022 AHA/ASA guideline rates resumption "reasonable/may be considered." Use for a clear vascular indication.
  • Statins: CAA-specific data are mixed; individualize (don't reflexively continue high-intensity statin after a lobar bleed without weighing it).
  • CAA-related inflammation → immunosuppress (see box).

Recurrence — stratify, don't just say "low"

Highest risk (~7.4%/year): multifocal/disseminated cSS, multiple prior ICHs, APOE ε2 or ε4, higher BP; cSS progression on serial MRI independently predicts new ICH (HR ~3.8), and baseline cSS / hemorrhagic presentation predict recurrence (OR ~3.4 / ~5.0). Lowest risk (~6% over 5 years): probable CAA on microbleeds alone (no ICH, no cSS).

Thrombolysis in a CAA patient — cortical siderosis is the danger marker

In the AcT thrombolysis analysis, cortical superficial siderosis burden was the dominant harmful marker — each affected sulcus raised symptomatic ICH (aOR ~3.9) and mortality. Notably, meeting Boston v2.0 was not associated with hemorrhagic outcomes (the older hemorrhagic-only criteria were) — so the non-hemorrhagic v2.0 markers don't carry the same bleeding risk; cSS does. Factor cSS into thrombolysis decisions.

CAA-related inflammation (CAA-ri) — treat it, it works

CAA-ri (subacute encephalopathy/seizures/headache + asymmetric vasogenic edema) is the treatable face of CAA. Immunosuppression clearly helps (JAMA Neurol series): treated → 94% clinical vs 50% untreated improvement, and far less recurrence (26% vs 71%, HR ~0.19).

  • First episode: IV methylprednisolone 1 g/day × 3–5 days → oral prednisone ~60 mg tapered over months.
  • Recurrent/refractory: cyclophosphamide, mycophenolate, or rituximab.

The ARIA connection — screen (and know the thresholds) before anti-amyloid antibodies

The anti-amyloid dementia mAbs (lecanemab, donanemab) cause ARIA (edema/effusion ARIA-E, microhemorrhage/siderosis ARIA-H) — and underlying CAA amplifies it. Get a baseline GRE/SWI MRI and weigh:

  • APOE ε4 homozygotes: ARIA-E ~33–45% (vs ~5–10% in non-carriers), with reported fatal cases — the EMA excluded ε4 homozygotes from lecanemab's authorization.
  • Pre-treatment microhemorrhages (≥2–4) raise ARIA-E (~OR 2.5); cortical superficial siderosis raises it (~OR 2.2) and is generally a contraindication.
  • Elevated blood pressure (MAP >107) is the sole modifiable ARIA risk factor — control it.

This is the practical bridge between CAA and the anti-amyloid dementia workup.

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
Acute lobar ICH → see the intracerebral hemorrhage page (ICU, BP, reversal). Otherwise outpatient neurology with BP optimization, antithrombotic-risk counseling, cognitive assessment, and a clear note in the chart that anticoagulation is high-risk.

Military medicine — cerebral amyloid angiopathy

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03, and aeromedical policy.

  • Older/retiree and veteran population: CAA is largely a disease of older adults — more relevant to retiree/VA care and MEB dispositions than to young active-duty members.
  • Retention: CAA presenting as intracerebral hemorrhage falls under DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b(1); persistent sequelae (severe headaches, seizures, cognitive deficit) under 5.26.c — Army AR 40-501 3-31f/k → DES where duty is impaired.
  • Key counseling point: flag in the record that anticoagulation is high-risk — it affects any future AF/VTE management decision.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• CAA is the top cause of lobar ICH in the elderly — recognize the phenotypes (lobar bleed, amyloid spells, lobar microbleeds, cortical superficial siderosis).
  • Boston criteria v2.0 (2022) added cSS and non-hemorrhagic markers (diagnose without a hemorrhage) — but it's insensitive for cognitive (non-ICH) presentations (~55%). Edinburgh CT criteria help when there's no MRI and now carry prognostic weight.
  • Anticoagulation is high-risk, not absolutely banned — stratify. Microbleeds-only AF may take a DOAC (COCROACH); prior ICH / disseminated cSS → LAAO or antiplatelet. Warfarin is out in favor of DOACs. Antiplatelet resumption is reasonable (RESTART — no significant ICH excess).
  • Recurrence is not uniformly "low" — ~7.4%/year in the high-risk (cSS/multiple ICH/APOE) group; ~6%/5 y in microbleeds-only. cSS is the key danger marker — also for thrombolysis (AcT: each affected sulcus ↑ symptomatic ICH).
  • CAA-related inflammation is immunosuppression-responsive — treat it (94% vs 50% improve; steroids → taper).
  • CAA drives ARIA risk — screen APOE ε4 homozygotes / microbleeds / cSS before anti-amyloid mAbs.
  • On the horizon: vascular Aβ deposits begin 30–40 years before the first ICH (long preclinical window); the first disease-modifying trial — an APP-targeting siRNA — is enrolling sporadic and Dutch-type CAA.

Key sources: Greenberg SM, Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy (NEJM 2026 — comprehensive review); Boston criteria v2.0 (Charidimou et al., Lancet Neurol 2022); CAA imaging reviews; 2022 AHA/ASA spontaneous ICH guideline; anti-amyloid mAb ARIA data.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Greenberg SM. Cerebral Amyloid Angiopathy. N Engl J Med. 2026 — comprehensive review (pathogenic timeline, risk-stratified antithrombotics, siRNA trial).
  2. Charidimou A, et al. The Boston criteria version 2.0 for CAA. Lancet Neurol. 2022; MRI-neuropathology validation (Neurology 2024, ~55% sensitivity for non-ICH); Rodrigues MA, et al. Edinburgh CT criteria & recurrent ICH — IPD meta-analysis (Lancet Neurol 2025).
  3. Antithrombotics after ICH: Al-Shahi Salman R, et al. COCROACH (Lancet Neurol 2023) and RESTART (Lancet 2019); 2022 AHA/ASA spontaneous ICH guideline; LAAO in AF + prior ICH/CAA (JNNP 2025; Int J Stroke meta-analysis 2025).
  4. Thrombolysis / CAA-ri / ARIA: Marzoughi S, et al. CAA markers & thrombolysis (AcT) (Stroke 2026); Regenhardt RW, et al. Immunosuppression in CAA-ri (JAMA Neurol 2020); anti-amyloid mAb ARIA data (lecanemab/donanemab; APOE ε4-homozygote risk, EMA exclusion).
  5. TRICARE / DoD: antihypertensives generic-formulary. Retention: DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b(1)/5.26.c, Army AR 40-501 3-31f/k.

Verified July 2026: CAA phenotypes, Boston criteria v2.0 (with its ~55% non-ICH sensitivity gap), Edinburgh CT criteria (diagnostic + prognostic), the risk-stratified antithrombotic approach (2026 NEJM review — DOAC reasonable for microbleeds-only AF per COCROACH; LAAO/antiplatelet for disseminated-cSS/multiple-ICH; RESTART supports antiplatelet resumption; warfarin avoided; resume ~7–8 wk), cSS as the dominant re-bleed/thrombolysis danger marker (AcT), recurrence stratification (~7.4%/y high-risk vs ~6%/5 y microbleeds-only), CAA-ri immunosuppression (IV methylprednisolone → prednisone taper), and ARIA thresholds reflect current literature. The specific effect sizes/rates were supplied by expert review with citations — confirm before quoting. Retention framing per DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b(1)/5.26.c (Army AR 40-501 3-31f/k) — verify before counseling.*

docs.title: Cerebral venous sinus thrombosis (CVST)
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: thrombosis of the dural venous sinuses or cortical veins — the great mimic and the great miss. It hits young people (often women on estrogen or peripartum), presents most often as a new, worsening headache, and the counter-intuitive rule that saves brains is: you anticoagulate even when there's hemorrhage.

🚩 Think CVST when the headache doesn't fit an artery

Suspect it with:

  • New/progressive or thunderclap headache — especially with papilledema, seizures, encephalopathy, or focal deficits.
  • A hemorrhage or infarct that crosses arterial territories (bilateral, parasagittal, or temporal — venous distribution).
  • Isolated raised intracranial pressure (headache + papilledema + diplopia mimicking IIH).
  • Risk factors: pregnancy/postpartum, estrogen (OCP), thrombophilia, malignancy, head/neck infection (mastoiditis, sinusitis), dehydration, IBD.

Get dedicated venous imaging — MRV or CTV. A "normal" non-contrast CT does not exclude it (look for a dense cord/sinus sign, or a bleed that doesn't fit an artery). For isolated cortical vein thrombosis (easily missed on standard MRV), use SWI or GRE sequences.

D-dimer helps only in low-probability patients

D-dimer sensitivity is ~90–97%, but ~10% of confirmed CVT has a normal D-dimer — especially isolated headache, subacute/chronic onset, or low thrombus burden. A normal D-dimer with a low clinical probability can help exclude CVT; in moderate/high-probability patients, image the veins regardless. Don't let a negative D-dimer stop the workup.

docs.title: Rapid approach
docs.text:
1. Image the veins: MRV or CTV (the diagnostic test — don't rely on non-contrast CT or a negative D-dimer).
  2. Anticoagulate — even if there's venous hemorrhage (the bleed is from the clot; treating the clot helps).
  3. Treat complications: seizures, raised ICP / threatened vision, large venous infarct.
  4. Find the cause: pregnancy/estrogen, thrombophilia, infection, malignancy.
  5. Plan duration of anticoagulation and counsel on estrogen avoidance / future pregnancy.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Confirm on MRV/CTV (± MRI showing venous infarct/hemorrhage).
  2. Start therapeutic anticoagulation now — LMWH preferred (even with hemorrhage present).
  3. Admit; treat seizures and raised ICP; neurosurgery on standby for a large infarct.
  4. Transition to oral anticoagulation and set the duration by provoked vs unprovoked/thrombophilia.
  5. Work up etiology; stop estrogen; counsel future-pregnancy prophylaxis.

docs.title: Acute treatment
docs.text:
Anticoagulate — yes, even with hemorrhage

  • Therapeutic LMWH (e.g., enoxaparin 1 mg/kg SC q12h) is first-line acutely — preferred over unfractionated heparin (better outcomes) unless renal failure or likely need for rapid reversal/procedures favors UFH infusion.
  • Intracranial hemorrhage from the CVST is NOT a contraindication — the venous congestion that caused the bleed improves with anticoagulation. This is the point everyone finds counter-intuitive; do it anyway.
  • Endovascular thrombectomy/thrombolysis is NOT routine — TO-ACT was negative. Reserve it for clinical deterioration despite adequate anticoagulation.

Manage the complications

  • Seizures: treat clinical seizures; give an ASM if there's a supratentorial lesion + seizure (high recurrence). No routine prophylaxis without a lesion/seizure.
  • Raised ICP / papilledema threatening vision: acetazolamide, therapeutic LP, monitor visual fields/acuity; shunt or optic-nerve-sheath fenestration if vision is failing.
  • Large venous infarct with mass effect / herniation: decompressive craniectomy is life-saving — operate EARLY (within ~48 h); delay worsens outcomes. DECOMPRESS2 (n=118): ~36% independent (mRS 0–2) and ~34% mortality at 12 months, and ~79% of survivors judged surgery worthwhile — so offer it even to severe presentations. Coma and fixed dilated pupils predict poor outcome.

VITT-CVT — a different disease; do NOT give heparin

If CVST follows an adenoviral COVID vaccine with thrombocytopenia (and a positive anti-PF4 ELISA) — vaccine-induced immune thrombotic thrombocytopenia. Now rare, but the management is opposite to reflex:

  • Avoid all heparin — use a non-heparin anticoagulant (argatroban or fondaparinux).
  • IVIG 1 g/kg × 2 days (± corticosteroids) — immunomodulation lowers mortality (≈29% vs 70%).
  • Do NOT transfuse platelets (fuels thrombosis).
  • Involve hematology urgently.

docs.title: Transition & duration
docs.text:
Oral anticoagulation and how long

  • Transition to an oral anticoagulant once stable. DOACs are now a reasonable alternative to warfarin for non-VITT, non-antiphospholipid CVST (2024 AHA statement) — the evidence base has grown well beyond dabigatran: DOAC-CVT (Lancet Neurol 2025, prospective, n=619) and ACTION-CVT (Stroke 2022, n=845) show similar recurrence with equal or less bleeding vs VKA. Apixaban is now the most commonly used agent in practice; dabigatran (RE-SPECT CVT) and rivaroxaban (SECRET) are also studied. Use warfarin for antiphospholipid syndrome (DOACs underperform there).
  • Duration:
    • Provoked (transient factor, e.g., estrogen): 3–6 months.
    • Unprovoked: 6–12 months.
    • Recurrent VTE or severe thrombophilia / antiphospholipid: indefinite.
  • Stratify by recurrence risk when choosing duration. Overall recurrent VTE is ~5 per 100 patient-years (cumulative ~8% at 2 y, ~12% at 5 y). Highest-risk → favor longer/indefinite anticoagulation: antiphospholipid antibodies (aHR ~3.9), prior VTE (aHR ~3.4), active cancer, and idiopathic CVT. (EXCOA-CVT, ongoing, is the first RCT of short vs long duration — until then, duration is expert-consensus.)
  • Stop estrogen-containing contraception permanently; in future pregnancies, give LMWH prophylaxis through pregnancy and postpartum (highest-risk window).

TRICARE — CVST prescribing

Enoxaparin (LMWH), warfarin, and acetazolamide are generic/formulary. DOACs are covered — apixaban is the most-used agent in practice (DOAC-CVT/ACTION-CVT), and a preferred DOAC exists on the Uniform Formulary. Verify the current preferred agent and any PA at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

docs.title: Long-term outcomes & follow-up
docs.text:
Most patients do well functionally — ~80–90% reach mRS 0–2 — but the mRS hides a real chronic burden, so counsel and follow accordingly:

  • Common residual symptoms: chronic headache, fatigue, cognitive impairment, and mood disturbance. ~42% had not returned to work/school at 6 months despite most being "independent" — ask about function, not just the mRS.
  • Epilepsy develops in >10% — higher with seizures at onset, hemorrhagic lesions, superior sagittal sinus involvement, or craniectomy.
  • Repeat venous imaging (MRV/CTV) to document recanalization and guide duration.
  • Dural arteriovenous fistula is a rare late complication (~2%) — consider it for new pulsatile tinnitus or worsening headache.
  • Heavy menstrual bleeding on anticoagulation is a common, adherence-limiting problem in young women — address it proactively (gynecology; it is not a reason to silently stop the DOAC).

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
Admit (stroke unit / ICU if depressed consciousness, large infarct, or refractory seizures/ICP). Neurosurgery available for decompression. Outpatient hematology/thrombophilia work-up and anticoagulation follow-up; repeat venous imaging to document recanalization.

Military medicine — cerebral venous thrombosis

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03, DoDI 1332.18, and aeromedical policy before counseling.

  • Demographics fit the force: young service members, estrogen contraception, dehydration (field/heat), and peripartum are all in play — keep CVST on the differential for a service member with a new persistent headache and papilledema.
  • Deployability: acute CVST and the anticoagulation that follows are deployment-limiting; estrogen contraception should be changed after CVST.
  • Retention / MEB: CVST isn't named verbatim, but its consequences are — a venous infarct/hemorrhage and any persistent sequelae (severe headaches, seizures, sensory/motor or cognitive deficit) fall under DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b/5.26.c (cerebrovascular / CNS sequelae, gated by 5.26.a; Army AR 40-501 3-31f/k). Residual deficit, seizures, or a thrombophilia requiring indefinite anticoagulation generally triggers the DES (MEB/PEB). Document etiology, imaging, anticoagulation plan, and duration.
  • Aeromedical: CVST history, seizures, and chronic anticoagulation are disqualifying/ waiver-dependent for flying duty.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• Anticoagulate through the hemorrhage. The venous bleed is a consequence of the clot; therapeutic LMWH (preferred over UFH) is first-line even when ICH is present — the single most counter-intuitive, most important teaching point.
  • Diagnose with venous imaging (MRV/CTV). Non-contrast CT and D-dimer miss it (D-dimer normal in ~10%, esp. isolated headache/subacute) — image low- or high-probability patients. SWI/GRE for isolated cortical vein thrombosis.
  • DOACs are now a reasonable alternative for non-VITT/non-antiphospholipid CVST — the evidence tripled with DOAC-CVT (2025) and ACTION-CVT; apixaban is the most-used agent, with dabigatran (RE-SPECT CVT) and rivaroxaban (SECRET) also studied. Warfarin for antiphospholipid.
  • Recurrence ~5/100 patient-years — longer/indefinite anticoagulation for antiphospholipid, prior VTE, cancer, or idiopathic; EXCOA-CVT (ongoing) will finally test duration.
  • VITT-CVT is a different disease — no heparin, IVIG + argatroban/fondaparinux, no platelets.
  • Endovascular therapy isn't routine — TO-ACT negative. Decompressive craniectomy is life-saving for a herniating venous infarct — operate early (DECOMPRESS2: ~36% independent, most survivors judged it worthwhile).
  • The chronic burden is under-appreciated — headache, fatigue, cognitive/mood symptoms, epilepsy (>10%), and a large return-to-work gap despite good mRS. Follow with repeat imaging and QoL attention.

Key sources: 2024 AHA CVT scientific statement; ESO CVT guideline; DOAC-CVT & ACTION-CVT (DOACs); RE-SPECT CVT (dabigatran); SECRET (rivaroxaban); TO-ACT (endovascular); DECOMPRESS2 (craniectomy); ACTION-CVT recurrence analysis; EXCOA-CVT (ongoing).

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Saposnik G, et al. Diagnosis and Management of Cerebral Venous Thrombosis: AHA scientific statement. Stroke. 2024; ESO CVT guideline (Ferro/Aguiar de Sousa); Field TS, Fragata I. Current Management of CVT. Stroke. 2026.
  2. DOACs: van de Munckhof A, et al. DOAC-CVT (Lancet Neurol 2025); Yaghi S, et al. ACTION-CVT (Stroke 2022) + systematic review; Ferro JM, et al. RE-SPECT CVT (dabigatran); SECRET (rivaroxaban).
  3. Coutinho JM, et al. Endovascular therapy vs standard care in severe CVT (TO-ACT). JAMA Neurol. 2020.
  4. Surgery / recurrence / VITT: Aaron S, et al. DECOMPRESS2 (Stroke 2024); Shu L, et al. ACTION-CVT recurrence predictors (Neurology 2022); Scutelnic A, et al. VITT-CVT management (Ann Neurol 2022); EXCOA-CVT (ongoing, ISRCTN25644448).
  5. TRICARE / DoD: enoxaparin/warfarin/acetazolamide generic-formulary; preferred DOAC per Uniform Formulary — verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Verified July 2026: venous imaging (MRV/CTV; SWI/GRE for cortical vein) for diagnosis, therapeutic anticoagulation including in the presence of venous hemorrhage (LMWH preferred over UFH), and non-routine endovascular therapy (TO-ACT negative) reflect current AHA and ESO guidance. Newer additions — DOAC-CVT/ACTION-CVT (DOACs a reasonable alternative, apixaban most-used), recurrence predictors, DECOMPRESS2, the VITT-CVT protocol, long-term-sequelae data, and EXCOA-CVT (ongoing) — were supplied by expert review with citations; confirm the specific figures before relying on them. Retention framing confirmed against the source: CVST's cerebrovascular/ hemorrhage and persistent-sequelae consequences fall under DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b/5.26.c (Army AR 40-501 3-31f/k) — CVST is not named verbatim. The TRICARE preferred DOAC was not pinned to a citable source — verify before counseling.*

docs.title: Cervical artery dissection
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: a tear in the wall of the carotid or vertebral artery in the neck — the leading cause of stroke in the young and middle-aged. The tell is pain first, ischemia later: neck/head pain (± Horner's or pulsatile tinnitus) that precedes a TIA or stroke by hours to days — a window where recognition prevents the stroke.

🚩 Young patient + neck/head pain + a local sign → image the vessels

Suspect dissection with:

  • New neck or head/face pain (often unilateral) after trauma — even trivial (MVC, sports, neck manipulation), heavy lifting, or spontaneously.
  • Local signs before the stroke: partial Horner's (ptosis + miosis, carotid), pulsatile tinnitus, a lower-cranial-nerve palsy, or posterior neck pain (vertebral).
  • Then ischemia — TIA/stroke in the corresponding territory, classically hours to days later.
  • Vascular risk clues: fibromuscular dysplasia, connective-tissue disease (vascular Ehlers-Danlos, Marfan, Loeys-Dietz), family history — and hypertension (genetically higher BP, especially diastolic, is a likely causal risk factor, with a larger effect than for non-dissection stroke).

Get dedicated vessel imaging now — you're trying to catch it in the pain-before-stroke window.

docs.title: Diagnose
docs.text:
Imaging — find the intramural hematoma / flap

  • CTA neck+head or MRI/MRA with fat-saturation — look for the crescent sign (intramural hematoma), tapered ("flame") occlusion, or a pseudoaneurysm. Fat-sat MRI is especially good for the mural hematoma.
  • DSA is the historical gold standard — reserve for ambiguous cases or planned intervention.
  • Look for fibromuscular dysplasia — present in ~6–14% of cervical dissections (up to ~40% have FMD in ≥1 arterial bed on whole-body CTA), and FMD raises recurrent-dissection risk — so image the other vessels, not just the culprit.

Connective-tissue / genetics screening — how far to go

A monogenic connective-tissue disorder (vascular EDS, Marfan, Loeys-Dietz, osteogenesis imperfecta) is found in <1% of dissections, but >50% have subtle ultrastructural connective-tissue changes on skin biopsy. Refer to genetics for a suspected syndrome, multiple/recurrent dissections, or familial cases. (The PHACTR1 locus links FMD, migraine, and dissection — a shared arterial-wall biology.)

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Suspect it in any young/middle-aged stroke or unexplained neck pain + local sign.
  2. Confirm on CTA or fat-sat MRI/MRA.
  3. Antithrombotic — risk-stratify (radiographic risk vs. bleeding risk), start early, for 3–6 months (below).
  4. Acute stroke from dissection? Standard reperfusion still applies (thrombolysis/thrombectomy).
  5. Re-image at ~3–6 months; stop or step down based on healing and residual risk.
  6. Counsel on trauma/manipulation avoidance; control blood pressure; screen for connective-tissue disease / FMD.

docs.title: Treatment
docs.text:
Antithrombotic — RISK-STRATIFY (start early); it's no longer a simple coin-flip

The RCTs (CADISS, TREAT-CAD) were underpowered and showed no clear winner, but larger data now favor tailoring (2024 AHA Scientific Statement). A meta-analysis including STOP-CAD (n≈5,000) found anticoagulation lowered ischemic stroke (RR ~0.63) but roughly doubled major bleeding (RR ~2.25) — so match the drug to the patient:

  • Favor anticoagulation (heparin/LMWH → DOAC or VKA) when high-risk radiographic features + low bleeding risk: occlusive dissection, intraluminal/free-floating thrombus, or severe stenosis. (STOP-CAD: only occlusive dissections had a stroke benefit from anticoagulation, aHR ~0.36; the CADISS+TREAT-CAD IPD meta-analysis: benefit only in those without a dissecting aneurysm.)
  • Favor an antiplatelet when lower-risk features or elevated bleeding risk: a large infarct, hemorrhagic transformation, or intradural (intracranial) extension. If antiplatelet: a short course of DAPT (21–90 days) → single antiplatelet is reasonable (analogous to minor-stroke/TIA data; ~44% of CADISS patients got dual).
  • If you anticoagulate, prefer a DOAC over warfarin (more convenient, likely better benefit–risk per the TREAT-CAD investigators — though direct data are small and no RCT exists), and consider switching to an antiplatelet before ~180 days to cut late bleeding risk.
  • Start early — recurrent ischemic events cluster in the first weeks (~87% within 30 days).
  • Intracranial dissection (higher SAH risk) is managed differently — individualize with neurology/neurosurgery.

Recurrence is uncommon and usually benign (STOP-CAD 2026)

2-year recurrent-dissection rate ~3.2%, median time ~53 days (~half within 2 months); predictors = younger age, migraine, FMD (presenting with stroke actually predicted lower recurrence). Reassuringly, only ~5 of 88 recurrences caused a new ischemic event.
