Predictors of a subsequent ischemic stroke by day 180: infarct on baseline imaging (aHR ~9.9), prior stroke (~7.3), presentation <7 days (~3.0), occlusive dissection (~2.3).

If it presents as an acute stroke — reperfuse normally

  • Extracranial dissection is NOT a contraindication to IV thrombolysis — treat an acute ischemic stroke by the usual reperfusion algorithm.
  • Thrombectomy for a large-vessel occlusion from the dissection.
  • Acute carotid stenting for a dissection-related tandem occlusion is not routine: pooled TITAN/ETIS registry data showed higher reperfusion (89% vs 68%) but no better 90-day outcome (and no excess sICH) — the acute-stenting benefit was seen in atherosclerotic, not dissection, tandem lesions. (The randomized TITAN trial is ongoing.) Reserve elective stenting for refractory ischemia despite medical therapy or an enlarging/symptomatic pseudoaneurysm.

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
Admit acute presentations (stroke unit; monitor for extension/ischemia). Prognosis is generally good — recurrence is low and most heal. Outpatient neurology follow-up with repeat vessel imaging and connective-tissue evaluation where indicated.

Military medicine — cervical artery dissection

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03, and aeromedical policy.

  • Fits the force: young service members, trauma/blast, load-bearing, combatives, and neck manipulation are all mechanisms — keep dissection high on the differential for a service member with neck/head pain and any focal sign.
  • Retention: a dissection that causes stroke/TIA is a cerebrovascular condition under DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b (stroke b(3); recurrent TIA b(6) unless etiology identified and definitively treated), Army AR 40-501 3-31f — a healed dissection with a treated cause and no residual deficit may retain per specialty review; residual deficit or recurrence trends to the DES.
  • Deployability / aeromedical: non-deployable during work-up/treatment; anticoagulation and a stroke history are deployment- and flight-status-limiting/waiver-dependent.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• The #1 cause of stroke in the young — and the pain precedes the stroke, so recognition in the neck-pain window is prevention.
  • Diagnose with fat-sat MRI/MRA or CTA (crescent sign / intramural hematoma); DSA only if ambiguous.
  • Antithrombotic choice is now risk-stratified, not a coin-flip (2024 AHA Scientific Statement). The RCTs (CADISS/TREAT-CAD) were underpowered; larger data (STOP-CAD, IPD meta-analysis) show anticoagulation lowers ischemic stroke (~RR 0.63) but ~doubles major bleeding — so anticoagulate high-risk radiographic features (occlusive dissection, intraluminal thrombus, no dissecting aneurysm) with low bleeding risk, and use an antiplatelet (± short DAPT) for lower-risk/high-bleed patients. Prefer a DOAC if anticoagulating; consider stepping to an antiplatelet by ~180 days.
  • Start early — recurrent ischemic events cluster in the first 30 days.
  • Don't withhold thrombolysis/thrombectomy for an acute stroke from an extracranial dissection; acute stenting of a dissection tandem occlusion isn't routine (TITAN/ETIS: more reperfusion, no better outcome).
  • Recurrence is uncommon (~3% at 2 y) and usually benign (STOP-CAD 2026); predictors = young age, migraine, FMD. Image all vessels for FMD (~6–14%); refer genetics for syndromic/recurrent/familial.
  • Control blood pressure — likely a causal risk factor, not just connective tissue.

Key sources: 2024 AHA Scientific Statement on cervical artery dissection (Yaghi et al.); CADISS (2015) & TREAT-CAD (2021); STOP-CAD (Stroke 2024) + secondary analyses (2025/2026); CADISS+TREAT-CAD IPD meta-analysis (JAMA Neurol 2024); FMD (JAMA Netw Open 2025); TITAN/ETIS (tandem occlusion); JACC Mendelian-randomization (BP causal).

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Yaghi S, et al. Treatment and Outcomes of Cervical Artery Dissection in Adults — AHA Scientific Statement. Stroke. 2024 — the current society-level document; risk-stratified antithrombotic algorithm.
  2. CADISS investigators. Antiplatelet vs anticoagulation (CADISS). Lancet Neurol. 2015; Engelter ST, et al. TREAT-CAD. Lancet Neurol. 2021 (aspirin not proven non-inferior).
  3. Larger data: Yaghi S, et al. Anticoagulation vs antiplatelets — meta-analysis (Stroke 2024) and STOP-CAD (Stroke 2024) + secondary analyses (recurrence 2026; subsequent-stroke predictors 2025); Kaufmann JE, et al. CADISS+TREAT-CAD IPD meta-analysis (JAMA Neurol 2024 — benefit only without dissecting aneurysm).
  4. FMD / genetics / BP: Nehme A, et al. FMD and spontaneous dissection (JAMA Netw Open 2025); Le Grand Q, et al. Mendelian randomization — BP causal for dissection (JACC 2023).
  5. Tandem occlusion: Marnat G, et al. Carotid dissection stenting in tandem occlusions — TITAN/ETIS pooled analysis (Stroke 2020/2021).
  6. TRICARE / DoD: aspirin/statin generic-formulary; preferred DOAC per Uniform Formulary (verify). Retention: DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b, Army AR 40-501 3-31f.

Verified July 2026: diagnosis by fat-sat MRI/MRA or CTA; antithrombotic choice is now risk-stratified per the 2024 AHA Scientific Statement — larger data (STOP-CAD; IPD meta-analysis) show anticoagulation lowers ischemic stroke (~RR 0.63) but ~doubles major bleeding, so anticoagulate high-risk radiographic features (occlusive/intraluminal thrombus, no dissecting aneurysm) with low bleeding risk and use an antiplatelet (± short DAPT) otherwise, prefer a DOAC if anticoagulating, treat ~3–6 months then re-image; thrombolysis/thrombectomy are not contraindicated for acute stroke from extracranial dissection, and acute stenting of a dissection tandem occlusion is not routine (TITAN/ETIS). Recurrence ~3% at 2 y and usually benign (STOP-CAD 2026); FMD in ~6–14%; BP is a likely causal factor. The specific effect sizes were supplied by expert review with citations — confirm before quoting. Retention framing per DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b (dissection not named verbatim; its stroke/TIA consequences apply), Army AR 40-501 3-31f — verify before counseling.*

docs.title: Intracerebral hemorrhage (ICH)
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: spontaneous (non-traumatic) bleeding into the brain parenchyma — the other half of "CT shows blood vs no blood." Higher early mortality than ischemic stroke, and the first hours are a bundle of parallel actions: reverse anticoagulation, drop the blood pressure, get neurosurgery on the phone, and protect against expansion. Do them together, not in sequence.

🚩 First 60 minutes — the ICH bundle (do these in parallel)

The INTERACT3 care bundle — started within 1 hour and held for 7 days — improves survival and function. Four moves, simultaneously:

  1. Blood pressure → SBP <140 (but not <130 — harmful). Smooth, sustained.
  2. Reverse anticoagulation to INR <1.5 / agent-specific reversal (below).
  3. Glucose to target (6.1–7.8 non-diabetic; 7.8–10 diabetic mmol/L) and temp <37.5 °C.
  4. Call neurosurgery now — cerebellar bleed, hydrocephalus, or large lobar clot may need the OR.

And immediately: airway/GCS, stop the causative drug, repeat CT at ~6 h (expansion is the enemy), elevate head of bed 30°, treat herniation.

docs.title: Rapid approach
docs.text:
1. CT confirms blood → this is ICH; note location, volume, IVH, and midline shift.
  2. Start the bundle (BP, reversal, glucose, temp) within the hour.
  3. Reverse the specific anticoagulant (below) — the single highest-yield drug decision.
  4. Neurosurgery consult for cerebellar/large/hydrocephalus; ICU for everyone.
  5. Find the cause — deep (hypertensive) vs lobar (CAA/secondary); image the vessels if atypical.
  6. Do not prognosticate early — avoid new DNR/care limits in the first 24–48 h (self-fulfilling).

docs.title: Diagnosis & etiology (where the bleed is tells you why)
docs.text:
Location Usual cause Deep — basal ganglia, thalamus, pons, cerebellum Hypertensive (small-vessel/Charcot-Bouchard) Lobar — cortical/subcortical, elderly Cerebral amyloid angiopathy (CAA) Lobar, young/normotensive, or atypical Secondary — AVM, aneurysm, dural AV fistula, tumor, CVST, vasculitis, drugs (sympathomimetics)
  • Image the vessels/lesion (CTA ± MRI/MRV) in the young, normotensive, lobar, or atypical bleed — don't miss an operable AVM, aneurysm, tumor, or venous cause.
  • CTA "spot sign" predicts hematoma expansion.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Confirm ICH on CT; record volume/IVH/location.
  2. Run the INTERACT3 bundle (BP <140, reversal, glucose, temp) within 1 h, hold 7 days.
  3. Reverse the anticoagulant by agent (below).
  4. Neurosurgery for cerebellar/hydrocephalus/large supratentorial; EVD for IVH/hydrocephalus.
  5. ICU supportive care — ICP, DVT prophylaxis, glucose/temp, dysphagia, no routine seizure ppx.
  6. Work up the cause; plan secondary prevention (BP is everything) and antithrombotic-resumption.

docs.title: Blood pressure
docs.text:
BP target — SBP <140, and don't overshoot

  • Target SBP <140 within 1 h, then keep 130–150 for ≥7 days. Lowering below 130 is potentially harmful.
  • BP variability is independently harmful (INTERACT2/ATACH-2) — so use a titratable infusion (nicardipine or clevidipine) for smooth, sustained control, not intermittent boluses. Add labetalol only as needed. Use an arterial line for moderate/large bleeds.
  • Avoid venous vasodilators — nitroprusside, nitroglycerin, hydralazine — they can raise ICP, impair hemostasis, and give unpredictable control.

docs.title: Anticoagulation & antiplatelet reversal
docs.text:
Reverse by agent — the highest-yield decision in the room

On this agent Give (dose) Warfarin / VKA 4-factor PCC, INR-based (Kcentra: INR 2–<4 → 25 units/kg; 4–6 → 35; >6 → 50; max 5000 units) + vitamin K 10 mg IV (slow infusion). PCC preferred over FFP — faster, lower volume Dabigatran Idarucizumab 5 g IV (two 2.5 g vials) Apixaban / rivaroxaban / edoxaban (factor Xa inhibitors) 4-factor PCC 50 units/kg (fixed ~2000 units is an alternative) — now the primary U.S. reversal agent. (Consider activated charcoal if last dose within ~2–4 h.) Unfractionated heparin Protamine 1 mg IV per 100 units of heparin given in the last ~2–3 h (max 50 mg/dose; give slow — hypotension/anaphylaxis) LMWH (enoxaparin) Protamine 1 mg IV per 1 mg enoxaparin if within 8 h (0.5 mg/mg if >8 h; max 50 mg) — LMWH only ~60% reversed Antiplatelet (aspirin/clopidogrel) Do NOT transfuse platelets — PATCH showed harm — unless a neurosurgical procedure is planned; DDAVP may be considered

Andexanet alfa (Andexxa) was WITHDRAWN from the U.S. market — 22 Dec 2025

The FDA determined the excess thrombotic risk outweighed benefit (thrombotic events ~10% vs 5.6%; more ischemic strokes and MIs), and AstraZeneca withdrew it. It is no longer available in the U.S. — use 4-factor PCC for factor Xa-inhibitor-associated ICH. (ANNEXA-I showed better hemostasis but the benefit was in small expansions only, with no mortality/ disability benefit and nearly doubled thrombosis.) A retrospective comparison found PCC had lower thrombotic risk (4.2% vs 7.9%) with similar hemostasis once severe injuries were excluded. Andexanet retains conditional EMA approval in Europe (apixaban/rivaroxaban).

Recheck coagulation after reversal; goal INR <1.5. Recheck a CT at ~6 h for expansion.

Tranexamic acid — not standard of care

TXA does not improve functional outcome in unselected ICH (TICH-2, STOP-AUST — at most a marginal reduction in expansion). It may reduce expansion in CTA spot-sign-positive patients (meta-analysis OR ~0.65), and TICH-3 (early-mortality endpoint) is ongoing — but don't give it routinely outside a trial.

docs.title: Surgery
docs.text:
Neurosurgical decisions

  • Cerebellar hemorrhage >3 cm, or with brainstem compression / hydrocephalus → emergent surgical evacuation (Class 1). Do not temporize these with an EVD alone.
  • Hydrocephalus / large IVH → external ventricular drain (EVD).
  • Minimally invasive evacuation — benefit is LOCATION-dependent:
    • Lobar or anterior basal-ganglia clot, ~30–80 mL, early → refer for trans-sulcal parafascicular surgery: ENRICH (2024) was positive for these locations.
    • Deep bleeds → don't expect benefit: MIND (2025, Artemis device, 70% deep) was negative at 180 days — early disability gains faded. So MIS is for the lobar/anterior basal-ganglia phenotype, not routine deep ICH.
  • Routine open craniotomy is not beneficial for most supratentorial ICH (STICH/STICH II negative; MISTIE III neutral).
  • Severe deep ICH (30–100 mL): decompressive craniectomy without clot evacuation showed a trend toward less death/severe disability in SWITCH (2024; RR 0.77, p=0.057) but was underpowered and most survivors remained severely disabled — reasonable to consider in selected patients, with frank goals-of-care discussion.
  • Deteriorating / herniating: osmotherapy (hypertonic saline or mannitol), head up, treat the cause; decompression is a salvage option.

docs.title: Supportive care
docs.text:
ICU bundle

  • Glucose to target; temperature <37.5 °C; euvolemia with isotonic fluids (no hypotonic).
  • Seizures: treat clinical/electrographic seizures; no routine prophylaxis. cEEG if unexplained depressed consciousness.
  • VTE prophylaxis: intermittent pneumatic compression from day 1; add pharmacologic prophylaxis after 24–48 h once the bleed is stable.
  • Dysphagia screen before PO; head of bed 30°; bowel/skin care; avoid steroids.
  • Don't limit care early — prognostic pessimism in the first 24–48 h is self-fulfilling; use a score to communicate, not to withdraw (below).

Prognosis — score it, but don't let it become a self-fulfilling prophecy

ICH score (0–6) — one point each unless noted: GCS 3–4 = 2, GCS 5–12 = 1 (GCS 13–15 = 0); volume ≥30 mL = 1; IVH present = 1; infratentorial origin = 1; age ≥80 = 1. Higher score → higher 30-day mortality.

  • Estimate volume fast with ABC/2 (A × B × C ÷ 2, in cm on CT) — the key input for the score and for surgical decisions.
  • Caveat: the ICH score was derived in cohorts with frequent early care limitation, so it overestimates mortality when aggressive care is given. The Max-ICH score was built to reduce that confounding and is better for treatment decisions. Give the patient 24–48 h of full care before any prognostic conversation.

docs.title: Secondary prevention & restarting antithrombotics
docs.text:
• Blood pressure control is the dominant lever — long-term <130/80 prevents recurrence (deep/hypertensive bleeds especially).
  • CAA (lobar, elderly, microbleeds) carries a high re-bleed risk — be cautious with any antithrombotic; avoid anticoagulation where possible.
  • Restarting anticoagulation after ICH (e.g., for AF) — genuinely uncertain, individualize. Observational and small-RCT data (COCROACH meta-analysis) suggest resuming an oral anticoagulant cuts ischemic events (HR ~0.27) but with a non-significant trend toward more re-bleeding; APACHE-AF and SoSTART showed high event rates either way and did not prove anticoagulation safe (SoSTART: 8% vs 4% recurrent ICH). Phase-3 trials (ASPIRE, ENRICH-AF, PRESTIGE-AF) are pending for a definitive answer.
    • If restarting, a common practice is to defer ~4–8 weeks and weigh the thrombotic indication against re-bleed risk.
    • Location matters: lobar / CAA bleeds carry a substantially higher re-bleed risk than deep/hypertensive bleeds — lean away from anticoagulation in CAA. LAAO is an option when anticoagulation is too risky.

TRICARE — ICH prescribing

Acute reversal agents (4F-PCC, idarucizumab, protamine, IV vitamin K) are inpatient/ pharmacy-stocked, not outpatient-formulary items. Andexanet alfa is no longer stocked — it was withdrawn from the U.S. market (Dec 2025) — so 4-factor PCC is the factor-Xa-inhibitor reversal agent in U.S. military pharmacies. Outpatient antihypertensives are generic/ formulary. Verify agent availability with your inpatient pharmacy.

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
ICU / neuro-critical-care for everyone; transfer to a center with neurosurgery and EVD capability if you don't have it. Early rehab once stable.

Military medicine — intracerebral hemorrhage

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03, DoDI 1332.18, and the relevant JTS neurosurgery/TBI CPGs before counseling.

  • Deployed: ICH forward is a neurosurgical-evacuation emergency — BP control, reversal if anticoagulated, airway, and urgent MEDEVAC; definitive care is rear.
  • Young service member with spontaneous ICH: hunt for a secondary cause (AVM, aneurysm, dAVF, CVST, sympathomimetic/stimulant use) — the etiology drives duty and the board.
  • Deployability / retention: DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b(1) names intracerebral (and subarachnoid) hemorrhage a below-retention cerebrovascular condition when it persists despite treatment and impairs duty (5.26.a); persistent sequelae — severe headaches, seizures, sensory/ motor or cognitive impairment — fall under 5.26.c (Army: AR 40-501 para 3-31f/3-31k). ICH with residual deficit, seizures, or an untreated vascular lesion is deployment-limiting and generally triggers the DES (MEB/PEB); document volume, deficit, etiology, and BP control.
  • Aeromedical: a history of ICH and most vascular lesions are disqualifying/waiver- dependent for flying duty.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• Care bundles beat single interventions. INTERACT3 (Lancet 2023) showed a goal-directed bundle — early intensive BP lowering (<140), glucose and temperature control, and rapid anticoagulation reversal, all within an hour and held 7 days — improved survival and function.
  • BP: aim <140, not lower — and smooth. Target SBP <140 (keep 130–150); <130 is potentially harmful. Variability is independently harmful — use a titratable infusion, not boluses, and avoid nitroprusside/nitroglycerin/hydralazine (ICP, hemostasis).
  • Reverse by agent — and note the big change: andexanet alfa was WITHDRAWN from the U.S. market (Dec 2025) for excess thrombosis. 4F-PCC is now the primary factor-Xa-inhibitor reversal agent; idarucizumab for dabigatran; 4F-PCC + vitamin K for VKA; protamine for heparin. Don't transfuse platelets for antiplatelet-associated ICH (PATCH = harm). TXA is not standard (TICH-2/STOP-AUST negative; TICH-3 ongoing).
  • Surgery is selective and location-dependent. Cerebellar >3 cm / brainstem compression / hydrocephalus → operate. Minimally invasive evacuation helps lobar/anterior basal-ganglia clots (ENRICH positive) but not deep ICH (MIND negative). SWITCH (2024): decompressive craniectomy for severe deep ICH trended toward benefit but was underpowered (most survivors severely disabled). Open craniotomy (STICH/STICH II) and MISTIE III were not beneficial.
  • Restarting anticoagulation is unresolved — COCROACH/APACHE-AF/SoSTART are inconclusive; ASPIRE/ENRICH-AF/PRESTIGE-AF pending. Lobar/CAA bleeds re-bleed more — lean away from anticoagulation.
  • Don't call it early — the ICH score overestimates mortality when full care is given (use Max-ICH for treatment decisions); prognostic nihilism is self-fulfilling.

Key sources: 2022 AHA/ASA spontaneous ICH guideline; INTERACT3 (bundle); FDA andexanet withdrawal (Dec 2025) + ANNEXA-I; PATCH (platelets); ENRICH (positive lobar) and MIND (negative deep) MIS trials; SWITCH (decompressive craniectomy); TICH-2/STOP-AUST (TXA); COCROACH/APACHE-AF/ SoSTART (anticoagulation resumption); ICH score / Max-ICH.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Greenberg SM, et al. 2022 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Spontaneous Intracerebral Hemorrhage: AHA/ASA. Stroke. 2022.
  2. Ma L, et al. The third Intensive Care Bundle with Blood Pressure Reduction in Acute Cerebral Haemorrhage (INTERACT3). Lancet. 2023.
  3. Connolly SJ, et al. Andexanet for Factor Xa Inhibitor–Associated Acute ICH (ANNEXA-I). NEJM. 2024; FDA Safety Communication — Update on the Safety of Andexxa (U.S. market withdrawal, 22 Dec 2025). Panos NG, et al. PCC vs andexanet for FXa-inhibitor ICH (Crit Care Med 2025) — PCC lower thrombotic risk.
  4. Surgery: Pradilla G, et al. ENRICH (NEJM 2024, positive lobar/anterior basal-ganglia); Arthur AS, et al. MIND (JAMA Neurol 2025, negative for deep ICH); Beck J, et al. SWITCH (Lancet 2024, decompressive craniectomy for severe deep ICH).
  5. Baharoglu MI, et al. Platelet transfusion in antiplatelet-associated ICH (PATCH) — harm. TXA: TICH-2, STOP-AUST (negative); TICH-3 (ongoing).
  6. Anticoagulation resumption: Al-Shahi Salman R, et al. COCROACH (Lancet Neurol 2023); APACHE-AF; SoSTART; ongoing ASPIRE / ENRICH-AF / PRESTIGE-AF.
  7. TRICARE / DoD: reversal agents inpatient-stocked; andexanet no longer available (withdrawn Dec 2025) → 4F-PCC; antihypertensives generic-formulary. Verify with inpatient pharmacy.

Verified July 2026: the ICH care bundle (INTERACT3), BP target SBP <140 with harm below 130 (smooth control, avoid variability and venous vasodilators), and agent-specific reversal follow the 2022 AHA/ASA guideline and later data. Andexanet alfa was withdrawn from the U.S. market on 22 Dec 2025 (FDA Safety Communication) — 4-factor PCC is now the primary factor-Xa-inhibitor reversal agent (confirmed via FDA). Surgery is location-dependent (ENRICH positive lobar; MIND negative deep; SWITCH craniectomy trend); TXA is not standard (TICH-2/STOP-AUST); anticoagulation resumption is unresolved (COCROACH/APACHE-AF/SoSTART; phase-3 pending); ICH score overestimates mortality under full care (Max-ICH). Retention confirmed against the source: DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b(1) (intracerebral/subarachnoid hemorrhage) and 5.26.c (persistent sequelae), Army AR 40-501 3-31f/k. TRICARE inpatient stocking of andexanet was not pinned to a citable source — verify locally.

docs.title: Stroke prevention — primary & secondary
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: two populations, one playbook run at different intensities — primary prevention (never had a stroke) and secondary prevention (prior ischemic stroke or TIA). Both are mostly relentless risk-factor control; hypertension is the single highest-yield lever in either column. Use the tabs below.

The three things that move the needle most

  1. Blood pressure — the #1 modifiable risk factor for stroke, primary and secondary.
  2. Antithrombotic matched to mechanism — antiplatelet vs anticoagulant (get this right).
  3. High-intensity statin to a low LDL — especially after an atherosclerotic event.

Everything else (glucose, weight, tobacco, diet, activity, AF detection) matters — but if you only nail three things, nail these.

Primary prevention (no prior stroke/TIA)Secondary prevention (prior ischemic stroke/TIA)

Who: anyone without a prior stroke/TIA — the goal is to prevent the first one. Anchored to the 2024 AHA/ASA Guideline for the Primary Prevention of Stroke (first update since 2014).

The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order):

  1. Estimate risk and set the frame with Life's Essential 8 (BP, lipids, glucose, weight, diet, activity, sleep, tobacco).
  2. Blood pressure to <130/80 — lifestyle + drugs. The biggest single lever.
  3. Statin by ASCVD risk (see lipids row).
  4. Screen for and anticoagulate AF by CHA₂DS₂-VASc.
  5. Treat diabetes/obesity — and use agents with proven stroke benefit (GLP-1 RA).
  6. Lifestyle — Mediterranean diet, activity, tobacco cessation, alcohol moderation.
  7. Screen women and address social determinants (below).
Risk factor Target / action Notes Hypertension <130/80 (stage 2, or stage 1 with high ASCVD risk) Lifestyle + antihypertensive; the dominant modifiable risk Lipids Statin by ACC/AHA eligibility: LDL ≥190, diabetes age 40–75, 10-yr ASCVD ≥7.5–20% (intermediate: use risk enhancers), or ≥20% (high) → moderate–high intensity Higher-risk → high-intensity Diabetes + high CV risk / CVD (A1c ≥7%) GLP-1 RA (semaglutide, dulaglutide) — Class 1 SUSTAIN-6 / REWIND cut stroke. SGLT2i is for HF/CKD — it is neutral on stroke, so don't pick it for stroke prevention Atrial fibrillation Anticoagulate (DOAC) — Class 1 if CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 (men) / ≥3 (women); Class 2a ("reasonable") at 1 (men) / 2 (women) DOAC over warfarin for non-valvular AF; not a hard cutoff Overweight/obesity Weight loss; consider GLP-1 Tobacco Cessation (counseling + pharmacotherapy) Diet Mediterranean diet + nuts/olive oil (PREDIMED) Physical activity ≥150 min/wk moderate Reduce sedentary time Alcohol Limit (no "cardioprotective" license) Salt substitution Potassium-based salt substitute — Class 2a SSaSS: ~22% relative stroke reduction (avoid in CKD/hyperkalemia risk) Inflammation (established CVD) Low-dose colchicine 0.5 mg/day — Class 2b (uncertain benefit) Stroke-specific trials neutral (CONVINCE, CHANCE-3); pooled benefit is coronary-driven. Avoid in symptomatic intracranial atherosclerosis (CHANCE-3 subgroup: possible harm)

Aspirin is NOT for routine primary prevention

Do not start aspirin to prevent a first stroke in most people — ASPREE, ARRIVE, and ASCEND showed the bleeding risk offsets the benefit. Specifically not recommended in diabetes, CKD, or adults >70 with CV risk factors. Reserve low-dose aspirin for narrowly selected high-ASCVD / low-bleeding-risk patients after a shared decision. Exception: in pregnancy, low-dose aspirin reduces preeclampsia in high-risk individuals (see below).

Women-specific prevention (new emphasis, 2024)

  • Screen everyone capable of pregnancy for modifiable risks, medical conditions, and social determinants (food insecurity, transportation).
  • Adverse pregnancy outcomes — preeclampsia, gestational HTN/diabetes, preterm birth — raise lifetime stroke risk; document them and manage vascular risk long-term.
  • Treat verified BP ≥160/110 in pregnancy or within 6 weeks postpartum; give low-dose aspirin to high-risk pregnancies to prevent preeclampsia.
  • Endometriosis, premature ovarian insufficiency, and early menopause are risk enhancers.

Social determinants of health

Screen for and address adverse SDOH wherever at-risk patients are seen — access, food security, transportation, and cost drive risk-factor control and are part of the guideline.

Who: anyone with a prior ischemic stroke or TIA — higher baseline risk, so higher-intensity control. Start it before discharge. Anchored to the 2021 AHA/ASA secondary-prevention guideline and later trials.

The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order):

  1. Define the mechanism (cardioembolic vs large-artery vs small-vessel vs ESUS) — it dictates the antithrombotic.
  2. Antithrombotic: antiplatelet or anticoagulant (never reflexively both).
  3. High-intensity statin → LDL <70 (± ezetimibe / PCSK9).
  4. BP <130/80.
  5. Diabetes / obesity — GLP-1 RA or SGLT2i where indicated.
  6. Mechanism-specific procedure (carotid, PFO, LAAO).
  7. Lifestyle + adherence (the intervention patients most often drop).

Every patient (start in hospital)

  • High-intensity statin — atorvastatin 80 mg (or rosuvastatin 20 mg, up to 40); target LDL <70 (TST); add ezetimibe → PCSK9 inhibitor to get there.
  • BP <130/80 once stable; restart/optimize antihypertensives.
  • Diabetes: A1c control; GLP-1 RA for its stroke benefit. SGLT2i is for HF/CKD — neutral on stroke (use it for those indications, not as a stroke-prevention drug).
  • Lifestyle: Mediterranean diet, activity, tobacco cessation, alcohol moderation, weight.
  • Adherence & follow-up — reconcile meds; this is where secondary prevention fails.

Antithrombotic — matched to mechanism:

Mechanism Antithrombotic Non-cardioembolic (most) Single antiplatelet — aspirin, clopidogrel, or ASA-dipyridamole Minor stroke (NIHSS ≤3) / high-risk TIA (ABCD² ≥4) 21-day DAPT (ASA + clopidogrel), then single agent (CHANCE/POINT); ticagrelor+ASA (THALES) if CYP2C19 LOF — watch the extra severe-bleeding/ICH risk Symptomatic intracranial atherosclerosis 90-day DAPT + aggressive risk-factor control (SAMMPRIS); avoid stenting first-line Cardioembolic (AF) DOAC (apixaban/rivaroxaban/dabigatran/edoxaban) over warfarin Symptomatic carotid 70–99% (or 50–69% selected patients) CEA or CAS within 2 weeks + medical therapy — greater benefit in men; less in women and retinal-only events ESUS / cryptogenic Antiplatelet (not anticoagulation — see box) + prolonged rhythm monitoring (≥30-day external or implantable monitor; 24–48 h telemetry misses most paroxysmal AF); anticoagulate only if AF is found PFO, age <60, otherwise-cryptogenic PFO closure (RESPECT/CLOSE/REDUCE) + antiplatelet — use the PASCAL classification to select ("probable" benefits; "unlikely" does not)

CYP2C19 genotyping is moving into practice

Clopidogrel is a prodrug: ~25% of White and ~60% of East Asian patients carry a CYP2C19 loss-of-function variant and under-activate it. Genotyping is increasingly used to guide antiplatelet choice — if a LOF variant is found, ticagrelor + ASA is preferred over clopidogrel + ASA for the minor-stroke/high-risk-TIA window (CHANCE-2).

AF timing — start the DOAC early

Don't sit on the old rigid "1-3-6-12" wait. The CATALYST meta-analysis (2025) shows early DOAC (≤4 days) reduces recurrence with no excess sICH. Individualize the exact day on infarct size / hemorrhagic transformation (sooner for small strokes, a touch later for large infarcts). LAAO if anticoagulation is truly contraindicated.

  • Stroke despite adequate anticoagulation (OAC failure): LAAO is an emerging option — observational data (STR-OAC LAAO) show a large risk reduction (HR ~0.33) added to OAC, and surgical LAAO cut stroke on top of anticoagulation (LAAOS III). RCT confirmation is pending (CHAMPION-AF, OPTION, OCCLUSION-AF) — reasonable to consider, not yet proven.

Do NOT empirically anticoagulate ESUS

Embolic stroke of undetermined source is not an indication for empiric anticoagulation. Four RCTs — NAVIGATE ESUS, RE-SPECT ESUS, ARCADIA, ATTICUS — all failed to beat aspirin, and NAVIGATE ESUS was stopped early for excess bleeding (rivaroxaban). Per the 2021 AHA/ASA guideline: use an antiplatelet, and hunt for AF with prolonged monitoring — anticoagulate only if you actually find it.

When monitoring detects brief device AF, the episode duration decides (2023 ACC/AHA AF guideline): ≥24 h + CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 → anticoagulation reasonable (2a); 5 min–24 h + CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥3 → may be reasonable (2b); <5 min → do not anticoagulate (Class 3).

Don't double up without a reason

Long-term DAPT is not secondary prevention — after the 21-day (or 90-day ICAD) window, drop to a single agent; extended DAPT only adds bleeding. And don't combine an antiplatelet with an anticoagulant unless there's a separate, explicit indication.

On the horizon — factor XIa inhibition (watch this space)

OCEANIC-STROKE (NEJM 2026) is the first positive phase-3 trial of a new antithrombotic class for stroke prevention in over a decade: asundexian 50 mg/day (a factor XIa inhibitor) added to antiplatelet therapy for non-cardioembolic stroke / high-risk TIA cut ischemic stroke 26% (HR 0.74) with no increase in major bleeding — the long-sought uncoupling of antithrombotic efficacy from bleeding risk. Not yet guideline-incorporated, and the companion milvexian trial (LIBREXIA-STROKE) is pending — but this may reshape non-cardioembolic prevention. Don't prescribe it off-study yet; know it's coming.

docs.title: The full acute-stroke workup and reperfusion algorithm
docs.text:
For the code-stroke side — last-known-well, imaging, thrombolysis, and thrombectomy — see the acute ischemic stroke page. This page is the prevention companion; secondary prevention is started during that admission and owned in clinic afterward.

TRICARE — prevention prescribing

  • Statins (atorvastatin/rosuvastatin), aspirin, and clopidogrel are generic/formulary, no PA — the backbone of both columns.
  • DOACs are covered; a preferred DOAC exists on the Uniform Formulary — verify the current preferred agent and any PA at the TRICARE Formulary Search.
  • GLP-1 RAs and PCSK9 inhibitors are typically PA-gated (indication/step-therapy) — plan for the prior authorization.
  • Ezetimibe is generic/formulary and an easy add-on toward LDL <70.

Military medicine — stroke prevention

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03, and aeromedical policy.

  • Risk-factor control is a readiness issue: untreated HTN, tobacco, and obesity drive both stroke risk and profile/PHA findings — treat aggressively.
  • Anticoagulation is deployment- and duty-limiting: AF on a DOAC, or a mechanical valve on warfarin, complicates deployment and many special-duty positions — factor it into MEB/profile discussions.
  • Aeromedical: anticoagulation and most post-stroke states are disqualifying/waiver- dependent for flying duty; confirm the standard for the class involved.
  • Young service members: don't anchor on "too young for stroke" — pursue dissection, PFO, hypercoagulable states, and substance use; the etiology drives the prevention plan.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• Primary prevention got its first refresh since 2014 (2024 AHA/ASA). Headlines: aspirin is out for routine primary prevention (ASPREE/ARRIVE/ASCEND); GLP-1 receptor agonists are in (Class 1) for diabetes with high CV risk (SUSTAIN-6/REWIND); BP <130/80; the Life's Essential 8 framework; potassium salt substitution (Class 2a, SSaSS) and low-dose colchicine (Class 2b) as newer additions; and explicit emphasis on women's risk (adverse pregnancy outcomes) and social determinants of health.
  • Know what does NOT reduce stroke. SGLT2 inhibitors are stroke-neutral (use them for HF/CKD, not for stroke); and empiric anticoagulation for ESUS failed in four RCTs (NAVIGATE ESUS, RE-SPECT ESUS, ARCADIA, ATTICUS) — treat ESUS with an antiplatelet and hunt for AF.
  • Secondary prevention is mechanism-driven. Antiplatelet for non-cardioembolic (short DAPT only in the CHANCE/POINT window; 90-day DAPT for ICAD per SAMMPRIS), DOAC for AF started early (CATALYST), carotid revascularization for symptomatic high-grade stenosis within 2 weeks, and PFO closure under 60 (select with the PASCAL classification).
  • Lipids: lower is better after atherosclerotic stroke — high-intensity statin to LDL <70 (TST), escalating with ezetimibe/PCSK9.
  • Blood pressure is the common denominator — the highest-yield lever in both columns.
  • On the horizon (not yet guideline): factor XIa inhibition — OCEANIC-STROKE (asundexian) cut ischemic stroke 26% with no excess bleeding in non-cardioembolic prevention (LIBREXIA/milvexian pending); CYP2C19 genotyping to steer clopidogrel vs ticagrelor (CHANCE-2); LAAO for OAC failure (observational, RCTs pending); and duration-based anticoagulation for device-detected subclinical AF. Colchicine is stroke-neutral in stroke-specific trials (CONVINCE, CHANCE-3) and possibly harmful in intracranial atherosclerosis — the pooled "benefit" is coronary-driven.

Key sources: 2024 AHA/ASA Primary Prevention of Stroke guideline; 2021 AHA/ASA Secondary Prevention guideline; PREDIMED (Mediterranean diet); ASPREE/ARRIVE/ASCEND (aspirin); SUSTAIN-6/REWIND (GLP-1); SSaSS (salt substitution); CHANCE/POINT/THALES (DAPT); SAMMPRIS (ICAD); NAVIGATE ESUS/ RE-SPECT ESUS/ARCADIA (ESUS negatives); CATALYST/ELAN (AF timing); TST (LDL target); NASCET (carotid); RESPECT/CLOSE/REDUCE + PASCAL (PFO); 2024 ACC arrhythmia-monitoring-after-stroke pathway.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Bushnell C, et al. 2024 Guideline for the Primary Prevention of Stroke: AHA/ASA. Stroke. 2024.
  2. Kleindorfer DO, et al. 2021 Guideline for the Prevention of Stroke in Patients With Stroke and TIA: AHA/ASA. Stroke. 2021.
  3. DAPT / antiplatelet: CHANCE; POINT; THALES; SAMMPRIS (intracranial atherosclerosis).
  4. AF timing: the CATALYST individual-patient meta-analysis (Lancet. 2025); ELAN.
  5. ESUS (all negative for empiric anticoagulation): NAVIGATE ESUS; RE-SPECT ESUS; ARCADIA; ATTICUS.
  6. PFO: RESPECT/CLOSE/REDUCE; PASCAL classification (Kent et al.) for patient selection. Arrhythmia monitoring: 2024 ACC Expert Consensus Pathway (≥30-day/implantable for cryptogenic).
  7. Lipids: Treat Stroke to Target (TST) — LDL <70 mg/dL after ischemic stroke.
  8. Newer primary-prevention additions: SSaSS (potassium salt substitution); colchicine — with stroke-specific caveats from CONVINCE (Lancet 2024) and CHANCE-3 (BMJ 2024, sICAS harm signal).
  9. Emerging (not yet guideline): OCEANIC-STROKE — asundexian (FXIa inhibitor) for non-cardioembolic secondary prevention (NEJM 2026); LIBREXIA-STROKE (milvexian) pending. CHANCE-2 — ticagrelor+ASA in CYP2C19 LOF carriers. STR-OAC LAAO / LAAOS III — LAAO with OAC failure. 2023 ACC/AHA AF guideline — duration-tiered anticoagulation for device-detected AF.
  10. TRICARE / DoD Uniform Formulary: statins/aspirin/clopidogrel/ezetimibe generic-formulary; preferred DOAC + GLP-1/PCSK9 PA status — verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Verified July 2026: primary-prevention content (no routine aspirin per ASPREE/ARRIVE/ASCEND; GLP-1 RA Class 1 for diabetes + high CV risk; SGLT2i stroke-neutral; BP <130/80; Life's Essential 8; potassium salt substitution Class 2a / colchicine Class 2b; AF anticoagulation Class 1 at CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2/≥3 and Class 2a at 1/2; women/SDOH emphasis) follows the 2024 AHA/ASA Primary Prevention of Stroke guideline. Secondary-prevention antithrombotic-by-mechanism, LDL <70 (TST), 21-day DAPT (CHANCE/POINT), 90-day DAPT for ICAD (SAMMPRIS), early DOAC (CATALYST), carotid within 2 weeks, no empiric anticoagulation for ESUS (NAVIGATE ESUS/RE-SPECT ESUS/ARCADIA/ATTICUS negative), and PFO closure <60 selected by PASCAL (RESPECT/CLOSE/REDUCE) are current. Emerging items (OCEANIC-STROKE/asundexian, CHANCE-2/CYP2C19, LAAO-for-OAC-failure, device-AF duration thresholds, colchicine stroke-specific data) are flagged as not-yet-guideline and their trial figures were supplied by expert review — confirm before acting on them clinically. AR 40-501 stroke retention paragraph and the TRICARE preferred DOAC were not pinned to a citable source — verify before counseling.*

docs.title: Aneurysmal subarachnoid hemorrhage (SAH)
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: blood in the subarachnoid space, usually from a ruptured saccular aneurysm — the classic thunderclap "worst headache of my life." Two battles, weighted equally: secure the aneurysm fast (it re-bleeds and kills), then survive the next two weeks of delayed cerebral ischemia, hydrocephalus, and hyponatremia in a neuro-ICU.

🚩 Thunderclap headache = SAH until excluded

  • Sudden, severe, maximal-at-onset headache (± brief LOC, vomiting, neck stiffness, sentinel "warning" headache days earlier). Don't wait for a stiff neck or a deficit.
  • Non-contrast CT first — near-100% sensitive within 6 h of onset.
  • CT negative but story convincing → LP for xanthochromia (or CTA) — a normal early CT does not fully exclude SAH after 6 h.
  • Confirmed SAH → CTA/DSA to find the aneurysm, and transfer to a high-volume neurovascular center now — outcomes are volume- and team-dependent.

docs.title: Rapid approach
docs.text:
1. Diagnose: non-contrast CT → LP if CT negative and suspicion high → CTA/DSA for the aneurysm.
  2. Grade severity (Hunt-Hess / WFNS) and bleed burden (modified Fisher → vasospasm risk).
  3. Prevent re-bleeding before securing: control BP, calm, analgesia, reverse anticoagulation.
  4. Secure the aneurysm within 24 h — coil vs clip, decided by a neurovascular team.
  5. Start nimodipine and admit to a neuro-ICU for the DCI watch.
  6. Manage complications: hydrocephalus (EVD), delayed cerebral ischemia, hyponatremia, seizures.

docs.title: Grade it — score every patient on arrival
docs.text:
Two different jobs, so score both: a clinical grade (Hunt-Hess or WFNS) captures how sick the patient is at presentation and predicts mortality/outcome and prognosis; a radiographic grade (modified Fisher) measures the blood burden on CT and predicts vasospasm / delayed cerebral ischemia (DCI). "Poor grade" = Hunt-Hess 4–5 or WFNS 4–5 — worse prognosis, but still treat aggressively (benefit of securing + lumbar drainage holds across grades).

Clinical — Hunt-Hess (1–5): severity → prognosisClinical — WFNS (1–5): GCS-based, more objectiveRadiographic — modified Fisher (0–4): blood → vasospasm/DCI risk Grade Clinical findings 1 Asymptomatic, or mild headache + slight nuchal rigidity 2 Moderate–severe headache, nuchal rigidity; no deficit except a cranial-nerve palsy 3 Drowsiness / confusion, or mild focal deficit 4 Stupor, moderate–severe hemiparesis, early decerebrate posturing 5 Coma, decerebrate posturing / moribund

Add +1 grade for serious systemic disease (HTN, diabetes, severe atherosclerosis, COPD) or severe angiographic vasospasm. Higher grade → higher mortality.

Grade GCS Motor deficit 1 15 No 2 13–14 No 3 13–14 Yes 4 7–12 ± (either) 5 3–6 ± (either)

WFNS is reproducible (built on the GCS) and is the scale the 2023 guideline leans on. Grades 1–2 are "good grade," 4–5 "poor grade."

Grade CT appearance Approx. symptomatic-vasospasm risk 0 No SAH and no IVH ~0% 1 Thin SAH (<1 mm), no IVH low (~6–24%) 2 Thin SAH + IVH intermediate 3 Thick SAH (≥1 mm), no IVH high (~33%) 4 Thick SAH + IVH highest (~40%)

"Thick" = cisternal/fissural blood ≥1 mm (filling a cistern/fissure). The modified Fisher improved on the original by counting IVH and by rising monotonically with risk — use it to anticipate the DCI watch, not to decide whether to secure the aneurysm.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. CT → (LP if needed) → CTA/DSA; confirm and localize the aneurysm.
  2. Pre-securing: BP control, analgesia/antiemetics, reverse anticoagulation, bed rest.
  3. Secure the aneurysm <24 h (coil preferred when equivalent — ISAT; clip for some MCA/wide-neck).
  4. Nimodipine 60 mg PO q4h × 21 days.
  5. Neuro-ICU: CSF diversion (lumbar drain preferred — EARLYDRAIN; EVD for hydrocephalus); monitor for DCI days 4–14; manage Na⁺, seizures, fever, Hb.
  6. DCI management: euvolemia → induced hypertension → endovascular rescue (± IV milrinone).

docs.title: Before the aneurysm is secured
docs.text:
Prevent the re-bleed (highest risk in the first hours)

  • Blood pressure: avoid hypertensive surges — a common target is SBP <160 (or MAP-guided) until secured; use a titratable IV agent (nicardipine/clevidipine/labetalol). Balance against cerebral perfusion.
  • Reverse any anticoagulation — 4-factor PCC for warfarin and for factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban/rivaroxaban; andexanet alfa was withdrawn from the U.S. market in Dec 2025), idarucizumab for dabigatran, protamine for heparin (see the ICH reversal table). Treat pain and agitation (both spike BP).
  • Secure early — definitive re-bleed prevention is treating the aneurysm within 24 h.
  • Short-course antifibrinolytic (tranexamic acid) before securing is not routinely recommended — ULTRA showed no functional benefit; reserve for special circumstances.

docs.title: Secure the aneurysm
docs.text:
Coil vs clip — a neurovascular-team decision, done early

  • Endovascular coiling is preferred when the aneurysm is amenable to both (ISAT: better functional survival) — especially posterior circulation and older/poorer-grade patients.
  • Surgical clipping for wide-neck, large MCA, or clot-with-mass-effect aneurysms, or where coiling isn't durable.
  • Do it within 24 h at a high-volume center with both capabilities.

docs.title: Nimodipine & the delayed-cerebral-ischemia watch
docs.text:
After securing — the two-week ICU course

  • Nimodipine 60 mg PO q4h × 21 days — Class 1; improves outcome (neuroprotective; it does not reliably reverse angiographic vasospasm). If it drops BP, split to 30 mg q2h.
  • DCI surveillance (days 4–14): serial exams ± transcranial Doppler/CTP; maintain euvolemia (avoid prophylactic hypervolemia / old "triple-H").
  • Prophylactic CSF drainage lowers DCI — and a lumbar drain is preferred: EARLYDRAIN (RCT, all grades) — lumbar drainage 5 mL/h started within 72 h cut unfavorable outcome by ~12% absolute and secondary infarction by ~11%; higher lumbar-drain volumes tracked with better outcomes (and higher EVD volumes with worse). Now the preferred CSF-diversion strategy for DCI prevention in suitable patients (no mass lesion / herniation risk).
  • Symptomatic DCI → induced hypertension (vasopressors to raise BP); refractory → endovascular intra-arterial vasodilators / balloon angioplasty. IV milrinone is an emerging adjunct (retrospective data: less delayed infarction and less need for endovascular rescue; no RCT — the 2023 guideline calls it "promising," not standard).
  • Hydrocephalus → EVD (common; acute or delayed).
  • Transfusion: SAHARA (NEJM 2025) found no significant difference between liberal (Hb ≤10) and restrictive (Hb ≤8) thresholds, though pooled neuro-ICU data (TRAIN/HEMOTION) trend toward liberal. Reasonable to transfuse at Hb ≤9–10, especially during the DCI-risk window.
  • Hyponatremia (cerebral salt wasting / SIADH) is common — treat with isotonic/hypertonic saline; do NOT fluid-restrict (worsens DCI). Fludrocortisone for salt wasting.
  • Seizures: treat clinical seizures with an ASM for ≤7 days (Class 1); avoid phenytoin (worse cognitive outcomes — use levetiracetam). Prophylaxis is reasonable (Class 2b) only in high-risk patients — HH ≥3, MCA aneurysm, intraparenchymal hemorrhage, hydrocephalus, or cortical infarct. cEEG for depressed/fluctuating consciousness (nonconvulsive seizures). Watch for neurogenic stunned myocardium and fever.

Explicitly NOT recommended after SAH (don't extrapolate from ischemic-stroke protocols)

The 2023 AHA/ASA guideline recommends against routine statin therapy and against IV magnesium after aSAH — don't start a statin reflexively here. Clazosentan (endothelin antagonist) reduces angiographic vasospasm but did not improve functional outcomes internationally (REACT negative; approved in Japan only) — not for routine use. Cilostazol 100–300 mg/day is a promising oral adjunct (meta-analyses: less vasospasm, infarction, and unfavorable outcome) but the evidence is small and predominantly Asian — not yet guideline-endorsed.

TRICARE — SAH prescribing

Nimodipine is generic/formulary — the one drug you must not skip. Acute BP agents and reversal products are inpatient-stocked. Aneurysm securing (coil/clip) is inpatient neurovascular care.

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
High-volume neuro-ICU with 24-h neurovascular (endovascular and neurosurgery) coverage. Transfer immediately if you can't secure the aneurysm and manage DCI locally. Long rehab and outpatient aneurysm/imaging follow-up.

Military medicine — subarachnoid hemorrhage

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03, DoDI 1332.18, and JTS CPGs before counseling.

  • Deployed: a thunderclap headache in theater with no CT/neurosurgery is an urgent MEDEVAC — control BP, analgesia, airway; definitive securing is rear at a neurovascular center.
  • Deployability / retention: DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b names subarachnoid hemorrhage (b(1)) and aneurysm (b(4)) below-retention cerebrovascular conditions when they persist despite treatment and impair duty (5.26.a); persistent sequelae (severe headaches, seizures, cognitive/motor deficit) fall under 5.26.c — Army AR 40-501 3-31f/k. A ruptured aneurysm and its recovery are non-deployable; residual deficit, seizures, or an unsecured/untreated aneurysm generally trigger the DES (MEB/PEB). A secured aneurysm with full recovery may return to duty per specialty review.
  • Aeromedical: a history of SAH / intracranial aneurysm is disqualifying/waiver-dependent for flying duty; confirm the standard for the class involved.
  • Screening context: family history and unruptured-aneurysm findings raise counseling/fitness questions — refer to neurovascular.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• Two theaters, weighted equally (2023 AHA/ASA paradigm). Securing the aneurysm early and neuro-critical-care management of DCI both determine outcome — manage at high-volume centers.
  • Diagnose fast: non-contrast CT is near-100% sensitive <6 h; after that, LP for xanthochromia (or CTA) if the story fits. Then CTA/DSA for the aneurysm.
  • Secure within 24 h; coiling is generally preferred where equivalent (ISAT), clipping for selected anatomy.
  • Nimodipine 60 mg q4h × 21 days is Class 1 — improves outcome (neuroprotection, not vasospasm reversal).
  • DCI (days 4–14): euvolemia → induced hypertension → endovascular rescue (± emerging IV milrinone); abandon prophylactic triple-H. Treat hyponatremia with salt, never fluid restriction.
  • New: prophylactic lumbar drainage prevents DCI. EARLYDRAIN (2023) — lumbar drainage 5 mL/h within 72 h cut unfavorable outcome ~12% absolute; a lumbar drain beats an EVD for this, and it's now the preferred CSF-diversion strategy in suitable patients.
  • Transfusion is a toss-up (lean slightly liberal). SAHARA (2025) found no significant difference between Hb ≤10 and Hb ≤8 thresholds; pooled data trend toward liberal — transfuse ~Hb ≤9–10.
  • Know what NOT to do: routine statins and IV magnesium are not recommended; clazosentan fails to improve function (REACT; Japan-only); cilostazol is a promising but unproven adjunct.
  • Re-bleed prevention is early securing — routine antifibrinolytics are not recommended (ULTRA negative).

Key sources: 2023 AHA/ASA aneurysmal SAH guideline; ISAT (coil vs clip); ULTRA (tranexamic acid); nimodipine trials; EARLYDRAIN (lumbar drainage); SAHARA (transfusion); REACT (clazosentan); milrinone and cilostazol series; modified Fisher / Hunt-Hess grading.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Hoh BL, et al. 2023 Guideline for the Management of Patients With Aneurysmal Subarachnoid Hemorrhage: AHA/ASA. Stroke. 2023.
  2. Molyneux A, et al. International Subarachnoid Aneurysm Trial (ISAT) — coiling vs clipping.
  3. Post R, et al. Ultra-early tranexamic acid in aSAH (ULTRA) — no functional benefit.
  4. Pickard JD, et al. Nimodipine in SAH — improved outcome (basis for the 21-day course).
  5. Grading scales: Hunt WE, Hess RM (1968, clinical grade); Teasdale GM, et al. WFNS scale (GCS-based); Frontera JA, et al. Modified Fisher scale — cisternal blood/IVH → symptomatic vasospasm risk.
  6. Wolf S, et al. Lumbar CSF drainage in aSAH (EARLYDRAIN). JAMA Neurol. 2023 — prophylactic lumbar drainage improved 6-month outcome.
  7. English SW, et al. Transfusion thresholds in aSAH (SAHARA). NEJM. 2025 — liberal vs restrictive, no significant difference; pooled neuro-ICU data (TRAIN/HEMOTION) trend liberal.
  8. REACT (clazosentan, negative internationally; approved Japan only); milrinone retrospective cohorts; cilostazol meta-analyses (small, predominantly Asian).
  9. TRICARE / DoD: nimodipine generic-formulary; acute BP/reversal agents inpatient-stocked. Verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Verified July 2026: diagnostic pathway (non-contrast CT <6 h → LP/CTA), early aneurysm securing with coiling generally preferred (ISAT), nimodipine 60 mg q4h × 21 days (Class 1), euvolemia + induced-hypertension DCI management, salt (not restriction) for hyponatremia, and no routine antifibrinolytics (ULTRA) follow the 2023 AHA/ASA aneurysmal SAH guideline; pre-securing BP target is SBP ≤160 (an SAH target — the ICH INTERACT3 <140 bundle does not apply here). Newer additions — EARLYDRAIN (prophylactic lumbar drainage), SAHARA (transfusion), milrinone, clazosentan (REACT negative / Japan-only), cilostazol, and the seizure-prophylaxis nuance (≤7-day treatment, Class 2b prophylaxis in high-risk, avoid phenytoin) — were supplied by expert review with citations; confirm the specific figures before relying on them. Retention confirmed against the source: DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b(1) (subarachnoid hemorrhage) and b(4) (aneurysm), gated by 5.26.a, Army AR 40-501 3-31f/k.*

docs.title: Transient ischemic attack (TIA)
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: a transient focal neurologic deficit from brain/retinal ischemia without infarction — a warning shot, not a benign event. The stroke that follows is preventable, and the risk is front-loaded to the first 48 hours, so a TIA is a same-day emergency work-up, not a "come back to clinic next week."

🚩 A TIA is a treated emergency — act in hours, not days

  • Definition is tissue-based: transient deficit with no infarction on imaging. If MRI shows a matching DWI lesion, it's a stroke (treat/secondary-prevent as such), not a TIA.
  • Highest recurrence is immediate — a real TIA carries a substantial 90-day stroke risk, concentrated in the first 2 days. Rapid work-up + prevention cuts that risk by up to ~80%.
  • Exclude mimics: seizure/Todd's, migraine aura (spreading, positive symptoms), hypoglycemia, syncope/pre-syncope, functional. TIA is sudden, maximal, focal, negative (loss of function).

docs.title: Rapid approach
docs.text:
1. Confirm it's vascular and transient (sudden, focal, negative symptoms; fully resolved).
  2. Risk-stratify (ABCD² — below) to decide admit vs. expedited-outpatient.
  3. Same-day work-up — MRI, vessel imaging, ECG/telemetry, echo (order set).
  4. Start secondary prevention immediately — don't wait for the work-up to finish.
  5. Find the mechanism and treat it (AF → anticoagulate; carotid → revascularize).

docs.title: Risk-stratify — ABCD² (and beyond)
docs.text:
ABCD² factor Points Age ≥60 1 BP ≥140/90 at presentation 1 Clinical: unilateral weakness 2 / speech disturbance without weakness 1 1–2 Duration ≥60 min 2 / 10–59 min 1 1–2 Diabetes 1

2-day stroke risk rises with score (~1% at 0–3, ~4% at 4–5, ~8% at 6–7). ABCD²-I adds carotid stenosis ≥50% and a positive DWI (2 points each) — a positive DWI or a high score means higher risk → admit / urgent work-up.**

Image everyone — don't let ABCD² gatekeep (the 2023 AHA/ASA ED statement retired it as a triage tool)

Clinical scores lack the sensitivity/specificity to safely identify who can skip urgent evaluation — ~20% of patients with ABCD² <4 still had a serious finding (significant carotid stenosis, intracranial stenosis, or AF) on work-up. So the 2023 AHA/ASA ED-TIA statement says give every TIA patient urgent brain + neurovascular imaging regardless of score, and let imaging + mechanism drive disposition, not the number.

  • Never send home a crescendo/recurrent TIA, a carotid/AF source, or a positive DWI — high-risk regardless of score.
  • The Canadian TIA Score (incorporates AF, carotid stenosis, DWI) out-discriminates ABCD² (AUC ~0.70 vs ~0.60; validated in >7,600 patients) — a better tool if you use one.

docs.title: The prescriptive flow (what I want done, in order)
docs.text:
1. Confirm TIA vs stroke vs mimic; get a glucose and a focused exam.
  2. Risk-stratify (ABCD², dual/crescendo pattern, known AF/carotid disease).
  3. Complete the same-day work-up (order set).
  4. Start antithrombotic + statin now (below) — before results are all back.
  5. Mechanism-directed prevention: AF → early DOAC; symptomatic carotid → revascularize within 2 weeks; ESUS → monitoring.
  6. Disposition: admit high-risk; otherwise a rapid-access TIA pathway with prevention started.

docs.title: Same-day work-up
docs.text:
Order it all today (a rapid TIA pathway cuts recurrent stroke ~80%)

  • MRI brain (DWI) — up to half of clinical "TIAs" show infarction (reclassifies to stroke and changes prognosis). CT if MRI unavailable. A negative early DWI doesn't fully reassure — false-negative rates run ~22–32% when MRI is done within ~3–4.5 h; if suspicion is high, image after ~6 h or repeat.
  • Vessel imaging — CTA or MRA of head and neck — find symptomatic carotid stenosis and dissection/intracranial disease.
  • ECG + telemetry / prolonged monitoring — hunt for atrial fibrillation (extend monitoring if cryptogenic).
  • Echocardiography for a cardioembolic source; labs (glucose, lipids, CBC, coags, A1c).

docs.title: Start secondary prevention immediately
docs.text:
Antithrombotic + statin — start today (don't wait for the work-up)

  • High-risk TIA (ABCD² ≥4) or minor stroke: 21-day DAPT. Aspirin + clopidogrel (clopidogrel 300–600 mg load → 75 mg/day; ASA 81 mg) for 21 days, then single agent — benefit is concentrated in the first 21 days (CHANCE/POINT); longer DAPT only adds bleeding.
    • DAPT is for the non-reperfusion, non-revascularization patient — the trials excluded those for whom thrombolysis, thrombectomy, or carotid revascularization was indicated. Sort out the reperfusion/carotid question first.
    • The window is wider than you think — up to 72 h. INSPIRES (NEJM 2023, China, n≈6,100) showed clopidogrel+aspirin started within 72 h still cut 90-day stroke (7.3% vs 9.2%) at the cost of more (still low) bleeding — a patient seen 1–3 days out can still benefit (Asian-population data; generalizability less certain, as with CHANCE).
    • Ticagrelor + aspirin (THALES) is an alternative — but flag the bleeding signal: it cut stroke/death (5.5% vs 6.6%) at the cost of more fatal bleeding/ICH (0.4% vs 0.1%). It's preferred in CYP2C19 loss-of-function carriers (CHANCE-2; LOF present in ~25% of White and ~60% of East Asian patients; benefit concentrated in the first week and in lower-risk carriers), if genotyping is available.
  • Lower-risk TIA: single antiplatelet — aspirin or clopidogrel (ASA-dipyridamole is no longer preferred/widely available).
  • High-intensity statin — atorvastatin 80 mg, target LDL <70 (TST/2021 AHA) — escalate ezetimibe → PCSK9 inhibitor to get there. (European ESC/EAS guidance targets <55 for very-high-risk ASCVD, which includes most atherosclerotic stroke/TIA — a stricter goal worth knowing.)
  • Blood pressure control toward <130/80 once stable.
  • Full detail + the mechanism-by-mechanism playbook: Stroke prevention page.
Atrial fibrillationSymptomatic carotid stenosisESUS / cryptogenic

Anticoagulate — a DOAC (over warfarin for non-valvular AF), and start early — after a TIA (no infarct) begin within a day or two. CATALYST (Lancet 2025, IPD meta-analysis, n=5,441) confirms early DOAC (≤4 days) even after ischemic stroke lowers the 30-day composite (2.1% vs 3.0%, OR 0.70) with no excess sICH — so don't sit on it. LAAO if anticoagulation is contraindicated.

70–99% (or 50–69% in selected patients): revascularize within 2 weeks (CEA first-line; CAS/ TCAR in selected anatomy) + medical therapy — see the carotid disease page. (In low-to-intermediate-risk symptomatic or asymptomatic ≥50% stenosis, ECST-2 found no benefit of revascularization over optimized medical therapy — the benefit tracks predicted risk.)

  • Antiplatelet, NOT empiric anticoagulation — NAVIGATE ESUS and RE-SPECT ESUS were negative; don't anticoagulate (or use ticagrelor) empirically for ESUS.
  • Prolonged rhythm monitoring — an insertable cardiac monitor finds AF in ~12–15% at 12 months (vs ~2–5% with external monitors); worth it even in non-cryptogenic stroke (STROKE-AF: ~12%). Anticoagulate by device-AF duration thresholds (≥24 h + CHA₂DS₂-VASc ≥2 → anticoagulate; <5 min → don't) — per the 2024 ACC arrhythmia-monitoring pathway; full detail on the prevention page.
  • PFO closure for age <60 with an otherwise-cryptogenic embolic event and high-risk PFO features (use the PASCAL classification).

docs.title: Disposition
docs.text:
• Admit / observe with an expedited work-up for high-risk features: ABCD² ≥4, crescendo or dual TIA, known AF or symptomatic carotid stenosis, or a positive DWI.
  • Lower-risk patients can go through a rapid-access TIA clinic/pathway — only if same-day imaging and prevention are actually available; otherwise observe.
  • Either way, secondary prevention starts at first contact.
  • Structured follow-up pays off — a disease-management program (scheduled visits, risk-factor targets, support) cut major cardiovascular events at 12 months (STROKE-CARD: 5.4% vs 8.3%, HR 0.63). Book the follow-up and own the risk factors, don't just discharge on pills.

Military medicine — TIA

Framework — verify against current AR 40-501, DoDI 6130.03, and aeromedical policy.

  • Retention hinges on the work-up. DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b(6) places recurrent TIA below retention standards unless the underlying etiology is identified and definitively treated (gated by 5.26.a); Army implements at AR 40-501 para 3-31f. So a single TIA with a fully worked-up, definitively-treated cause (e.g., carotid revascularized, AF anticoagulated) can retain — an unexplained or recurrent TIA trends toward the DES (MEB/PEB). Document the etiology work-up, the treatment, and duty impact.
  • Deployability: non-deployable during work-up; a treated single-cause TIA may return to duty per specialty review; anticoagulation for AF is itself deployment/special-duty-limiting.
  • Aeromedical: a history of TIA is disqualifying/waiver-dependent for flying duty.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• TIA is defined by tissue, not time — transient deficit without infarction; a DWI lesion makes it a stroke. ~Half of clinical TIAs are actually infarcts on MRI.
  • The danger is immediate — highest stroke risk in the first 48 h, so same-day imaging + started prevention; rapid TIA pathways cut recurrent stroke ~80%.
  • 21-day DAPT for high-risk TIA/minor stroke (CHANCE/POINT), then single agent — and the window extends to 72 h (INSPIRES), so a patient seen days out still benefits; ticagrelor+ASA / CHANCE-2 for CYP2C19 LOF. Add high-intensity statin to LDL <70; BP <130/80.
  • ABCD² is effectively retired as a triage gate (2023 AHA/ASA ED statement) — ~20% of ABCD²<4 had serious findings; image everyone, let imaging + mechanism decide. The Canadian TIA Score (AUC ~0.70 vs ~0.60) is the better tool. A negative early DWI (<3–4.5 h) can be falsely reassuring (~22–32% false-negative) — repeat/delay if suspicious.
  • Sort out reperfusion/carotid before DAPT — DAPT eligibility assumes thrombolysis/thrombectomy/ carotid revascularization is not indicated. Ticagrelor+ASA carries a fatal-bleeding/ICH signal (0.4% vs 0.1%) — not a free swap.
  • Treat the mechanism — early DOAC for AF (CATALYST), carotid revascularization within 2 weeks for high-risk symptomatic stenosis (benefit tracks predicted risk — ECST-2), no empiric anticoagulation for ESUS, ICM for occult AF, PFO closure <60. Full detail on the prevention page.
  • Structured follow-up works (STROKE-CARD) — book it; own the risk factors. Factor XIa inhibitors are the emerging lower-bleeding antithrombotic to watch.

Key sources: 2023 AHA/ASA ED-TIA scientific statement; Canadian TIA Score (Perry, BMJ 2021); CHANCE/POINT/INSPIRES/THALES/CHANCE-2 (DAPT); CATALYST (Lancet 2025, AF timing); 2024 ACC arrhythmia-monitoring-after-stroke pathway; ECST-2 (Lancet Neurol 2025); Furie & Kelly Secondary Prevention after Ischemic Stroke (NEJM 2026); 2021 AHA/ASA secondary-prevention guideline; TST (LDL).

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Amin HP, et al. Diagnosis, Workup, Risk Reduction of TIA in the ED — AHA Scientific Statement. Stroke. 2023 — image everyone; don't triage by ABCD²; Perry JJ, et al. Canadian TIA Score (BMJ 2021, AUC ~0.70).
  2. Easton JD, et al. Definition and evaluation of TIA — AHA/ASA statement (tissue-based definition); Tanaka K, et al. DWI positivity by time after transient events (Neurology 2024).
  3. Wang Y, et al. CHANCE; Johnston SC, et al. POINT; THALES (bleeding signal); CHANCE-2 (CYP2C19); Gao Y, et al. INSPIRES — DAPT up to 72 h. NEJM. 2023.
  4. Dehbi HM, et al. Early DOAC after stroke + AF (CATALYST). Lancet 2025; Spooner MT, et al. 2024 ACC arrhythmia-monitoring-after-stroke pathway (JACC 2025); Donners SJA, et al. ECST-2 (Lancet Neurol 2025); Furie KL, Kelly PJ. Secondary Prevention after Ischemic Stroke. NEJM. 2026.
  5. Kleindorfer DO, et al. 2021 AHA/ASA guideline for secondary prevention of stroke/TIA.
  6. TRICARE / DoD: antiplatelets/statins generic-formulary; preferred DOAC per Uniform Formulary (verify). Retention: DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b(6) (recurrent TIA), Army AR 40-501 3-31f.

Verified July 2026: the tissue-based TIA definition, ABCD²/ABCD²-I risk stratification, same-day work-up (MRI/vessel imaging/rhythm monitoring), immediate 21-day DAPT for high-risk TIA (CHANCE/POINT; INSPIRES extends the start window to 72 h; CHANCE-2 for CYP2C19 LOF), high-intensity statin to LDL <70, and mechanism-directed prevention (early DOAC, carotid revascularization within 2 weeks) reflect current AHA/ASA guidance. The 2023 AHA/ASA ED-TIA statement effectively retires ABCD² as a triage gate — image everyone; imaging/mechanism drive disposition (Canadian TIA Score superior; AUC ~0.70). The newer points — INSPIRES 72-h window (Asian data), the DAPT reperfusion/carotid-exclusion, the ticagrelor bleeding signal, CATALYST early-DOAC, ICM/2024-ACC monitoring thresholds, ECST-2, DWI false-negatives, STROKE-CARD follow-up, and the LDL <55 European target — were supplied by expert review with citations; confirm before quoting. Retention citation confirmed against the source: DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.b(6) — recurrent TIA unless etiology identified and definitively treated — Army AR 40-501 3-31f. TRICARE preferred DOAC not pinned — verify before counseling.*

docs.title: Traumatic brain injury (TBI)
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

One-liner: brain injury from an external force — the signature injury of the post-9/11 military, and mostly a mild TBI (concussion) problem. The acute event is usually the easy part; the value is in managing the long tail of comorbidities — headache, sleep, mood/PTSD, cognition, dizziness, vision, and the ones people forget (neuroendocrine, autonomic, suicide risk). Treat them by domain (each has its own page in the sidebar under TBI), and the patient gets better.

🚩 Acute TBI — exclude a structural emergency first

  • Image (non-contrast CT) for red flags: worsening/severe headache, vomiting, ↓ or fluctuating consciousness, focal deficit, seizure, anticoagulation, or a high-risk mechanism (use a validated head-CT rule). Suspect cervical-artery dissection with neck trauma/pain.
  • Second-impact syndrome: a second head impact before the first concussion has recovered can be catastrophic (diffuse cerebral edema) — remove from activity and do not return to play/duty while symptomatic.
  • Moderate/severe TBI → trauma/neurosurgery, ICP management, and the ICH/increased-ICP pathways; this page focuses on the post-acute and chronic course.

docs.title: Classify the severity
docs.text:
Severity GCS LOC Post-traumatic amnesia Imaging Mild (concussion) 13–15 ≤30 min <24 h Normal (or complicated-mild if abnormal) Moderate 9–12 30 min–24 h 1–7 days Often abnormal Severe ≤8 >24 h >7 days Abnormal

Confirming an mTBI diagnosis — 2023 ACRM criteria

The table above grades severity; to confirm the diagnosis of mild TBI, the 2025 AAPM&R guideline endorses (Strong for) the 2023 ACRM diagnostic criteria as the preferred standard — useful in Work-ups 2–3 to separate true mTBI from mimics. Require all four: (1) a plausible mechanism; (2) ≥1 acute clinical sign (LOC, post-traumatic amnesia, disorientation, or an acute seizure); (3) ≥2 acute symptoms (headache, dizziness, nausea, fogginess, etc.); and (4) ≥1 objective finding (cognitive impairment, balance deficit, or oculomotor dysfunction). A 2026 structured interview standardizes their application.

docs.title: Which patient is in front of you? — pick the pathway
docs.text:
The work-up is completely different by phase. Sort the patient into one of three before anything else:

Presentation Setting Pathway Acute head injury, just happened ER / aid station / field Work-up 1 — Acute TBI (ER) Recent concussion, still symptomatic (days–weeks) clinic / concussion cell Work-up 2 — Acute-phase mTBI (outpatient) Symptoms persisting >~4 wks, or moderate–severe TBI in the chronic phase, or repeated concussions TBI/neuro clinic Work-up 3 — Long-term TBI follow-up

docs.title: Work-up 1 — Acute TBI in the ER
docs.text:
The acute head-injury evaluation

  1. ABCs + C-spine precautions; GCS, pupils, focal exam; glucose; note anticoagulation/ antiplatelet use, LOC, and post-traumatic amnesia.
  2. Decide on CT with a validated rule (e.g., Canadian CT Head Rule). Get a non-contrast head CT for any high-risk feature: GCS <15 at 2 h, suspected open/depressed skull fracture, any basilar-skull-fracture sign (hemotympanum, raccoon eyes, Battle's sign, CSF oto/ rhinorrhea), ≥2 episodes of vomiting, age ≥65; or medium-risk: retrograde amnesia ≥30 min, dangerous mechanism. Anticoagulation/coagulopathy or post-traumatic seizure → image regardless. Add CTA neck if dissection is a concern. Blood biomarkers (GFAP + UCH-L1) can help rule out the CT in adult mTBI within 12 h — see box below.
  3. If there's a bleed: reverse anticoagulation (specifics in the box below, or the ICH reversal table), BP control, and neurosurgery for a surgical lesion (EDH/SDH/contusion) or raised ICP. Consider TXA within 3 h for mild–moderate TBI (GCS 9–15) with intracranial hemorrhage; avoid in isolated severe TBI (box below). Moderate/severe → trauma bay + ICP/PbtO₂ pathway (box below).
  4. Disposition: admit/observe for an abnormal CT, GCS <15, persistent vomiting, anticoagulation, or an unsafe home; a normal CT with a normal exam and improving symptoms can go home with a reliable observer, written return precautions, and no return to play/duty the same day.
  5. In uniform: after a potentially concussive event, MACE2 and the mandated recovery period apply (military box).

Blood biomarkers — GFAP + UCH-L1 (rule out the CT)

The FDA-cleared GFAP + UCH-L1 assay (Abbott i-STAT, cleared 2018) helps rule out intracranial injury in adult mTBI evaluated within 12 h of injury. A negative panel — GFAP <30 pg/mL and UCH-L1 <360 pg/mL (2024 ACS Best Practices thresholds) — carries an NPV approaching 100%. A 2025 meta-analysis found 100% pooled sensitivity (95% CI 99–100%) with ~31% specificity; a 2026 review estimated a 20–30% reduction in CT use. Highest value where CT is limited (austere/deployed) — a negative panel can safely omit the scan. It does not override a red flag that already mandates imaging.

Anticoagulation reversal & TXA (acute bleed) — dosed

Reversal (2024 ACS Best Practices / Neurocritical Care Society):

  • Warfarin → 4-factor PCC (Kcentra), INR-based, dosed on actual body weight capped at 100 kg: INR 2–3.9 → 25 units/kg (max 2500), INR 4–6 → 35 units/kg (max 3500), INR >6 → 50 units/kg (max 5000). (Many trauma centers use a fixed 1500–2000 units to save time — give it, then check INR.) Plus vitamin K 10 mg IV by slow infusion over 10–30 min (sustains reversal; PCC alone wears off). Recheck INR at 15–60 min, target <1.5.
  • Dabigatran → idarucizumab 5 g IV (two 2.5 g / 50 mL vials, consecutive boluses). If unavailable: activated PCC (FEIBA) 50 units/kg and consider hemodialysis.
  • Factor Xa inhibitors (apixaban/rivaroxaban) → 4-factor PCC 50 units/kg (or a fixed 2000 units) — andexanet alfa has been withdrawn from the US market.
  • Antiplatelet (aspirin/clopidogrel): do not routinely transfuse platelets for spontaneous/traumatic ICH (PATCH-signal harm) unless a neurosurgical procedure is planned; consider desmopressin (DDAVP) 0.4 µg/kg IV ×1.
  • Repeat CT at 6–24 h to check for hematoma progression before starting VTE prophylaxis.

Tranexamic acid — mixed and evolving. Consider TXA within 3 h (1 g IV bolus + 1 g infusion, or a 2 g bolus) for mild–moderate TBI (GCS 9–15) with intracranial hemorrhage — CRASH-3 reduced head-injury–related death in this group, and a prehospital-trial subgroup found a 2 g bolus cut 28-day mortality in confirmed ICH (17% vs 27%). Avoid in isolated severe TBI (GCS ≤8) — no benefit (CRASH-3), with a signal of possible harm. A 2024 meta-analysis (9 RCTs, n=14,747) found no overall mortality/disability benefit, though TXA may limit hematoma expansion.

VTE prophylaxis timing (2024 ACS Best Practices) — dosed

Balance re-bleeding against clot risk. Agent — LMWH preferred over UFH: enoxaparin 30 mg SC q12h or 40 mg SC daily (reduce to 30 mg daily if CrCl <30), or UFH 5000 units SC q8–12h when LMWH is contraindicated. Start mechanical prophylaxis (SCDs) on admission in the meantime.

  • Low-risk nonoperative TBI: start within 24 h if follow-up CT shows no ICH progression.
  • Moderate/high-risk nonoperative TBI: within 24–48 h if follow-up CT is stable.
  • Post-craniotomy/craniectomy: within 24–48 h if the postoperative CT is stable.

A 2025 Military TBI Initiative meta-analysis found no increase in ICH progression with prophylaxis started within 72 h.

Moderate–severe TBI — ICP / PbtO₂ / CPP targets (with doses)

For GCS <9 with an abnormal CT, place ICP monitoring (Brain Trauma Foundation, 4th ed.); treat ICP >22 mmHg. Target CPP 60–70 mmHg — avoid driving CPP >70 (increased ARDS risk). PbtO₂ monitoring target >20 mmHg is an emerging complement: OXY-TC (Lancet Neurol 2023) cut brain-hypoxia time with dual ICP + PbtO₂ monitoring, though 6-month extended-GOS was not significantly improved. Where invasive monitoring is unavailable (austere/deployed), the 2025 Brussels (B-ICONIC) consensus guides non-invasive ICP.

Tiered ICP control — head of bed 30°, normocarbia (PaCO₂ 35–40), sedation/analgesia first; then for a surge:

  • Hyperosmolar bolus (either): hypertonic saline 3% 250 mL (or 23.4% 30 mL via central line) or mannitol 0.5–1 g/kg IV — mannitol needs a normal BP (osmotic diuresis) and serum osm <320; hypertonic saline is preferred if hypotensive.
  • Brief hyperventilation (PaCO₂ 30–35) only as a temporizing bridge to the OR/definitive care.
  • Refractory: neurosurgery for decompressive craniectomy / CSF drainage; not routine steroids (harmful in TBI — CRASH).

docs.title: Work-up 2 — Acute-phase outpatient mTBI (days–weeks out, still symptomatic)
docs.text:
The concussion-clinic / aid-station work-up

  1. History: mechanism, LOC/PTA, and a symptom inventory across domains (headache, dizziness, cognitive, sleep, mood), prior concussions, and persistence risk factors (cognitive symptoms, pre-existing anxiety/depression or sleep disorder, female sex, high acute symptom burden — see box below).
  2. Targeted exam: neuro + cervical spine, a vestibular/ocular-motor screen (VOMS), and a balance check (BESS). Use the right tool for the phase — SCAT6 on the sideline/≤72 h, SCOAT6 in the office at 3–30 days (or MACE2 in uniform) — plus a symptom scale (PCSS / Neurobehavioral Symptom Inventory). Cutoffs in the box below.
  3. Imaging is usually NOT needed — a normal exam with improving symptoms doesn't warrant it (AHS/ACR); image only for red flags, focal deficits, or worsening/atypical course.
  4. Manage: brief relative rest (24–48 h) → early sub-threshold aerobic exercise, sleep hygiene, and symptom-targeted treatment (treat the headache phenotype; avoid opioids/ butalbital). Reassure — most recover in days–weeks.
  5. Graded return to activity/duty; step back a stage on symptom recurrence. Treat the high-persistence-risk patient aggressively and early.
  6. Follow up; if not recovering by ~4 weeks → escalate to Work-up 3 / a multidisciplinary concussion clinic.

Concussion assessment tools — which, and when

  • SCAT6 — sideline/acute, within 72 h of injury.
  • SCOAT6 — office-based subacute assessment at 3–30 days; the 6th Amsterdam Consensus (2023) created it specifically for the clinic-follow-up gap.
  • VOMS — positive if symptom provocation ≥2 on any item or NPC ≥5 cm (AUC 0.89 for concussion identification).
  • mSIT Plus (2026 NCAA–DoD CARE, n=59,901) — a streamlined 6-question index + modified VOMS, AUC 0.94; built for time-limited field/military screening.
  • MACE2 — the mandated DoD sideline tool after a concussive event.

Persistence risk factors — who won't recover fast (treat early, aggressively)

2025 evidence (JAMA Netw Open meta-analysis, McIntosh et al.; NCAA–DoD CARE Consortium):

  • Cognitive symptoms (difficulty concentrating) — strongest predictor (OR >3).
  • Pre-existing anxiety/depression or a sleep disorder.
  • Acute clinical signs (LOC, amnesia).
  • Female sex — significant but a smaller effect.
  • High acute symptom burden (SCAT total >45), prior concussion history, and delayed reporting predict symptoms persisting >29 days.

docs.title: Work-up 3 — Long-term TBI follow-up (persistent — the common military clinic visit)
docs.text:
Structured multi-domain review (persistent post-concussive / chronic / repeated TBI)

  1. Re-confirm the diagnosis and rule out a new/secondary process if the pattern changed.
  2. Systematically screen and treat every comorbidity domain — work the domain pages below (headache, sleep, mood/PTSD, cognition, vestibular, vision, neuroendocrine, autonomic, seizures, pain/substance). This is where persistent TBI is won or lost.
  3. Don't miss the quiet ones: neuroendocrine (hypopituitarism), OSA, and suicide risk — screen deliberately.
  4. Refer to a multidisciplinary TBI clinic (neuro + rehab + behavioral health + sleep + vestibular/PT + endocrine + neuropsych) for refractory/complex cases.
  5. Own the disposition: profile, MEB/DES, VA rating documentation, and — for a repeated- impact history — honest TES/CTE counseling (below). Track longitudinally with a symptom inventory and treat to target.

docs.title: Manage the comorbidities — by domain
docs.text:
Persistent post-TBI symptoms are multidomain and overlapping — treat each explicitly (this is the engine of Work-up 3, and drives symptom care in Work-up 2); improving one (sleep, mood, headache) lifts the others. Each domain has its own page (nested under TBI in the sidebar):

  • Headache — the commonest symptom; phenotype-directed (full detail on the post-traumatic headache page).
  • Sleep — fix it first; CBT-I, screen for OSA.
  • Mood & PTSD — CBT + SSRI/SNRI, prazosin; screen suicide every visit.
  • Cognitive — treat the contributors first; cognitive rehab.
  • Vestibular / dizziness — BPPV/Epley; vestibular rehab.
  • Vision / oculomotor — convergence insufficiency; vision therapy.
  • Neuroendocrine — hypopituitarism screening — the quiet, treatable miss.
  • Autonomic — dysautonomia; exercise reconditioning.
  • Post-traumatic seizures — 7-day prophylaxis ≠ epilepsy prevention.
  • Pain & substance — opioid-sparing; screen substances.

docs.title: Return to activity / duty
docs.text:
Brief relative rest → early sub-threshold aerobic exercise → graded, supervised return (school/ work/cognitive load before full contact/duty); step back a stage on symptom recurrence. In uniform, follow the Progressive Return to Activity stages (military box). The headache-specific detail is on the post-traumatic headache page.

Sub-threshold aerobic exercise prescription

Start early — within 24–72 h, not after a rest period. Prescribe activity kept below the symptom-exacerbation threshold (a >2-point rise on a 0–10 symptom scale = "more than mild" = went too hard):

  1. Find the threshold with the Buffalo Concussion Treadmill Test (BCTT) — the HR at which symptoms rise >2 points.
  2. No treadmill? Prescribe at ~50% of age-predicted max HR (max = 220 − age).
  3. Dose ~20 min/day below that threshold; advance as tolerated, backing off on a >2-point rise.

RCT evidence: early aerobic exercise cut median recovery 17 → 13 days (p=0.009) and persistent symptoms 32% → 21% (p=0.04). Strong for in both the Amsterdam 2023 consensus and the 2025 AAPM&R guideline (2025 NEJM review, Leddy).

docs.title: Long-term: CTE / neurodegeneration & suicide risk
docs.text:
Traumatic encephalopathy syndrome (TES) — counsel carefully, don't over-diagnose

Chronic traumatic encephalopathy (CTE) is a neuropathological diagnosis (perivascular p-tau at sulcal depths) — only confirmable at autopsy.
