In life, the 2021 NINDS consensus criteria define Traumatic Encephalopathy Syndrome (TES): substantial repetitive head-impact exposure (contact sport, military blast), progressive cognitive impairment (memory/executive) and/or neurobehavioral dysregulation (explosivity, impulsivity), not fully explained by another disorder. It is a clinical syndrome, not a confirmed CTE diagnosis — counsel honestly about uncertainty, treat the modifiable comorbidities, and avoid nihilism.

The 2021 NINDS TES criteria are explicitly research criteria — use them cautiously in clinical and medicolegal settings. Do not disclose provisional "levels of certainty" for underlying CTE pathology to patients: it can produce a nocebo effect — worsening mental health and deterring pursuit of treatable conditions. Keep counseling focused on the modifiable comorbidities.

Suicide risk is elevated after TBI — screen and act

TBI (especially with mood/PTSD, chronic pain, and repeated injury) independently raises suicide risk. Screen for suicidal ideation at every visit, treat depression/PTSD/pain/sleep aggressively, restrict means, and connect to behavioral health / the Veterans Crisis Line.

TRICARE — TBI prescribing

Most symptom-directed drugs are generic/formulary, no PA — SSRIs/SNRIs, prazosin, trazodone, melatonin, the oral headache preventives, levetiracetam, and standard hormone replacements. CGRP agents, onabotulinumtoxinA (chronic-migraine phenotype), and stimulants carry the usual PA/specialist requirements. Multidisciplinary rehab, CBT-I, vestibular/vision therapy, and behavioral health go through normal specialty/rehab referral. Verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Military medicine — TBI / concussion (mTBI)

Quoted from DoDI 6130.03 Vol 1/Vol 2, AR 40-501, the VA/DoD mTBI CPG (2021), and DoDI 6490.11; DES governed by DoDI 1332.18 / DoDM 1332.18. mTBI is protocolized in DoD — follow the mandated pathway, not just this page.

  • Event-driven protocol: after a potentially concussive event (blast, vehicle, fall, blunt), DoD policy mandates evaluation + a mandatory recovery period — screen with MACE2 and manage per the deployed-concussion protocol before return to duty.
  • Return to activity: use the Progressive Return to Activity stages; symptom recurrence → step back a stage.
  • Deployability: acute concussion is temporarily non-deployable pending clearance; persistent multi-domain sequelae are deployment-limiting; multiple concussions raise the return-to-duty bar.
  • Profile (DA 3349): temporary during recovery; permanent for persistent, functionally limiting post-concussive syndrome.
  • EPTS / LOD: combat/duty-incurred TBI is typically in the line of duty — document event, mechanism, timeline (drives LOD and VA rating).
  • Retention — DoD & Army: DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.h places TBI with persistent sequelae — severe headaches, cognitive/sensory/motor impairment, or seizures — below retention. Army: migraine-phenotype PTH ≥3 incapacitating days/mo under AR 40-501 3-31g, and residual persistent severe headache / other neuro sequelae under 3-31k → DES (MEB/PEB). Document objective frequency, failed trials, neuropsych findings, and duty impact.
  • Accession — DoDI 6130.03 Vol 1 para 6.26: persistent post-concussive symptoms >1 month, a history of head trauma with persistent focal deficit, or ≥3 mild brain injuries are disqualifying for entry.
  • Aeromedical (AR 40-501 ch. 4, table 4-2): head-injury standards are graded by how long the post-traumatic syndrome persists — e.g., PTH resolving ~<14 days → temporary aviation suspension; post-traumatic syndrome persisting >~6 weeks → permanent DQ / 2-yr termination of aviation service. Confirm the exact table-4-2 tier for the class.

docs.title: 📚 Discover — evidence brief
docs.text:
• Sort by phase first — the work-up is different for acute TBI in the ER (CT-rule triage, reversal, neurosurgery/disposition), acute-phase outpatient mTBI (symptom inventory + VOMS/balance, usually no imaging, exercise + graded return), and long-term follow-up (systematic multi-domain comorbidity review — the common military clinic visit).
  • TBI is mostly a mild-TBI, chronic-comorbidity problem — the acute event resolves; the persistent symptoms (headache, sleep, mood/PTSD, cognition, dizziness, vision) are what disable, and they're multidomain and interlinked.
  • Treat by domain and fix sleep + mood first — they gate everything else; headache-specific CBT, CBT-I, and vestibular/vision rehab are the highest-value non-drug tools.
  • Don't miss the quiet ones: post-traumatic hypopituitarism (screen moderate–severe/blast TBI in the first year — treatable), OSA, and suicide risk (screen every visit).
  • Rest is not the treatment — brief relative rest then early sub-threshold aerobic exercise (within 24–72 h; BCTT threshold or ~50% max HR) and graded return; avoid opioids/butalbital.
  • Acute mTBI triage is getting a biomarker — a negative GFAP + UCH-L1 panel (within 12 h, NPV ~100%) can safely omit the CT and may cut CT use 20–30% — high value in austere/deployed care.
  • Acute bleed management has specifics — agent-specific reversal, TXA within 3 h for mild–moderate TBI with ICH (avoid in isolated severe), VTE prophylaxis within 24–48 h once the follow-up CT is stable (LMWH preferred), and for GCS <9 ICP >22 mmHg / CPP 60–70 / PbtO₂ >20 targets.
  • Confirm mTBI with the 2023 ACRM criteria (mechanism + acute sign + ≥2 symptoms + an objective finding) — the 2025 AAPM&R-preferred standard, beyond the GCS/LOC/PTA severity grade.
  • CTE is a post-mortem diagnosis; TES (2021 NINDS) is a research clinical syndrome — counsel with honesty about uncertainty, don't disclose provisional CTE-certainty levels (nocebo), treat the modifiable.
  • Post-traumatic seizure prophylaxis is 7 days for early seizures in moderate–severe TBI — it does not prevent epilepsy.

Key sources: VA/DoD mTBI CPG (2021); DoDI 6490.11 + TBICoE Progressive Return to Activity; 2024 ACS Best Practices in the Management of TBI; 2025 AAPM&R Action Collaborative TBI guideline + 2023 ACRM mTBI diagnostic criteria; 2025 NEJM sport-related concussion review (Leddy); 6th Amsterdam concussion consensus (2023); CRASH-3; Brain Trauma Foundation guidelines + OXY-TC (2023); post-TBI hypopituitarism guidance (British Neurotrauma Group); 2021 NINDS Traumatic Encephalopathy Syndrome criteria.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. VA/DoD. Clinical Practice Guideline for the Management and Rehabilitation of Post-Acute Mild Traumatic Brain Injury (2021).
  2. DoD. DoDI 6490.11 — Management of mTBI/Concussion in the Deployed Setting; TBICoE Progressive Return to Activity.
  3. Silverberg ND, Lee K, Mikolić A, et al. Action Collaborative on TBI Care — adapted CPG (Ann Fam Med 2025) — comorbidity-directed management; endorses the 2023 ACRM diagnostic criteria.
  4. Manley GT, Albert GW, Brophy GM, et al. Best Practices in the Management of Traumatic Brain Injury (American College of Surgeons, 2024) — GFAP/UCH-L1 CT decision, anticoagulation reversal, VTE prophylaxis timing.
  5. GFAP + UCH-L1 biomarkers: Puravet A, Oris C, Pereira B, et al. Can GFAP and UCH-L1 predict intracranial injury after mild TBI? (Ann Emerg Med 2025; 100% pooled sensitivity); San Miguel L, et al. GFAP and UCH-L1 for ruling out intracranial lesions after mTBI (J Clin Med 2026).
  6. TXA: CRASH-3 trial collaborators (Lancet 2019); Rowell S, et al. Prehospital TXA for TBI — ICH subgroup (J Trauma Acute Care Surg 2024); Al Lawati K, et al. meta-analysis (Intensive Care Med 2021); LaGrone LN, et al. AAST/ACS Damage-Control Resuscitation protocol (2024).
  7. VTE prophylaxis: Johnson PL, et al. Timing and agent for VTE prophylaxis in severe TBI (J Trauma Acute Care Surg 2024; LMWH preferred); Dengler BA, et al. Safety of early chemoprophylaxis after TBI — Military TBI Initiative (J Neurosurg 2025).
  8. ICP / PbtO₂ / CPP: Brain Trauma Foundation guidelines (ICP monitoring GCS <9, threshold 22 mmHg, CPP 60–70); Payen JF, et al. OXY-TC (Lancet Neurol 2023; dual ICP + PbtO₂); Robba C, et al. Brussels/B-ICONIC non-invasive ICP consensus (Intensive Care Med 2025).
  9. Assessment tools: Patricios JS, et al. 6th Amsterdam concussion consensus (Br J Sports Med 2023; SCAT6/SCOAT6); Kontos AP, et al. VOMS discriminative validity (Am J Sports Med 2021); Rooks LT, et al. mSIT Plus — NCAA–DoD CARE (Br J Sports Med 2026).
  10. Aerobic exercise & recovery: Leddy JJ. Sport-Related Concussion (N Engl J Med 2025); BCTT sub-threshold prescription; AAPM&R (2025) and Amsterdam (2023) Strong-for recommendations.
  11. ACRM diagnostic criteria: Silverberg ND, Iverson GL, Cogan A, et al. (Arch Phys Med Rehabil 2023); Lange RT, et al. Structured interview for the ACRM criteria (J Neurotrauma 2026).
  12. Persistence risk factors: McIntosh SJ, et al. Factors associated with persisting symptoms after concussion (JAMA Netw Open 2025); Rooks LT, et al. NCAA–DoD CARE (Sports Med 2025).
  13. Neuroendocrine: Tan CL, et al. Screening and management of pituitary dysfunction following TBI — British Neurotrauma Group guidance; post-traumatic hypopituitarism meta-analyses.
  14. Katz DI, Bernick C, Dodick DW, et al. 2021 NINDS consensus diagnostic criteria for Traumatic Encephalopathy Syndrome (TES) (Neurology 2021) — research criteria; nocebo caution.
  15. TRICARE / DoD: symptom-directed drugs generic-formulary; CGRP/onabotulinumtoxinA/stimulants PA-gated. Retention DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 para 5.26.h, Army AR 40-501 3-31g/k; accession Vol 1 6.26; aeromedical AR 40-501 ch. 4 table 4-2. Verify against the source.

Verified July 2026: TBI severity classification (GCS/LOC/PTA) and the 2023 ACRM diagnostic criteria; acute-ER management — GFAP + UCH-L1 CT-rule-out thresholds (GFAP <30 / UCH-L1 <360 pg/mL), agent-specific anticoagulation reversal, TXA (consider ≤3 h in mild–moderate TBI with ICH; avoid in isolated severe), VTE-prophylaxis timing (LMWH preferred), and ICP/PbtO₂/CPP targets — reflect the 2024 ACS Best Practices, CRASH-3, Brain Trauma Foundation, and OXY-TC (2023) sources cited above. Concussion-clinic tools (SCAT6 ≤72 h, SCOAT6 3–30 d, VOMS, mSIT Plus), sub-threshold aerobic exercise (BCTT / ~50% max HR, start 24–72 h), updated persistence risk factors, the domain-based comorbidity framework, 7-day seizure prophylaxis (does not prevent epilepsy), post-traumatic hypopituitarism screening, and the CTE-vs-TES distinction (CTE neuropathological; 2021 NINDS TES a research clinical syndrome — nocebo caution) reflect current VA/DoD, AAPM&R (2025), Amsterdam (2023), and NINDS (2021) consensus sources. The DoD retention/accession/aeromedical citations (DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 5.26.h / Vol 1 6.26; AR 40-501 3-31g/k, ch. 4 table 4-2; DoDI 6490.11; MACE2) are quoted from those issuances — confirm current editions and the exact table-4-2 tier before counseling.*

docs.title: TBI comorbidity — Autonomic
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

Part of the TBI work-up & comorbidities hub.

One-liner: two very different problems live under "autonomic." (A) mTBI autonomic dysfunction — orthostatic and exercise intolerance, palpitations, temperature dysregulation — is an outpatient issue, very treatable with graded reconditioning. (B) Paroxysmal sympathetic hyperactivity (PSH) is a severe-TBI ICU emergency — different population, setting, and drugs. Keep them separate.

Why it happens — and why exercise is both test and treatment

mTBI causes diffuse axonal injury of brainstem autonomic centers and impairs cerebrovascular autoregulation, so the brain can't hold steady perfusion when blood pressure or metabolic demand changes. During exertion this creates a demand–perfusion mismatch → headache, dizziness, fog. Standardized testing shows blunted sympathetic baroreflex and reduced HR/BP variability in the first two weeks. Graded exercise restores autonomic function and autoregulation — which is exactly why exercise is both diagnostic (BCTT) and therapeutic.

docs.title: (A) mTBI autonomic dysfunction / exercise intolerance / POTS
docs.text:
Assess — orthostatic vitals + the Buffalo Concussion Treadmill Test

  • Symptoms: orthostatic intolerance/lightheadedness, exertional symptom exacerbation, palpitations/tachycardia, sweating and temperature dysregulation.
  • Orthostatic vitals / POTS: sustained HR rise ≥30 bpm (≥40 in adolescents) within 10 min of standing/tilt, without orthostatic hypotension. Concussion is a recognized POTS trigger, and POTS resolution tends to parallel return to activity — reinforcing exercise as treatment.
  • Buffalo Concussion Treadmill Test (BCTT) — the validated exercise-tolerance test (~94% sensitivity, ~95% specificity for concussion): walk at 3.3 mph / 0% incline, increase incline 1° per minute, rate symptoms every minute; stop at a ≥3-point symptom rise (0–10 scale) or volitional exhaustion. In community (non-athlete) adults, ~56% show exercise intolerance at 1 week and ~48% at 1 month.

Sub-symptom-threshold aerobic exercise (SSTAE) — the prescription

The HR at the BCTT symptom threshold is the target (if no treadmill, use ~50% of age-predicted max HR, max = 220 − age):

  1. Exercise at 80% of the symptom-threshold HR, 20 min/day, 5–6 days/week.
  2. Retest every 2–3 weeks and advance the target HR upward as tolerance improves.
  3. Stay sub-threshold — a >2–3-point symptom rise means back off.

Adult RCT evidence: the 2026 Valaas trial (n=81, persistent PCS 3–24 mo) improved exercise tolerance at 12 weeks (sustained at 6 mo); the ACTBI trial (n=52) improved quality of life vs stretching (p=0.047), no adverse events. Nuance: SSTAE reliably improves exercise tolerance, but its effect on overall symptom burden in adults is less robust than in adolescent athletes — both treatment and control groups tend to improve over time.

Orthostatic intolerance / POTS pharmacotherapy — non-drug first

Drugs here are borrowed from the general OH/POTS literature, not TBI-specific. Exhaust non-pharmacological measures first (they are the cornerstone):

  • First-line (non-drug): fluids 2–3 L/day, salt 10–12 g/day, compression garments, and the graded exercise program above.
  • If insufficient, symptom-directed: beta-blocker or ivabradine (palpitation-dominant); midodrine (hypotension-dominant); atomoxetine (an NRI — for brain fog with low catecholamines); pyridostigmine as an adjunct. Fludrocortisone for refractory volume support.

Temperature dysregulation — practical, and military-relevant

Post-TBI thermoregulatory dysfunction can present as heat intolerance, excessive sweating, or impaired sweating — a real hazard for troops exercising in the heat. Use graded heat acclimatization and modified PT standards during recovery; document heat intolerance as a duty/deployability consideration.

docs.title: (B) Paroxysmal sympathetic hyperactivity (PSH) — severe-TBI ICU
docs.text:
PSH — recognize, standardize, and treat the storm

Who: 10–30% of moderate–severe TBI; ~80% of all PSH follows TBI. Definition: simultaneous paroxysmal, transient surges in sympathetic activity (↑HR, ↑BP, ↑RR, hyperthermia, sweating) and motor activity (posturing) in survivors of severe acquired brain injury.

Diagnose with the PSH Assessment Measure (PSH-AM, 2014 consensus): a Clinical Feature Scale (grading HR, BP, RR, temperature, sweating, posturing) + an 11-item Diagnosis Likelihood Tool — standardizes diagnosis and tracks response.

PSH management ladder (2017 Lancet Neurology / 2024 ACS)

  • Find and treat triggers first — ~80% of paroxysms are allodynic (pain, urinary retention, movement, suctioning). Minimize noxious stimuli.
  • Abort/suppress: opioids (morphine) are the most-used first-line agent for allodynic responses.
  • Prevent/blunt: propranolol has the most evidence (low-quality data suggest lower mortality in severe TBI with PSH); alpha-2 agonists (clonidine, dexmedetomidine). A 2026 RCT found propranolol + gabapentin cut PSH incidence to 10% (vs 33% propranolol alone, 60% standard care) — though gabapentin prolonged recovery time.
  • Adjuncts/refractory: benzodiazepines, bromocriptine, baclofen (intrathecal for refractory cases), dantrolene.
  • Feed the storm: resting energy expenditure can rise up to 3× baseline during paroxysms — weight loss of 25–29% is reported. Prioritize nutritional support.

Military note: exercise intolerance directly limits return-to-duty fitness — the BCTT + SSTAE progression doubles as a documentable recovery metric; heat intolerance is a deployability consideration. See the TBI hub and the exercise prescription on the hub Return-to-activity section.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Meyfroidt G, Baguley IJ, Menon DK. Paroxysmal sympathetic hyperactivity — the storm after acute brain injury (Lancet Neurol 2017); Manley GT, et al. Best Practices in the Management of TBI (ACS 2024) — PSH as a distinct entity + management.
  2. Baguley IJ, Perkes IE, Fernandez-Ortega JF, et al. PSH after acquired brain injury — consensus definition/nomenclature/diagnostic criteria (PSH-AM) (J Neurotrauma 2014); Panjaitan JAP, et al. PSH — diagnosis, treatments, outcomes (Surg Neurol Int 2024).
  3. Negm EM, et al. Propranolol vs propranolol-gabapentin for PSH prevention after moderate–severe TBI — RCT (BMC Anesthesiol 2026).
  4. Mercier LJ, Batycky J, Campbell C, et al. Autonomic dysfunction in adults following mTBI — SR (NeuroRehabilitation 2022); Pelo R, et al. Dysregulated cardiovascular autonomic responses to orthostatic challenge in mTBI (J Neurotrauma 2026).
  5. Purkayastha S, Stokes M, Bell KR. ANS dysfunction in mTBI — pathophysiology/symptoms (Brain Inj 2019); Callaway CCM, Kosofsky BE. Autonomic dysfunction following mTBI (Curr Opin Neurol 2019).
  6. Leddy JJ. Sport-related concussion (N Engl J Med 2025); Leddy J, et al. Controlled exercise in concussion management (PM R 2016); DeGroot A, et al. BCTT in community adults with mTBI (PM R 2024).
  7. Valaas LV, et al. Sub-symptom-threshold aerobic exercise for persistent PCS — RCT (Phys Ther 2026); Mercier LJ, et al. Aerobic exercise in persisting PCS — ACTBI RCT (Arch Phys Med Rehabil 2025); McIntyre M, et al. SSTAE for persistent concussion — SR (Am J Phys Med Rehabil 2020).
  8. Bryarly M, Phillips LT, Fu Q, Vernino S, Levine BD. POTS — JACC Focus Seminar (J Am Coll Cardiol 2019); Wieling W, et al. Diagnosis and treatment of orthostatic hypotension (Lancet Neurol 2022); Sicard V, et al. Orthostatic autonomic dysregulation in pediatric concussion (JAMA Netw Open 2025).

Verified July 2026: the separation of mTBI autonomic dysfunction from PSH, the pathophysiology (cerebrovascular-autoregulation basis of exercise intolerance), the BCTT protocol and diagnostic accuracy, the SSTAE prescription (80% of symptom-threshold HR, 20 min, 5–6 d/wk, retest q2–3 wk) with adult-RCT nuance, POTS criteria and concussion-trigger data, the non-drug-first orthostatic pharmacotherapy algorithm, and the PSH-AM tool plus management ladder (triggers/opioids/propranolol/ alpha-2/gabapentin/nutrition) reflect the 2017 Lancet Neurology review, 2024 ACS Best Practices, 2014 PSH-AM consensus, and the peer-reviewed sources cited above. Confirm current ICU protocols and specialty-referral pathways before applying.

docs.title: TBI comorbidity — Cognitive
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

Part of the TBI work-up & comorbidities hub.

One-liner: post-TBI complaints of attention, processing speed, memory, and executive function are common — but much of it is treatable contributors, not fixed damage. Fix those first, then rehabilitate what remains. TRACK-TBI found 13.5% of mTBI patients had a poor cognitive outcome at 1 year (vs 4.5% of controls), memory and processing speed most affected.

Impairment profiles are heterogeneous — treat the profile, not a label

At 6 months (TRACK-TBI), the dominant deficit in moderate–severe TBI was processing speed (42%), then memory (27%) and executive function (20%); in mTBI, processing speed (17–19%) and memory (14–17%) predominated. Global impairment across all domains is uncommon — which argues for personalized rehabilitation, not one-size-fits-all. A large cohort (n=15,764) found attention and executive function are the most TBI-sensitive domains (≈2× the effect of processing speed/working memory), and that lasting deficits track with ≥3 lifetime mTBIs — a key counseling point for service members continuing high-risk activity.

docs.title: Assessment
docs.text:
Neuropsychological testing — when, and why

  • Not routinely within the first 30 days (VA/DoD) — first-48-hour deficits usually resolve in days–weeks; early testing mislabels normal recovery.
  • Most useful at >30 days when symptoms persist and functional impact needs documenting.
  • In the military, it drives disposition: RBANS significantly predicted return-to-duty vs medical retirement (p=0.001), and 25% of members cleared on symptom-resolution alone had not yet achieved neurocognitive recovery — so use testing as an adjunct to symptom-based clearance, not an afterthought.

Performance validity testing (PVT) — include it in every evaluation

In compensation/litigation contexts, an estimated ~40% of mTBI cases fail effort tests, which invalidates the cognitive scores; failing even a single PVT predicts significantly lower performance. Failure is commoner with uncomplicated mTBI, compensation-seeking, and longer time since injury. PVT failure ≠ malingering — interpret in context (pain, fatigue, depression, medication effects, poor engagement). This is directly load-bearing for MEB/VA ratings, where cognitive documentation drives the outcome — a report without validity testing is incomplete.

docs.title: Manage
docs.text:
Step 1 — treat the treatable drivers first

A large share of "cognitive impairment" resolves once these are addressed — review all meds at every visit (polypharmacy is the rule in multi-comorbid TBI):

  • Treat: sleep, mood/PTSD, pain, and substances.
  • Avoid (cognition-toxic): benzodiazepines, anticholinergics, bupropion and traditional antipsychotics (seizure threshold), lithium (confusion).
  • Minimize: opioids, topiramate (word-finding), sedating antihistamines, gabapentin/pregabalin (sedation).

Step 2 — non-drug: cognitive rehab + exercise

  • Cognitive rehabilitation is clinician-directed (SLP/OT-led). Clinician-directed beats self-administered computerized "brain training" — self-directed computer training was less effective and associated with worse outcomes (VA/DoD).
  • Compensatory strategy instruction has the strongest functional effect (SMD ~0.58, ASHA 2022) — calendars, checklists, routines, reduced multitasking.
  • Trial 4–6 sessions to confirm benefit; prolonged treatment without gains fosters a "sick role" and is discouraged.
  • Assistive tech should be familiar/commercial (smartphone apps, alarms, calendars) — custom devices have higher abandonment.
  • Early sub-threshold aerobic exercise aids neuroplasticity and has cognitive (not just headache) benefit (2025 AAPM&R Good Practice) — see the exercise prescription on the TBI hub.

Step 3 — pharmacotherapy (dosed, specialist-guided)

  • Methylphenidate — best-evidenced, domain-specific (2026 ACRM guideline). Start 5 mg BID, titrate toward ~0.3 mg/kg BID (commonly 10–20 mg BID); monitor BP/HR. Usually well tolerated, no increased seizure risk in the evidence base.
    • Should be offered → sustained attention & processing speed (d 0.39–0.45).
    • May be offered → selective attention (d 0.33).
    • Should NOT be offered → executive function or working memory (no benefit).
    • Should be offered → depressive symptoms when rapid response and/or cognitive impairment are priorities (d 1.06; overlaps mood/PTSD).
    • A 2024 meta-analysis (14 RCTs, n=4,434) confirms a small but real cognitive effect (d 0.34).
  • Amantadine 100 mg BID (up to 200 mg BID) — for moderate–severe TBI in the subacute phase. The NEJM 2012 RCT (n=184) showed it accelerates functional recovery in disorders of consciousness; benefit is greatest started in the first week for <1 month, and a 2025 meta-analysis found it significant only in the first ~6 weeks (no long-term advantage). Not for chronic mTBI.
  • Donepezil 5 → 10 mg/day — emerging; a 2024 meta-analysis showed a large effect (d 1.68) but on only 3 studies, and a small crossover RCT improved short-term memory and sustained attention. Insufficient for a guideline recommendation — specialist-guided option.

Long-term: repeated exposure & dementia risk

mTBI is an emerging dementia risk factor, and TRACK-TBI shows some patients decline after an apparent recovery. Risk concentrates with repeated exposure (≥3 lifetime mTBIs) — fold this into honest counseling about continuing high-risk activity, and connect it to the TES/CTE discussion on the TBI hub.

TRICARE — cognitive prescribing

Generic methylphenidate (Schedule II — stimulant/drug-testing rules apply), amantadine, and donepezil are formulary. Cognitive rehabilitation (SLP/OT) and neuropsychological testing go through normal rehab/behavioral-health referral. Verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Military note: objective neuropsych findings (with PVTs) carry weight in retention/DES decisions, and RBANS predicts return-to-duty vs medical retirement — document them. See the TBI hub for the retention/aeromedical framing.

docs.text:

  2. Bryant AM, Rose NB, Temkin NR, et al. Profiles of cognitive functioning at 6 months after TBI — TRACK-TBI (JAMA Netw Open 2023) — heterogeneous profiles.
  3. Lennon MJ, Brooker H, Creese B, et al. Lifetime TBI and cognitive-domain deficits in late life — PROTECT-TBI (J Neurotrauma 2023) — attention/executive most sensitive; ≥3 mTBIs.
  4. Arciniegas DB, Seel RT, Macciocchi SN, et al. Practice guideline for methylphenidate use in persons with TBI — ACRM (Arch Phys Med Rehabil 2026); Seel RT, et al. NDRIs for TBI symptoms — ACRM SR (Arch Phys Med Rehabil 2026).

  6. Giacino JT, Whyte J, Bagiella E, et al. Amantadine for severe TBI — RCT (N Engl J Med 2012); Mohamed MS, et al. meta-analysis (J Trauma Acute Care Surg 2022); Grüdtner DOM, et al. RCT meta-analysis (Clin Neurol Neurosurg 2025) — benefit first ~6 weeks only.

  8. VA/DoD. Management and Rehabilitation of Post-Acute mTBI CPG (2021) — clinician-directed rehab > self-directed; medications to avoid/minimize; neuropsych testing timing.
  9. ASHA. Cognitive Rehabilitation for Cognitive Dysfunction after Acquired Brain Injury — CPG (Am J Speech Lang Pathol 2022) — compensatory strategy instruction SMD ~0.58.
  10. Cook PA, Johnson TM, Martin SG, et al. Predictors of return-to-duty vs medical retirement in blast-related mTBI (J Neurotrauma 2018) — RBANS; Porter SJ, Johnson DE. ANAM TBI-Mil battery in concussion recovery (Mil Med 2020) — 25% cleared before neurocognitive recovery.
