Verified July 2026: prevalence and heterogeneous impairment profiles (TRACK-TBI 2022/2023; PROTECT-TBI), the 2026 ACRM domain-specific methylphenidate recommendations, amantadine's subacute moderate–severe role (NEJM 2012; 2025 meta-analysis), donepezil as emerging, clinician-directed cognitive rehabilitation with compensatory-strategy priority (VA/DoD; ASHA 2022), the medications-to-avoid/minimize list, neuropsych testing timing (against routine <30 days; RBANS for disposition), performance-validity testing, and the dementia-risk counseling point reflect the sources cited above. Confirm current TRICARE formulary/PA status and Schedule II constraints before prescribing.

docs.title: TBI comorbidity — Headache
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

Part of the TBI work-up & comorbidities hub.

The most common post-TBI symptom. The rule is treat the phenotype, start early, and cap acute-medication days — PTH is a high-risk medication-overuse setting.

Name the phenotype (it drives treatment)

  • Migraine-like — most common (~90%); the migraine acute/preventive ladders apply.
  • Tension-type-like.
  • Cervicogenic — neck-driven; manual therapy (2025 AAPM&R Good Practice) and occipital/ cervical nerve blocks.
  • Occipital neuralgia — occipital nerve blocks.
  • Vestibular migraine — commonly missed; overlaps the vestibular/dizziness domain — screen for it.

ICHD-3 framing: acute PTH = onset within 7 days of injury, resolving by 3 months; persistent PTH = beyond 3 months. With a pre-existing headache disorder, call it PTH only if it clearly worsened (≈ doubled frequency/severity) in temporal relation to the injury — the distinction drives escalation and VA/MEB documentation.

  • Acute: stratified by severity (NSAID → triptan, or a gepant/lasmiditan where triptans are unsuitable); prefer a gepant (no MOH). The only positive acute-PTH RCT is IV metoclopramide 10 mg + diphenhydramine 25 mg (n=81; 1.4 pts greater pain relief vs placebo). Scheduled (not PRN) analgesics in the first 72 h post-injury are an emerging way to blunt acute-phase severity.
  • Preventive (start at ≥8 monthly headache days if acute therapy is optimized and headaches remain disabling — Lancet Neurol 2021; >10 days/mo or disabling — 2025 AAPM&R): amitriptyline/nortriptyline (also helps sleep/mood), venlafaxine, candesartan, propranolol; onabotulinumtoxinA — the strongest positive PTH-specific RCT (↓ cumulative headaches/wk 43% vs +35% placebo), so consider it earlier than "chronic-migraine only." Nerve blocks are high-yield — consider early.
  • CGRP agents: used by extrapolation from migraine — PTH-specific data are weak. The one completed placebo-controlled CGRP-mAb RCT in PTH (fremanezumab) was negative; erenumab has only open-label support. "Receptor- vs ligand-targeting preferred" is not established in PTH.
  • Non-drug: headache-specific CBT (McGeary 2022 RCT in post-9/11 veterans: HIT-6 −3.4, and PTSD −6.5 — the strongest non-drug PTH evidence, ideal for this population), aerobic exercise, sleep, cervical PT.
  • Emerging — neuromodulation: FDA-cleared devices (eTNS, nVNS, REN, sTMS) for migraine-phenotype PTH, useful when MOH risk or drug access is limited; small rTMS/TMS pilots are promising but preliminary.
  • Watch MOH: cap analgesics-with-caffeine, triptans, or opioids/butalbital at <10 days/mo (simple analgesics <15); avoid opioids and butalbital outright.

Full detail — doses, the acute/preventive ladders, the CBT and aerobic-exercise evidence, and the military/TRICARE specifics — are on the dedicated post-traumatic headache page.

docs.title: TBI comorbidity — Mood & PTSD
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

Part of the TBI work-up & comorbidities hub.

One-liner: depression, anxiety, and PTSD track tightly with TBI in service members and drive outcome more than seizure/symptom counts do — treat them explicitly, screen with validated tools, and screen for suicide every visit. In military/veteran TBI, PTSD runs ~36–37% (≈4× the odds vs non-TBI military), depression ~30% in the first year (~80% of episodes begin within 6 months), and anxiety 11–24%. A 2024 JAMA Netw Open cohort showed both mild and moderate–severe TBI carry a decade-long, non-plateauing excess risk of depression, anxiety, and suicidal ideation/attempt.

Screen for suicidal ideation at every visit

TBI independently raises suicide risk (amplified by mood/PTSD, chronic pain, and repeated injury). Screen (C-SSRS or PHQ-9 item 9), treat the drivers aggressively, restrict means, and connect to behavioral health / the Veterans Crisis Line (dial 988, then 1). (Also flagged on the TBI hub.)

Screen and measure — validated tools, standard cutoffs (2025 AAPM&R, Strong for)

Standard primary-care cutoffs apply without modification in TBI. Screen early (~1 month) and again in anyone still symptomatic — peak onset is 2–6 months post-injury.

  • Depression — PHQ-2 → PHQ-9.
  • Anxiety — GAD-2 → GAD-7.
  • PTSD — PCL-5.
  • Suicidality — C-SSRS or PHQ-9 item 9.
  • Alcohol misuse — AUDIT-C.

docs.title: Depression & anxiety
docs.text:
Treat by target — CBT + an antidepressant (dosed)

  • Psychotherapy: CBT / behavioral activation is first-line and combines well with medication.
  • SSRI/SNRI (depression evidence base is broad): sertraline 50 mg/day → 50–200, escitalopram 10 → 20 mg, or venlafaxine XR 75 → 225 mg. Avoid highly anticholinergic/sedating agents (worsen cognition).
  • Methylphenidate — strongest TBI-specific evidence for depressive symptoms. The 2026 ACRM practice guideline (Should be offered) recommends it when rapid response and/or co-occurring cognitive impairment are priorities: start 5 mg BID, titrate toward ~0.3 mg/kg BID (commonly 10–20 mg BID); monitor BP/HR. (Overlaps the cognitive domain.)
  • Early prevention (not yet guideline-endorsed): sertraline 50 mg/day started soon after TBI reduced incident depression (NNT ~6 in one RCT).

docs.title: PTSD
docs.text:
PTSD — trauma-focused therapy first (VA/DoD 2023, Strong for)

TBI does not preclude trauma-focused therapy — these work in comorbid TBI. Offer a first-line trauma-focused psychotherapy:

  • Cognitive Processing Therapy (CPT), Prolonged Exposure (PE), or EMDR.
  • Written Exposure Therapy (WET) — 2025 APA-suggested alternative: fewer sessions, no homework, lower dropout — feasible in primary care / deployed settings. A 2025 JAMA Psychiatry RCT (n=700) found WET and SSRIs comparably effective in primary care; for SSRI non-responders, switching to venlafaxine beat WET augmentation.

PTSD — pharmacotherapy (dosed)

Guideline-recommended agents are specific — not all SSRIs qualify:

  • First-line (VA/DoD 2023, Strong for): paroxetine 20 → 60 mg, sertraline 50 → 200 mg, or venlafaxine XR 75 → 225 mg. Escitalopram is not PTSD-recommended (no PTSD-specific evidence), and fluoxetine was dropped from the recommended list on updated evidence.
  • Prazosin — nuance the evidence. The large PACT trial (NEJM 2018, n=304 veterans) was negative for nightmares/PTSD at 26 weeks. But a 2025 meta-analysis (10 RCTs, n=648) found prazosin improves nightmares (SMD −0.64) and insomnia (SMD −0.65) — not overall PTSD. VA/DoD suggests against it for overall PTSD but gives a Weak for specifically for nightmares/sleep. Use it targeted: 1 mg qhs, titrate slowly (to ~5–15 mg) by orthostatic-BP tolerance — a two-for-one with post-traumatic headache.

docs.title: Irritability / aggression
docs.text:
Post-TBI agitation/aggression (dosed) — and what to avoid

  • Amantadine 100 mg BID — best TBI-specific evidence (multiple RCTs) for irritability/ aggression.
  • Beta-blockers (propranolol, pindolol) — reduce the intensity of agitation (titrate from a low dose as BP/HR allow).
  • Methylphenidate — improves anger measures (dose as above), doubles for depression/cognition.
  • Avoid haloperidol — may hinder neurocognitive recovery and prolong post-traumatic amnesia.
  • Avoid benzodiazepines for agitation — cognitive impairment and paradoxical disinhibition.

docs.title: Emerging & adjunctive
docs.text:
Stellate ganglion block (SGB) — relevant to the military population

A local-anesthetic block of the cervical sympathetic chain. A 2020 JAMA Psychiatry RCT (n=113 active-duty) found two right-sided SGBs cut CAPS-5 by −12.2 points vs sham; a 2026 VA series (n=579) reported 68% clinically meaningful improvement at 1 week. Hyperarousal symptoms respond best, effects last ~3–6 months, and it can be repeated. The VA actively offers SGB — reasonable adjunct for refractory hyperarousal.

MDMA-assisted therapy — not FDA-approved

The FDA declined to approve MDMA-assisted psychotherapy for PTSD in August 2024 (trial- validity, expectancy, and functional-unblinding concerns); an additional Phase 3 trial is required. Both the 2025 APA and 2023 VA/DoD CPGs rate psychedelics as insufficient evidence to recommend for or against — don't adopt prematurely.

Moral injury — distinct from PTSD (a military-specific concept)

Distress from acts (or failures to act) that violate one's moral code — presenting with shame, guilt, and existential distress rather than fear-based symptoms. It may not respond to standard trauma-focused therapies built around fear extinction. Emerging treatments include Adaptive Disclosure and Impact of Killing. Recognize it; screen when the narrative is about transgression, not threat.

Treat the domains together — they're bidirectional

  • SSRIs/SNRIs also reduce headache frequency (2025 AAPM&R) — a dual-purpose choice when mood and headache coexist.
  • Treating sleep (CBT-I; prazosin for nightmares) substantially improves mental health — treat concurrently or prioritize by severity.
  • Refer for headache-specific CBT, not just PTSD care: it improved both the headache and PTSD in veterans (McGeary 2022), whereas treating PTSD alone did not fix the headache (see the PTH page).

TRICARE — mood/PTSD prescribing

Sertraline, paroxetine, escitalopram, venlafaxine, prazosin, amantadine, propranolol, and generic methylphenidate (Schedule II — stimulant rules apply) are formulary/generic. CPT/PE/EMDR/WET, and SGB go through behavioral-health / pain or anesthesia referral. Verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Military note: PTSD, depression, and suicide risk are central to deployability, profile, and MEB/DES decisions — document screening, diagnosis, treatment trials, and response. See the TBI hub for the retention/aeromedical framing.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Halabi C, Izzy S, DiGiorgio AM, et al. TBI and risk of incident comorbidities (JAMA Netw Open 2024) — decade-long excess risk of depression/anxiety/suicidality.
  2. Fakhoury M, et al. Depression following TBI — overview (Rev Neurosci 2021); Levin HS, Diaz-Arrastia RR. Diagnosis/prognosis/management of mTBI (Lancet Neurol 2015) — prevalence.
  3. Silverberg ND, Lee K, Mikolić A, et al. Action Collaborative on TBI Care — adapted CPG (Ann Fam Med 2025) — screening tools/timing; SSRI–headache dual benefit; treat sleep.
  4. Gitaari M, Mikolic A, Panenka WJ, Silverberg ND. Diagnostic accuracy of mental-health screening tools after mTBI (JAMA Netw Open 2024) — standard cutoffs valid.
  5. Schnurr PP, Hamblen JL, Wolf J, et al. 2023 VA/DoD PTSD/ASD CPG — synopsis (Ann Intern Med 2024) — CPT/PE/EMDR Strong-for; paroxetine/sertraline/venlafaxine; prazosin nightmares Weak-for.
  6. Zoellner LA, et al. APA PTSD treatment CPG (2025) — WET alternative; psychedelics insufficient evidence; Fortney JC, et al. Primary-care PTSD comparative effectiveness — RCT (JAMA Psychiatry 2025).
  7. Jorge RE, Acion L, Burin DI, Robinson RG. Sertraline for preventing mood disorders after TBI — RCT (JAMA Psychiatry 2016).
  8. Arciniegas DB, Seel RT, Macciocchi SN, et al. Practice guideline for methylphenidate use in persons with TBI — ACRM (Arch Phys Med Rehabil 2026); Feinberg C, et al. Pharmacological interventions in mTBI — SR (JAMA Neurol 2021).
  9. Raskind MA, Peskind ER, Chow B, et al. Prazosin for PTSD in veterans (PACT) — RCT (N Engl J Med 2018); Mendes TP, et al. Prazosin for nightmares/insomnia in PTSD — SR/meta-regression (Prog Neuropsychopharmacol Biol Psychiatry 2025).
  10. Klimenko T, et al. Pharmacological interventions for agitation after TBI — updated SR (J Neurotrauma 2026); Rahmani E, et al. Agitation/aggression in TBI — SR of reviews (J Head Trauma Rehabil 2021); Hicks AJ, et al. Aggression after TBI — SR (Front Neurol 2019).
  11. Rae Olmsted KL, Bartoszek M, Mulvaney S, et al. Stellate ganglion block for PTSD — RCT (JAMA Psychiatry 2020); Hollifield M, et al. SGB for PTSD — case series (Int J Environ Res Public Health 2026); Bielawiec T, et al. SGB for PTSD — comprehensive review (Curr Psychiatry Rep 2026).
  12. VA/DoD. Management and Rehabilitation of Post-Acute mTBI CPG (2021) — avoid bupropion (seizure threshold), lithium, benzodiazepines, anticholinergics.
  13. Mikolić A, et al. PTSD treatment in patients with a history of TBI — SR (Clin Psychol Rev 2019); Ragsdale KA, Voss Horrell SC. PE and CPT in veterans with TBI history (J Trauma Stress 2016) — trauma-focused therapy effective in comorbid TBI.

Verified July 2026: prevalence (military PTSD ~36–37%, depression ~30% first year; JAMA Netw Open 2024 decade-long risk), screening tools/timing (PHQ-9/GAD-7/PCL-5/C-SSRS/AUDIT-C, standard cutoffs, ~1-month + repeat), PTSD psychotherapy hierarchy (CPT/PE/EMDR Strong-for + WET) and pharmacotherapy (paroxetine/ sertraline/venlafaxine; escitalopram not PTSD-indicated; fluoxetine dropped), the nuanced prazosin position (PACT negative overall; Weak-for nightmares), methylphenidate for depression (ACRM 2026), amantadine-led agitation management, SGB, and MDMA regulatory status reflect the 2023 VA/DoD, 2025 APA, 2025 AAPM&R, and 2026 ACRM sources cited above. Confirm current TRICARE formulary/PA status and Schedule II/IV constraints before prescribing.

docs.title: TBI comorbidity — Neuroendocrine (hypopituitarism)
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

Part of the TBI work-up & comorbidities hub.

One-liner: the quiet, high-yield miss. Post-traumatic hypopituitarism is common, and its symptoms — fatigue, low mood, low libido, cognitive fog, weight change — mimic the rest of the TBI picture, so it gets blamed on "post-concussive syndrome" and never screened. It is treatable, and it changes disposition. Pooled prevalence of any pituitary-axis dysfunction is ~33% (higher <3 months, much of it transient; persistent ~15% on strict criteria with repeat testing). By axis: GH ~18%, gonadal ~16%, ACTH ~10%, TSH ~6%.

Why the pituitary is vulnerable

Its blood supply runs through long hypophyseal portal vessels — shearing, raised ICP, and hypotension cause ischemic necrosis; basal skull fracture can directly injure the gland or transect the stalk; and anti-pituitary/anti-hypothalamic antibodies appear after TBI and predict delayed-onset deficiency. The GH-secreting somatotrophs sit laterally, fed by the most vulnerable portal vessels — which is why GH is the axis most often lost.

docs.title: Who to screen
docs.text:
Screen the right patients — not every mTBI

  • Screen prospectively: complicated mild TBI (clinically mild but an intracranial abnormality on imaging), moderate, and severe TBI.
  • Uncomplicated mTBI: screen only if suggestive symptoms persist (fatigue, low mood, low libido, cognitive fog, weight change, poor recovery) — testing all mTBI is neither realistic nor cost-effective.
  • Lower the threshold with: basal skull fracture, diffuse axonal injury, raised ICP, prolonged ICU stay, older age, female sex, multiple acute-phase hormone deficiencies, radiological abnormalities.

docs.title: When to screen — the timeline matters
docs.text:
Screening timeline (what to test when)

  1. Acute phase (ICU) — test only two things:
    • Adrenal insufficiency — random cortisol; treat empirically if hemodynamically unstable (below).
    • Diabetes insipidus / SIADH — follow sodium and urine output.
    • Do NOT test GH, gonadal, or thyroid axes acutely — critical illness confounds the results and acute replacement of these doesn't improve outcome.
  2. 3–6 months — first comprehensive screen (morning): cortisol (± ACTH-stimulation if borderline), IGF-1, testosterone/estradiol + LH/FSH, TSH + free T4, prolactin. Watch for loss of secondary hair, new oligo/amenorrhea, sexual dysfunction, weight change, polydipsia, poor recovery.
  3. 12 months — repeat if deficiencies were found at 3–6 months (some recover, some worsen) or if new symptoms appear.
  4. Beyond 12 months — stay alert: new deficiencies can emerge even 5 years out — re-test on new symptoms.

docs.title: Don't-miss emergencies
docs.text:
Acute adrenal insufficiency — treat before you confirm

Life-threatening in the ICU: refractory hypotension, hyponatremia, hemodynamic instability. A random cortisol <10 µg/dL in an acutely ill TBI patient is highly suspicious; <3 µg/dL is diagnostic. If suspected and the patient is unstable, give stress-dose hydrocortisone 50–100 mg IV q8h — do not wait for confirmatory testing. Chronically it is subtle (fatigue, weight loss, orthostasis, hyponatremia) and easily mislabeled "post-concussive"; an undiagnosed patient can go into adrenal crisis with intercurrent illness — which is exactly why screening matters.

Sodium disorders — DI, SIADH, and cerebral salt wasting

  • Central DI (~2.9% acutely, more in severe TBI): polyuria >3 L/day, polydipsia, hypernatremia, dilute urine. Usually transient (hours–days); ~7% permanent.
  • SIADH (commoner than DI acutely): hyponatremia, concentrated urine, euvolemia — usually self-limited; fluid restriction.
  • Triphasic response (classic, rare): DI → SIADH → permanent DI over days as the posterior pituitary necroses and dumps stored AVP.
  • Distinguish SIADH from cerebral salt wasting — euvolemic vs hypovolemic hyponatremia; treatment is opposite (fluid restriction vs saline replacement). Get this right.

docs.title: Individual axes
docs.text:
GH axis — most affected, hardest to diagnose

  • IGF-1 is a screening test, not diagnostic — a normal IGF-1 does not exclude GH deficiency (sensitivity ~50%).
  • Confirm with dynamic testing: the insulin tolerance test (ITT) is gold standard; glucagon stimulation or macimorelin are alternatives. GHRH-arginine is no longer recommended (arginine supply).
  • rhGH replacement improved IGF-1, fatigue, mood, physical performance, and cognition across studies (with neuroimaging changes — increased cortical thickness/gray matter), and 6–12 months improved processing speed, memory, depression, and quality of life — but the evidence base is still limited (small, heterogeneous). The BIAFAC concept (benefit in patients below normal but above classic GH-deficiency cutoffs) is evolving.

Gonadal axis — relevant to the military population

Hypogonadotropic hypogonadism is the 2nd commonest deficiency (~16%): low libido, erectile dysfunction, fatigue, mood change, oligo/amenorrhea.

  • Acute phase: low testosterone is expected (critical illness) — do not treat.
  • Chronic (>3–6 months): persistent hypogonadism warrants replacement — testosterone in men, estrogen/progesterone in premenopausal women.
  • Confirm it's central (low/inappropriately normal LH/FSH) before attributing low testosterone to TBI rather than a primary gonadal cause — relevant given how often testosterone is prescribed in TBI veterans.

The symptom-overlap trap — the practical rule

Fatigue, disrupted sleep, altered cognition, weakness, exercise intolerance, and declining mental health can come from either TBI or hypopituitarism — you cannot tell them apart clinically. The rule: if a TBI patient isn't improving despite appropriate multi-domain treatment (sleep, mood, cognitive), check the pituitary.

Treat the confirmed deficiency (endocrinology)

Refer for targeted hormone replacement — replacing the deficient axis can dramatically improve fatigue, mood, and cognition that resisted everything else. Replace glucocorticoid before thyroid hormone (thyroxine can precipitate adrenal crisis in undiagnosed cortisol deficiency).

Military note: a screenable, treatable cause of "persistent post-concussive symptoms" that changes the disposition — document the axis, the confirmatory test, and the replacement. See the TBI hub for the retention/aeromedical framing.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Kelestimur F, Agha A, Ioachimescu AG, et al. Approach to the patient with TBI-induced pituitary dysfunction (J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2026) — primary reference: who/when to screen, timeline.
  2. Aljboor GS, Tulemat A, Al-Saedi AR, et al. Acute and chronic hypopituitarism following TBI — SR/ meta-analysis (Neurosurg Rev 2024) — prevalence 33%; by-axis rates.
  3. Zhang CD, Ioachimescu AG. Clinical manifestations and treatment of hypopituitarism due to TBI (Best Pract Res Clin Endocrinol Metab 2025); Unluhizarci K, Urhan E. Epidemiology and risk factors (same issue 2025).
  4. Feldt-Rasmussen U, Klose MC. Pathophysiology and diagnosis of neuroendocrine abnormalities in TBI (Best Pract Res Clin Endocrinol Metab 2025); Bensalah M, et al. Cortisol in acute TBI — prospective study (Clin Endocrinol 2018).
  5. Temizkan S, Kelestimur F. Clinical and pathophysiological approach to TBI-induced pituitary dysfunction (Pituitary 2019); Tanriverdi F, et al. Pituitary dysfunction after TBI (Endocr Rev 2015) — portal-vessel/somatotroph vulnerability.
  6. De Bellis A, Bellastella G, Maiorino MI, et al. Role of autoimmunity in pituitary dysfunction due to TBI (Pituitary 2019) — APA/AHA and delayed-onset deficiency.
  7. Batson RD, et al. Outcomes of rhGH therapy in TBI — scoping review (Best Pract Res Clin Endocrinol Metab 2025); Szarka N, et al. GH on neuropsychological outcomes/QoL after TBI — SR (J Neurotrauma 2021).
  8. Fleseriu M, Hashim IA, Karavitaki N, et al. Hormonal replacement in hypopituitarism in adults — Endocrine Society CPG (J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2016); Fleseriu M, et al. Hypopituitarism (Lancet 2024) — dynamic GH testing, replacement sequencing.
  9. Debert CT, Lithgow K. Evaluation of neuroendocrine deficits following TBI — role of the neurorehabilitation specialist (Growth Horm IGF Res 2026) — symptom-overlap trap.

Verified July 2026: prevalence (any dysfunction ~33%; persistent ~15%; GH 18%/gonadal 16%/ACTH 10%/TSH 6%), the refined who-to-screen framework (complicated mild + moderate/severe; symptomatic uncomplicated mTBI only), the screening timeline (acute cortisol/sodium only → 3–6-month panel → 12-month repeat → new deficits up to ~5 years), pathophysiology, the acute adrenal-insufficiency thresholds and stress-dose hydrocortisone, DI/SIADH/CSW distinctions, GH diagnostic caveats (IGF-1 screening only; dynamic testing; rhGH evidence), gonadal-axis management, and glucocorticoid-before-thyroid sequencing reflect the 2026 JCEM review (Kelestimur et al.), the 2025 Best Practice & Research Clinical Endocrinology & Metabolism special issue, the Endocrine Society (2016) guideline, and the sources cited above. Confirm current endocrinology referral pathways and TRICARE coverage before disposition.

docs.title: TBI comorbidity — Pain & substance use
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

Part of the TBI work-up & comorbidities hub.

One-liner: chronic pain and substance use are common, bidirectional with TBI, and both are worsened by the reflex reach for opioids — keep the plan opioid-sparing, multimodal, and mechanism-based, and screen actively. Chronic pain affects ~40–51% after complicated mild-to-severe TBI (≈2× the general population), with a median of 4 pain locations and pain interference in ~55%. Predictors: female sex, military service, pre-injury migraine, penetrating injury, and poor sleep/mood/PTSD.

docs.title: Classify the pain by mechanism — it drives the drug
docs.text:
Post-TBI pain phenotypes (IASP framework)

Mixed phenotypes are common (neuroinflammation + central sensitization) — which is why treatment must be multimodal.

  • Nociceptive (tissue injury/inflammation — musculoskeletal, cervicogenic, fracture) → NSAIDs, physical therapy, targeted injections.
  • Neuropathic (nerve lesion — occipital neuralgia, peripheral nerve injury, central pain) → TCA/SNRI, gabapentinoid, nerve blocks.
  • Nociplastic (altered processing without clear tissue/nerve damage — central sensitization, fibromyalgia-like) → SNRI, exercise, CBT, neuromodulation (drugs alone underperform).

docs.title: Treat — multimodal and opioid-sparing
docs.text:
Pharmacologic ladder (dosed, with TBI-specific caveats)

  • TCA — nortriptyline (preferred) or amitriptyline 10–75 mg qHS — first-line neuropathic (NNT ~4.6) and a two-for-one with sleep/headache prevention. Use nortriptyline (less anticholinergic) and start low — anticholinergic load worsens cognition.
  • SNRI — duloxetine 60–120 mg/day (strongest evidence) or venlafaxine XR 150–225 mg/day — first-line neuropathic (NNT ~7.4); also treats comorbid depression.
  • Gabapentinoid — gabapentin 1200–3600 mg/day or pregabalin 150–600 mg/day — effective for neuropathic pain, but sedation/cognitive effects are a real problem in TBI — weigh against the cognitive comorbidity, titrate slowly.
  • NSAIDs — for nociceptive/musculoskeletal pain; limit duration and cap acute-medication days when headache coexists (MOH, below).
  • Topicals — lidocaine 5% patch, capsaicin — for localized neuropathic pain, minimal systemic/cognitive effect — underutilized in TBI.

Every acute analgesic is a potential MOH contributor

Medication-overuse headache sits at the pain–headache intersection: ~27% of moderate–severe PTH patients have probable MOH at 2–4 months (88% on simple analgesics, only 7% on a preventive). Cap acute-medication days (<10–15/month by agent) and start preventive therapy early in anyone with coexisting headache (see headache).

Non-drug — the disease-specific evidence

  • Collaborative care is the only RCT-proven chronic-pain treatment in TBI. The TBI Care RCT (JAMA Netw Open 2024, n=158) — up to 12 sessions over 16 weeks with a care manager delivering CBT for pain plus weekly expert-team review — cut pain interference (3.46 vs 5.03), sustained at 8 months, and improved pain severity, depression, and anxiety.
  • Graded aerobic exercise — treats pain, autonomic dysfunction, and mood together (see autonomic).
  • Cervical PT — for the very common cervicogenic component.
  • Nerve blocks (greater/third occipital, cervical medial branch) — high-yield for cervicogenic/occipital pain (also on the headache page).
  • Neuromodulation (rTMS, peripheral nerve stimulation) — emerging; rTMS carries a weak recommendation for neuropathic pain.

Opioids — VA/DoD 2022 CPG framework, and a TBI mortality signal

  • Strong recommendation against initiating long-term opioid therapy for chronic pain.
  • Already on opioids? Use a collaborative, patient-centered taper — gradual (5–20% every 4 weeks) while optimizing non-opioid therapy. Rapid tapering is harmful (destabilizes patients, worsens depression, increases suicidal ideation, drives illicit use).
  • Suspect OUD at any point → treat it (buprenorphine/naloxone, naltrexone).
  • TBI-specific: strong opioids carry the highest hazard for 30-day mortality after TBI (HR 1.41), and CNS-affecting drugs are far commoner in TBI patients than controls (37% vs 24%) — another reason to stay opioid-sparing.

docs.title: Screen the substances
docs.text:
Substance-use screening — tools, timing, and the numbers

New-onset SUD affects ~4.2% within 5 years (global cohort, n≈1.89 M): nicotine 2.4%, alcohol 1.1%, cannabis 0.9%, opioids 0.4% — and ~50% of eventual SUD is diagnosed within 3 years. In active-duty airmen, mTBI raised the hazard for alcohol and nicotine dependence and drug abuse (highest early); among post-9/11 veterans with TBI, 26.9% received opioids (of those, 30% also benzodiazepines, 36% sedative-hypnotics).

  • Tools: AUDIT / USAUDIT-C (alcohol — consider a higher cutoff ≥11 after TBI vs the usual ≥8), DAST-10 (drugs), or TAPS (tobacco, alcohol, prescription misuse, illicit — one instrument). All validated in TBI.
  • When: at intake and follow-up, with particular attention in the first 3 years (highest risk).
  • Then: treat and involve behavioral health (see mood/PTSD).

Why TBI and substance use feed each other

TBI → SUD: reward-circuit neuroplasticity, chronic neuroinflammation / microglial priming (a "two-hit" vulnerability), impaired white-matter integrity in reward pathways, and frontal disinhibition/impulsivity. SUD → TBI/worse recovery: substances raise injury risk (falls, MVCs, assaults) and impair neuroplasticity, sleep, and medication adherence.

Cannabis — an explicit military caveat

Cannabis is the 3rd most common new-onset SUD after TBI (~0.9%) and is increasingly used for chronic pain by veterans. For active-duty service members it remains prohibited — a fitness-for-duty / UCMJ issue regardless of state law. The VA cannot prescribe or recommend cannabis but should discuss it openly with transitioning/veteran patients.

Military note: opioid/substance issues carry fitness-for-duty, drug-testing, and readiness implications — an added reason to stay opioid-sparing and screen early. See the TBI hub.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Papic C, Wyborn J, Schultz R, et al. Prevalence, predictors, and treatment of chronic pain after complicated mild-to-severe TBI — SR/meta-analysis (Pain 2025); Hoffman JM, et al. pain-location/ interference data (see ref 2).
  2. Hoffman JM, Curran M, Barber J, et al. Collaborative care for chronic pain after TBI — RCT (TBI Care) (JAMA Netw Open 2024) — pain interference 3.46 vs 5.03, sustained 8 months.
  3. Esneault BS, Maddox MB, Loewe EM, et al. Pharmacologic and nonpharmacologic pain management in TBI — multidisciplinary review (J Clin Med 2025) — IASP mechanism classification.
  4. Soliman N, Moisset X, Ferraro MC, et al. Pharmacotherapy and non-invasive neuromodulation for neuropathic pain — SR/meta-analysis (Lancet Neurol 2025) — NNTs; rTMS; Sokol R, et al. Nonopioid management of chronic noncancer pain (Am Fam Physician 2025).
  5. Nygaard C, Schytz HW, Odgaard L, et al. PTH 2–4 months after mTBI — phenotypes and treatment patterns (Cephalalgia 2026) — 27% probable MOH; Kothari SF, et al. Persistent PTH in adolescents/ young adults (Sci Rep 2022).
  6. Sandbrink F, Murphy JL, Johansson M, et al. 2022 VA/DoD Opioid CPG — synopsis (Ann Intern Med 2023); Cajanus K, et al. CNS-affecting medications and mortality after TBI (Neurosurgery 2024) — strong-opioid HR 1.41.
  7. Hoglund ZT, Sollenberger C, Scott KW, et al. New-onset SUD following TBI — global multicenter analysis (TriNetX) (J Clin Med 2026); Miller SC, et al. Addiction-related disorders after mTBI in active-duty airmen (Am J Psychiatry 2013); Holmer HK, et al. Opioid/sedative-hypnotic prescriptions in post-9/11 veterans with TBI (J Head Trauma Rehabil 2021).
  8. Bryce S, Spitz G, Ponsford J. Validity of the AUDIT and DAST after TBI (J Head Trauma Rehabil 2014); Mehta S, et al. Screening questionnaires for substance abuse post brain injury — review (Brain Inj 2019); Brasel KJ, et al. ACS Best Practices — screening/intervention for mental health and substance use in trauma (2022).
  9. Jammoul M, et al. TBI and opioids — twin plagues (Biol Psychiatry 2024); Best KM, et al. Dynorphin linking TBI, chronic pain, and SUD (J Neurotrauma 2022); Olsen CM, Corrigan JD. Does TBI cause risky substance use? (Biol Psychiatry 2022) — bidirectional mechanisms.

Verified July 2026: prevalence (chronic pain ~40–51%, ~2× general population; median 4 locations; SUD ~4.2% at 5 years), the IASP mechanism classification, the dosed pharmacologic ladder with TBI cognitive/ sedation caveats, the MOH intersection, the TBI Care collaborative-care RCT (the only disease-specific chronic-pain RCT), the 2022 VA/DoD Opioid CPG taper framework and the strong-opioid HR 1.41 mortality signal, SUD screening tools/timing (AUDIT cutoff ≥11, DAST-10, TAPS; first-3-year window), the bidirectional TBI–SUD mechanisms, and the cannabis military caveat reflect the sources cited above. Confirm current TRICARE formulary/PA status and service-specific substance policies before counseling.

docs.title: TBI comorbidity — Post-traumatic seizures
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

Part of the TBI work-up & comorbidities hub.

One-liner: distinguish early provoked seizures from post-traumatic epilepsy (PTE), and know the one rule that trips people up — prophylaxis is a 7-day intervention that does not prevent epilepsy.

docs.title: Know the risk — it stratifies sharply by severity
docs.text:
Prevalence: early PTS vs post-traumatic epilepsy

  • Early PTS (≤7 days): up to 15% in severe TBI (down to <5% with prophylaxis); only 0–4% in mild/moderate (mostly <2%).
  • Subclinical (nonconvulsive) seizures: ~25% of moderate–severe TBI on continuous EEG — the main reason to monitor.
  • Post-traumatic epilepsy (late, unprovoked): ~⅓ of severe TBI survivors; 4–15% in moderate–severe; 45–53% in penetrating injury; mTBI roughly triples baseline risk (absolute ~1–4%).
  • 10-year epilepsy risk (Swedish registry, n≈112,000): 12.9% focal cerebral, 8.1% diffuse, 7.3% extracerebral, 2.6% mild — vs 0.9% in controls.
  • Repeated TBI doubles it: a Danish cohort (n≈2.5 M) found HR 2.04 after a first TBI and HR 4.45 after a second, staying elevated for decades.

Risk factors for PTE (2024 ACS Best Practices) — who to watch long-term

Evacuated SDH, contusion + SDH, evacuated intraparenchymal hematoma; GCS 3–8; early seizures (especially delayed); LOC/amnesia >1 day; >7 days to follow commands; skull fracture, unelevated depressed fracture, dural penetration; nonreactive pupil(s), parietal lesions; age >65, female sex, family history of epilepsy, history of depression.

docs.title: Acute management
docs.text:
Early-seizure prophylaxis — a 7-day intervention

  • Who gets it: moderate–severe TBI with intracranial bleeding / risk factors → 7 days of prophylaxis. Not recommended in TBI without intracranial bleeding or in isolated traumatic SAH (2024 ACS).
  • Agent — levetiracetam is preferred: levetiracetam 500–1000 mg IV/PO q12h (or ~20 mg/kg load) or phenytoin (20 mg/kg load then maintenance with levels). Meta-analyses show equivalent efficacy (OR ~0.85), but levetiracetam has fewer interactions, no drug-level monitoring, and shorter ICU stays — the 2026 Brain Trauma Foundation penetrating-TBI guideline names it the modern preferred agent.
  • Start early, stop at 7 days. AAN (Level A) — start ASAP after severe TBI to reduce early PTS; do not continue beyond 7 days (Level B).
  • Mild/moderate TBI: the benefit is small — a 2024 JAMA Neurology meta-analysis found an absolute risk reduction of 0.6% (NNT 167) — weigh against ASM harms and the real risk of inappropriate continuation past 7 days.

The rule: prophylaxis does NOT prevent epilepsy

The landmark Temkin RCT showed phenytoin cut early PTS from 14.2% → 3.6% but had no effect on late PTS/PTE — replicated across meta-analyses and the basis of every current guideline. Early PTS are associated with later PTE, but treating early seizures does not reduce PTE risk — so an early seizure should prompt closer long-term surveillance, not indefinite prophylaxis.

Continuous EEG — the 'won't wake up' after TBI

Nonconvulsive seizures occur in ~25% of moderate–severe TBI and are tied to worse outcomes and hippocampal atrophy. Consider cEEG in any moderate–severe TBI with unexplained altered consciousness ("won't wake up" → cEEG). The 2024 ACS guideline supports cEEG for seizure detection (and emerging detection of secondary ischemia); in the multicenter INTREPID study, EEG background features (absent posterior-dominant rhythm, absent sleep transients, predominant delta, discontinuous background) were independently associated with functional outcome.

Penetrating TBI — highest risk, least certainty

Penetrating TBI carries the highest PTE risk (45–53%), and frontal/temporal injuries are likely more epileptogenic. The 2026 Brain Trauma Foundation penetrating-TBI guideline flags pTBI as higher-risk than blunt but did not reach consensus on prophylaxis duration or cessation criteria — a genuine uncertainty for providers managing combat-related penetrating injury.

docs.title: Late seizures & follow-up
docs.text:
Late (>7-day) unprovoked seizure = post-traumatic epilepsy

  • A late unprovoked seizure means epilepsy — start treatment and work it up. Risk is highest after penetrating or severe TBI and can emerge months–years later.
  • Driving (2025 AAN position statement): exclusively provoked seizures from factors unlikely to recur may not need a seizure-free interval — but sleep deprivation and alcohol withdrawal are not exceptions. For unprovoked seizures the minimum interval is ~3 months, individualized by a medical advisory board.
  • Full management — the status-epilepticus ladder, first-seizure work-up, and ASM selection — is on the seizure & status epilepticus page and the antiseizure medications page.

Horizon — antiepileptogenesis (no proven therapy yet)

The key unmet need: current ASMs prevent early seizures but do not modify epileptogenesis. The 2026 Epilepsia consensus roadmap (Zanier et al.) targets neuroinflammation (IL-1β/NLRP3), blood-brain-barrier dysfunction, and mTOR signaling. Preclinical candidates — IL-1 receptor antagonist (anakinra), mTOR inhibitor (rapamycin), glutamate modulator (ceftriaxone) — reduce seizures in animals but are unvalidated in humans. Candidate biomarkers: early epileptiform EEG (≈3× PTE risk within 14 days), serum S100B (HR ~2.12 in complicated mTBI), and neuroinflammatory markers. Military relevance: PTE is ~5% of all epilepsy and TBI is the signature military injury — moderate–severe/penetrating cohorts are a natural population for future prevention trials.

Military note: post-traumatic epilepsy is handled as any seizure disorder (AR 40-501 3-31i / DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 5.26.c → generally below retention; flying-duty disqualifying) — see the TBI hub and seizure page.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Manley GT, Albert GW, Brophy GM, et al. Best Practices in the Management of TBI (American College of Surgeons 2024) — prophylaxis indications, risk factors, cEEG.
  2. Pease M, Mittal A, Merkaj S, et al. Early seizure prophylaxis in mild and moderate TBI — SR/ meta-analysis (JAMA Neurol 2024) — ARR 0.6%, NNT 167; early-to-late relationship.
  3. Karlander M, Ljungqvist J, Zelano J. Post-traumatic epilepsy in adults — nationwide register study (J Neurol Neurosurg Psychiatry 2021); Shafiyev J, et al. EEG and 5-year PTE risk after mTBI (Epilepsia 2026).
  4. Lolk K, Dreier JW, Christensen J. Repeated TBI and risk of epilepsy — Danish nationwide cohort (Brain 2021) — HR 2.04 first / 4.45 second injury.
  5. Karamian A, et al. Levetiracetam vs phenytoin for seizure prevention in TBI — SR/meta-analysis (Clin Neurol Neurosurg 2024); Huo X, et al. ASM prophylaxis in brain injury — network meta-analysis (Front Pharmacol 2022).
  6. Chang BS, Lowenstein DH. Practice parameter: antiepileptic drug prophylaxis in severe TBI — AAN (Neurology 2003); Carney N, et al. Guidelines for the Management of Severe TBI, 4th ed. (Brain Trauma Foundation 2016).
  7. Bell RS, Selph S, Ghajar J, et al. BTF Guidelines for the Management of Penetrating TBI, 2nd ed. (Neurosurgery 2026) — pTBI risk; levetiracetam preferred; no consensus on duration.
  8. Lee H, Mizrahi MA, Hartings JA, et al. Continuous EEG after moderate–severe TBI (INTREPID) (Crit Care Med 2019); Laing J, et al. Early post-traumatic seizures in moderate–severe TBI (JAMA Neurol 2022).
  9. Tolchin B, Krauss GL, Spanaki MV, et al. Seizures, driver licensure, and medical reporting — AAN position statement (Neurology 2025).
  10. Antiepileptogenesis: Zanier ER, et al. Consensus roadmap for post-traumatic epilepsy (Epilepsia 2026); Pease M, Gupta K, Moshé SL, et al. Insights into epileptogenesis from PTE (Nat Rev Neurol 2024); Yazdi SM, et al. Emerging biomarkers and therapeutic targets (Seizure 2025); Lolk K, et al. S100B as a biomarker for epilepsy after TBI (Epilepsia 2026).

Verified July 2026: prevalence/risk stratification (early PTS 15% severe vs <2% mild; PTE ~⅓ severe, 45–53% penetrating, ~1–4% mild; repeated-TBI HR 4.45), the ACS 2024 risk-factor list, prophylaxis indications (7 days with intracranial bleeding; not for isolated tSAH; NNT 167 in mild/moderate), levetiracetam-preferred dosing and equivalence to phenytoin, the AAN "stop at 7 days" and Temkin "does-not-prevent-epilepsy" evidence, ~25% subclinical seizures / cEEG for the "won't wake up" patient, the penetrating-TBI distinction, the 2025 AAN driving guidance, and the antiepileptogenesis horizon reflect the sources cited above. The DoD retention/aeromedical citations (AR 40-501 3-31i; DoDI 6130.03 Vol 2 5.26.c) are quoted from those issuances — confirm current editions before counseling.

docs.title: TBI comorbidity — Sleep
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

Part of the TBI work-up & comorbidities hub.

One-liner: sleep disruption sustains every other TBI symptom (headache, mood, cognition, fatigue) — fix it first. Sleep–wake disturbance affects 30–70% of mTBI and ~46% of chronic TBI patients (insomnia the commonest), and a VA cohort of ~200,000 veterans confirmed TBI independently raises the risk of insomnia, hypersomnia, and sleep apnea years after injury.

Sleep gates recovery — the relationship is bidirectional

Poor sleep worsens post-traumatic headache (OR ~2.9 in the first 6 weeks), cognition, and mood/anxiety; conversely, treating sleep improves outcomes across every other domain. The 2025 AAPM&R guideline explicitly prioritizes sleep as a treatment target because it gates recovery. Improving sleep often unmasks how much of the "post-concussive" picture was sleep-driven.

Screen and measure — validated instruments

  • ISI (Insomnia Severity Index) — 7 items, validated in TBI; ≥10 = clinical insomnia.
  • PSQI (Pittsburgh Sleep Quality Index) — global sleep quality; >5 = poor sleep.
  • ESS (Epworth Sleepiness Scale) — daytime sleepiness; ≥10 = excessive.
  • STOP-Bang — OSA risk (below).
  • Actigraphy — objective sleep–wake/circadian patterns when PSG isn't indicated.
  • PSG ± MSLT — for suspected OSA, narcolepsy, or hypersomnia (MSLT confirms objective sleepiness and separates it from fatigue).

docs.title: Name the disorder — it's not all insomnia
docs.text:
The post-TBI sleep differential (with rough prevalence)

  • Insomnia — most common; onset and/or maintenance difficulty.
  • Obstructive sleep apnea — 20–77% across studies; drives "refractory" fatigue/cognitive complaints (work-up below).
  • Post-traumatic hypersomnia (~11–22%) — sleep need ≥2 h/day above pre-injury, from hypothalamic injury / reduced hypocretin (orexin) signaling.
  • Narcolepsy / narcolepsy-like (~6%) — loss of hypocretin neurons → excessive daytime sleepiness, occasionally cataplexy. Exclude before starting a DORA (contraindicated).
  • Periodic limb movement disorder (~7%).
  • Parasomnias — nightmares, REM sleep behavior disorder — especially with comorbid PTSD.
  • Fatigue ≠ sleepiness. Fatigue is daytime tiredness without objective sleepiness on MSLT and needs different management (activity pacing, mood/pain treatment) — do not reach for a wake-promoting agent for pure fatigue.

docs.title: Insomnia
docs.text:
Step 1 — CBT-I is first-line (Strong for)

CBT-I before drugs. VA/DoD 2019 Chronic Insomnia CPG gives it a Strong for recommendation; a 2026 network meta-analysis (22 RCTs, n=1,299 TBI patients) found CBT the most consistently effective intervention across ISI/PSQI/ESS — outperforming every drug tested. 70–80% get enduring benefit and ~50% reach remission.

  • No in-person CBT-I? Use internet-delivered CBT-I (eCBT-I / SHUTi) — a 2024 JAMA Netw Open RCT in military TBI patients showed feasibility and efficacy, and it fixes the access barrier (military CBT-I referrals rose 372% over 2005–2014).
  • Pair with sleep-hygiene / circadian measures: fixed wake time, morning light, evening screen/caffeine limits — relevant to shift work and deployment.

Step 2 — pharmacotherapy if CBT-I is insufficient (dosed)

Adjunct to CBT-I, not a substitute. Match the drug to onset vs maintenance; reassess and taper.

  • Melatonin (prolonged-release) 2–5 mg, ~2 h before bedtime — best TBI-specific evidence: an RCT (n=33) improved PSQI (−1.79 vs placebo, p≤0.0001), sleep efficiency, anxiety, and fatigue; a 2023 scoping review found benefit in 8/9 studies, well tolerated. Sleep-onset benefit mainly — less for maintenance.
  • Dual orexin receptor antagonists (DORAs) — suvorexant 10–20 mg, lemborexant 5–10 mg, or daridorexant 25–50 mg qhs. Work for both onset and maintenance, less cognitive impairment than benzodiazepines, low abuse potential; a 2025 network meta-analysis shows all three beat placebo. Mechanistically apt (TBI disrupts the orexin system) — but contraindicated in narcolepsy (exclude first). Likely PA-gated on TRICARE.
  • Low-dose doxepin 3–6 mg qhs — FDA-approved for sleep-maintenance insomnia; the only sedating antidepressant with consistent insomnia efficacy (2024 NEJM review).
  • Trazodone 25–100 mg qhs — widely used off-label (helpful with comorbid mood), but no TBI-specific RCT and "inconsistent efficacy evidence" for general insomnia; a head-to-head vs melatonin vs combination trial is underway (2026).
  • Avoid: chronic benzodiazepines / Z-drugs (cognition, dependence, fall/duty risk) and antihistamines (diphenhydramine, hydroxyzine — anticholinergic/cognitive load, weak evidence).

docs.title: Excessive daytime sleepiness / hypersomnia
docs.text:
Wake-promoting agents — for objective sleepiness, not fatigue (dosed)

First exclude and treat OSA and confirm objective sleepiness (ESS/MSLT). Then, for post-traumatic hypersomnia (AASM 2021 Conditional recommendation):

  • Modafinil 100–200 mg each morning, or armodafinil 150–250 mg each morning. A 2025 meta-analysis (3 RCTs, n=158) showed reduced ESS (MD −1.65, p=0.04); armodafinil 250 mg gave the clearest objective sleep-latency benefit (+7.2 vs +2.4 min). Watch for treatment- emergent insomnia (RR ~3.7).
  • Schedule IV controlled substances — relevant for military prescribing and deployability.

docs.title: Obstructive sleep apnea
docs.text:
OSA — screen, test in tiers, treat (Strong for PAP)

  • Screen with STOP-Bang (Snoring, Tiredness, Observed apnea, Pressure, BMI, Age, Neck, Gender): ≥3 intermediate, ≥5 high risk. Have a low threshold — OSA prevalence in TBI is 20–77%.
  • Phased, cost-effective work-up: STOP-Bang → home sleep apnea (portable) testing → lab PSG if portable testing is negative/discordant but suspicion remains.
  • PAP therapy is first-line (VA/DoD 2019 OSA CPG, Strong for). Set expectations: CPAP reliably improves AHI but may not fully resolve daytime sleepiness in TBI — reassess for a coexisting central hypersomnia if sleepiness persists on adherent PAP.

Light therapy — non-drug, no interactions, deployable

Useful for circadian misalignment, sleepiness, and comorbid low mood — attractive in the military setting. Morning blue-wavelength light (~469 nm, 30 min) phase-advanced sleep, cut daytime sleepiness, and improved executive function in an mTBI RCT (n=32); a 2022 meta-analysis (6 RCTs, n=278) found reduced sleep disturbance (SMD −0.63) and depression (SMD −1.00). Morning bright light ~10,000 lux, 60 min improved insomnia, mood, and pain in a veteran cohort.

TRICARE — TBI sleep prescribing

Melatonin, trazodone, doxepin, modafinil/armodafinil are generic/formulary (modafinil-class are Schedule IV; generic modafinil is preferred over branded). DORAs (suvorexant, lemborexant, daridorexant) are PA-gated — document CBT-I ± prior-agent failure. CBT-I, eCBT-I, PSG, and PAP go through normal sleep-medicine/behavioral-health referral. Verify at the TRICARE Formulary Search.

Military note: untreated OSA, chronic hypnotics, and Schedule IV wake-promoting agents are duty/deployability considerations — document the work-up, diagnosis, and PAP adherence. See the TBI hub for the retention/aeromedical framing.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Ouellet MC, Beaulieu-Bonneau S, Morin CM. Sleep-wake disturbances after TBI (Lancet Neurol 2015) — bidirectional relationship, OSA/CPAP caveats.
  2. Leng Y, Byers AL, Barnes DE, et al. TBI and incidence of sleep disorders in ~200,000 US veterans (Neurology 2021) — independent risk.
  3. Castriotta RJ, Murthy JN. Sleep disorders in patients with TBI (CNS Drugs 2011); Viola-Saltzman M, Musleh C. TBI-induced sleep disorders (Neuropsychiatr Dis Treat 2016) — full spectrum/prevalence.
  4. Baumann CR, et al. Sleep-wake disturbances 6 months after TBI (Brain 2007); Loss of hypocretin neurons with TBI (Ann Neurol 2009) — hypersomnia/narcolepsy mechanism.
  5. Wang Y, Yan J, Su L, Wang B. Treatment of sleep disorders following TBI — SR and network meta-analysis (J Head Trauma Rehabil 2026) — CBT most effective.
  6. Malarkey ME, Fu AJ, Mannan N, et al. Internet-guided CBT-I in patients with TBI — RCT (JAMA Netw Open 2024) — eCBT-I in military TBI.
  7. Martin JL, Mysliwiec V, Chowdhuri S, Ulmer CS. VA/DoD CPGs for sleep disorders (J Clin Sleep Med 2020) — CBT-I and PAP Strong-for.
  8. Grima NA, et al. Efficacy of melatonin for sleep disturbance following TBI — RCT (BMC Med 2018); Cassimatis M, et al. Melatonin scoping review (Arch Phys Med Rehabil 2023).
  9. Morin CM, Buysse DJ. Management of insomnia (N Engl J Med 2024) — doxepin/trazodone/antihistamine evidence; Kishi T, et al. DORAs network meta-analysis (Transl Psychiatry 2025).
  10. Maski K, Trotti LM, Kotagal S, et al. Treatment of central disorders of hypersomnolence — AASM CPG (J Clin Sleep Med 2021); João RB, et al. Modafinil/armodafinil for EDS after TBI — SR/MA (Brain Inj 2025); Menn SJ, et al. Armodafinil RCT (J Clin Sleep Med 2014).
  11. Johnson K, et al. OSA in TBI — scoping review (Laryngoscope 2025); Tsalatsanis A, et al. Cost- effectiveness of sleep-apnea diagnosis/treatment in moderate–severe TBI (J Head Trauma Rehabil 2025).
  12. Srisurapanont K, et al. Blue-wavelength light therapy for post-TBI symptoms — SR/MA (PLoS One 2022); Killgore WDS, et al. Blue-light RCT in mTBI (Neurobiol Dis 2020); Elliott JE, et al. Morning bright light in veterans with TBI (PLoS One 2022).
  13. Silverberg ND, Lee K, Mikolić A, et al. Action Collaborative on TBI Care — adapted CPG (Ann Fam Med 2025) — prioritize sleep as a treatment target.

Verified July 2026: prevalence (30–70% mTBI, ~46% chronic; VA n≈200,000), the disorder spectrum with approximate prevalences, CBT-I/eCBT-I as first-line (VA/DoD Strong-for; 2026 NMA), dosed pharmacotherapy (melatonin 2–5 mg, DORAs, doxepin 3–6 mg, trazodone caveats), modafinil/armodafinil for hypersomnia (AASM 2021 Conditional), STOP-Bang phased OSA work-up + PAP (VA/DoD Strong-for), and light therapy reflect the sources cited above. Confirm current TRICARE formulary/PA status and Schedule IV prescribing constraints before counseling.

docs.title: TBI comorbidity — Vestibular / dizziness
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

Part of the TBI work-up & comorbidities hub.

One-liner: dizziness is the second most common post-mTBI symptom after headache — 50–80% acutely, persisting in 25–30% beyond 3 months. The highest-yield moves are to catch the curable causes (BPPV), name the specific diagnosis (many have specific treatments), and refer for vestibular rehabilitation — not to reach for a vestibular suppressant. In blast mTBI, the otolith organs (utricle/saccule) are more vulnerable to overpressure than the semicircular canals, which shifts testing and rehab.

docs.title: Name the diagnosis — it's not all "post-concussive dizziness"
docs.text:
Differential of post-traumatic dizziness (each treats differently)

  • BPPV — commonest and curable; post-traumatic BPPV is distinct (below).
  • Labyrinthine concussion — direct inner-ear injury → acute vestibular hypofunction without fracture; looks like vestibular neuritis but with a trauma history.
  • Vestibular migraine — a major driver of persistent post-traumatic dizziness, especially with a migraine-phenotype PTH; responds to migraine preventives, not suppressants.
  • PPPD (persistent postural-perceptual dizziness) — Bárány Society 2017 diagnosis: chronic non-spinning dizziness/unsteadiness ≥3 months, worse upright, with self-motion, and with complex visual stimuli; mTBI is a recognized trigger. Treat with SSRI/SNRI + CBT + vestibular rehab, not suppressants (below).
  • Superior semicircular canal dehiscence (SSCD) — trauma unmasks a thin superior canal → sound/pressure-induced vertigo, autophony, pulsatile tinnitus (~45% report a preceding traumatic event). Diagnose with high-res CT temporal bone + VEMP.
  • Perilymph fistula — oval/round-window disruption after barotrauma/impact → fluctuating hearing loss + positional vertigo.
  • Secondary endolymphatic hydrops — post-traumatic Ménière-like: episodic vertigo, hearing loss, tinnitus.
  • Central vestibular dysfunction — diffuse axonal injury of vestibular nuclei/cerebellum/ vestibulo-thalamic pathways; may coexist with peripheral disease.

Acute — exclude a central cause first

New acute vestibular syndrome after trauma: exclude posterior-circulation stroke or vertebral/carotid dissection — use HINTS (a normal head-impulse, direction-changing nystagmus, or skew deviation is central), plus red flags (headache/neck pain, focal signs). Don't call new dizziness "concussion" until the central cause is excluded.

docs.title: Assess
docs.text:
Screening & bedside tools (with cutoffs)

  • VOMS (Vestibular/Ocular-Motor Screening) — smooth pursuit, saccades, VOR, visual-motion sensitivity, near-point convergence. Abnormal = symptom provocation ≥2 points on any item, or NPC >5 cm.
  • Dix-Hallpike (posterior canal) and supine roll test (horizontal canal) — positional BPPV.
  • Dynamic visual acuity (DVA) — VOR during head movement; ≥2-line drop from static acuity is abnormal.
  • DHI (Dizziness Handicap Inventory) — 25 items; >30 = moderate handicap; tracks response.
  • BESS (Balance Error Scoring System) — the SCAT6/MACE2 balance test.
  • FGA (Functional Gait Assessment) — 10-item dynamic gait; more sensitive than the Berg Balance Scale in mTBI (head turns, eyes-closed walking).

docs.title: Treat
docs.text:
BPPV — post-traumatic BPPV is harder than idiopathic

  • Reposition, don't suppress. Dix-Hallpike → Epley for posterior canal; supine roll → Lempert (barbecue) roll for horizontal canal.
  • Check all canals: post-traumatic BPPV is multi-canal in ~50–67% (vs ~10% idiopathic) and more often bilateral — a Dix-Hallpike alone will miss horizontal-canal disease.
  • Expect higher recurrence and more maneuvers. Diagnose early — median time to first repositioning is ~13.5 days in polytrauma, and earlier treatment improves outcomes.

Vestibular rehabilitation therapy (VRT) — first-line for persistent symptoms

  • Refer for VRT for persistent dizziness/imbalance/oculomotor symptoms. A 2024 meta-analysis (6 RCTs) showed improved DHI (SMD −0.33) and VOMS (SMD −0.40) at end of treatment; individually tailored multimodal (cervical + vestibular + oculomotor) therapy had a moderate effect (SMD 0.63) and made patients ~3× more likely to return to sport by 8 weeks.
  • Goal-based reassessment: VA/DoD recommends vestibular/balance therapy beyond the acute phase but discourages prolonged therapy without improvement — reassess against functional goals.
  • Provider matters — ideally a therapist with post-graduate vestibular-rehab training.

Cervicogenic dizziness — identify and treat the neck

Common after whiplash/blast (cervical proprioceptive dysfunction). Suspect when dizziness is provoked by neck movement, with neck pain and restricted cervical ROM. Identify with the cervical joint-position-error test and smooth-pursuit neck-torsion test. Treat with cervical manual therapy + proprioceptive retraining (Langevin 2022 RCT improved cervical and vestibular measures).

PPPD — break the dizziness→anxiety→avoidance cycle

Persistent dizziness often becomes maladaptive: dizziness → anxiety → movement avoidance → deconditioning → worse dizziness (the mechanism behind PPPD) — high-yield to catch in service members, where avoidance degrades duty performance. Treat all three arms: SSRI/SNRI (sertraline, venlafaxine), CBT targeting dizziness-related anxiety/avoidance (see mood/PTSD), and graded vestibular exposure via rehab. Suppressants make it worse.

Vestibular suppressants — mostly a don't after concussion

  • Not recommended routinely (VA/DoD) — they delay central compensation and promote counterproductive adaptation.
  • If symptoms are severe enough to limit function, a brief (<1 week) trial of meclizine 25 mg is the preferred agent. Avoid benzodiazepines (clonazepam/diazepam/lorazepam — sedation, cognitive impairment, dependence).
  • Never treat BPPV with a suppressant — reposition instead.

When to refer for formal vestibular lab testing

Escalate beyond bedside (VNG, calorics, rotary chair, VEMP, posturography) for:

  • Persistent dizziness >4 weeks despite initial management.
  • Atypical nystagmus not consistent with BPPV.
  • Suspected SSCD (order high-res CT temporal bone + VEMP) or perilymph fistula (fluctuating hearing loss + positional vertigo).
  • Failure to respond to vestibular rehab.
  • Need for objective documentation (MEB/VA rating).

Military note: persistent vestibular dysfunction is deployment- and (with the vision/oculomotor overlap) special-duty-limiting; blast exposure favors otolith injury (VEMP-testable). Document objective testing for the TBI hub retention/aeromedical framing.

docs.text:
1. Gianoli GJ. Post-concussive dizziness — review and clinical approach (Front Neurol 2022); Fife TD, Kalra D. Persistent vertigo and dizziness after mTBI (Ann N Y Acad Sci 2015) — differential.
  2. Owens MR, Larkin E, Tenhoeve SA, et al. Vestibular dysfunction after mTBI — SR (Clin Neuropsychol 2025) — prevalence; Akin FW, et al. Vestibular consequences of mTBI and blast (Brain Inj 2017) — otolith vulnerability.
  3. VA/DoD. Management and Rehabilitation of Post-Acute mTBI CPG (2021) — VRT, suppressant guidance, goal-based reassessment, referral.
  4. Kaae C, et al. Vestibulo-ocular dysfunction in mTBI — utility of VOMS (NeuroRehabilitation 2022); Kontos AP, et al. VOMS and concussion rehab (J Athl Train 2017) — VOMS cutoffs.
  5. Joyce JM, et al. Balance measures in moderate-severe TBI (Front Neurol 2022); D'Silva LJ, et al. Oculomotor deficits and dynamic mobility in chronic mTBI (Front Neurol 2021) — FGA/DVA.
  6. Kong TH, Seo YJ. Post-traumatic BPPV — mechanisms, phenotypes, structured pathway (Front Neurol 2026); Kim H, et al. Early management of traumatic BPPV (Injury 2022) — multi-canal, delayed dx.
  7. Aljabri A, et al. Efficacy of VRT for mTBI — SR/meta-analysis (J Head Trauma Rehabil 2024); Reid SA, et al. Physical interventions after concussion — SR/MA (Br J Sports Med 2022) — multimodal.
  8. Langevin P, Frémont P, Fait P, et al. Cervicovestibular rehabilitation in adults with mTBI — RCT (J Neurotrauma 2022) — cervicogenic dizziness.
  9. PPPD: Bárány Society 2017 consensus; Webster KE, et al. Pharmacological interventions for PPPD (Cochrane 2023); Hallett M, et al. Functional neurological disorder (Lancet Neurol 2022).
  10. Third-window / SSCD / PLF: McCrary HC, et al. SSCD following head trauma (Laryngoscope 2021); Formeister EJ, et al. Traumatic events preceding SSCD (Otolaryngol Head Neck Surg 2024); Kontorinis G, Lenarz T. SSCD narrative review (J Laryngol Otol 2022); Wallace A, et al. Perilymphatic fistula / third-window (Otol Neurotol 2026).
  11. Gottlieb M, Peksa GD, Carlson JN. HINTS for central causes of acute vestibular syndrome — SR (Cochrane 2023).

Verified July 2026: prevalence (50–80% acute, 25–30% persistent) and blast-otolith vulnerability, the expanded differential (vestibular migraine, PPPD [Bárány 2017], SSCD, labyrinthine concussion, perilymph fistula, hydrops, central), assessment cutoffs (VOMS ≥2/NPC >5 cm, DHI >30, DVA ≥2 lines, FGA), the distinct features of post-traumatic BPPV (multi-canal 50–67%), VRT evidence and goal-based reassessment, cervicogenic-dizziness work-up, the "suppressants delay compensation" guidance (brief meclizine only; no benzodiazepines), and referral criteria for formal testing reflect the VA/DoD (2021) CPG and the peer-reviewed sources cited above. Confirm current specialty-referral pathways before disposition.

docs.title: TBI comorbidity — Vision / oculomotor
docs.text:
Last reviewed: July 2026

Part of the TBI work-up & comorbidities hub.

One-liner: convergence insufficiency and oculomotor dysfunction are common after TBI and drive reading difficulty, headache, and "screen intolerance" — screen routinely, because office-based vision therapy has now-strong (Level 1) evidence. Convergence insufficiency runs ~36% and accommodative dysfunction ~43% after TBI, visual-field loss ~18% overall; 69% of persisting concussions have ≥1 vision disorder, and >40% of blast-TBI veterans report diplopia.

docs.title: Name the diagnosis — it's a spectrum
docs.text:
Post-TBI visual/oculomotor differential (2022 AAN/NANOS taxonomy)

  • Convergence insufficiency (CI) — the classic; reduced near convergence → near blur, diplopia, reading fatigue.
  • Accommodative insufficiency (AI) — as common as CI (~43%); blur/headache/fatigue with near work; the near triad (accommodation–convergence–miosis) is linked, so AI and CI usually coexist.
  • Saccadic dysfunction — impaired rapid refixation → slow, inaccurate reading (horizontal and vertical).
  • Smooth-pursuit dysfunction — poor tracking → motion sensitivity, trouble following lectures/conversation.
  • Visual-field loss — ~18% overall, far higher in moderate–severe (40%) than mTBI (7%).
  • Cranial-nerve palsies — CN IV (trochlear) is the most susceptible to minor trauma; CN III and VI less so; presents as binocular diplopia.
  • Higher-order visual-cognitive dysfunction — visual-spatial processing, spatial bias, visual attention — mimics an ocular problem; differentiate with OT vision / behavioral-neurology assessment.

docs.title: Assess
docs.text:
Screening & bedside tools (with cutoffs)

Start with the VA/DoD basic PCP eye exam — near and distance acuity, confrontation fields, pupils, eye movements, external exam, nystagmus — before specialist referral.

  • VOMS — 5 domains (smooth pursuit, horizontal/vertical saccades, NPC, horizontal VOR, visual- motion sensitivity). Abnormal = symptom provocation ≥2 points on any item, or NPC >5 cm (AUC 0.85–0.89).
  • King-Devick — rapid number-naming (saccadic speed), <1 min, sensitivity ~81%; any worsening from baseline is abnormal — adds the vision piece SCAT6/MACE2 miss.
  • Near point of convergence (NPC) — target to the nose; normal ≤5 cm, higher suggests CI.
  • Accommodative amplitude — push-up or minus-lens vs age-expected norm (Hofstetter); reduced = AI.
  • Near visual acuity — test separately from distance (commonly overlooked).

Urgent eye-care referral — red flags (VA/DoD)

Vision loss or decline, new diplopia, abnormal pupils, abnormal external eye exam, abnormal visual behavior (unexpectedly bumping into things), nystagmus, or acute ocular symptoms (severe eye pain, flashes/floaters, severe photophobia) → urgent neuro-ophthalmology/ophthalmology. Exclude a cranial-nerve or structural cause — localize the palsy (CN III, IV, VI) before attributing diplopia to convergence insufficiency or accommodative dysfunction.

docs.title: Treat
docs.text:
Convergence / accommodative insufficiency — office-based therapy (Level 1)

  • Refer for office-based vergence/accommodative therapy with movement (OBVAM) — the CONCUSS RCT (BJSM 2026), the first randomized trial for concussion-related CI, found 88% success with immediate therapy vs 8% delayed at 6 weeks (NPC improved 7.9 vs 1.8 cm; PFV +17.5Δ vs 2.5Δ; 79% symptom improvement vs 13%), and a secondary analysis showed 86% resolution of accommodative insufficiency vs 28%. Refer early — immediate therapy shortens the symptomatic period and speeds return to activity.
  • Evidence hierarchy — prescribe the right thing:
    • Office-based vergence/accommodative therapy is best (CITT high-certainty; CONCUSS extends it to concussion CI).
    • Home pencil push-ups alone = placebo — a common misconception; do not prescribe as standalone treatment.
    • Saccade and pursuit training have insufficient evidence; there is no rationale for binasal occlusion, yoked prisms, or monocular vertical prisms (AAN/NANOS 2022).
    • Trial, then reassess — VA/DoD rates the evidence low and cautions against prolonged therapy that fosters a "sick role."

Photophobia — relieve, but don't lock in dark adaptation

  • FL-41 or gray-tinted lenses for symptomatic relief (both improve light tolerance; 2026 cohort).
  • But prolonged tinted-lens use may impede long-term adaptation — 1-year light-intolerance recovery was less likely in tinted-lens wearers (AAN/NANOS 2022). Pair with graded light exposure; avoid indefinite dark-adaptation behavior.
  • Treat the underlying migraine phenotype — individualized spectral filters in a military cohort (n=392) cut monthly migraines 14.8 → 1.9 with 74% migraine-free and >70% less medication (see headache).

Diplopia / strabismus — prisms first

  • For small-angle strabismus or decompensated phorias, prism glasses give immediate relief and are first-line before surgery or vision therapy.
  • Fresnel prisms are a good temporary measure while monitoring for spontaneous recovery — traumatic CN palsies often improve over 3–6 months. Persistent/large-angle or afferent problems → neuro-ophthalmology.

Vision, vestibular, and headache overlap — treat them together

Oculomotor deficits and symptom severity track with poorer dynamic mobility — a vision–vestibular–balance triad that benefits from coordinated multimodal rehab. The VOMS deliberately spans both vestibular and oculomotor domains because they can't be cleanly separated (see vestibular).

Military note: aircrew and other visually-demanding duties are especially sensitive to persistent oculomotor deficits; document objective findings (NPC, VOMS, King-Devick, fields) for the TBI hub retention/aeromedical framing.

docs.title: References
docs.text:
1. Merezhinskaya N, Mallia RK, Park D, et al. Visual deficits associated with TBI — SR/meta-analysis (Optom Vis Sci 2019) — CI 36.3%, accommodative dysfunction 42.8%, visual-field loss 18.2%.
  2. Master CL, Bacal D, Grady MF, et al. Vision and concussion — symptoms, signs, evaluation, treatment (Pediatrics 2022); Thiagarajan P, Ciuffreda KJ. Accommodative/pupillary dysfunction in mTBI (NeuroRehabilitation 2022).
  3. Subramanian PS, Barton JJS, Ranalli P, et al. Consensus statement on visual rehabilitation in mTBI (Neurol Clin Pract 2022) — AAN/NANOS taxonomy; evidence for/against specific therapies; tinted-lens caution.
  4. Ventura RE, Balcer LJ, Galetta SL. Neuro-ophthalmology of head trauma (Lancet Neurol 2014) — CN palsies, King-Devick; Kaae C, et al. VOMS in mTBI (NeuroRehabilitation 2022) — vision-vestibular overlap.
  5. VA/DoD. Management and Rehabilitation of Post-Acute mTBI CPG (2021) — basic PCP eye exam, urgent referral red flags, evidence caution.
  6. Mucha A, et al. Brief VOMS assessment (Am J Sports Med 2014); Ferris LM, et al. VOMS predictive accuracy — NCAA-DoD CARE (Am J Sports Med 2021; Vision Res 2022) — cutoffs/AUC.
  7. Le RK, et al. King-Devick sensitivity/specificity (J Athl Train 2023); Harmon KG, et al. Validity of King-Devick in NCAA D-I athletes (Sports Health 2026).
  8. Alvarez TL, Scheiman M, Hajebrahimi F, et al. CONCUSS RCT of vergence/accommodative therapy for concussion-related symptomatic CI (Br J Sports Med 2026); Scheiman M, et al. CONCUSS secondary analysis — accommodative dysfunction (Br J Sports Med 2026).
  9. Scheiman M, Kulp MT, Cotter SA, et al. Interventions for convergence insufficiency — network meta-analysis (Cochrane 2020) — office-based > home > pencil push-ups (= placebo).
  10. Aguilar MC, et al. Light-filtering lenses and visual photosensitivity in TBI (Invest Ophthalmol Vis Sci 2026); Tosta S, et al. Individualized spectral filters in military brain trauma (Brain Inj 2024); Bansal S, Green K. Colored filters post-TBI — review (NeuroRehabilitation 2022).
  11. Simpson-Jones ME, Hunt AW. Vision rehabilitation interventions following mTBI — scoping review (Disabil Rehabil 2019) — prisms.

Verified July 2026: prevalence (CI ~36%, AI ~43%, visual-field loss ~18%; 69% of persisting concussions with ≥1 vision disorder), the AAN/NANOS diagnostic spectrum, assessment cutoffs (VOMS ≥2/NPC >5 cm, King-Devick, accommodative amplitude), the CONCUSS RCT (Level 1 evidence for office-based vergence/accommodative therapy in concussion CI), the evidence hierarchy (office-based > home; pencil push-ups = placebo; saccade/pursuit training and yoked/binasal prisms unsupported), the nuanced photophobia guidance (FL-41 relief but graded exposure; individualized spectral filters), prisms for diplopia, and the VA/DoD urgent-referral red flags reflect the 2022 AAN/NANOS statement, VA/DoD (2021) CPG, and the peer-reviewed sources cited above. Confirm current specialty-referral pathways before disposition.
